title: AINTMA: Agentic AI Architecture for Autonomous Test Management with Generative Intelligence, Secure Cloud Communication and Adaptive Quality Analytics
comment: 11 pages, 2 figures, 4 tables, Submitted to AICCONS (AIP Conference Proceedings format)
summary: Modern software quality assurance demands intelligent, autonomous systems capable of adaptive decision-making across distributed cloud environments. This paper presents AINTMA (Agentic Intelligent Test Management Architecture), a multi-agent agentic AI system that transforms traditional test management into an autonomous quality intelligence ecosystem. AINTMA deploys six specialized AI agents (Test Discovery, Risk Assessment, Reinforcement Learning Prioritization, Execution Orchestration, Generative Quality Intelligence, and Cloud Security Monitor) coordinated through a secure multi-agent communication framework over a cloud-native microservices infrastructure. The Generative Quality Intelligence agent employs large language models to produce plain language quality narratives, defect risk summaries, and data-augmented test recommendations. The RL Prioritization agent models test selection as a Markov Decision Process, learning contextual policies from large-scale historical test execution data (47 features, rolling 36-month window). Secure cloud communication is enforced through a zero-trust API gateway with OAuth2/JWT authentication, encrypted inter-agent messaging, and multi-tenant isolation. Evaluation across 12 heterogeneous software projects over 18 months demonstrates: 88.4% test prioritization accuracy (APFD, vs. 51.2% random, 82.1% best commercial baseline); 43% test cycle time reduction; defect escape rate reduced from 8.3% to 2.1%; 340% ROI at 9-month payback. The agentic architecture scales to 50,000+ test cases with sub-400ms response time, and the generative intelligence module achieves 4.3/5.0 developer usefulness rating. AINTMA demonstrates that agentic AI, combining autonomous multi-agent coordination, generative intelligence and secure smart connectivity, can fundamentally advance software quality management in cloud-scale enterprise environments.

AI.tldr: AINTMA is a cloud-native, multi-agent AI architecture that autonomously manages and optimizes software testing, using RL-based prioritization, LLM-based quality insights, and secure distributed coordination to improve speed, accuracy, and ROI at enterprise scale.
AI.motivation: Traditional software test management in cloud-scale environments is manual, siloed, and unable to adapt quickly to changing risk, scale, and distributed architectures. Existing tools lack autonomous decision-making, integrated risk-aware prioritization, and human-friendly, data-driven quality insights. There is a need for an intelligent, secure, scalable system that can continuously optimize what to test, when, and why, across large, heterogeneous projects.
AI.method: The paper proposes AINTMA, an Agentic Intelligent Test Management Architecture, composed of six specialized AI agents: Test Discovery, Risk Assessment, RL-based Test Prioritization, Execution Orchestration, Generative Quality Intelligence, and Cloud Security Monitor. These agents run on a cloud-native microservices platform and communicate via a secure, zero-trust, OAuth2/JWT-protected multi-agent framework with encrypted messaging and multi-tenant isolation. The RL agent formulates test prioritization as an MDP trained on large-scale historical test execution data (47 features, 36‑month window), while the generative agent uses large language models to create narrative explanations, risk summaries, and test recommendations.
AI.result: Across 12 heterogeneous projects over 18 months, AINTMA achieves: 88.4% test prioritization effectiveness (APFD) versus 51.2% for random and 82.1% for the best commercial baseline; 43% reduction in test cycle time; reduction in defect escape rate from 8.3% to 2.1%; 340% ROI with a 9‑month payback period. The architecture scales to more than 50,000 test cases with sub‑400 ms response times, and the generative module receives a 4.3/5.0 usefulness rating from developers.
AI.conclusion: AINTMA shows that an agentic, multi-agent AI architecture that combines reinforcement learning, generative models, and secure cloud-native coordination can substantially improve software quality management in enterprise cloud environments. It delivers higher defect detection efficiency, faster test cycles, strong scalability, and positive economic returns, indicating that autonomous quality intelligence ecosystems are a viable evolution beyond traditional test management tools.

title: Marking the Wrong Symptoms: Evaluating LLM Watermarks in Medical Texts
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly integrated into clinical workflows, stressing the need for reliable traceability of model-generated output with watermarking. Yet, most watermarks are evaluated on general-purpose benchmarks, leaving domains like medicine, where small token-level perturbations can result in significant semantic changes, underexplored. In this work, we present the first rigorous study of how LLM watermarks affect medical performance, benchmarking 5 watermarking schemes across 11 LLMs and 7 VLMs on various tasks spanning unimodal and multimodal clinical reasoning. Importantly, we complement existing evaluations by introducing a human-expert-validated pipeline for systematically auditing medical reasoning quality, terminological precision, and induced hallucinations. Our results reveal that watermarking can induce substantial degradation across multiple failure modes, including lexical corruption, hallucinated terminology, and amplified misattribution or omission of image findings. Notably, we find that the absence of domain-specific analyses, combined with aggregate metrics that miss failures inherent to clinical text, can systematically obscure practical watermark-induced degradations. Our findings establish domain-specific evaluation as a prerequisite for the safe deployment of watermarked models in medicine, where current benchmarks can otherwise mask clinically consequential failures.

AI.tldr: First systematic study of how watermarking large language and vision-language models affects medical tasks, showing that watermarks can significantly harm clinical reasoning and concluding that domain-specific evaluation is essential before deployment in medicine.
AI.motivation: While watermarking is important for tracing AI-generated text, existing evaluations use generic benchmarks and ignore high-stakes domains like medicine, where even small textual perturbations can have large clinical consequences. There is no rigorous understanding of how watermarking interacts with medical reasoning quality and safety.
AI.method: Benchmark five different watermarking algorithms applied to 11 LLMs and 7 VLMs on a suite of unimodal (text-only) and multimodal (image+text) clinical reasoning tasks. Develop and apply a human-expert-validated auditing pipeline to assess medical reasoning quality, terminological precision, and hallucinations, going beyond standard aggregate metrics.
AI.result: Watermarking often degrades clinical performance in multiple ways: it corrupts medical terminology, increases hallucinated terms, and worsens misattribution or omission of findings in text and image-based reasoning. Many of these failures are not captured by standard, aggregate evaluation metrics, so generic benchmarks systematically under-report the harm induced by watermarking in medical settings.
AI.conclusion: Watermarking schemes that look acceptable on general benchmarks can introduce clinically significant errors in medical applications. Therefore, domain-specific, fine-grained evaluation is necessary before deploying watermarked LLMs/VLMs in medicine; otherwise, important safety-related degradations remain hidden.

title: ClickGuard: Detecting and Spoiling Clickbait News with Informativeness Measures and Large Language Models
summary: This paper presents an AI-driven browser extension that identifies clickbait to help users avoid misleading Internet articles. Moving beyond traditional detection, the application employs a hybrid machine learning architecture that combines transformer-based embeddings with linguistically motivated features and a custom "baitness" score. After evaluating various natural language processing techniques -- from classic vectorizers to large language model (LLM) embeddings -- an XGBoost-based model was developed that achieves an F1-score of 91% on the open combined dataset. Most importantly, the tool can warn users before and after they access a clickbait article. After opening an article, the user receives a percentage score indicating the likelihood that it is clickbait. The prediction is explained based on the analyzed metrics, including those specifically developed within the proposed system. The browser extension also provides a clickbait spoiler -- a one- to two-sentence summary of the entire article. Demo video:https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IJ1gkQV82C4}{https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IJ1gkQV82C4

AI.tldr: An AI-powered browser extension detects and explains clickbait, warning users and summarizing articles, using a hybrid NLP model that reaches 91% F1 on public data.
AI.motivation: Clickbait headlines and articles mislead readers, waste time, and erode trust online. Existing clickbait detection is often limited to research settings, focuses mainly on headlines, and does not provide actionable, user-facing tools or understandable explanations. The authors aim to move from pure detection benchmarks to a practical, interpretable system that can help everyday users recognize and avoid misleading content in real browsing scenarios.
AI.method: The authors design a browser extension backed by a hybrid machine learning pipeline. Text from candidate articles is encoded using transformer-based embeddings and enriched with linguistically inspired handcrafted features. They introduce a custom 'baitness' score derived from these metrics. Multiple NLP representations are compared, including traditional vectorizers and LLM-based embeddings. An XGBoost classifier is trained on an open combined dataset to distinguish clickbait from non-clickbait. The model outputs both a probability of clickbait and feature-based explanations, which are integrated into the browser extension UI alongside a spoiler summary produced for each article.
AI.result: The final XGBoost-based hybrid model achieves an F1-score of 91% on an open combined dataset for clickbait detection. The implemented browser extension can issue warnings both before and after article access, present a numerical likelihood score for clickbait, show interpretable metric-based explanations including custom system features, and generate a brief one- to two-sentence spoiler summary of the article content. A demo video illustrates the system in action.
AI.conclusion: The paper demonstrates that a hybrid architecture combining transformer embeddings, linguistically motivated features, and gradient-boosted trees can deliver high-performance clickbait detection (91% F1) that is practical and explainable for end users. By implementing the model as a browser extension with real-time warnings, a quantitative clickbait likelihood score, explanation of contributing factors, and concise spoilers, the work bridges the gap between academic clickbait detection research and everyday user protection against misleading online content.

title: Stochastic Sampling is Epistemically Shallow: The Dimensionality Gap Between Temperature Variation and Model Diversity in LLMs
comment: 8 pages, 4 figures, 3 tables. Accepted at EIML@ICML 2026
summary: When a language model gives different answers on repeated runs, does that variation reveal what it does not know? Self-consistency turns the variation into a per-question uncertainty estimate via majority voting. But does the same variation reveal cross-question structure -- related questions flipping together, the way a diverse ensemble does? We compare two regimes on the same questions: one model run $100$ times at $τ=1$ versus an ensemble of $24$ LLMs run once each at $τ=0$. A Marchenko--Pastur random-matrix test separates signal from sampling noise on both sides. Within any single model, at most one dimension rises above noise across five families and three benchmarks (MMLU, HellaSwag, GSM8K). Across the ensemble, four eigenvalues clear the noise edge, while a matched-difficulty Bernoulli null produces at most one in $500$ Monte Carlo draws. Self-consistency gives accurate per-question uncertainty but no detectable cross-question structure; only a diverse ensemble surfaces what a model does not know.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates whether variations in repeated runs of a single LLM contain the same kind of structured information about "what the model doesn’t know" as variations across a diverse ensemble of different LLMs. It finds that while self-consistency (many samples from one model) yields good per-question uncertainty estimates, it does not reveal meaningful cross-question structure; such structure only appears when using an ensemble of diverse models.
AI.motivation: Language models often give different answers when sampled multiple times with temperature. This variability is already used for self-consistency to improve accuracy and get per-question uncertainty. However, in classical ensemble learning, diversity across models encodes rich structure: related questions tend to succeed or fail together, exposing clusters of weaknesses and strengths. The authors want to know whether sampling a single model many times can replicate that cross-question structure, or whether only a true ensemble of distinct models can reveal deeper patterns about what the model (or models) do not know.
AI.method: They study two regimes on the same question sets: (1) a single LLM sampled 100 times at temperature τ=1 (stochastic self-consistency regime), and (2) an ensemble of 24 different LLMs, each sampled once at τ=0 (deterministic ensemble regime). They evaluate on three benchmarks (MMLU for knowledge and reasoning, HellaSwag for commonsense inference, GSM8K for math word problems) and five model families. For each regime they form a model-by-question correctness matrix and apply a Marchenko–Pastur random matrix test to its spectrum, which allows them to distinguish genuine low-dimensional structure from pure sampling noise. They compare the number and size of eigenvalues above the Marchenko–Pastur noise edge across regimes and against a matched-difficulty Bernoulli null model generated via Monte Carlo.
AI.result: In the self-consistency regime (one model, many stochastic samples), they find that within any single model, the spectrum of the correctness matrix shows at most one eigenvalue above the Marchenko–Pastur noise threshold across all five model families and three benchmarks. This indicates essentially no detectable cross-question structure beyond a single global factor like overall difficulty. In contrast, in the ensemble regime (24 models, one deterministic sample each), four eigenvalues rise clearly above the noise edge. A matched-difficulty Bernoulli null model, designed to reflect only per-question difficulty and independent errors, almost never produces as many large eigenvalues (at most one in 500 Monte Carlo draws), confirming that the extra eigenvalues in the real ensemble reflect genuine shared structure in which questions tend to flip together across models.
AI.conclusion: Self-consistency with a single model is effective for obtaining accurate per-question uncertainty estimates, but the variation across samples does not encode rich cross-question structure: it behaves like mostly independent coin flips conditioned on per-item difficulty. In contrast, a diverse ensemble of different LLMs exhibits multiple significant latent dimensions of correlated successes and failures, revealing structured patterns of what models know and do not know. Therefore, to diagnose and exploit cross-question structure in model performance—such as discovering clusters of systematically hard questions—one needs diversity across models, not merely stochasticity from one model.

title: What is Good? Extracting and Testing Implicit Theories of Literary Quality from LLM Reasoning Traces
summary:
What makes writing "good" remains a persistent question in literary studies and computational linguistics. We present a two-study investigation of how reasoning-enabled LLMs evaluate literary quality.
  In Study 1, we construct a benchmark of 30 real texts spanning six quality tiers, from canonical literature to anonymous forum posts, and extract the model's implicit theory of quality from its reasoning traces. Across five DeepSeek replications, the model achieves 79.3% mean tier-classification accuracy. The traces reveal a consistent stated theory: the model values intentionality over correctness, prioritizing craft, depth, and distinctive voice. A familiarity experiment with style-matched but unrecognizable passages suggests that source recognition may inflate scores, although this is confounded by genuine quality differences between canonical originals and researcher-written pastiches.
  In Study 2, we probe this theory through systematic degradation of five canonical prose passages. We apply six manipulations - vocabulary simplification, rhythm flattening, imagery removal, voice genericization, structure simplification, and combined degradation - and reevaluate each version. Vocabulary simplification causes the smallest quality loss (0.41 +/- 0.46 points), far below structure (2.78) or voice (2.34) loss. Combined degradation is devastating (-5.64) but subadditive. An exploratory comparison with Qwen QwQ shows the same broad qualitative pattern.
  Together, these studies suggest that LLM judgments of writing quality are holistic, author-specific, and more sensitive to structural than lexical features, with implications for automated writing feedback and computational aesthetics.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how reasoning-enabled large language models (LLMs) judge literary quality, finding they emphasize structure, voice, and holistic craft over vocabulary and surface correctness.
AI.motivation: There is a long-standing question in literary studies and computational linguistics about what makes writing "good," and LLMs are increasingly used for writing evaluation and feedback. However, we lack a clear understanding of the internal criteria and biases that such models apply when judging literary quality. This paper aims to uncover and characterize the implicit theory of literary quality held by reasoning-enabled LLMs, both to better understand the models and to inform their use in automated writing assessment and computational aesthetics.
AI.method: The paper conducts two studies using reasoning-enabled LLMs. In Study 1, the authors build a benchmark of 30 real texts categorized into six quality tiers ranging from canonical literature to anonymous forum posts. They run five replications with a DeepSeek model, asking it to classify each text's quality tier and then analyze its chain-of-thought reasoning traces to infer the model's implicit theory of quality. They also conduct a familiarity experiment using style-matched but unrecognizable passages to test whether source recognition affects quality judgments. In Study 2, they take five canonical prose passages and systematically degrade them via six targeted manipulations: vocabulary simplification, rhythm flattening, imagery removal, voice genericization, structure simplification, and a combined degradation. The model re-evaluates each version, and the authors measure how each manipulation affects perceived quality. An exploratory comparison is made with another model, Qwen QwQ, on the same degradation paradigm.
AI.result: In Study 1, across five replications, the DeepSeek model attains a mean accuracy of 79.3% in assigning texts to their correct quality tiers. Analysis of reasoning traces shows a consistent explicit theory of quality: the model emphasizes intentionality, craft, depth, and a distinctive authorial voice over mere correctness. The familiarity experiment suggests that recognition of canonical sources may partially inflate quality scores, but this effect is difficult to isolate from genuine quality differences between canonical texts and researcher-written imitations. In Study 2, degradation experiments show that simplifying vocabulary causes the least drop in judged quality (around 0.41 ± 0.46 points), while simplifying structure (2.78 points loss) and genericizing voice (2.34 points loss) have much larger negative effects. Applying all degradations together results in a large but subadditive quality loss of about −5.64 points. Qwen QwQ exhibits a broadly similar pattern of sensitivities across manipulations.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning-enabled LLMs appear to hold a relatively stable and human-interpretable theory of literary quality that prioritizes structural coherence, author-specific voice, and holistic craft over lexical sophistication or surface correctness. Their quality judgments are influenced by familiarity with canonical texts but cannot be fully reduced to source recognition. The different sensitivity to structural and stylistic manipulations versus vocabulary changes suggests that LLM-based evaluators are more attuned to higher-level narrative and stylistic organization than to word-level ornamentation. These findings have implications for how LLMs should be designed and used in automated writing feedback systems and in research on computational aesthetics, highlighting both their promise and their potential biases.

title: JAXBench: Benchmarking Autonomous TPU Kernel Optimization
summary: Rigorous benchmarks have driven progress in autonomous GPU kernel performance optimization by establishing a shared target to hillclimb on, but no equivalent exists for TPUs. We present JAXBench, a TPU-native benchmark suite for AI-generated kernel optimization on Google Cloud TPUs. JAXBench comprises 50 JAX workloads that are both relevant and provide headroom for optimization. We extract 17 production ML operators from architectures in the public MaxText library such as Llama-3.1, DeepSeek-V3, Mixtral, Mamba-2, and AlphaFold2, and translate 33 operators from KernelBench that are validated for correctness and set with new problem sizes that achieve high TPU v6e MXU utilization. Eight of the 17 production operators ship with hand-optimized Pallas kernels from the public Tokamax library and block-size tuned to establish an expert upper-bound baseline. We evaluate four feedback-driven methods on generating candidate Pallas kernels for JAXBench. Across the full suite with Gemini 3 Flash, we find that target-specific context matters more than model scale on a sparsely-documented DSL like Pallas. Conditioning on curated TPU documentation raises per-sample correctness from 5.8% to 37.3% and solves 48 of 50 benchmarks at a 1.28x geomean speedup. Search structure yields significant gains once correctness is achieved, with Autocomp's beam-search pipeline reaching a 1.36x geomean speedup over XLA. On the 8 hand-tuned kernels, Autocomp reaches 1.60x geomean over XLA, recovering most of the 2.08x Tokamax upper bound but trailing on the specialized paged and ragged attention operators. High-quality TPU kernel optimization remains a challenging task, and we release the JAXBench benchmark, evaluation harness, and baseline results to support open source contributions.

AI.tldr: Introduces JAXBench, a TPU-native benchmark suite for evaluating AI-generated JAX/Pallas kernels on Google Cloud TPUs, and shows that documentation-aware, search-based methods can generate kernels that significantly outperform XLA and approach expert hand-tuned performance.
AI.motivation: There is no established, rigorous benchmark for autonomous kernel optimization on TPUs comparable to what exists for GPUs, hampering systematic progress on AI-generated kernel compilers and optimizers for TPU hardware. Existing workloads and operators are either GPU-centric, not tuned for modern TPU architectures, or lack realistic, production-grade scenarios. The authors aim to create a standardized, TPU-native benchmark that captures real ML workloads and allows quantitative comparison of automated kernel generation methods against strong expert baselines.
AI.method: The authors construct JAXBench, a suite of 50 JAX workloads runnable on Google Cloud TPUs. They: (1) extract 17 real production ML operators from public MaxText architectures (e.g., Llama-3.1, DeepSeek-V3, Mixtral, Mamba-2, AlphaFold2); (2) port 33 operators from the existing KernelBench benchmark, adjusting problem sizes to ensure high MXU utilization on TPU v6e; and (3) include 8 operators that also have hand-optimized Pallas kernels from the Tokamax library with tuned block sizes to provide expert upper bounds. They then benchmark four feedback-driven kernel-generation methods that produce Pallas kernels, focusing especially on Gemini 3 Flash and Autocomp’s beam-search pipeline. They experiment with different prompt conditioning—especially adding curated TPU-specific documentation—and with different search structures such as beam search over candidate kernels. They evaluate each method by correctness rate, fraction of benchmarks solved, and geomean speedup over the standard XLA baseline.
AI.result: Conditioning the model on curated TPU documentation substantially improves kernel correctness: per-sample correctness increases from 5.8% without documentation to 37.3% with it. With this documentation conditioning, the system solves 48 out of 50 JAXBench benchmarks and achieves a 1.28x geometric mean speedup over XLA across the suite. Introducing structured search (Autocomp’s beam search) on top of correctness-enabling context yields further performance gains, reaching a 1.36x geomean speedup over XLA across all tasks. On the subset of 8 operators that also have expert hand-tuned Tokamax kernels, the automated method achieves a 1.60x geomean speedup over XLA, capturing most of the 2.08x Tokamax upper bound but still underperforming on more specialized paged and ragged attention operators.
AI.conclusion: JAXBench provides the first TPU-native, realistic benchmark suite for AI-generated kernel optimization in JAX/Pallas, filling a key gap compared to established GPU benchmarks. The experiments show that, for a low-level and sparsely documented DSL like Pallas, high-quality TPU-specific documentation and prompt conditioning are more critical than model size for achieving correctness, and that search-based generation strategies are effective once correctness is reached. Despite achieving substantial speedups over XLA and approaching expert-tuned performance for many operators, there remains a non-trivial gap on complex attention-style kernels, highlighting that TPU kernel optimization is still challenging. The authors release JAXBench, its evaluation harness, and baseline results to catalyze further open-source research and progress in automated TPU kernel optimization.

title: Knowledge Injection Exists in MoE? Exploring Expert-Aware Contrast Decoding in MoE for Mitigating LLMs'Hallucinations
summary: Existing LLM hallucination mitigation methods, including prompt engineering and model optimization, either hardly alter models'internal knowledge or have poor cross-domain generalization. Contrastive decoding mitigates hallucinations by using layer-wise differences in LLMs. However, prior studies only explore transformer-based models (e.g., GPT), ignoring other effective frameworks like mixture-of-experts (MoE) models. Since MoE alters the traditional transformer architecture, we conduct empirical studies to investigate whether similar layer-wise differences exist in MoEs. Our results show that they do not exist in MoE with shared experts; nevertheless, across different MoEs, higher layers exhibit distinct expert activation patterns between factual and non-factual outputs. Building on these, we propose EAACD, an expert-aware adaptive contrast decoding that uses expert differences in MoE's higher layers to mitigate hallucinations on QA tasks. EAACD splits high-layer experts into a higher-reliability group and several lower-reliability groups based on their confidence and consistency. It contrasts the higher-reliability group's prediction with each lower-reliability group's prediction to calibrate the model's original predictions. To strengthen this contrast, EAACD amplifies hallucinations from lower-reliability experts via attention and masking to provide stronger negative references. EAACD outperforms all baselines on four datasets.

AI.tldr: The paper studies hallucination mitigation in mixture-of-experts (MoE) LLMs and proposes EAACD, an expert-aware adaptive contrastive decoding approach that leverages expert activation differences in higher layers to reduce hallucinations in QA tasks.
AI.motivation: Most hallucination mitigation methods for LLMs either fail to change internal knowledge meaningfully or generalize poorly across domains, and existing contrastive decoding work focuses only on transformer-based models, overlooking MoE architectures. Because MoE changes the standard transformer structure, it is unclear whether the same layer-wise contrastive signals used in transformers exist in MoEs, motivating an empirical investigation and new decoding method specialized for MoEs.
AI.method: The authors empirically analyze MoE models to see whether layer-wise differences between factual and non-factual outputs exist, focusing on expert activation patterns across layers and between different MoE models. They find that within a single shared-expert MoE these differences are absent, but across different MoE models, higher layers show distinct activation patterns. Leveraging this, they design EAACD, which partitions high-layer experts into a high-reliability group and multiple low-reliability groups using confidence and consistency metrics. EAACD then performs adaptive contrastive decoding by comparing predictions from the high-reliability group with those from each low-reliability group, amplifying hallucinations from low-reliability experts using attention reweighting and masking to create strong negative references during decoding.
AI.result: Empirical experiments on four QA datasets show that EAACD reduces hallucinations more effectively than all baseline methods, demonstrating that expert-aware contrastive decoding tailored to MoE architectures yields better hallucination mitigation than prior approaches designed for standard transformers.
AI.conclusion: Layer-wise contrastive signals used in standard transformer-based LLMs do not directly carry over to shared-expert MoE architectures, but higher-layer expert activation differences across MoE models can be exploited. By organizing experts into reliability-based groups and contrasting their predictions, EAACD effectively mitigates hallucinations in MoE LLMs and outperforms existing methods on multiple QA benchmarks.

title: DC-Leap: Training-Free Acceleration of dLLMs via Draft-Guided Contiguous Leaping Decoding
summary: While parallel decoding is central to the efficiency of Diffusion Large Language Models (dLLMs), current strategies are often hindered by overly conservative confidence thresholds. These thresholds, necessitated by the Joint Probability Dependence Error (JPDE), result in redundant denoising iterations and suboptimal inference speeds. To overcome this, we propose DC-Leap, a training-free framework that enables reliable acceleration of dLLMs in the moderate-confidence regime. DC-Leap introduces a Dynamic Contiguous Verification strategy that integrates strictly-ordered causal constraints into the parallel decoding process. By progressively validating token dependencies, this mechanism effectively neutralizes the JPDE, enabling reliable acceleration with comparable performance. Furthermore, DC-Leap incorporates the draft-guided decoding mechanism, where the draft helps extend the context by leaping forward across multiple tokens, providing look-ahead context and retaining the structural benefits of bidirectional attention during inference. Extensive experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrate that DC-Leap achieves substantial speedups, up to 53.19x on MBPP for long-sequence generation, and up to 105.02x when combined with KV-Cache with comparable generation quality. Code is available at https://github.com/ffh-wyls/DC-Leap .

AI.tldr: DC-Leap is a training-free framework that accelerates diffusion LLMs by safely using moderate-confidence parallel decoding, eliminating redundant steps while preserving generation quality.
AI.motivation: Parallel decoding in diffusion LLMs is slowed down by conservative confidence thresholds required to avoid Joint Probability Dependence Error (JPDE), leading to many unnecessary denoising iterations and limiting practical speedups.
AI.method: DC-Leap introduces Dynamic Contiguous Verification, which injects strictly ordered causal constraints into parallel decoding and progressively validates token dependencies to cancel JPDE; it also adds draft-guided decoding, where a draft sequence leaps forward several tokens to provide look-ahead context and maintain bidirectional structural benefits without retraining.
AI.result: Experiments on standard benchmarks show DC-Leap significantly speeds up diffusion LLM inference, with up to 53.19x acceleration on MBPP for long-sequence generation and up to 105.02x when combined with KV-Cache, while keeping generation quality comparable to baselines.
AI.conclusion: By dynamically enforcing causality and using draft-guided look-ahead during decoding, DC-Leap enables reliable, training-free acceleration of diffusion LLMs in moderate-confidence regimes, mitigating JPDE and achieving large practical speedups without sacrificing output quality.

title: Is MoE Routing a Huffman Code? Discovering the Frequency-Diversity Law in Chain-of-Thought
summary: Mixture-of-Experts architectures have revolutionized scaling, yet the underlying logic of their routing remains a black box. In this paper, we uncover a fundamental governing principle: MoE routing is not merely selection, but a manifestation of Huffman Coding. We introduce the Frequency-Diversity Law, revealing that state-of-the-art models, such as Phi-3.5-MoE and Gemma-4-27B-A4B, spontaneously act as information-theoretic engines. These models allocate sparse expert resources for common tokens while invoking high-diversity expert committees for rare, complex tasks found in chain-of-thought trajectories. However, we identify a critical redundancy trap in Qwen3.5-35B-A3B: when effective sparsity (k/E_eff) is sufficiently low, load-balancing inadvertently imposes functional redundancy, masking the underlying Huffman efficiency signal. To bridge this gap, we propose Subset Difference Pruning, a surgical strategy to eliminate functional duplicates. We demonstrate that pruning does not degrade reasoning; instead, it unleashes the model's latent Huffman efficiency, forcing the logic to collapse into streamlined, high-density paths. Our findings suggest that the next generation of MoEs should move beyond forced load-balancing toward Minimum Description Length (MDL) optimality, assigning shorter expert-routing codes to high-frequency information and longer, more diverse codes to low-frequency information, thereby transforming routing from a heuristic into a principled compression engine.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) routing behaves like Huffman coding, with expert usage patterns reflecting information-theoretic compression principles, and proposes pruning methods to remove redundant experts and enhance this efficiency.
AI.motivation: MoE models scale efficiently by activating only a subset of experts per token, but the logic behind how tokens are routed to experts is poorly understood. The authors want to uncover whether there is a deeper governing principle to routing beyond heuristic load-balancing, and to explain observed patterns in expert usage in modern MoE LLMs.
AI.method: The authors analyze routing behavior in state-of-the-art MoE LLMs (e.g., Phi-3.5-MoE, Gemma-4-27B-A4B, Qwen3.5-35B-A3B), focusing on how frequently different experts are selected for tokens of varying frequency and complexity, especially in chain-of-thought reasoning trajectories. They formulate the Frequency-Diversity Law to describe how common vs. rare tokens map to sparse vs. diverse expert committees. They then identify redundancy caused by load-balancing at low effective sparsity and propose Subset Difference Pruning to remove functionally duplicate experts, observing the effect on routing patterns and reasoning performance.
AI.result: They find that many MoE models naturally follow an information-theoretic behavior akin to Huffman coding: frequent tokens are routed through fewer experts (shorter codes), while rare or complex reasoning tokens engage more diverse expert sets (longer codes). In Qwen3.5-35B-A3B, heavy load-balancing creates redundant experts that obscure this pattern. Applying Subset Difference Pruning removes such redundancy without harming reasoning ability and makes the underlying Huffman-like efficiency more apparent, producing more streamlined, high-density routing paths.
AI.conclusion: MoE routing can be interpreted as an emergent Huffman coding mechanism governed by a Frequency-Diversity Law. Load-balancing can introduce harmful redundancy that hides this efficiency. By pruning redundant experts and designing routing to optimize Minimum Description Length (MDL), future MoE architectures can treat routing as a principled compression process: frequent information gets short, efficient routing codes, whereas rare, complex information uses longer, more diverse expert paths.

title: InferenceBench: A Benchmark for Open-Ended LLM Inference Optimization by AI Agents
summary: AI agents are increasingly used to automate research and development tasks, yet existing benchmarks typically evaluate them on prescribed workflows or narrow action spaces. Even nominally open-ended tasks can often be solved by retrieving a well-known recipe and tuning a few hyperparameters, making it unclear whether strong results reflect genuine optimization or memorized solutions. We introduce InferenceBench, where an agent must deploy an OpenAI-compatible inference server and optimize the speed of LLM inference. Each agent receives a target LLM, one H100 GPU, an optimization scenario, and a wall-clock time budget of two hours. Three optimization scenarios isolate distinct bottlenecks of inference (prefill latency, decode latency, and concurrent request throughput) and a fourth balances all three at the same time. Across 15 frontier agent configurations, agents reliably improve over a naive PyTorch baseline (up to $8.08\times$) and often match or exceed serving engines with default settings ($4.05\times$ for vLLM), but still fall below a simple hyperparameter search under the same time budget (up to $11.53\times$). Qualitative analysis of agent trajectories shows that although agents enumerate many relevant optimization techniques, they overwhelmingly converge on a single inference framework. They test only a few distinct configurations and spend the remaining budget re-measuring, repairing, or optimizing hyperparameters rather than exploring substantially different strategies. This suggests the bottleneck is not domain knowledge, but the ability to propose diverse configurations, evaluate them systematically, and submit the best identified solution. Overall, InferenceBench reflects the ability of agents to operate in an open-ended AI engineering setting, where memorized solutions lead to limited improvements.

AI.tldr: InferenceBench is a benchmark that tests AI agents on end-to-end, open-ended LLM inference optimization rather than narrow or recipe-like tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing AI agent benchmarks often rely on scripted workflows, limited action spaces, or tasks solvable by recalling standard recipes and tweaking a few hyperparameters, which blurs whether good performance arises from real problem solving or memorization. The authors want a benchmark that captures agents’ ability to operate in realistic, open-ended AI engineering tasks, specifically optimizing LLM inference systems under practical constraints.
AI.method: The authors propose InferenceBench, where an agent must deploy an OpenAI-compatible LLM inference server on a single H100 GPU and optimize its speed within a two-hour wall-clock budget. The benchmark defines three focused optimization scenarios—prefill latency, decode latency, and concurrent request throughput—and a fourth combined scenario that balances all three. They evaluate 15 advanced agent configurations on these scenarios, comparing their performance to a naive PyTorch baseline, to existing serving engines with default configurations (e.g., vLLM), and to a simple hyperparameter search procedure operating under the same time budget. They also qualitatively inspect agent trajectories to understand the strategies and behaviors agents actually use during optimization.
AI.result: The agents consistently improve performance over a naive PyTorch baseline, achieving up to 8.08× speedup, and frequently match or outperform default configurations of serving engines such as vLLM, with up to 4.05× improvement. However, they still underperform a simple time-limited hyperparameter search, which can reach up to 11.53× speedup. Trajectory analysis shows that agents tend to identify many relevant optimization ideas but in practice gravitate toward a single inference framework, run only a small number of distinct configurations, and then spend most of their time re-measuring, fixing issues, or tuning hyperparameters within that narrow choice.
AI.conclusion: InferenceBench reveals that current AI agents can meaningfully optimize LLM inference in an open-ended environment but are limited in their ability to systematically explore diverse configurations and to rigorously evaluate and select among them. Their bottleneck appears less about missing domain knowledge and more about search, exploration, and decision-making over complex configuration spaces. The benchmark thus better reflects agents’ real-world AI engineering capabilities and highlights the need for improved methods that go beyond memorized solutions and local hyperparameter tuning.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Large Language Model Framework for Identification of Cutaneous Immune-Related Adverse Events
summary: This study evaluated a retrieval-augmented, multi-agent large language model (LLM)-driven, human-in-the-loop framework for detecting cutaneous immune-related adverse events (cirAEs) from clinical notes. Compared with unassisted manual review, the LLM-assisted workflow improved accuracy (F1 = 0.88 vs 0.77), inter-rater agreement measured by Cohen's kappa (kappa = 0.82 vs 0.50), and reduced average review time by approximately half. This framework pilots how LLMs can be applied to identify immune-related toxicities across organ systems and, more broadly, enable accurate, scalable, and transparent adverse event data extraction.

AI.tldr: The paper presents and evaluates a retrieval-augmented, multi-agent LLM framework with human oversight to detect cutaneous immune-related adverse events from clinical notes, showing better accuracy, agreement, and speed than manual review.
AI.motivation: Manual identification of cutaneous immune-related adverse events from unstructured clinical notes is time-consuming, inconsistent between reviewers, and difficult to scale; there is a need for a more accurate, efficient, and reproducible method to extract these adverse event data from electronic health records.
AI.method: The authors built a retrieval-augmented, multi-agent large language model system embedded in a human-in-the-loop workflow to scan clinical notes and flag potential cirAEs. Human reviewers then validated and refined the LLM outputs. Performance of this LLM-assisted workflow was compared with traditional unassisted manual chart review using metrics such as F1 score, Cohen’s kappa for inter-rater agreement, and time per case reviewed.
AI.result: Relative to unassisted manual review, the LLM-assisted framework achieved higher accuracy in cirAE detection (F1 = 0.88 vs 0.77), substantially improved inter-rater agreement (Cohen’s kappa = 0.82 vs 0.50), and reduced the average review time by about 50%.
AI.conclusion: A retrieval-augmented, multi-agent LLM with human oversight can more accurately, consistently, and efficiently detect cutaneous immune-related adverse events from clinical notes than manual review alone. This approach can be generalized to identify immune-related toxicities in other organ systems and supports scalable, transparent extraction of adverse event data from clinical documentation.

title: DecodeShare: Tracing the Shared Subspace of LLM Decode-Time Decisions
summary: Large language models (LLMs) handle many tasks with one set of parameters, but under KV-cached inference it is unclear what task-general structure, if any, is used at decode time rather than during prefill. We propose DecodeShare, a protocol that identifies a low-dimensional subspace consistently shared across tasks in decode-time hidden states, and then tests its causal role by removing that subspace only during decoding. In our experiments, disturbing the discovered shared subspace degrades decision performance far more than disturbing either a prefill-derived or random subspace under the same intervention budget. We further show this decode-shared subspace has practical consequences for activation steering: common steering directions can overlap the task-general decode channel. Projecting out this shared subspace directly separates the functional roles of the two components, while evaluating steering vectors at decode-time yields more reliable signal for downstream deployment than prefill-based proxies. Despite its compactness, the shared subspace can serve as a high-leverage causal channel at decode time. Code is available at: https://github.com/Zishan-Shao/decodeshare.git.

AI.tldr: The paper discovers a compact, low-dimensional subspace in LLM hidden states that is consistently used across tasks during decoding, and shows that this subspace is causally important for model decisions and activation steering.
AI.motivation: While LLMs are known to be multi-task with a single set of parameters, it is unclear what shared representation, if any, is actually used at decode time under KV-cached inference, and how this affects causal interventions like activation steering. The authors want to identify whether there is a task-general, decode-time structure that plays a central role in decision making and can be exploited or must be handled carefully in interventions.
AI.method: They introduce DecodeShare, a protocol to (1) discover a low-dimensional subspace that is consistently shared across tasks in the decode-time hidden states, and then (2) test its causal role by selectively removing (projecting out) this subspace only during decoding. They compare the effect of disturbing this decode-shared subspace against disturbing a prefill-derived subspace or a random subspace under the same intervention budget. They also analyze how this subspace interacts with activation steering directions and how projecting it out can separate functional roles between the shared decode channel and steering components.
AI.result: Removing the discovered decode-shared subspace during decoding causes a much larger degradation in decision performance than removing either an analogous prefill-based subspace or a random subspace, under matched intervention strength. They find that commonly used activation steering directions often overlap with this task-general decode channel, and that operating in decode-time states yields more reliable steering signal than using prefill-based approximations. Projecting out the shared subspace helps disentangle task-general processing from steering effects.
AI.conclusion: There exists a compact, low-dimensional subspace in LLM decode-time activations that is shared across tasks and plays a high-leverage, causally important role in decisions. This subspace also interacts nontrivially with activation steering methods: it can both confound steering if not accounted for and, when explicitly separated, enable cleaner, more reliable controllability. DecodeShare provides a practical way to identify and manipulate this decode-time causal channel.

title: More Is Not More: What Matters for Diversity in LLM Opinions?
summary: Large language models are increasingly used to simulate diverse human opinions in open-ended tasks such as synthetic surveys, focus group modeling, and public opinion prediction. However, LLM outputs exhibit systematic opinion homogenization. Practitioners have explored various interventions to increase diversity, but the landscape remains fragmented: different methods are evaluated in isolation with incomparable metrics, and in practice they are typically deployed and upgraded simultaneously, making it difficult to attribute gains to specific components. To advance a more scientific understanding of LLM output diversity, we design a factorial experiment that separates two primary intervention dimensions: input conditioning (operationalized through persona depth) and interaction architecture. We evaluate all conditions on 100 real-user open-ended questions across 7 models, measuring diversity with multiple complementary metrics. Our findings challenge several common assumptions. First, more persona detail does not monotonically increase diversity. The initial step of persona conditioning already captures the majority of the gain, while further elaboration with demographic detail does not consistently improve and can reduce diversity on some models. Second, rather than seeking a single best interaction architecture, we find that different architectures explore largely non-overlapping opinion regions. Combining multiple architectures yields broader coverage than optimizing any one. Third, commonly attempted low-cost alternatives such as raising sampling temperature and adding diversity instructions produce negligible effects compared to structured interventions. Overall, our work demonstrates that diversity is not a product of scaling along any single dimension, but is highly sensitive to the structural form and combination of interventions.

AI.tldr: The paper systematically studies how to increase opinion diversity in LLM-generated responses and shows that structural design of personas and interaction architectures matters more than simple tweaks like temperature or generic diversity prompts.
AI.motivation: LLMs are being used as proxies for human opinions in tasks like synthetic surveys and public opinion analysis, but their outputs tend to be homogenized and less diverse than real populations. Many ad-hoc interventions (personas, instructions, sampling tweaks) are used to increase diversity, but evaluations are fragmented, making it unclear which levers actually work and how they interact. The authors aim to create a more rigorous, comparable framework to understand and improve LLM opinion diversity.
AI.method: The authors run a factorial experiment that varies two main intervention dimensions: (1) input conditioning via different levels of persona detail (from no persona to more elaborate, demographically rich personas), and (2) interaction architecture, i.e., how multiple simulated agents are instantiated and engaged. They test all combinations of these conditions across 7 LLMs on 100 real-user open-ended questions. Opinion diversity is quantified using multiple complementary metrics so that different aspects of diversity are captured and compared.
AI.result: They find: (1) Adding some persona conditioning boosts diversity, but further enriching personas with detailed demographics does not reliably help and can even reduce diversity for some models. (2) Different interaction architectures explore different, largely non-overlapping regions of opinion space; no single architecture is uniformly best, and combining several yields broader opinion coverage. (3) Common, cheaper interventions—like increasing temperature or giving generic instructions to be diverse—have negligible impact compared to these structured persona and architecture interventions.
AI.conclusion: Diversity in LLM-generated opinions is not achieved by simply turning up a single knob (e.g., more persona detail, higher temperature, or one preferred architecture). Instead, it depends sensitively on how personas are structured and how multi-agent interactions are architected, and combining diverse architectures is more effective than optimizing any single setup. The work provides a more scientific basis for designing LLM-based synthetic opinion studies and cautions against over-reliance on simplistic diversity interventions.

title: PlanE: Meta Planning of Data, Tuning, and Inference for Extractive-based LLMs
summary: Enhancing the task-specific capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) primarily requires substantial instruction-tuning datasets. However, the sheer volume of such data imposes a considerable annotation cost, and a lack of optimization methods for tailoring LLMs to specific tasks. To address the above issues, we propose a \textbf{Plan}ning framework for constructing \textbf{E}xtractive-based LLMs called \textbf{PlanE}, which includes data decomposition, instruction tuning, and prompt inference. Additionally, we introduce a Data-Tuning-Inference (DTI) planner, aimed at selecting the optimal base-LLM and its DTI combinations for specific datasets to improve construction efficiency. The experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of our PlanE from two views: (1) across different datasets using the same base-LLM, and (2) on the same dataset using different base-LLMs. Furthermore, we validate the generalizability of the proposed DTI planner under different optimization objectives. The codes are publicly available at https://github.com/gugugu-469/PlanE.

AI.tldr: PlanE is a planning framework that builds extractive-focused LLMs more efficiently by decomposing data, tuning instructions, and planning prompts, plus a DTI planner that chooses the best base model and tuning/inference combo for each dataset.
AI.motivation: Instruction-tuning LLMs for specific tasks needs huge labeled datasets, which are expensive to annotate and lack systematic methods to optimize models for each task. The paper aims to reduce annotation cost and provide a principled way to customize LLMs to extractive tasks.
AI.method: They propose PlanE, a planning framework with three components: (1) data decomposition to restructure datasets for extractive tasks; (2) instruction tuning tailored to those decomposed data; and (3) prompt inference strategies for using the tuned models. On top of this, they design a Data-Tuning-Inference (DTI) planner that, given a dataset, automatically selects the most suitable base LLM and the best combination of data processing, tuning, and inference schemes to maximize performance or efficiency.
AI.result: Experiments show PlanE improves performance in two scenarios: holding the base LLM fixed and varying datasets, and holding the dataset fixed and varying base LLMs. The DTI planner is shown to generalize under different optimization objectives, such as accuracy or efficiency, finding good configurations automatically.
AI.conclusion: PlanE offers a systematic framework for building extractive LLMs that reduces reliance on massive instruction-tuning datasets and better adapts base LLMs to specific tasks. The DTI planner further automates model and configuration selection, demonstrating robust gains and generalizability across datasets, models, and optimization goals.

title: LLM-INSTRUCT at UZH Shared Task 2026: Constraint-Aware Retrieval and Selective Debate for Paragraph-Level Argument Mining
comment: Accepted to the 13th Workshop on Argument Mining (ArgMining 2026) at ACL 2026
summary: We present LLM-INSTRUCT, the winning system for the UZH Shared Task at ArgMining 2026 on paragraph-level argument mining in UN and UNESCO resolutions. The task requires paragraph-type classification, prediction of a subset of 141 official tags, and directed relation prediction under a strict JSON schema setting using only open-weight models up to 8B parameters. We frame the task as constrained structured prediction. The system first narrows the candidate tag space with metadata-aware dense retrieval, then applies constrained decoding with per-dimension caps, escalates only uncertain cases to a three-agent debate branch, and finally validates the output schema. On the official leaderboard, LLM-INSTRUCT ranked 1st overall, with 1st in F1 and 5th in LLM-as-a-Judge. During development, our configuration search further improved Task 1b Micro-F1 from 35.83% to 40.08% while keeping the internal Task 2 score at 4.421. The main lesson is simple: reducing the decision space before generation improves both accuracy and submission robustness. Our code and supporting scripts are publicly available at: https://github.com/LLM-Instruct-at-UZH-Shared-Task-2026/Method

AI.tldr: LLM-INSTRUCT is a constrained structured prediction system that won the ArgMining 2026 UZH Shared Task on paragraph-level argument mining in UN/UNESCO resolutions by narrowing the decision space before generation.
AI.motivation: To tackle a challenging argument mining task over formal UN and UNESCO resolutions using only open-weight models ≤8B, under a strict JSON schema, covering multi-label paragraph tagging and relation prediction, where naive large-label-space generation is inaccurate and brittle.
AI.method: They cast the problem as constrained structured prediction: (1) use metadata-aware dense retrieval to prune the 141-tag candidate space; (2) perform constrained decoding with per-dimension caps; (3) escalate uncertain cases to a three-agent debate branch; and (4) run a final schema validator to enforce the JSON output constraints.
AI.result: LLM-INSTRUCT ranked 1st overall and 1st in F1 on the official leaderboard (5th in LLM-as-a-Judge). Through configuration search, they improved Task 1b Micro-F1 from 35.83% to 40.08% while holding an internal Task 2 score at 4.421.
AI.conclusion: Carefully reducing and structuring the decision space before and during generation—via retrieval-based pruning, constrained decoding, selective debate, and schema validation—substantially boosts both predictive accuracy and robustness of structured submissions in complex argument-mining tasks.

title: Benchmarking the Personalization Capabilities of Large Language Models
summary: Personalization, the act of varying a message to induce action from a specific receiver while keeping sender, channel, and time fixed, has a long tradition in psychology and marketing as a two-party problem in which sender and receiver have independent objectives. Large language models remove the bounded-inventory constraint of classical retrieval-and-ranking approaches by generating a continuum of message variants conditioned on inferred receiver state, raising the question of how well current models perform personalization in the classical sense. Existing LLM personalization benchmarks measure sender-side adaptation, in which the receiver is the same user the model is serving. The two-party question, whether a generated message induces its intended action in a third party, has been investigated only through A/B tests and small-scale human studies that cannot be re-run against a new model on demand. We adapt the Bayesian Persuasion framework of Kamenica and Gentzkow (2011) to generative agents and instantiate the formulation in sales, where receiver actions are routinely logged against the outreach that induced them. We release SDR-Bench, a public corpus of 6,279 customer success stories spanning 22 industries and approximately 200 enterprises, served through a temporally constrained simulation that prevents future-data leakage. Across frontier LLMs and deep-research agents, we observe a consistent personalization plateau and on a Fortune 100 tech cohort no model statistically separates successful from unsuccessful outreach. A field deployment with 12 professional sales representatives validates the framework, with 48 percent of model-generated content rated immediately useful and senior-expert agreement at Pearson 0.82. We release SDR-Arena and SDR-Bench publicly to support reproducible study of generative personalization at scale.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how well current large language models (LLMs) can perform classical two-party personalization—crafting messages that actually change a third party’s behavior—using real-world sales data, and finds that models hit a personalization performance plateau.
AI.motivation: Classical personalization in psychology and marketing is about a sender tailoring a message to a distinct receiver with their own objectives. Traditional retrieval-and-ranking methods were limited by finite message inventories. LLMs can now generate a continuum of personalized messages conditioned on inferred receiver states, raising the need to rigorously assess whether they truly improve persuasion in the classical, two-party sense. Existing benchmarks largely focus on adapting to the user directly interacting with the model (sender-side personalization), not on influencing a third-party receiver’s real behavior. Moreover, prior studies of persuasive effectiveness rely on A/B tests and small human evaluations that are not easily repeatable for new models. This motivates a scalable, reproducible framework and dataset to measure generative personalization that actually drives actions from independent receivers.
AI.method: The authors adapt the Bayesian Persuasion framework of Kamenica and Gentzkow (2011) to the setting of generative agents (LLMs) tasked with crafting persuasive messages. They instantiate this framework in a sales context where outreach emails and resulting customer actions are routinely logged. They construct SDR-Bench, a public corpus of 6,279 customer success stories from ~200 enterprises across 22 industries, and serve it via a temporally constrained simulation that avoids future-data leakage. Using this setup, they benchmark multiple frontier LLMs and deep-research agents on their ability to personalize outreach that induces desired actions. They also conduct a real-world field deployment with 12 professional sales representatives, who evaluate the usefulness of model-generated content and provide ratings allowing comparison with senior experts.
AI.result: In controlled simulations, all tested frontier LLMs and deep-research agents exhibit a “personalization plateau”: they fail to meaningfully differentiate between outreach that historically succeeded versus failed. Specifically, on a cohort from a Fortune 100 technology company, no model achieves statistically significant separation between successful and unsuccessful outreach in terms of predicted or generated content effectiveness. In the field deployment, however, 48% of model-generated messages are rated as immediately useful by professional sales reps, and the agreement between model outputs and senior expert evaluations reaches a Pearson correlation of 0.82, indicating substantial alignment with expert judgment even if behavioral lift is limited.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that despite LLMs’ ability to generate highly tailored messages, their capacity for true classical personalization—changing the behavior of an independent third-party receiver—appears to plateau with current technology. Existing personalization benchmarks that only assess adaptation to the interacting user miss this two-party effectiveness dimension. By introducing SDR-Bench and the SDR-Arena framework, the authors provide a reproducible, scalable way to evaluate generative personalization using real-world sales data and temporally aware simulations. Their field results show that models can still provide practically useful content to sales professionals and align well with expert preferences, but that measurable gains in persuasive impact on customers remain elusive, highlighting an important gap and future research direction in LLM-driven personalization.

title: Skill-Contracted Agents for Evidence-Aware Materials Literature Analysis
summary: Materials science literature analysis requires simultaneous attention to composition, processing, characterization, and property relationships, yet conventional retrieval-augmented generation pipelines struggle to reconcile heterogeneous tasks within a single retrieve-then-generate architecture. Here we present AlphaAgent, a skill-driven agent framework that decouples retrieval-based question answering from paper-level report generation through explicit skill contracts. A dedicated retrieval skill rewrites user requests into material-specific search intents, queries a curated index of more than 300,000 papers from the Journal Citation Reports Metallurgy and Metallurgical Engineering category, and reformulates queries when initial evidence is insufficient. A separate report-generation skill parses full-text PDFs to produce structured per-paper analytical reports and cross-paper summaries. In a blind evaluation on 40 materials-science questions, half of which required deep analytical reasoning, AlphaAgent substantially outperformed a baseline system matched for underlying model, document index, and retrieval scale, with the largest gains in mechanistic explanation and awareness of credibility boundaries. These results indicate that explicit task separation, refined retrieval intent, and evidence-aware generation improve large-language-model-based literature analysis for materials research.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AlphaAgent, a skill-driven agent framework that separates retrieval-based QA from paper-level report generation to improve literature analysis in materials science.
AI.motivation: Conventional retrieval-augmented generation systems struggle with the heterogeneous and coupled nature of materials science tasks, which require simultaneous understanding of composition, processing, characterization, and properties. There is a need for a system that can better handle complex, analytical, and mechanistic questions over large materials-science corpora.
AI.method: The authors design AlphaAgent, an agent framework with explicit skill contracts that separate concerns: a retrieval skill that rewrites user queries into materials-specific search intents, searches a curated index of 300k+ metallurgy papers, and adaptively reformulates queries; and a report-generation skill that parses full-text PDFs to create structured analytical reports and cross-paper summaries. They benchmark AlphaAgent against a baseline with the same model, index, and retrieval scale on a blind evaluation of 40 materials-science questions, half requiring deep reasoning.
AI.result: AlphaAgent significantly outperforms the baseline system on the 40-question blind evaluation, especially on tasks requiring mechanistic explanations and correct recognition of the limits of available evidence. This demonstrates the benefits of explicit skill separation and improved retrieval intent formulation.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly separating retrieval and report-generation skills, refining retrieval intent, and making generation aware of evidence boundaries leads to better large-language-model-based literature analysis in materials science. The AlphaAgent framework offers a more robust and analytically capable approach for materials research questions than conventional monolithic RAG pipelines.

title: Robust Critics: Defending LLMs Against Multi-Turn Attacks
summary:
When a user asks a language model something harmful, is it a genuine attack or a misunderstood but well-meaning question? This ambiguity is one of the central challenges of LLM safety. A model that assumes the worst harms legitimate users; one that assumes the best is easily exploited. The problem is compounded in multi-turn dialogue, where an attacker's true intent may only reveal itself gradually across many exchanges, yet existing safety frameworks apply a contextual bandit treatment, ignoring the trajectory of the conversation.
  To that end, we propose Dialogue Critic Guided Sampling (DCGS), a framework that addresses this by inferring user intent at every turn of dialogue. Instead of applying a fixed rule about what is or is not safe, DCGS learns what the user's intent is likely to be based on the full conversational history and generates responses accordingly. Formally, we model adversarial dialogue as a Markov Decision Process and learn value and regret-based critics at both the individual token and utterance (full response) levels, scoring candidate responses via an action-value critic. We prove that this inference-time reweighting approximates exponential tilting of the base policy, guaranteeing improvement in expected return for any finite candidate pool, a property that group-relative objectives do not exhibit. Evaluated on CARES-18k, WildJailbreak, Redbench, and Harmbench, DCGS outperforms strong robust baselines and frontier models on adversarial dialogue tasks. DCGS also transfers to frontier models, improving their robustness without fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Dialogue Critic Guided Sampling (DCGS), a method that dynamically infers user intent over multi-turn conversations to make LLMs safer and more robust to adversarial dialogue, improving robustness across several benchmarks and transferring to frontier models without fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM safety methods typically treat prompts in isolation (contextual bandit-style), assuming fixed rules about what is safe and ignoring the conversation trajectory. This makes them either over-defensive (hurting helpfulness) or easily exploitable, especially in multi-turn adversarial dialogues where malicious intent emerges gradually. The authors want a principled way to infer user intent over time and condition safety decisions on the full dialogue history.
AI.method: They model adversarial dialogue as a Markov Decision Process (MDP) and introduce Dialogue Critic Guided Sampling (DCGS). DCGS trains value-based and regret-based critics at both token and utterance levels. At inference, for each turn it samples candidate responses from a base policy (the underlying LLM) and re-scores them using an action-value critic that incorporates the entire conversation history and an estimate of user intent. This reweighting is shown to approximate exponential tilting of the base policy. The critics allow fine-grained safety control at both token and full-response levels without retraining the base model.
AI.result: On several adversarial dialogue benchmarks (CARES-18k, WildJailbreak, Redbench, Harmbench), DCGS outperforms strong robust baselines and frontier models at resisting harmful or jailbreak-style prompts. The method also transfers to stronger frontier models: even when applied to them without any fine-tuning, DCGS improves their robustness, showing the framework is modular and model-agnostic.
AI.conclusion: Dialogue safety can be improved by modeling multi-turn adversarial conversations as an MDP and using critic-guided sampling that infers user intent at each turn. DCGS provides a theoretically justified reweighting (exponential tilting) of the base LLM’s outputs, provably improving expected returns over any finite candidate pool. Empirically, it enhances robustness to harmful dialogue across multiple benchmarks and can be attached to existing frontier models without retraining, suggesting a practical, plug-and-play route to safer LLM deployment in adversarial settings.

title: Position: Natural Language Should Not Fully Replace Formal Languages
comment: To be published in ICML 2026 (position track)
summary: Recent advances in large language models and their widespread adoption have prompted claims that natural language could entirely replace formal languages, such as programming languages for software design. In this position paper, we argue that this perspective overlooks fundamental linguistic properties of natural language, specifically that it is optimized for underspecification in open-ended contexts. We introduce a formal framework centered on *task specificity*, defining it as the information-theoretic reduction of uncertainty in an output space -- such as all possible images -- given a user's specific requirements. We prove a *specificity crossover theorem*, showing the existence of a threshold beyond which the cost to express formal requirements into natural language exceeds the cost of direct formal specification. By analyzing case studies across modalities, such as image generation, code synthesis, and audio production, we demonstrate that natural language excels at low specificity tasks, while formal languages are advantageous on tasks with stricter requirements. We conclude that natural and formal languages are complementary tools and advocate the development of hybrid systems that allow users to move across the specificity spectrum.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that natural language cannot fully replace formal languages because it is optimized for underspecified, open‑ended communication, and proposes a framework showing when natural versus formal specification is more efficient.
AI.motivation: With large language models making natural language a powerful interface for many tasks, some have claimed we may no longer need formal languages (like programming languages) for precise specification. The authors want to rigorously examine whether natural language alone can scale to highly precise, constrained tasks, or whether there are inherent limits that keep formal languages necessary.
AI.method: They build an information-theoretic framework around the concept of task specificity, defined as the reduction in uncertainty over an output space given a user’s requirements. Within this framework they prove a “specificity crossover theorem” that characterizes a threshold where describing requirements in natural language becomes more costly than using a formal language. They then apply this to qualitative case studies across different modalities (image generation, code synthesis, audio production).
AI.result: They formally show that as the required specificity of a task increases, the cost of adequately specifying it in natural language eventually exceeds the cost of formal specification. Their case studies support this: natural language works well for loosely constrained, open-ended tasks, while formal languages become superior when constraints and precision needs tighten.
AI.conclusion: Natural and formal languages serve different but complementary roles. Natural language is ideal for low-specificity, open-ended tasks, whereas formal languages excel in high-specificity, tightly constrained tasks. The authors advocate designing hybrid systems where users can fluidly move between natural language and formal specification depending on the needed level of specificity.

title: Incomplete Prompt Jailbreaks in Large Language Models
comment: Accepted to ACL 2026 Findings. 15 pages (9 pages for main body), 13 figures
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly released as open-weight models with safeguards against harmful requests. Nevertheless, sentence completion remains vulnerable to incomplete harmful prompts. In this work, we formalize this phenomenon as incomplete prompt jailbreaks (IPJ) and provide a systematic empirical characterization of when and how incomplete prompts elicit harmful continuations. We analyze diverse attractor types associated with incomplete sentence continuation and show that LLMs systematically delay refusal until sentence termination. We further demonstrate that training models to refuse incomplete harmful prompts via parameter tuning is insufficient, failing to generalize across both content domains and attractor types. To enable fine-grained control, we identify two functional neurons: termination and continuation neurons. By clarifying their roles in sentence completion, we highlight the potential of neuron-level interventions for more precise and robust IPJ defenses.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how large language models can still produce harmful content when given incomplete harmful prompts and proposes neuron-level interventions to better control this behavior.
AI.motivation: Although open-weight LLMs include safety mechanisms, they remain vulnerable when users provide incomplete but suggestive harmful prompts that the model then completes harmfully. Existing safety training does not fully address this, and there is a need to understand and control the model’s internal mechanisms governing sentence completion and refusal timing.
AI.method: The authors define the phenomenon of incomplete prompt jailbreaks (IPJ) and empirically analyze how and when incomplete prompts lead to harmful continuations. They categorize types of attractors that influence incomplete sentence continuation and study the timing of refusals. They then fine-tune models to refuse such prompts and evaluate generalization across domains and attractor types. Finally, they perform mechanistic analysis to identify specific neurons responsible for sentence termination and continuation and test neuron-level interventions.
AI.result: They find that LLMs often postpone refusal until after a sentence is completed, leaving a window where harmful continuations can occur. Safety fine-tuning targeted at incomplete harmful prompts does not generalize well across different content areas or prompt structures. Through mechanistic probing, they discover two distinct functional neurons—termination and continuation neurons—whose activations correlate with how the model decides to end or continue sentences, and show that manipulating these neurons can influence completion behavior.
AI.conclusion: Incomplete prompt jailbreaks are a systematic vulnerability in LLMs, not just edge cases, and current parameter-level safety training is insufficient to robustly prevent harmful continuations. Understanding and controlling dedicated termination and continuation neurons offers a promising, more granular approach to mitigating IPJs and improving the reliability of LLM safety behaviors in sentence completion tasks.

title: Moir: Let the Model Direct Its Own Story for Robust Cross-Domain Knowledge Editing
summary: While language models remain frozen at their training state, the world evolves continuously. Knowledge editing has emerged as a key alternative to full retraining, but its deployment is bottlenecked by the erosion of core capabilities: mathematical and programmatic reasoning collapse while encyclopedic recall remains intact. We trace this asymmetric degradation to a distributional mismatch. Covariance-based editors preserve only the subspaces spanned by their reference corpus, but fail to capture the operative distribution shaped by post-training such as SFT and DPO. Static external corpora, including Wikipedia and even the original pretraining mixture, cannot recover this shifted manifold. We propose Moir, which estimates the preservation covariance $C$ directly from the model itself by sampling from its own decoding distribution.
