Seeding generation with a single random vocabulary token bypasses the instruction-following templates that otherwise dominate sampled outputs, exposing the broader subspaces the model has internalized. Moir requires no external data and serves as a drop-in component for any covariance-based editor, a practical advantage given that the pre- and post-training corpora of most modern LLMs are not publicly accessible. Across OLMo-2, Llama-3.1, and Qwen-3 (7-8B), under both MEMIT and AlphaEdit and in batch and sequential regimes, Moir consistently extends preservation in the most vulnerable domains, most strikingly on Qwen3-8B after 20,000 AlphaEdit batch edits, it retains 79.9% GSM8K accuracy compared to 10.9% with the Wikipedia baseline. These results suggest that aligning the preservation distribution with the model's operative distribution is a key factor in non-destructive editing, and that the model itself may be the most accessible source of that distribution for deployed systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Moir, a method to make knowledge editing of language models less destructive by better preserving core capabilities like math and coding.
AI.motivation: Language models become outdated as the world changes, but retraining is expensive. Knowledge editing can update specific facts, yet existing methods often severely damage important skills such as mathematical and programmatic reasoning, even when encyclopedic knowledge is preserved. The authors aim to understand why this asymmetric capability degradation happens and how to prevent it.
AI.method: They analyze covariance-based knowledge editors and argue that they only preserve the subspace defined by a chosen reference corpus, which usually mismatches the model’s actual operative distribution after post-training (SFT, DPO). They propose Moir, which estimates the preservation covariance directly from the model by sampling from its own decoding distribution. To avoid being dominated by instruction-following templates, they seed decoding with a single random vocabulary token, thus revealing a broader internal subspace. Moir is used as a plug-in component for existing covariance-based editors such as MEMIT and AlphaEdit, requiring no external data.
AI.result: On several models (OLMo-2, Llama-3.1, Qwen-3 7–8B), under different editors (MEMIT, AlphaEdit) and editing regimes (batch and sequential), Moir significantly improves preservation of vulnerable capabilities. For instance, on Qwen3-8B with 20,000 AlphaEdit batch edits, GSM8K math accuracy remains at 79.9% with Moir, versus only 10.9% when using Wikipedia-based covariance.
AI.conclusion: Aligning the preservation distribution with the model’s true operative distribution is crucial for non-destructive knowledge editing. The model’s own decoding behavior can effectively approximate this distribution, making it a practical and data-free way to build better covariance estimates for editors when original training corpora are inaccessible.

title: VeriSimpl: Robust Optimization Modeling from Natural Language using Simplification-based Verification
summary: Natural language interfaces can greatly benefit the accessibility and usability of optimization modeling, and recent advances in large language models (LLMs) show promise in automatically translating textual problem descriptions into executable solver formulations. However, a key challenge for existing approaches is to ensure that the inferred formulation correctly implements the intended task, even if it may execute without errors. We introduce VeriSimpl, a solver LLM framework for robust natural-language-to-optimization formalization. Our approach is based on the idea of simplification-based verification, where the optimization solver is leveraged to generate simplified diagnostic queries about a candidate formulation to allow the LLM to tractably reason about the correctness of the formulation with respect to the task description. We present such simplification strategies along different dimensions with respect to problem constraints and decision variables, which allow the LLM to reason locally under fixed global contexts. Evaluations on a range of optimization benchmarks show how our approach provides consistent improvements in accuracy over existing methods, while also providing a novel high-precision self-verification signal.

AI.tldr: VeriSimpl is a framework that uses LLMs plus solver-driven simplification and verification queries to robustly translate natural-language optimization problems into correct formal solver models.
AI.motivation: Natural language interfaces to optimization can make modeling much more accessible, but current LLM-based approaches often generate solver code that runs without errors yet does not faithfully implement the intended task. There is a need for a method that can check and improve the semantic correctness of automatically inferred optimization formulations, closing the gap between textual descriptions and executable models.
AI.method: The paper proposes VeriSimpl, a solver-LLM framework built around simplification-based verification. Given a candidate optimization formulation produced from natural language, the solver is used to construct simplified diagnostic queries about the model—e.g., by simplifying or isolating constraints and decision variables under fixed global contexts. These subproblems are small and localized enough that the LLM can reason about them tractably. By iteratively analyzing and correcting the candidate formulation using these simplified queries, the system improves alignment between the formal model and the original task description. The authors design concrete simplification strategies along different dimensions of the optimization problem, particularly over constraints and decision variables, enabling local verification while preserving global semantics.
AI.result: On multiple optimization benchmarks, VeriSimpl yields higher accuracy in translating natural language descriptions into correct optimization models compared with existing LLM-based methods. The framework also produces a high-precision self-verification signal—diagnostic feedback indicating whether the inferred formulation is likely correct—leading to more reliable natural-language-to-optimization pipelines.
AI.conclusion: Simplification-based verification, where an optimization solver generates tractable diagnostic queries for an LLM, can significantly improve the robustness and correctness of natural-language-to-optimization modeling. VeriSimpl demonstrates that combining solver reasoning with LLM-based semantic understanding offers a practical path toward reliable natural language interfaces to optimization solvers, both improving translation accuracy and providing useful self-verification signals.

title: Break Through the Compression Bottleneck: From Theory to Practice
summary: As the parameter size of language models continues to grow, effective model compression is required to reduce their computational and memory overhead. Existing compression methods suffer from bottleneck issues: when the compression ratio is increased, performance degrades significantly. Low-rank decomposition and quantization are two prominent compression methods that have been proven to significantly reduce the computational and memory requirements of Large Language Models (LLMs) while maintaining model accuracy. Evidently, combining these two methods will break through the existing compression bottleneck. However, how these two methods interact when combined remains a critical question for developers, as many assume they are orthogonal, meaning their combination would not introduce additional errors beyond those independently introduced by each method. This paper provides the first mathematical proof that low-rank decomposition and quantization are non-orthogonal. We validate these findings through a series of experiments on large language models. Our results demonstrate that these methods are non-orthogonal, and their combination leads to significant performance degradation. Importantly, we propose a novel approach Diagonal Adhesive Method (DAM), which can effectively combine the two methods and mitigate the performance loss. Our research provides deep insights into model compression and lays a solid theoretical and experimental foundation for future related studies.

AI.tldr: The paper studies combining low-rank decomposition and quantization for compressing large language models (LLMs), proves they are mathematically non-orthogonal, shows empirically that naive combination severely hurts performance, and proposes a new Diagonal Adhesive Method (DAM) that allows effective joint use with reduced performance loss.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly large, creating high computational and memory costs. Existing compression methods like low-rank decomposition or quantization alone hit bottlenecks: higher compression ratios lead to big accuracy drops. Many practitioners believe these two techniques are orthogonal and can be safely combined without extra error, but whether this assumption holds is unclear. Understanding their true interaction is crucial to design better, more aggressive compression schemes for LLMs without unacceptable degradation.
AI.method: The authors conduct a theoretical analysis of the error behavior when applying low-rank decomposition and quantization together to LLM weights, and prove that the errors from these two methods are non-orthogonal, i.e., they interact in a way that amplifies overall error beyond the sum of independent contributions. They then empirically test various combinations of low-rank and quantization schemes on large language models to validate the theory and measure performance degradation. Based on the analysis, they design the Diagonal Adhesive Method (DAM), a new way to integrate low-rank and quantization that restructures or constrains the decomposition (e.g., via diagonal components) to reduce harmful error interaction.
AI.result: Theoretical results show rigorously that low-rank decomposition and quantization are non-orthogonal in their error contributions when applied jointly to LLMs. Experiments on several LLMs confirm that naive combinations of these two compression techniques cause significant performance drops, more severe than expected from applying each alone. The proposed DAM approach substantially mitigates this degradation, enabling more aggressive compression while preserving model performance better than baseline combination strategies.
AI.conclusion: Low-rank decomposition and quantization, though powerful individually for LLM compression, are mathematically and empirically non-orthogonal: their naive combination creates strong error interactions and considerable performance loss. Recognizing and modeling this interaction is essential for designing effective compression pipelines. The proposed Diagonal Adhesive Method (DAM) offers a principled way to jointly apply these methods and serves as a foundation for future research on tightly integrated compression strategies for large language models.

title: SonicSampler: Unified Tile-Aware Kernels for LLM Sampling and Speculative Verification
summary: Sampling in LLM inference comprises a combinatorial set of logit processing, token selection, and verification operations for speculative decoding. However, existing implementations either accelerate only subsets of this pipeline, rely on multiple kernel launches, or assume homogeneous sampling behavior across a batch, limiting support for dynamic serving workloads and preventing efficient CUDA Graph execution. We present $\textbf{SonicSampler}$, a unified suite of tile-aware Triton kernels that vertically fuses the complete sampling pipeline into a fixed, workload-aware execution model. Our kernels support dynamic per-request sampling behaviors, including grammar-constrained decoding, repetition, frequency and presence penalties, logit bias, temperature scaling, top-$k$ / top-$p$ / min-$p$ filtering, and speculative verification - within a single batched kernel while remaining fully CUDA Graph-compatible. Central to our approach is a novel hierarchical two-stage top-$k$ algorithm that achieves up to $\textbf{10x speedup}$ over competitive baselines and exploits the low-entropy structure of LLM outputs to enable efficient selection over large vocabularies. Across heterogeneous speculative decoding workloads, SonicSampler achieves up to $\textbf{16x speedup}$ over state-of-the-art baselines while preserving flexible batched execution.

AI.tldr: SonicSampler is a unified, fused sampling pipeline for LLM inference that uses tile-aware Triton kernels and a new hierarchical two-stage top-k algorithm to greatly speed up dynamic, batched speculative decoding while remaining CUDA Graph-compatible.
AI.motivation: LLM sampling involves multiple steps (logit processing, token selection, speculative verification) that are often implemented separately, incurring kernel-launch overheads, assuming homogeneous sampling settings across a batch, and making it hard to use CUDA Graphs for dynamic serving workloads. Existing approaches either optimize only parts of the pipeline or do not handle heterogeneous per-request behaviors well, leading to inefficiencies for real-world, dynamic inference scenarios.
AI.method: The authors design SonicSampler, a suite of tile-aware Triton kernels that vertically fuse the entire sampling pipeline—logit transformations, penalties, temperature scaling, filtering (top-k/top-p/min-p), and speculative verification—into a single, unified execution model. The kernels are designed to support heterogeneous, per-request sampling configurations within one batch and to be compatible with CUDA Graphs. A key methodological contribution is a hierarchical two-stage top-k algorithm that leverages the typically low-entropy distribution of LLM logits to efficiently perform selection over very large vocabularies.
AI.result: The new hierarchical two-stage top-k algorithm provides up to 10× speedup over competitive implementations for top-k selection. When applied to end-to-end speculative decoding across heterogeneous workloads, SonicSampler achieves up to 16× speedup relative to state-of-the-art baselines, while still supporting flexible batched execution and dynamic sampling behaviors.
AI.conclusion: By fusing all components of LLM sampling into tile-aware Triton kernels and introducing an efficient hierarchical top-k selection scheme, SonicSampler significantly accelerates speculative decoding for LLMs, particularly in heterogeneous, dynamic serving environments. It overcomes limitations of prior systems regarding kernel-launch overhead, batch homogeneity assumptions, and CUDA Graph compatibility, demonstrating that a unified, workload-aware sampling kernel can yield substantial performance gains without sacrificing flexibility.

title: Making Open-Source Text LLM Watermarks Durable Against Merging
summary: Open-source LLMs (OSMs)arereaching near state-of-the-art performance, prompting prior works to trace the text they generate by embedding text watermarking algorithms directly into their weights. Yet, OSMs are subject to post-training modifications, which has been shown to remove the watermark. Model merging in particular, a prominent method used for combining expert knowledge and preventing catastrophic forgetting, strongly removes such OSM watermarks. A key question is how to enable OSM watermarks that survive subsequent merging. In this work, we show for the first time how to design an OSM watermark that is durable against model merging. We propose Merge-Adversarial Training, an adversarial training algorithm to distill text watermarks into model weights while being robust to subsequent model merging. Our approach consistently outperforms all baselines (e.g. with SLERP up to +51 percentage points (pp) TPR@1%FPR with +25 pp on average) while preserving downstream capabilities. We also for the first time evaluate OSM watermarks against realistic merge scenarios, representing common use-cases such as combining expert capabilities or preventing catastrophic forgetting, and with 3 prominent merging algorithms. More broadly, our findings suggest that adversarial training is a reliable approach for increasing OSM watermark durability against post-training modifications.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a watermarking approach for open-source language models that remains detectable even after models are merged with others, a process that typically erases existing watermarks.
AI.motivation: Existing text watermarks embedded into open-source LLM weights are easily destroyed by common post-training modifications, especially model merging, which is widely used to combine expert capabilities and mitigate catastrophic forgetting. This undermines the ability to reliably trace model outputs and attribute them to specific open-source models.
AI.method: The authors propose Merge-Adversarial Training, an adversarial training procedure that explicitly optimizes the model so that its text watermark signal is preserved even after subsequent model merging. They train models with the watermark while simulating or accounting for different merge operations so that the resulting weights encode a watermark robust to these transformations.
AI.result: Across multiple experiments and realistic merge scenarios using three prominent merging algorithms, the proposed method significantly improves watermark detectability after merging, achieving up to +51 percentage points TPR at 1% FPR (and +25 pp on average) compared to baselines, while maintaining downstream task performance.
AI.conclusion: Adversarially training open-source LLMs with merge-aware objectives can yield watermarks that are substantially more durable to post-training modifications, especially model merging, without sacrificing model utility. This suggests adversarial training is a practical direction for robust, persistent watermarking of open-source models in realistic deployment workflows.

title: Benchmarking Large Language Models on Multi-Sensor Physical Hazard Assessment
comment: 14 pages, 6 figures. Benchmark dataset, evaluation code, and raw results publicly available at: https://github.com/Faizaniqbal52/PhysicalHazardBenchmark
summary: We present an empirical benchmark evaluating how five large language models assess multisensor physical hazard data. Testing 60 scenarios across three categories - multi-sensor joint assessment, response proportionality, and pattern disambiguation - with 1,800 API calls at temperature 0.0, we find that all tested models consistently produced no precautionary warning signal across the tested scenarios where multiple sensors are simultaneously elevated below their individual safety limits, while achieving near-perfect accuracy on single-sensor threshold violations. All five models (ChatGPT-4o, Gemini 2.5 Flash, DeepSeek, Kimi, Llama 3.1 8B) score near zero on Category A multi-sensor scenarios (Q2: 0.000-0.208; Q3: 0.000-0.592) compared to strong performance on single-sensor scenarios (Category B Q1: 0.975-1.000). Structured tabular formatting shows no consistent advantage over plain prose; ChatGPT-4o performs significantly better under prose (p = 0.001). These findings have direct implications for practitioners deploying the tested models in physical safety monitoring systems.

AI.tldr: Benchmarking large language models on multisensor physical hazard assessment reveals strong performance on single-sensor threshold violations but systematic failure to issue precautionary warnings when multiple sensors are jointly elevated below individual limits.
AI.motivation: To understand whether leading large language models can reliably assist in physical safety monitoring contexts that involve interpreting multiple sensor streams, and to identify systematic strengths and weaknesses in their handling of complex, sub-threshold hazard patterns.
AI.method: The authors design an empirical benchmark of 60 scenarios divided into three categories: (A) multi-sensor joint assessment, (B) response proportionality, and (C) pattern disambiguation. They query five large language models (ChatGPT-4o, Gemini 2.5 Flash, DeepSeek, Kimi, Llama 3.1 8B) via 1,800 API calls at temperature 0.0, present data either as structured tables or plain prose, and measure accuracy in detecting hazards or issuing precautionary warnings under both single-sensor and multi-sensor conditions.
AI.result: All evaluated models almost always fail to produce precautionary warnings when multiple sensors are simultaneously elevated but remain below their individual safety thresholds, yielding near-zero scores on multi-sensor Category A scenarios (Q2: 0.000-0.208; Q3: 0.000-0.592). In contrast, they achieve near-perfect accuracy on single-sensor threshold violations (Category B Q1: 0.975-1.000). Presentation format (tabular vs. prose) does not yield a consistent advantage overall, although ChatGPT-4o performs significantly better with prose than with tables (p = 0.001).
AI.conclusion: State-of-the-art large language models, while highly reliable at detecting explicit single-sensor threshold breaches, systematically underreact to multi-sensor sub-threshold patterns, failing to issue precautionary warnings in scenarios where joint elevation should raise concern. As a result, practitioners should be cautious about using these models as primary decision-makers in physical safety monitoring systems and consider complementary safeguards, domain-specific logic, or additional model training to handle multi-sensor hazard reasoning.

title: Routing Subspaces: Auditing Evaluation-to-Deployment Mismatch in Fine-Tuned Language Models
summary: Safety evaluations often assume that behavior observed during testing reflects behavior in ordinary use, but fine-tuning can break this assumption. A checkpoint can appear fixed under evaluation-style prompts while the same behavior persists under ordinary-use prompts. Output scores reveal this mismatch but do not locate it. We investigate whether the distinction is encoded in a stable internal site and introduce an approach that fits a paired activation contrast at a path-patching-informed mid-depth window, then modifies the resulting coordinate on held-out prompts. The intervention closes the evaluation-to-deployment gap in ten of twelve model--behavior settings (six of the eight settings with $n{\geq}120$ paired questions) across four full-matrix instruction-tuned model instances; a fifth model supports localization and edit-provenance checks, and deployment-framed rates change by at most $6.1$pp. The two flat cells, both sycophancy, indicate that a single-coordinate audit is not sufficient when the installed distinction is higher-rank or missed by the depth heuristic. The audit is a diagnostic for fine-tuned checkpoints, not a training-time defense or a guarantee of deployment safety.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how fine-tuned language models can pass safety evaluations yet still behave differently in realistic use, and proposes a mechanistic audit that identifies and edits an internal feature separating “evaluation-style” from “ordinary-use” behavior.
AI.motivation: Safety tests assume that model behavior under evaluation prompts matches behavior in real deployment. Fine-tuning can break this: a model may look safe on benchmark-style prompts yet exhibit problematic behavior on natural, user-like prompts. Existing metrics can detect that there is a mismatch in outputs but cannot pinpoint where in the model it arises or how to intervene. The authors aim to mechanistically localize and adjust the internal representation that drives this context-dependent behavior, to better audit fine-tuned checkpoints.
AI.method: They use mechanistic-interpretability tools, especially path patching, to identify a mid-depth “window” in the network where the distinction between evaluation-style and ordinary-use behavior is encoded. Within this window, they fit a low-dimensional (essentially one-coordinate) activation contrast that separates the two behaviors based on paired prompts (same question framed as eval vs deployment). They then intervene by editing this coordinate on held-out prompts, effectively pushing the model toward one behavior or the other, and assess whether this closes the evaluation–deployment behavior gap across multiple behaviors and models. They also use an additional model to check localization robustness and provenance of the edit.
AI.result: Across four instruction-tuned model instances and twelve model–behavior settings, their single-coordinate intervention substantially reduces the mismatch between evaluation and deployment behavior in ten cases, especially when they have at least 120 paired questions per setting. Deployment-framed outcome rates shift by up to 6.1 percentage points in the tested fifth model, showing that edits are localized and do not wildly distort behavior. Two sycophancy-related cases show little improvement, suggesting that the relevant distinction there is either represented in a higher-rank subspace or not captured by their chosen depth window.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that, for many fine-tuned behaviors, the difference between “safe under evaluation” and “problematic in deployment” is encoded in a relatively simple, stable internal direction that can be located and causally manipulated. Their proposed audit can diagnose and often reduce evaluation-to-deployment gaps in fine-tuned checkpoints, but it is not a substitute for robust training-time defenses or a guarantee of real-world safety, and single-coordinate approaches can fail when behaviors are more distributed or missed by heuristic localization choices.

title: Semi-Supervised Text-Attributed Graph Distillation
comment: Technical report for the paper "Semi-Supervised Text-Attributed Graph Distillation" accepted KDD2026
summary:
{\em Text-Attributed Graphs} (TAGs) have emerged as an expressive data model for integrating graph topology with rich textual semantics. Existing representation learning methods over TAGs suffer from severe scalability bottlenecks, particularly together with {\em Large Language Models} (LLMs). While data distillation offers a promising data-centric solution, existing methods fail to capture the complex interplay between graph and text modalities, struggle with the label scarcity inherent in semi-supervised settings, and lack the ability to produce the human-readable textual attributes required for downstream LLM-based tasks.
  To address these challenges, we propose \algo{}, a unified semi-supervised framework guided by the {\em Wasserstein Distance} (WSD). Grounded in our empirical findings on real TAGs, \algo{} introduces a graph-text collaborative encoding module that utilizes dual-pathway encoders (graph-aware and -free) within a collaborative self-training scheme to harvest reliable pseudo-labels and fuse complementary graph-text features. Furthermore, we develop a theoretically grounded WSD-based graph sketching algorithm and a cost-effective LLM text synthesis module, which leverages cluster-based keyword extraction to generate coherent, human-readable summaries for condensed nodes. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that \algo{} achieves a state-of-the-art performance-compression trade-off in terms of both GNN- and LLM-based downstream tasks, enabling effective and efficient TAG learning or analytics.

AI.tldr: A new semi-supervised, Wasserstein-distance-guided framework for scalable representation learning on text-attributed graphs that preserves both graph structure and textual semantics while enabling efficient GNN and LLM downstream tasks.
AI.motivation: Text-attributed graphs combine network structure with rich node text, but existing methods scale poorly (especially with LLMs), do not fully exploit graph–text interactions, struggle under label scarcity in semi-supervised settings, and often cannot produce human-readable textual attributes needed for LLM-based applications. There is a need for a data distillation/compression framework that jointly handles graph and text, is label-efficient, and yields interpretable text summaries compatible with LLMs.
AI.method: The paper proposes a unified semi-supervised framework, called \algo{}, structured around three main components: (1) a graph-text collaborative encoding module with dual-pathway encoders—one graph-aware and one graph-free—operating in a collaborative self-training scheme to obtain reliable pseudo-labels and to integrate complementary graph and text signals; (2) a Wasserstein-distance-based graph sketching algorithm that theoretically guides how to condense the original text-attributed graph into a smaller representative one; and (3) a cost-efficient LLM-based text synthesis pipeline that uses cluster-based keyword extraction to generate coherent, human-readable summaries as textual attributes for the condensed (sketched) nodes.
AI.result: On standard text-attributed graph benchmarks, \algo{} achieves a superior trade-off between performance and compression compared with existing distillation and representation learning methods. It maintains or improves accuracy for both GNN-based and LLM-based downstream tasks while significantly reducing graph size and computational cost, demonstrating scalability and effectiveness of the proposed components.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Wasserstein-guided semi-supervised framework successfully addresses scalability, label scarcity, and interpretability challenges in learning from text-attributed graphs. By jointly modeling graph and text modalities, leveraging pseudo-labels through collaborative self-training, and generating human-readable textual summaries for compressed graphs, \algo{} enables efficient and effective learning and analytics for a wide range of downstream GNN and LLM applications on TAGs.

title: TopoGuard: Graph Theory Based Defenses Against Split-Knowledge Attacks on RAG
summary: Production Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) systems rely on aggregating multiple external documents to answer complex queries. However, the retrieved documents introduce a new threat surface that can be exploited to launch split-knowledge attacks. In this attack, the adversary injects documents that are individually benign but create false associations when combined and fed to language models. This paper shows that the new attack is structurally invisible to existing per-document filters, like LlamaGuard. To address this issue in RAG, this work introduces TopoGuard, a family of graph theory-based methods specifically targeting the split-knowledge attacks by building a semantic similarity graph from retrieved documents and detecting contexts with malicious topology. Grounded on the theoretical analysis, the TopoGuard family has been proven to be effective and robust even with noisy inputs. Extensive experiments are conducted on two retrieval datasets and compared with multiple baseline methods. Specifically, the TopoGuard-$λ_2$+Entity catches 21$\times$ more attacks than LlamaGuard-2-8B at 1\% FPR (32.6\% vs 1.5\% recall) on the HotpotQA dataset. Compared with production RAG detection systems using large language models, the proposed TopoGuard variants run efficiently at sub-millisecond latency and stay robust under adaptive adversaries and benign cross-domain queries.

AI.tldr: Paper introduces TopoGuard, a graph-based defense for Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems that detects and blocks split-knowledge attacks created by combining individually benign documents.
AI.motivation: Existing RAG systems aggregate multiple retrieved documents to answer complex queries. Adversaries can exploit this by injecting documents that are harmless in isolation but misleading in combination, forming "split-knowledge" attacks. Current safety filters (e.g., per-document classifiers like LlamaGuard) examine each document separately, so they fail to detect malicious cross-document interactions. There is a need for methods that reason over the joint structure of retrieved evidence rather than treating documents independently, while also being efficient enough for production RAG systems.
AI.method: TopoGuard builds a semantic similarity graph over the retrieved documents (and entities they mention). Nodes represent documents (and/or entities), and edges connect semantically similar pieces of content. The method then analyzes the topology of this graph using graph-theoretic signals such as eigenvalues (e.g., λ2 of the Laplacian), community structure, and patterns involving entity nodes. Certain suspicious topologies—indicative of coordinated but subtly injected evidence—are flagged as potential split-knowledge attacks. Different TopoGuard variants use different graph constructions and features (e.g., TopoGuard-λ2+Entity augments the document graph with entity information) and are designed to be robust under noise and adaptive adversaries. The algorithms are implemented to run at very low latency suitable for production use.
AI.result: On two retrieval datasets, including HotpotQA, TopoGuard significantly outperforms existing safety filters and RAG-detection baselines. For example, TopoGuard-λ2+Entity detects 21× more attacks than LlamaGuard-2-8B at 1% false positive rate on HotpotQA (32.6% vs. 1.5% recall). Compared with production RAG detectors that rely on large language models, TopoGuard variants achieve sub-millisecond latency and maintain robustness against adaptive adversaries as well as benign cross-domain queries.
AI.conclusion: Split-knowledge attacks pose a substantial, previously under-addressed threat to RAG systems because they exploit cross-document interactions that per-document filters cannot see. By explicitly modeling the semantic relationships among retrieved documents as a graph and detecting anomalous topologies, TopoGuard provides an effective and efficient defense. Theoretical analysis and experiments show that TopoGuard is robust to noise and adaptive attackers, scales to production settings, and considerably improves attack detection over existing tools like LlamaGuard and LLM-based RAG safety systems.

title: Beyond Liars' Bench: The Impact of Lie Typology, Depth, and Sparsity on Deception Detection in LLMs
comment: Presented at the AI Transparency Conference 2026, forthcoming in the AI Transparency Journal
summary: Training probes to detect deceptive outputs from large language models is still an open problem. Recent work has demonstrated that detection probes fail especially in out-of-domain scenarios -- training on one type of lie does not transfer well to deception scenarios involving other types of lies. In this work, we conduct a systematic study on how various factors impact detection performance: representation depth, probe expressivity, sparse feature representations, and the lie typology of the training data. To this end, we augment standard benchmark training data with a supplementary dataset containing diverse types of deception, including fabrication, omission, and exaggeration examples. Analyzing these factors across seven probe types, our experimental results show that the optimal representation depth is highly dataset-dependent, more expressive probes provide only selective gains over linear baselines, and sparse autoencoder features perform similarly to dense hidden states. Ultimately, we demonstrate that the choice of training data and lie typology substantially changes detectability, highlighting that deception detection is a highly representation-dependent problem.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how well different “probes” can detect deceptive outputs from large language models and finds that detection success depends strongly on the type of lies and representations used.
AI.motivation: Detecting when large language models are being deceptive is important for safety and reliability, but existing detection probes often fail to generalize, especially when the deception differs from what they were trained on. The authors aim to systematically understand which factors—like representation depth, probe complexity, feature sparsity, and the diversity of lie types in training data—most affect detection performance.
AI.method: The authors augment standard deception-detection benchmarks with a new dataset that includes varied deception types (fabrication, omission, exaggeration). They then test seven different probe architectures over different representation depths, using both dense hidden states and sparse autoencoder features, and compare linear vs more expressive probes. They analyze how these design choices and the lie typology of the training data impact in-domain and out-of-domain detection accuracy.
AI.result: They find that: (1) the best representation depth for detection varies significantly by dataset; (2) more expressive, non-linear probes only sometimes outperform simple linear baselines; (3) sparse autoencoder-derived features do not consistently improve detection over standard dense hidden states; and (4) the composition of the training data, particularly the types of lies included, substantially alters how detectable deception is and how well detection transfers across settings.
AI.conclusion: Deception detection in large language models is highly dependent on internal representations and the specific types of lies included in training. Architectural tweaks to probes or using sparse features yield limited, context-dependent gains, whereas careful selection and diversification of lie typologies in training data is crucial for building more robust deception detectors.

title: Preference Tuning as Spectral Update Reorganization
summary: Preference-based post-training is usually understood through endpoint behavior, yet the learned update that produces this behavior remains largely opaque. We study RLHF and related preference optimization through the spectral structure of their induced parameter updates. By decomposing effective LoRA updates and reloading their spectral components as plug-in modules, we turn preference-induced updates into objects that can be isolated, recomposed, and directly intervened on. Across model families, optimization algorithms, and supervision regimes, these updates consistently develop a spectral head--tail organization. A compact head emerges early and carries the dominant endpoint shift, while a heterogeneous residual tail remains. The split is functional rather than merely descriptive. Plug-in intervention shows that the head accounts for the visible behavioral departure from the base model, while the tail is weak in isolation. Cross-run recomposition further shows that mixed adapters follow the source of the head, indicating that the head carries run-level solver bias. This endpoint dominance does not imply learning sufficiency. Head-only learning is non-vacuous but fails to recover the full solution, especially on out-of-distribution behavior. Tail-only learning yields little visible gain, yet the full solution is not recovered without the tail. These findings recast preference post-training as structured update reorganization rather than a monolithic behavioral correction, and suggest that alignment gain and coverage loss are tied to how the learned update itself is organized.

AI.tldr: They analyze preference-based post-training (like RLHF) by decomposing LoRA parameter updates into spectral components, revealing a structured "head–tail" organization that explains how alignment behavior emerges and its limits.
AI.motivation: Preference-based post-training is typically evaluated only by endpoint behavior, leaving the actual learned parameter updates opaque. The authors want to understand the internal structure of these updates to explain how alignment is implemented, why runs differ, and why alignment often trades off with coverage/generalization.
AI.method: They represent RLHF and related preference-optimization updates as effective LoRA adapters and perform a spectral decomposition of these updates. By reloading selected spectral components as plug-in modules, they can isolate the "head" (top spectral components) and the "tail" (remaining components), then intervene by swapping, ablating, or recombining them across runs, model families, algorithms, and supervision regimes. They evaluate resulting behavioral changes, including out-of-distribution performance.
AI.result: Across diverse settings, the learned updates consistently split into a compact spectral head and a more diffuse tail. The head emerges early in training, dominates the endpoint behavioral shift from the base model, and carries run-specific solver bias; mixed adapters follow the source of the head. The tail, while weak in isolation and not strongly visible in simple behavioral metrics, is necessary for fully recovering the trained model’s solution, particularly for out-of-distribution behavior. Head-only or tail-only updates both fail to match the full trained model.
AI.conclusion: Preference-based post-training should be viewed as a structured reorganization of parameters into distinct spectral components rather than a single homogeneous behavioral correction. The main visible alignment effect comes from a compact spectral head that encodes run-level biases, but complete and robust behavior depends on a broader tail. This structured organization helps explain both alignment gains and accompanying coverage/generalization losses, suggesting that future alignment methods must control not just endpoint behavior but the internal spectral structure of learned updates.

title: Enabling Scalable Topology Inference in Distribution Systems via Constrained Multi-Source Inference
summary: Accurate distribution system topology is essential for outage localization, voltage analytics, and operation of distribution grids, yet maintaining reliable connectivity records remains challenging in practice due to heterogeneous and imperfect utility data. Existing topology identification methods often rely primarily on electrical similarity or spatial records alone, which become unreliable in dense feeders and under inconsistent metadata conditions. This paper formulates distribution topology identification as a constrained inference problem that refines a utility-provided base topology using heterogeneous evidence while enforcing spatial feasibility and physical operational constraints. Instead of reconstructing connectivity from scratch, the proposed framework detects inconsistent assignments, performs localized reconnection within constrained neighborhoods to ensure scalability, and iteratively enforces physical feasibility to produce operationally consistent topology estimates. In addition, a falsification-driven reliability metric evaluates how strongly each inferred connection is supported relative to alternative feasible assignments, enabling utilities to prioritize verification efforts while preserving system-wide observability. The framework is validated using operational data from three feeders comprising more than $8{,}000$ AMI meters in collaboration with a large U.S. utility. Results demonstrate over $95\%$ topology reconstruction accuracy while significantly reducing computational effort compared with global inference approaches. The study further shows that correlation-based methods alone produce ambiguous assignments in dense urban feeders, whereas combining electrical measurements with spatial and operational constraints enables robust and scalable topology recovery under realistic deployment conditions.

AI.tldr: They improve distribution grid topology identification by refining an existing utility topology using multiple data sources and operational constraints, achieving high accuracy and scalability.
AI.motivation: Distribution system applications like outage management and voltage analysis require accurate knowledge of which customers and devices are connected to which parts of the grid. Utilities’ topology records are often outdated or inconsistent due to heterogeneous data sources, dense urban feeders, and missing or incorrect metadata. Existing methods that use only electrical similarity (e.g., correlations of smart meter data) or only spatial information can be unreliable in these conditions and may not scale well. There is a need for a method that can robustly correct and complete a given base topology while respecting realistic physical and spatial constraints.
AI.method: The paper frames topology identification as a constrained inference problem that starts from a utility-provided base topology instead of building connectivity from scratch. It (1) detects inconsistent connectivity assignments using heterogeneous evidence (electrical measurements, spatial data, metadata), (2) performs localized reconnection only within constrained neighborhoods to maintain scalability, and (3) iteratively enforces spatial feasibility and physical operational constraints (e.g., radiality, voltage/flow plausibility) to ensure that the resulting topology is operationally consistent. It also introduces a falsification-driven reliability metric that quantifies how strongly each inferred connection is supported versus alternative feasible assignments, guiding utilities on where to focus field verification.
AI.result: Using data from three real distribution feeders with more than 8,000 AMI meters from a large U.S. utility, the proposed framework reconstructs topology with over 95% accuracy. It substantially reduces computation time compared with global inference approaches that attempt to search over the full space of possible topologies. The study also empirically shows that correlation-based, measurement-only methods yield ambiguous or incorrect connections in dense urban feeders, while the new multi-evidence, constraint-based framework maintains accuracy and robustness under such challenging conditions.
AI.conclusion: Refining an existing utility topology through constrained, localized inference that jointly uses electrical data, spatial information, and operational constraints provides a practical, scalable, and accurate solution for distribution topology identification. The framework achieves high reconstruction accuracy in real utility systems, is computationally efficient, and offers a reliability metric that helps prioritize field verification. Combining heterogeneous evidence and physical feasibility checks outperforms purely correlation-based methods, especially in dense feeders and in the presence of inconsistent metadata, making the approach suitable for real-world deployment in modern distribution grids.

title: AsymVerify at SemEval-2026 Task 6: Asymmetric Confidence-Gated Verification for Political Evasion Detection
comment: Accepted to SemEval-2026 Task 6 (CLARITY) at ACL 2026. Team AsymVerify placed 2nd of 41 teams on the official Subtask 1 leaderboard. Task: https://konstantinosftw.github.io/CLARITY-SemEval-2026/. Code: https://github.com/kaons-research/AsymVerify-ACL. Website: https://kaons.com/
summary: Political evasion is difficult to detect because evasive answers often appear cooperative while avoiding concrete commitment. We present AsymVerify, a confidence-gated verification system for SemEval-2026 Task 6, a three-way classification of Clear Reply, Ambivalent, and Clear Non-Reply responses. AsymVerify scored 0.85 Macro F1 on the evaluation split (D_eval, n=237), placing 2nd out of 41 teams on the official leaderboard. The system first classifies each question-answer pair, then selectively applies downgrade verification (CR/CNR -> AMB) or upgrade verification (AMB -> CR) to low-confidence predictions. Development analysis shows that errors concentrate at the Ambivalent boundary in both directions, motivating this asymmetric two-verifier design while confidence gating keeps additional inference cost low. On D_dev (n=308), AsymVerify with GLM-4.7 gains +17.1 Macro F1 over single-pass classification at 1.48 calls/example, and the upgrade verifier alone improves every tested LLM backend on D_dev by +6.8 to +15.2 Macro F1 over its single-pass baseline. Code is available at https://github.com/kaons-research/AsymVerify-ACL.

AI.tldr: AsymVerify is a confidence-gated, two-stage verification framework that improves political reply classification (Clear Reply, Ambivalent, Clear Non-Reply) by selectively re-checking low-confidence predictions, achieving state-of-the-art Macro F1 with modest extra LLM calls.
AI.motivation: Detecting political evasion is challenging because evasive responses can sound cooperative while avoiding commitment. Existing systems struggle particularly around the ambiguous, ambivalent category. The paper aims to build a more reliable classifier for political replies in SemEval-2026 Task 6 by explicitly addressing this ambiguity and reducing errors near the Ambivalent decision boundary, while keeping computational cost low.
AI.method: The authors propose AsymVerify, a two-stage, confidence-gated verification system. First, a base LLM classifies each question-answer pair into Clear Reply (CR), Ambivalent (AMB), or Clear Non-Reply (CNR). Then, only low-confidence predictions are re-examined by specialized verifiers: a downgrade verifier that can convert low-confidence CR/CNR predictions to AMB, and an upgrade verifier that can turn low-confidence AMB into CR. This asymmetric design targets the main error modes (misclassifications involving AMB) and is applied selectively based on confidence thresholds to limit additional inference cost. The system is evaluated with different LLM backends, including GLM-4.7.
AI.result: On the SemEval-2026 Task 6 evaluation split (D_eval, n=237), AsymVerify achieves a Macro F1 of 0.85 and ranks 2nd out of 41 teams. On the development set (D_dev, n=308), AsymVerify with GLM-4.7 improves Macro F1 by +17.1 over a single-pass classifier at an average cost of 1.48 model calls per example. Using only the upgrade verifier yields gains of +6.8 to +15.2 Macro F1 across all tested LLM backends versus their single-pass baselines, demonstrating robustness of the approach.
AI.conclusion: AsymVerify effectively improves political reply classification by focusing on the difficult Ambivalent boundary with an asymmetric verification strategy. Confidence-gated re-checking of low-confidence predictions substantially boosts Macro F1 while keeping inference overhead modest. The method generalizes across multiple LLM backends and sets a strong benchmark on SemEval-2026 Task 6, suggesting that targeted verification modules are a powerful, model-agnostic way to enhance reliability in nuanced classification tasks.

title: Routing Without Training: Controllable-Ratio LLM Offloading via Reliability Gating
summary: Local-cloud collaboration is a practical way to deploy large language models under resource constraints, but existing methods often rely on trained routers or collaboration-aware finetuning that tie routing behavior to a particular operating regime. In this work, we show that such training may be unnecessary: the local model's own inference-time agreement across sampled responses already provides a strong signal for deciding when to trust local execution and when to offload to a stronger cloud model. We propose CARGO, a training-free routing framework that estimates this agreement through prompt-varied sampling, applies Bayesian early stopping for sample-efficient uncertainty control, and supports arbitrary target collaboration ratios through lightweight deployment-time calibration. Across diverse reasoning and question-answering tasks, multiple local LLM families and scales, and both pretrained and finetuned local models, CARGO consistently outperforms other training-free baselines and in several settings surpasses supervised learned routers. These results suggest that effective and adaptable local-cloud collaboration can emerge directly from the local model's intrinsic response behavior, without requiring an additional trained router.

AI.tldr: CARGO is a training-free framework for routing between local and cloud LLMs by using the local model’s own output agreement as an uncertainty signal.
AI.motivation: Deploying large LLMs under resource constraints often requires local-cloud collaboration, but current approaches rely on trained routers or collaboration-aware finetuning, which lock routing behavior to specific regimes and add training overhead. The authors aim to find a simpler, more flexible way to decide when to use a local model versus offloading to a stronger cloud model.
AI.method: The authors propose CARGO, which measures the local model’s inference-time agreement across multiple, prompt-varied sampled responses as a proxy for confidence. It incorporates Bayesian early stopping to limit the number of samples needed for reliable uncertainty estimation and includes a lightweight deployment-time calibration step to support arbitrary local/cloud collaboration ratios, all without additional router training.
AI.result: On diverse reasoning and QA tasks, across several local LLM families, sizes, and both pretrained and finetuned variants, CARGO consistently outperforms other training-free routing baselines and in multiple settings even exceeds the performance of supervised learned routers that require training.
AI.conclusion: Effective and adaptable local-cloud collaboration can be achieved without training specialized routers or finetuning for collaboration; instead, one can leverage the intrinsic agreement behavior of local LLMs with a training-free framework like CARGO to obtain strong performance and flexible deployment trade-offs.

title: Answer-then-Edit: Reasoning Skeleton Editing for Anti-Distillation with Preserved Utility
summary: Proprietary large language models (LLMs) entail substantial intellectual and financial investment, making them valuable intellectual property (IP). However, even when deployed via black-box APIs, these models remain vulnerable to unauthorized knowledge distillation, which allows adversaries to cheaply extract and replicate model capabilities. To address this issue, anti-distillation (AD) has been proposed to generate defensive outputs that hinder distillation effectiveness, overcoming the limitation of watermarking-based approaches that rely on post-hoc verification. However, existing AD methods based on internal model perturbations struggle to balance anti-distillability and utility (e.g., answer accuracy and naturalness) of reasoning traces, with stronger defenses often causing significant utility loss. To fill this gap, we propose \textbf{\underline{S}}keleton-\textbf{\underline{G}}uided \textbf{\underline{R}}easoning \textbf{\underline{E}}diting (SGRE), an \textit{Answer-then-Edit} framework that performs post-hoc trace modification for anti-distillation. In the answer stage, the teacher model first generates clean reasoning traces, preserving the original reasoning accuracy while enabling more flexible control over trace naturalness. In the editing stage, we draw inspiration from Cognitive Load Theory (CLT) and introduce a three-stage strategy consisting of reasoning skeleton extraction, skeleton graph coarsening, and skeleton verbalization. These operations jointly perturb reasoning structures and augment textual complexity to amplify extraneous load on student models, hindering their acquisition of underlying reasoning patterns. Extensive experiments across diverse LLMs demonstrate that SGRE achieves state-of-the-art performance in reducing distillation effectiveness, while maintaining lossless reasoning accuracy and superior trace naturalness.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes SGRE, an Answer-then-Edit framework that post-processes LLM reasoning traces to block unauthorized knowledge distillation while preserving answer accuracy and naturalness.
AI.motivation: Proprietary LLMs are costly IP but can be reverse-engineered via knowledge distillation, even through black-box APIs. Existing anti-distillation defenses perturb internal model states, but they struggle to maintain a good balance between being hard to distill and retaining utility (accuracy and naturalness). The authors aim to design a defense that strongly reduces distillation effectiveness without harming answer quality or making traces unnaturally degraded.
AI.method: They introduce Skeleton-Guided Reasoning Editing (SGRE), an Answer-then-Edit pipeline. First, the teacher model generates normal, accurate reasoning traces. Then, in a separate editing stage inspired by Cognitive Load Theory, they modify these traces via three steps: (1) extract a reasoning skeleton that captures core logical structure; (2) coarsen this skeleton into a graph that perturbs and simplifies the visible reasoning structure; and (3) verbalize this modified skeleton back into text. These edits structurally perturb the reasoning traces and increase their textual and structural complexity, creating extraneous cognitive load for student models trying to learn from them, thus hindering distillation.
AI.result: Across multiple LLMs and benchmarks, SGRE substantially reduces the success of knowledge distillation attacks relative to prior anti-distillation methods, yet keeps the original reasoning accuracy unchanged and preserves or improves perceived naturalness of the reasoning traces. It outperforms previous state-of-the-art in the tradeoff between anti-distillation strength and utility.
AI.conclusion: Post-hoc editing of reasoning traces, guided by an abstracted reasoning skeleton and designed to increase extraneous load for student models, is an effective way to defend proprietary LLMs against distillation attacks. SGRE achieves strong protection without sacrificing answer correctness or human-acceptable reasoning quality, suggesting that Answer-then-Edit style defenses may be preferable to internal-perturbation approaches for safeguarding LLM IP.

title: PersonaTrail: Benchmarking Personalized Web Agents through Browsing Trails
summary: Recent advances in large language models have enabled web agents to autonomously execute complex tasks. In practice, users frequently provide underspecified instructions, requiring agents to infer the missing context from their raw browsing histories. Existing benchmarks fail to capture this form of personalization, as they either restrict tasks to fully explicit prompts or abstract web interaction history into simplified forms. To bridge this gap, we introduce PersonaTrail, a benchmark for personalized web agents operating in a managed open web environment. By leveraging realistic browsing trajectories as user history, PersonaTrail evaluates an agent's ability to infer user preferences and recall information from past browsing sessions. We further propose Preference-Aware Contextual Memory (PACMem), a framework that decomposes raw browsing histories into two types of structured memory: factual memories that summarize individual sessions and preference memories that distill recurring behavioral patterns. At inference time, the agent retrieves the most relevant entries from these memories to guide personalized navigation. Extensive experiments show that PACMem consistently outperforms existing memory-based baselines on both tasks.

AI.tldr: PersonaTrail is a benchmark and PACMem is a memory framework for evaluating and improving personalized web agents using realistic browsing histories.
AI.motivation: Existing web-agent benchmarks either rely on fully explicit prompts or oversimplified representations of user history, so they do not test an agent’s ability to infer user preferences and missing context from real browsing behavior. The paper aims to create a more realistic setting where agents must use raw browsing trajectories to personalize actions and handle underspecified instructions.
AI.method: The authors introduce PersonaTrail, a benchmark built from realistic web browsing trajectories in a managed open-web environment. They design tasks where agents must use prior browsing sessions as implicit context. They then propose Preference-Aware Contextual Memory (PACMem), which converts raw browsing logs into two structured memory types: (1) factual memories that summarize individual sessions, and (2) preference memories that capture recurring behavior patterns. At inference, the agent retrieves relevant factual and preference memories to condition its decision-making and navigation.
AI.result: In experiments on the PersonaTrail benchmark, agents equipped with PACMem consistently outperform existing memory-based baselines across the evaluated tasks, showing better use of browsing history for personalization and context recall.
AI.conclusion: Modeling user history as structured factual and preference memories enables web agents to better infer user intent and personalize behavior in realistic, underspecified-task settings. PersonaTrail provides a more faithful benchmark for such capabilities, and PACMem offers an effective memory mechanism that advances the state of the art in personalized web agents.

title: Belief Propagation in LLM World Models: Measuring Strategic Information Bias with Prediction Markets
comment: Accepted at UNLP 2026. 12 pages, 8 figures, 11 tables. Dataset available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/OpenBabylon/unlp-ukraine-forecasting
summary:
Every information ecosystem produces beliefs that shape strategic decisions. Both human analysts and AI systems inherit the blind spots of their information sources. We show that LLMs, combined with prediction markets, function as a calibrated instrument for measuring how far ecosystem-induced beliefs deviate from an external reference: LLMs extract the beliefs a text corpus implies, and prediction market price trajectories, anchored at resolution by realised outcomes, provide the calibration reference against which to quantify the deviation.
  We isolate the bias contribution of specific text through ablation: varying information context while holding the model fixed, with a contaminated model that knows actual outcomes as control. Applied to 111 Ukraine-related prediction markets, comprising approximately 93,000 predictions across four models, we find that English news context systematically biases territorial predictions, wrong 64 to 72 percent of the time when it pushes predictions toward territorial capture. A contaminated model that knows actual outcomes shows the same error rate, indicating that the bias originates primarily in the text. Supplementing with Ukrainian military-analytical sources reduces the bias for all clean models, while absolute-error gains are partial and model-dependent.
  We show that the distortion originates primarily in the sources, not the models. Consistent across four architectures, it will persist in any system that processes them and propagate into downstream decisions.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a way to measure and localize bias in information ecosystems by using LLMs to extract implied beliefs from texts and prediction markets as a calibration standard, showing that much of the bias about territorial outcomes in the Ukraine war comes from the sources rather than the models.
AI.motivation: Information ecosystems shape beliefs that guide strategic decisions, but both human and AI analysts inherit systematic blind spots from their sources. There is a need for a rigorous, quantitative way to measure how much beliefs induced by particular corpora deviate from reality, and to distinguish bias from models versus bias from input texts.
AI.method: The authors couple large language models with prediction markets. LLMs read different text contexts and output the probabilistic beliefs those texts imply about specific future events. Prediction market price trajectories, which are anchored at resolution by realised outcomes, serve as a calibrated reference for true probabilities. By varying the information context (e.g., English news vs. Ukrainian military-analytical sources) while holding the model fixed, and comparing to a “contaminated” model that already knows the true outcomes, they perform an ablation-style analysis to isolate the bias contribution of the text sources.
AI.result: On a dataset of 111 Ukraine-related prediction markets with about 93,000 predictions across four LLMs, English-language news context systematically biases territorial-outcome predictions. When this news pushes forecasts toward territorial capture, it is wrong 64–72% of the time. A control model that knows actual outcomes shows a similar error pattern, indicating that the bias is driven by the text rather than model ignorance. Adding Ukrainian military-analytical sources reduces this directional bias for all clean models, although the improvement in overall absolute error is only partial and varies by model.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that combining LLMs with prediction markets can quantify and localize source-induced bias in belief formation. For Ukraine territorial predictions, the main distortions arise from the information sources, not from the LLM architectures themselves. Because this distortion is consistent across multiple models, it will persist in any system that ingests the same sources and will propagate into downstream strategic decisions unless the source bias is corrected or diversified.

title: Tractable Hierarchical Control of Autoregressive Language Models
summary: Constraining the generation of autoregressive large language models (LLMs) is an important component of integrating language models into formal systems. In the generation of code and data for tasks like program synthesis, ensuring that language models produce syntactically valid output is a prerequisite for processing such output. These languages (such as SQL or JSON) are often designed as $LR(k)$ context-free grammars. By distilling the LLM to a tractable probabilistic model, its autoregressive generation can be steered and masked to incorporate the probability of satisfying logical constraints, ensuring high quality output that is guaranteed to be valid. This paper demonstrates that the satisfaction of any $LR(k)$ grammar of finite duration can be calculated in polynomial time, an improvement over the exponential time of applying previous methods to such grammars. This result enables efficient constraint and steering of LLM generation towards output that better satisfies formal syntactic constraints.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a polynomial-time method to enforce LR(k) grammar constraints during LLM generation, improving over exponential-time prior methods.
AI.motivation: To safely and reliably integrate autoregressive LLMs into formal systems, especially in domains like program synthesis or structured data generation, we need a way to guarantee that generated sequences obey formal syntactic constraints, typically expressed as LR(k) grammars. Existing constraint-satisfaction approaches can be exponential for such grammars, limiting practical use.
AI.method: They distill the LLM into a tractable probabilistic model and then derive an algorithm that, during autoregressive generation, computes in polynomial time the probability that a partial sequence can be extended to a full sequence satisfying a given finite-duration LR(k) grammar. This enables masking and steering token choices so that only tokens that can still lead to valid parses are allowed or prioritized.
AI.result: They theoretically prove that the satisfaction of any finite-duration LR(k) grammar can be computed in polynomial time with their approach, contrasting with the exponential complexity of prior generic constraint-satisfaction methods on the same class of grammars. This provides a computational foundation for efficient constrained decoding for LR(k) languages.
AI.conclusion: The work establishes that grammar satisfaction for finite-duration LR(k) grammars is tractable in polynomial time in the context of LLM generation, enabling efficient, guaranteed-valid decoding for syntactically constrained languages like SQL or JSON, and thus more reliable integration of LLMs into formal systems.

title: Naver-News-KO: A Korean News Summarization Dataset for Open-Source Fine-Tuning of Summarization Models
comment: Technical report; 7 pages, 1 figure, 4 tables. Dataset: https://huggingface.co/datasets/daekeun-ml/naver-news-summarization-ko
summary: We release Naver-News-KO, a Korean news summarization dataset of 27,400 (document, summary) pairs collected from Naver News over a ten-day window in July 2022 across two categories (Economy and IT/Science; 77/23 split), with train/validation/test partitions of 22,194 / 2,466 / 2,740 and a mean per-record document-to-summary character-compression ratio of 6.03x. The dataset has been publicly hosted on the Hugging Face Hub since January 2023 and, as of May 2026, receives approximately 33,000 downloads per month; community-maintained Korean summarization models fine-tuned on it include Gemma-2B-ko and Gemma2-9B variants. This technical report (i) documents the collection protocol, the column schema, and the split construction, (ii) reports corpus-level statistics (length distributions, compression ratio, and a measured 16.8% near-duplicate title-Jaccard overlap between test and train that users should be aware of), (iii) positions the resource against other open Korean summarization corpora, (iv) provides a Lead-3 extractive reference point (ROUGE-1 55.1, ROUGE-L 50.6) and two reproducible fine-tuned baselines -- KoBART (R-1 56.6, BERTScore-F1 81.5) and Gemma-2B-ko with LoRA (R-1 55.3, BERTScore-F1 78.3) -- with release-time training scripts, and (v) clarifies the licensing and intended-use scope of the resource. The goal is to provide a citable reference for downstream work that already uses this dataset, not to propose a new benchmark.

AI.tldr: Technical report introducing and documenting Naver-News-KO, a 27.4k-pair Korean news summarization dataset, its construction, statistics, baselines, and usage notes.
AI.motivation: Provide a formal, citable description and analysis of the widely used Naver-News-KO Korean news summarization dataset, clarifying how it was built, its characteristics, and appropriate usage, rather than proposing a new benchmark.
AI.method: Collect 27.4k document–summary pairs from Naver News (Economy and IT/Science) over 10 days in July 2022; define a column schema and train/val/test splits; compute corpus statistics (lengths, compression ratios, near-duplicate overlap via title Jaccard), compare with other Korean summarization corpora, establish simple extractive and abstractive baselines (Lead-3, KoBART, Gemma-2B-ko+LoRA) with released training scripts, and describe licensing/intended use.
AI.result: Created Naver-News-KO with 22,194 / 2,466 / 2,740 train/val/test instances, mean 6.03x character compression, and 16.8% near-duplicate title overlap between train and test; reported baseline performance (Lead-3: R-1 55.1/R-L 50.6; KoBART: R-1 56.6/BERTScore-F1 81.5; Gemma-2B-ko+LoRA: R-1 55.3/BERTScore-F1 78.3); dataset is widely adopted with ~33k monthly downloads and multiple community models fine-tuned on it.
AI.conclusion: Naver-News-KO is a well-specified, openly available Korean news summarization dataset with documented collection protocol, characteristics, baselines, and licensing. It is intended as a standard, citable resource to support ongoing work in Korean summarization, not as a new benchmark proposal, and users should be aware of its domain coverage and the measured train–test near-duplicate overlap.

title: The Devil is in the Spectrum: Mitigating Representation Collapse in LLMs via Topologically Regularized Side-Path
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are fundamentally limited by representation collapse, a bottleneck that severely degrades long-context performance. We identify that existing approaches risk drifting into one of two pathological extremes: homogenization collapse (e.g., attention sinks causing rank deficiency) and isolation collapse (e.g., local attention causing context disconnection). Through spectral analysis of attention dynamics, we derive an intrinsic trade-off between mixing efficiency (spectral gap) and information capacity (effective rank) that standard mechanisms struggle to balance. To resolve this dilemma, we propose the Topologically Regularized Side-Path (TRSP), a non-invasive architectural intervention that achieves spectral balance. TRSP employs a parameter-free Triangular Box mechanism, scaled by a lightweight, length-aware gate, to regularize the token interaction topology. By integrating proximal coupling to preserve effective rank and distal propagation to support non-degenerate mixing, TRSP promotes a geometrically healthier transition operator without altering core attention. Experiments show significant improvements across general capabilities and long-context benchmarks. Notably, on NoLiMa at $8\times$ the training length, TRSP retains $83\%$ accuracy and surpasses the Differential Transformer and Gated Attention by approximately 30 and 50 percentage points, respectively. Code available at: https://github.com/Eziotao-tyd/TRSP.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies a fundamental limitation in LLMs called representation collapse that harms long-context performance and proposes a new architectural add-on, TRSP, that balances information mixing and capacity to greatly improve long-context accuracy.
AI.motivation: Long-context LLMs often fail to effectively use very long inputs because their internal representations collapse: either all tokens become too similar (homogenization) or distant parts of the context become disconnected (isolation). Existing architectural fixes such as attention sinks or local attention tend to push the model into one of these two extremes. The authors aim to understand this phenomenon more formally and design a mechanism that preserves both good information mixing across long sequences and sufficient representational diversity.
AI.method: The authors perform spectral analysis of the attention transition operator to reveal a trade-off between mixing efficiency (captured by the spectral gap) and information capacity (captured by the effective rank). They show that standard attention mechanisms struggle to optimize both simultaneously, leading to two failure modes: homogenization collapse and isolation collapse. To address this, they introduce Topologically Regularized Side-Path (TRSP), a non-invasive architectural side-path added on top of existing attention. TRSP uses a parameter-free Triangular Box mechanism to impose a structured, topology-aware token interaction pattern, scaled by a small length-aware gating module. This side-path provides proximal coupling to maintain high effective rank and distal propagation to ensure non-degenerate mixing, while leaving the core attention computation unchanged.
AI.result: Across general capability and long-context benchmarks, models equipped with TRSP outperform baselines that use standard attention or previous long-context architectures. On the NoLiMa benchmark at 8 times the training sequence length, TRSP maintains 83% accuracy and outperforms Differential Transformers and Gated Attention models by about 30 and 50 percentage points, respectively. These results demonstrate that TRSP significantly improves length generalization and long-context performance without modifying the main attention mechanism.
AI.conclusion: Representation collapse is a key limitation in LLM long-context performance and arises from an intrinsic trade-off between mixing and information capacity in attention. By explicitly regularizing the interaction topology through a topological side-path, TRSP achieves a more balanced spectral profile and mitigates both homogenization and isolation collapse. This leads to substantial and robust gains in long-context benchmarks, suggesting that spectral and topological regularization is a promising direction for scaling LLM context length without sacrificing representational quality.

title: GLAN-QnA-KR: A Seedless Taxonomy-Driven Korean Instruction Corpus
summary: We release GLAN-QnA-KR, a 303,581-row openly redistributable Korean instruction-QA corpus produced via the seedless taxonomy-driven GLAN synthesis pipeline with Microsoft's Phi-3.5-MoE-instruct as the producer model (generation: 2024-12; release: 2024-12; licence: OpenRAIL). The corpus spans a flat taxonomy of 1,084 English-labelled disciplines paired with Korean question/answer text, a 100-900 difficulty scale, and a median of 313 question characters and 1,098 answer characters per record. Two properties are atypical for synthetic instruction data at this scale: (i) exact duplicate questions number only 1 in 303,581 rows and character-trigram near-duplicate clusters at Jaccard >= 0.9 number zero in a 5,000-sample probe, and (ii) a two-layer contamination audit against KMMLU, KoBEST (five sub-tasks), and HAE-RAE-Bench shows a maximum test-vs-corpus question-level character-trigram Jaccard of 0.163 with zero test items at Jaccard >= 0.7, and a maximum multilingual-E5 cosine of 0.901 with a single test item at cosine >= 0.90 and zero at >= 0.95, across 20,000 sampled GLAN questions and seven evaluation sets. At the time of release, this is, to our knowledge, the largest single-pipeline synthetic Korean instruction corpus verifiable on the Hugging Face Hub and the only Korean >=100k-row corpus built under a seedless taxonomy-driven protocol. This note documents the generation protocol, corpus statistics, the contamination audit, and the licensing boundary in a form suitable for downstream citation.

AI.tldr: Introduction of GLAN-QnA-KR, a large, openly redistributable Korean instruction-QA corpus generated by a seedless taxonomy-driven pipeline using Phi-3.5-MoE-instruct, with detailed stats and contamination audits.
AI.motivation: High-quality, large-scale Korean instruction data is scarce, especially corpora that are both openly redistributable and carefully de-duplicated/contamination-audited. The authors aim to provide a large, verifiable Korean QA corpus suitable for training and evaluating LLMs, addressing issues of duplication, data contamination with benchmarks, and unclear licensing in existing resources.
AI.method: They use the GLAN synthesis pipeline with a seedless, taxonomy-driven protocol: 1,084 English-labelled disciplines define a flat taxonomy, within which Microsoft Phi-3.5-MoE-instruct generates Korean question–answer pairs with difficulty scores from 100 to 900. They perform deduplication and near-duplicate analysis via character-trigram Jaccard similarity, and run a two-layer contamination audit against major Korean benchmarks (KMMLU, KoBEST, HAE-RAE-Bench) using both character-trigram Jaccard and multilingual-E5 cosine similarity across sampled questions and evaluation sets. They also define and document the licensing boundary under OpenRAIL.
AI.result: They obtain a 303,581-row Korean instruction-QA corpus with a median of 313 characters per question and 1,098 per answer, spanning 1,084 disciplines and difficulty scores from 100 to 900. Duplicate and near-duplicate analysis shows only 1 exact duplicate in the full corpus and zero near-duplicate clusters at Jaccard ≥ 0.9 in a 5,000-row sample. Contamination audits show very low overlap with seven evaluation sets: maximum question-level character-trigram Jaccard 0.163 with no test items ≥ 0.7, and maximum multilingual-E5 cosine 0.901 with only one item ≥ 0.90 and none ≥ 0.95.
AI.conclusion: GLAN-QnA-KR is a large, diverse, and relatively contamination-free Korean instruction-QA corpus generated via a single, seedless taxonomy-driven pipeline and released under OpenRAIL.
