title: RE-AD: Real-Time Requirement Adherence for Data Labeling
comment: Accepted to The Fifth Generation, Evaluation & Metrics Workshop (GEM) workshop at ACL 2026
summary: Human-annotated data remains fundamental to training frontier Large Language Models (LLMs). However, crowd-sourced annotations often suffer from quality issues stemming from annotator misunderstanding or lack of engagement. To address this, we introduce a real-time requirement adherence (RE-AD) framework that leverages LLMs to proactively validate labeling quality. Our methodology involves decomposing Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) into atomic rules via self-reflection, categorizing them by complexity, and applying tiered validation strategies. Evaluated on a synthetic benchmark, the system achieved an F1 score of 0.749. Furthermore, production deployment resulted in annotators accepting and fixing 82% of the errors flagged by the framework. We include ablation studies to demonstrate the impact of our core design decisions.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a framework (RE-AD) that uses LLMs to monitor and improve the quality of human annotations in real time by checking adherence to task requirements, showing strong performance in both synthetic benchmarks and real-world deployment.
AI.motivation: High-quality human-annotated data is essential for training advanced LLMs, but crowd-sourced labels frequently contain errors due to misunderstanding of instructions or low annotator engagement. Existing QC methods are often offline, slow, or coarse-grained, so there is a need for more proactive, fine-grained, and scalable quality control during the annotation process itself.
AI.method: The authors design the RE-AD framework, which uses an LLM to (1) decompose Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) into fine-grained atomic rules via self-reflection, (2) categorize these rules by complexity, and (3) apply different validation strategies (a tiered approach) depending on rule complexity. The system runs in real time during annotation to detect and flag possible violations of labeling requirements, and provides feedback to annotators to correct mistakes.
AI.result: On a synthetic benchmark designed to test rule adherence, RE-AD achieves an F1 score of 0.749 at detecting violations. In real production deployment, annotators accepted and corrected 82% of the errors flagged by the framework, indicating both reasonably high precision and practical usefulness of the feedback. Ablation studies show that each major design component (rule decomposition, complexity categorization, tiered validation) contributes meaningfully to overall performance.
AI.conclusion: RE-AD demonstrates that LLMs can effectively serve as real-time quality controllers for human annotation work by operationalizing SOPs into checkable rules and dynamically validating adherence. The approach improves label quality in practice and offers a scalable way to enforce complex instructions; design ablations support the importance of the proposed components.

title: AISE-Bench: A Full-Cycle Curated Benchmark for Information Seeking on Academic Knowledge Graphs
summary: Large language models (LLMs) augmented with tools are emerging as autonomous agents capable of using Web engine, APIs, and code to solve complex, long-horizon tasks. Current tool-using benchmarks for information seeking on academic graphs rely on synthetic templates, simplified solution spaces, or narrow tasks such as paper-centric tasks, leaving key challenges underexplored - realistic user intent, complex multi-step API planning, rich parameter filling for APIs, grounded answers with references, and comprehensive evaluation of both the process and the outcome. We introduce AISE-Bench, a real-world, full-cycle annotated benchmark for information seeking on academic knowledge graphs. AISE-Bench release contains 1,133 QA pairs, including query taxonomies, full API execution trajectories, validated parameters, and source-grounded answers with reference links. To support high-quality annotation, we design a customized agent workflow to enable annotators to plan, execute, and revise complex API workflows efficiently. We develop a comprehensive evaluation protocol measuring answer quality, reference grounding, API-planning correctness, and execution success. Among the 14 evaluated methods, even the strongest model (PLAY2PROMPT with Gemini-3-Pro) achieves only moderate performance and often struggles with API planning and execution. AISE-Bench establishes a challenging new testbed for quantitatively evaluating and improving the stepwise correctness, grounded summarization, and traceable reasoning of multi-step API-using LLM agents. Our code and data are available at https://aise-bench.github.io/.

AI.tldr: AISE-Bench is a challenging, fully annotated benchmark to evaluate tool-using LLM agents on realistic, multi-step information-seeking tasks over academic knowledge graphs.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for tool-using LLMs on academic graphs are limited: they rely on synthetic templates, simplified solution spaces, or narrow paper-centric tasks. As a result, they do not adequately capture realistic user intents, complex multi-step API planning, rich parameter specification, grounded answering with explicit references, or comprehensive evaluation of both reasoning process and final outcome. There is a need for a realistic, end-to-end benchmark to drive progress on autonomous, tool-augmented LLM agents in academic information seeking.
AI.method: The authors construct AISE-Bench, a real-world benchmark of 1,133 question–answer pairs over academic knowledge graphs. For each question, they provide a query taxonomy, full API execution trajectories, validated API parameters, and source-grounded reference-linked answers. They design a customized agent workflow to help human annotators plan, execute, and revise complex API workflows efficiently during dataset creation. They also propose a multifaceted evaluation protocol that scores answer quality, correctness and grounding of references, correctness of API planning, and execution success of API calls. They then evaluate 14 different LLM-based tool-using methods, including PLAY2PROMPT with Gemini-3-Pro, on the benchmark.
AI.result: AISE-Bench offers a rich, full-cycle annotation of realistic academic information-seeking tasks that involve multi-step API usage. Empirical evaluation of 14 methods shows that performance is generally modest: even the strongest system, PLAY2PROMPT with Gemini-3-Pro, only reaches moderate scores and frequently fails in API planning and execution. This demonstrates that current LLM agents struggle with complex, tool-based academic search and reasoning tasks as represented in AISE-Bench.
AI.conclusion: AISE-Bench provides a challenging new testbed for evaluating and improving multi-step, tool-using LLM agents on academic information seeking. By including detailed API trajectories, validated parameters, and grounded answers, along with a comprehensive evaluation protocol, it enables quantitative assessment of stepwise reasoning correctness, grounded summarization, and traceable tool use. The moderate performance of existing methods underscores substantial headroom for future research on more reliable and effective tool-using LLM agents. The benchmark’s code and data are publicly released for the community.

title: Learn2Zinc: Fine-tuning Small Language Models for Text-to-Model Translation in MiniZinc
comment: CP 2026 Workshop on LLMs meet Constraint Solving
summary: Large language models excel at code generation for mainstream programming languages but struggle with rare, domain-specific languages such as MiniZinc, a constraint modeling language for combinatorial problems. We investigate whether targeted fine-tuning can teach small language models (0.6B to 20B parameters) to generate syntactically correct and semantically valid MiniZinc models from natural language problem descriptions. Our key finding is that syntax errors dominate failures when working with this domain specific language: the out-of-the-box execution accuracy of small language models such as Qwen3, LLaMa, Gemma, and GPT-OSS is near-zero. We propose a cross-model error bootstrapping approach that collects syntax errors from multiple LLM runs and leverage those to curate an error correction training dataset. This dataset allows us fine-tune small language models that consistently improves both direct code generation and chain-of-thought approaches across all model sizes. With self-reflection and ensembling, our approach achieves up to 98\% execution accuracy. In parallel, solution accuracy still remains at 35\%, indicating that while syntax is learnable, constraint reasoning remains a challenge. We contribute our fine-tuning pipeline, datasets, and models to opens-source for further research on text-to-model translation.

AI.tldr: They fine-tune small LLMs to generate MiniZinc code from natural language, using syntax-error bootstrapping to dramatically improve syntactic execution accuracy while semantic solution accuracy remains modest.
AI.motivation: General-purpose LLMs perform poorly on rare, domain-specific languages like MiniZinc, with near-zero executable code due to syntax errors. There is a need to make small models reliably generate syntactically correct and semantically meaningful MiniZinc from natural language, enabling broader use of LLMs for combinatorial modeling and constraint programming.
AI.method: 1) Evaluate several small LLMs (0.6B–20B) on NL-to-MiniZinc to characterize error types. 2) Observe that syntax errors dominate, with very low initial execution accuracy. 3) Propose cross-model error bootstrapping: run multiple LLMs repeatedly, collect their diverse syntax errors, and build an error-correction training dataset by pairing faulty code with corrected versions. 4) Fine-tune small LLMs on this curated dataset to improve both direct generation and chain-of-thought modeling. 5) Augment inference with self-reflection and model ensembling to further refine outputs. 6) Measure execution accuracy (syntax+runtime validity) and solution accuracy (correctness of resulting solutions).
AI.result: Fine-tuned small LLMs show substantial gains in generating syntactically correct and executable MiniZinc. With self-reflection and ensembling, execution accuracy reaches up to 98%, a huge improvement from near-zero for base models. However, solution accuracy—the percentage of instances where the generated model yields correct solutions—remains around 35%, showing that reasoning about constraints and problem structure is still limited even when syntax is mostly correct.
AI.conclusion: Targeted fine-tuning using cross-model error bootstrapping effectively teaches small LLMs the syntax of a niche constraint modeling language, yielding very high execution accuracy for NL-to-MiniZinc generation. Yet, mastering syntax does not automatically confer strong constraint reasoning or modeling skills, as indicated by modest solution accuracy. The work highlights the separation between syntactic competence and semantic/modeling competence, and provides an open-source pipeline, datasets, and models to support further research on text-to-formal-model translation and improved reasoning in domain-specific languages.

title: ExecuGraph: A Multi-Agent, Execution-Grounded Framework for Reliable Backend Code Synthesis with Large Language Models
comment: Submitted to Data Science and Management
summary: Large Language Models generate plausible backend code, but a single-pass paradigm provides no guarantee of correctness or runtime reliability. We present ExecuGraph, a multi-agent framework that places execution-based validation at the center of backend code synthesis. Six specialized agents (Planner, Code Generator, Logical Reviewer, Evaluator, Optimizer, and Explainer) are coordinated by a typed directed workflow with a bounded retry budget, implemented on LangGraph with locally hosted models (Ollama) and an optional retrieval layer for algorithmic technique recall. A subprocess-isolated sandbox with a wall-clock timeout guards every evaluation. We evaluate on a curated 30-problem DSA suite (internal-30), HumanEval (n=64), and an APPS-introductory subset, contrasting ExecuGraph against a single-agent one-shot baseline and a single-agent execution-retry baseline (a Reflexion-style ablation that isolates the contribution of multi-agent decomposition). On internal-30, the three conditions are statistically indistinguishable (n=30; paired Wilcoxon p=0.59 MF vs. SO, p=0.08 SR vs. SO); 95% bootstrap confidence intervals on all pairwise mean differences include zero. On HumanEval, multi-full edges ahead by +3.1 pp. The strongest signal is cross-model: with DeepSeekCoder V2 Lite, graph-category accuracy improves from 57.5% (oneshot) to 80.0% (multi-full), a +22.5 pp jump that supports a scaling hypothesis: the value of multi-agent decomposition grows with base-model capability. The framework's primary contribution is methodological: a single codebase that collapses by configuration into one-shot, execution-retry, and per-agent ablation conditions, enabling controlled measurement of each lever's marginal contribution. A per-agent ablation, retry-budget sweep, error-class taxonomy, and test-source audit are reported.

AI.tldr: ExecuGraph is a multi-agent, execution-centered framework for generating and validating backend code, compared to single-agent and execution-retry baselines.
AI.motivation: Single-pass LLM code generation often yields syntactically correct but semantically faulty backend code, with no guarantee of correctness or runtime robustness. There is a need for a systematic, empirical way to incorporate execution feedback and multi-agent decomposition while allowing controlled ablations to measure their contributions.
AI.method: They build ExecuGraph, a LangGraph-based, typed directed workflow of six specialized agents (Planner, Code Generator, Logical Reviewer, Evaluator, Optimizer, Explainer) using locally hosted models (Ollama) and optional retrieval. Each code candidate is executed in an isolated sandbox with timeouts. They evaluate across three configurations: one-shot single agent, single-agent with execution-based retries (Reflexion-style), and the full multi-agent workflow. Benchmarks include a custom 30-problem DSA set, HumanEval, and an introductory APPS subset. They perform per-agent ablations, vary the retry budget, and categorize errors, with statistical tests (paired Wilcoxon, bootstrap CIs) to compare conditions.
AI.result: On the internal 30-problem suite, there is no statistically significant difference among the three conditions; confidence intervals of pairwise mean differences include zero. On HumanEval, the multi-agent setup modestly outperforms baselines by about 3.1 percentage points. The largest improvement is for DeepSeekCoder V2 Lite: accuracy increases from 57.5% (one-shot) to 80.0% (multi-agent), a 22.5 percentage point gain, suggesting that more capable base models benefit more from multi-agent decomposition. They also provide detailed analyses from ablations, retry sweeps, error taxonomies, and test-source audits.
AI.conclusion: ExecuGraph demonstrates that execution-centric, multi-agent workflows for backend code generation can provide measurable benefits over simpler baselines, especially when paired with stronger base models. The main contribution is methodological: a unified, configurable framework that cleanly factors one-shot, execution-retry, and multi-agent variants, enabling controlled studies of how each design choice—agents, retries, retrieval—impacts performance and reliability.

title: Dropping the Anchor: Statistical Context Summarization for Distributed Systems via Pulsar Attention
summary: Inference with large language models (LLMs) on long sequences is computationally expensive due to the quadratic complexity of self-attention. Distributed blockwise methods such as Star Attention reduce this cost by sharding context across hosts, but rely on prepending a static, content-blind copy of the first block to every host. We propose Pulsar Attention, which replaces the static anchor with two lightweight, content-aware components: a small attention-sink prefix that stabilizes softmax, and compact cross-block summaries built via a Max-IDF heuristic that selects chunks containing globally rare tokens. This reduces the Phase 1 per-GPU FLOPs by up to 3.3$\times$ over Star Attention while retaining an identical KV cache footprint. On RULER and BABILong with Llama-3.1-8B, Pulsar Attention outperforms both Star Attention and dense attention at sequence lengths up to 128K tokens, with absolute gains of up to 4.7% over the dense baseline.

AI.tldr: Pulsar Attention is a distributed attention mechanism that accelerates long-context LLM inference by replacing Star Attention’s static, content-blind anchor block with lightweight, content-aware prefixes and summaries, achieving up to 3.3× FLOPs reduction and better accuracy than dense attention up to 128K tokens.
AI.motivation: Long-context LLM inference is very expensive because standard self-attention scales quadratically with sequence length. Existing distributed methods like Star Attention reduce cost by sharding the context but depend on copying an identical, static first block to each device, which is computationally inefficient and content-agnostic. The authors are motivated to design a more efficient, content-aware mechanism that preserves or improves quality while further reducing computational overhead, without increasing KV cache size.
AI.method: They introduce Pulsar Attention, a distributed blockwise attention variant. Instead of prepending a full static first block to every host as an anchor, Pulsar uses two smaller, content-aware components: (1) a short attention-sink prefix that serves as a numerically stable anchor for the softmax operation, and (2) compact cross-block summaries created using a Max-IDF heuristic. The Max-IDF heuristic identifies and selects chunks that contain globally rare (high inverse document frequency) tokens, under the assumption that such tokens are more informative for long-range interactions. These summaries plus the sink tokens replace the large, repeated anchor block used in Star Attention, while keeping the same KV cache footprint per block.
AI.result: In distributed long-context inference, Pulsar Attention reduces Phase 1 per-GPU FLOPs by up to 3.3× relative to Star Attention, without increasing key–value cache memory. On long-context evaluation benchmarks RULER and BABILong, using Llama-3.1-8B at context lengths up to 128K tokens, Pulsar achieves higher performance than both Star Attention and standard dense attention, with absolute accuracy gains up to 4.7% over the dense baseline.
AI.conclusion: Pulsar Attention offers a more compute-efficient and accurate way to run LLMs on very long sequences in distributed settings. By replacing Star Attention’s static, content-blind anchor with small, content-aware sink tokens and IDF-based cross-block summaries, it cuts floating-point cost substantially while maintaining the same KV cache footprint and delivering better long-context performance than both Star Attention and dense attention on benchmarks up to 128K tokens.

title: FlowEdit: Information-Theoretic Control of LLM Reasoning Flows for Ill-posed Problems Involving Conflicts
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) perform strongly on well-specified reasoning tasks with a feasible answer. However, problems encountered in the open world can become ill-posed due to inconsistent conditions, conflicting statements, or mutually incompatible requirements, admitting no valid responses. We argue that reasoning of such ill-posed problems involving conflicts require novel LLM capabilities to make hidden conflicts explicit, maintain competing hypotheses via multiple reasoning branches, and generate alternative responses in a single pass, all of which are challenging due to the limitation of the next-token prediction mechanism in LLMs. To this end, we propose FlowEdit, a novel framework that leverages information-theoretic principles to quantify and regulate internal reasoning flows of LLMs, for generating a full set of alternative responses under valid hypotheses. FlowEdit can be viewed as enforcing a branch-aware reasoning process using two dual information-theoretic objectives on the model's internal reasoning representations: maximizing the information flow from each selected hypothesis to the branch outcome, while minimizing the overlap and conditional dependence across sibling branches, to provide a diverse, informative set of responses with broad coverage. We show that this is achieved through tractable variational bounds under boundary embeddings being ε-sufficient, optimizing the underlying conditional mutual information in LLM reasoning process. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FlowEdit outperforms leading proprietary models, improving exact-set-match accuracy by 68%, while boosting overall response informativeness by 24%. We further show that flow regulation surfaces in the token stream as a redistribution of next-token entropy that concentrates inside each branch, amplifies at flow boundaries, and scales with the number of flows the problem requires.

AI.tldr: FlowEdit is a framework that restructures LLM internal reasoning into explicit, competing branches so the model can handle ill-posed, conflict-ridden problems and output diverse, mutually consistent alternative answers in one pass.
AI.motivation: Real-world problems are often ill-posed because they contain contradictions or incompatible requirements, so there may be no single valid answer. Standard LLMs, optimized for next-token prediction, struggle to detect and represent these hidden conflicts and typically force a single linear reasoning path. The authors want a principled way to manage multiple conflicting hypotheses inside the model and produce a complete set of alternative responses that reflect different consistent interpretations of the input.
AI.method: The authors propose FlowEdit, which regulates the internal reasoning flow of LLMs using information-theoretic objectives. They create explicit reasoning branches corresponding to different hypotheses, and then optimize two dual objectives on internal representations: (1) maximize the information flow from each hypothesis to its branch’s outcome, and (2) minimize overlap and conditional dependence between sibling branches. They derive tractable variational bounds under an assumption that boundary embeddings are ε-sufficient, effectively optimizing conditional mutual information to enforce branch-aware reasoning and produce diverse branches with broad coverage of plausible solutions.
AI.result: In experiments, FlowEdit outperforms strong proprietary baselines on benchmarks involving conflicting or ill-posed problems. It yields a 68% improvement in exact-set-match accuracy (matching the full set of valid responses) and a 24% increase in response informativeness. Analysis of token-level behavior shows that flow regulation redistributes next-token entropy: entropy becomes concentrated within branches, spikes at branch boundaries, and scales with the number of reasoning flows needed by the task.
AI.conclusion: FlowEdit demonstrates that explicitly regulating information flow within LLMs can convert linear next-token reasoning into branch-aware, multi-hypothesis reasoning suitable for ill-posed, conflict-laden tasks. By maximizing per-branch information and minimizing cross-branch dependence, the framework produces diverse, informative sets of alternative answers and reveals interpretable signatures in the token entropy patterns during generation.

title: CAMeR: Keyword-Gated Hybrid Activation for Adaptive Memory Retention in LLM Agents
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents operating over extended dialogues accumulate vast amounts of information, yet existing memory systems either retain everything indiscriminately or apply uniform forgetting heuristics that fail to distinguish relevant from irrelevant knowledge. We present CAMeR (Context-Activated Memory Reinforcement), a memory retention framework combining keyword-gated hybrid activation -- a joint symbolic (word-level Jaccard) and sub-symbolic (embedding cosine) gating mechanism -- with adaptive weight dynamics. CAMeR computes a hybrid similarity score for each memory-query pair; memories exceeding a threshold receive reinforcement while all memories undergo controlled decay. We introduce CAMeR-Bench, a 76-memory, 100-round benchmark spanning 8 topic clusters with graded activation frequency, designed to test adaptive retention where existing benchmarks (LoCoMO, LongMemEval) cannot. On CAMeR-Bench, CAMeR's keyword gate achieves a 1.6$\times$ larger retention gap between high-frequency and never-referenced memories compared to embedding-only gating (scissors gap: 0.039 vs. 0.024), while time-driven baselines (Oblivion, SuperLocalMemory) collapse to near-zero weights over 100 rounds. CAMeR's top-5 retrieval saves 83.2\% tokens versus full-context approaches (39k vs. 231k cumulative) while producing weight signals that improve retrieval precision. Through 8 ablation conditions we establish that the keyword gate -- not learnable decay -- is the primary performance driver at this scale. Our findings demonstrate that hybrid symbolic-neural gating provides a simple yet effective mechanism for adaptive memory retention in LLM agents.

AI.tldr: Proposes CAMeR, a hybrid symbolic+neural memory retention framework for LLM agents that selectively reinforces relevant memories and forgets others, outperforming existing time-based or embedding-only baselines on a new long-dialogue benchmark.
AI.motivation: LLM agents in long, multi-round dialogues accumulate large amounts of memory. Existing memory systems either store everything or use naive, uniform forgetting strategies that cannot differentiate important, frequently used memories from irrelevant ones, leading to inefficiency and degraded retrieval quality. There is a need for a simple, effective mechanism that adaptively retains useful knowledge while discarding less relevant information over time.
AI.method: Introduce CAMeR (Context-Activated Memory Reinforcement), which uses a hybrid gating mechanism combining symbolic keyword overlap (word-level Jaccard similarity) and sub-symbolic embedding similarity (cosine) to compute a hybrid similarity score between each memory and the current query. Memories whose similarity exceeds a threshold receive reinforcement (weight increases), while all memories simultaneously undergo controlled decay. Design a new benchmark, CAMeR-Bench, containing 76 memories across 8 topic clusters, evaluated over 100 rounds with graded activation frequencies to test frequency-sensitive retention. Compare against time-driven baselines (Oblivion, SuperLocalMemory), embedding-only gating, and multiple ablation variants, measuring retention gaps, retrieval precision, and token savings from top-k retrieval versus full-context usage.
AI.result: On CAMeR-Bench, CAMeR’s keyword-based hybrid gate yields a 1.6× larger retention gap between frequently activated and never-used memories compared to embedding-only gating (scissors gap 0.039 vs 0.024). Time-driven baselines suffer heavy decay, driving all memory weights near zero by 100 rounds. CAMeR’s top-5 retrieval reduces cumulative token usage by 83.2% relative to full-context methods (39k vs 231k tokens) while producing more informative weight distributions that enhance retrieval precision. Ablation experiments show that the keyword gate is responsible for most of the performance gains, while more complex, learnable decay mechanisms contribute relatively little at this scale.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid symbolic-neural gating, as instantiated in CAMeR, enables adaptive memory retention in LLM agents by reinforcing genuinely relevant memories and allowing others to decay, outperforming purely time-driven or embedding-only strategies. The keyword-based gate is the main contributor to performance, suggesting that simple, interpretable symbolic signals combined with embeddings can provide efficient, token-saving memory management for long-running LLM agents. CAMeR-Bench offers a targeted evaluation setting for such adaptive retention methods.

title: MKEvolve: A Modular Multi-Agent Framework for Kernel Code Generation
summary: Despite rapid progress in LLM-based code generation, writing correct and performant kernels for hardware accelerators remains a key bottleneck in scaling modern ML workloads. We present MKEvolve (Modular Kernel Evolve), a framework that iteratively co-evolves a modular decomposition of complex PyTorch modules and the LLM-generated kernel for each submodule, refining the decomposition by splitting and fusing across iterations while independently improving each subkernel via LLM-driven beam search. The resulting kernels are programmatic compositions of independently verified subkernels, making them configurable (subkernel implementations are swappable), interpretable (errors and speedups are traceable to specific subkernels), and readily adaptable to related model architectures. Experiments with Triton on KernelBench L2 and L3, spanning multi-operator sequences and full model architectures, show that MKEvolve improves both correctness and speedup over end-to-end direct synthesis baselines while reducing LLM token usage by up to 35%.

AI.tldr: MKEvolve is a framework that automatically breaks complex PyTorch modules into submodules, evolves a GPU kernel for each using an LLM plus search, and iteratively refines both the decomposition and kernels to get faster and more correct accelerator code with fewer tokens than direct end-to-end synthesis.
AI.motivation: LLM-based code generation has advanced, but generating correct, efficient kernels for accelerators (e.g., GPUs via Triton) for complex ML workloads is still difficult and a major scalability bottleneck. End-to-end kernel synthesis for whole modules tends to be brittle, less interpretable, hard to adapt to new architectures, and expensive in LLM tokens. There is a need for a method that can systematically produce high-quality kernels while being modular, configurable, interpretable, and token-efficient.
AI.method: MKEvolve introduces a co-evolution process between (1) a modular decomposition of a complex PyTorch module into submodules and (2) the LLM-generated kernel for each submodule. Initially, the system decomposes a module into submodules and uses an LLM with beam search to generate candidate kernels for each submodule. Each subkernel is independently verified for correctness and performance. Based on these results, the framework iteratively refines the decomposition: it can split submodules further or fuse them, while continuing to improve each subkernel via LLM-driven beam search across iterations. The final overall kernel is a programmatic composition (a wrapper) that invokes the verified subkernels in sequence or composition.
AI.result: On the KernelBench L2 and L3 benchmarks using Triton, which include multi-operator sequences and full model architectures, MKEvolve outperforms end-to-end direct kernel synthesis baselines. It achieves higher correctness rates and better speedups (performance) while also cutting LLM token usage by up to 35%, indicating improved efficiency in the generation process.
AI.conclusion: Modular, iterative co-evolution of decomposition and subkernels enables better LLM-based accelerator kernel generation than monolithic end-to-end synthesis. MKEvolve produces configurable and interpretable kernels where individual subkernels can be swapped, errors and performance gains can be localized, and the resulting code can be more easily adapted to related models, all while improving correctness, runtime speedup, and token efficiency.

title: THOR: A Theta-Gamma Hierarchical Oscillatory Reasoning Framework for Multi-hop QA
summary: Multi-hop question answering requires retrieving and integrating evidence from multiple contexts. Despite the rapid progress of current research, multi-hop reasoning remains constrained by two persistent limitations: attention decay, where the model's focus on main question degrades as the reasoning chain grows, and error accumulation, where mistakes propagate across hops and compounds into final failure. Inspired by Theta-Gamma hierarchical oscillation which decouples global planning from local retrieval, enabling efficient attention transfer between hops and a verification and repair mechanism that interrupts the accumulation of errors in the wrong paths, we present THOR, a brain-inspired Theta-Gamma hierarchical oscillatory reasoning framework. Extensive comparative experiments and specific validation experiments on multi-hop QA benchmarks demonstrate that THOR improves answer accuracy and robustness while mitigating limitations, showcasing its generalization across different backbones.

AI.tldr: They propose THOR, a brain-inspired hierarchical oscillatory reasoning framework to improve multi-hop question answering by reducing attention decay and error accumulation.
AI.motivation: Multi-hop QA models struggle because their attention to the main question weakens as reasoning chains grow (attention decay), and early mistakes propagate through later hops (error accumulation), reducing accuracy and robustness.
AI.method: They design THOR, a Theta-Gamma hierarchical oscillatory reasoning framework inspired by neural oscillations in the brain. It separates global planning from local retrieval to better transfer attention between reasoning hops and adds a verification-and-repair mechanism to detect and correct wrong reasoning paths during multi-hop inference.
AI.result: On multi-hop QA benchmarks, THOR achieves higher answer accuracy and greater robustness compared with existing methods and works well across different backbone models, indicating strong generalization.
AI.conclusion: A brain-inspired hierarchical oscillatory reasoning approach can mitigate attention decay and error accumulation in multi-hop QA, improving both accuracy and robustness and generalizing across model backbones.

title: Inducing Comparability of Factorised Probability Distributions
summary: To allow for principled comparison between two probabilistic graphical models defined over non-identical variable sets, they have to be lifted to a common measurable space. To this end, we propose an extension scheme for any two given models and establish the formal foundation: Unmatched components are completed using conditionally uniform (Laplace) extensions such that the resulting joint distributions differ from the original ones only by multiplicative constants and coincide under projection. This preserves the probabilistic semantics while enabling the application of well-defined distributional discrepancy measures. We establish the invariance of the induced joint under projection and use the extensions to provide a minimal structural extension of two factor graphs to the smalles common measurable space as well as to a common graphical structure by a deterministic algorithm. In addition, we discuss structural and measure-theoretic properties and identify promising criteria for comparison methodologies.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a formal way to extend and compare probabilistic graphical models defined over different variable sets by lifting them to a shared measurable space.
AI.motivation: Existing distributional discrepancy measures typically assume that probabilistic graphical models are defined over the same set of variables, making principled comparison difficult when the variable sets differ. A general, semantically sound way to compare such heterogeneous models is missing.
AI.method: Introduce a general extension scheme that lifts any two graphical models with non-identical variable sets to a common measurable space. Missing components in each model are completed via conditionally uniform (Laplace) extensions. The authors prove that these extensions alter the original joint distributions only by multiplicative constants and that the extended distributions coincide with the originals under projection. They also give a deterministic algorithm that minimally extends two factor graphs both to the smallest common measurable space and, if desired, to a common graphical structure. Structural and measure-theoretic properties of these extensions are analyzed.
AI.result: The authors show that the induced joint distributions from the extensions are invariant under projection, that the extensions yield minimal structural changes necessary to embed models into a shared space, and that standard distributional discrepancy measures become applicable. They characterize key structural and measure-theoretic properties of the extended models and outline criteria that can guide the design of comparison methodologies.
AI.conclusion: By using conditionally uniform (Laplace) extensions, probabilistic graphical models over different variable sets can be lifted to a common measurable space without changing their semantics up to multiplicative constants. This provides a principled, minimal, and algorithmic way to create a shared representation that enables rigorous comparison of models using established discrepancy measures, and lays groundwork for developing systematic comparison methodologies based on the identified structural and measure-theoretic properties.

title: Instruct-FD: Can Your Full-Duplex Speech System Follow Turn-Taking Instructions?
summary: Current full-duplex (FD) spoken dialogue systems can produce fluid interactions, yet it remains unclear whether they can adapt their turn-taking behavior when explicitly instructed. This is critical for real-world deployment, where conversational policies vary across applications (e.g., proactive tutoring vs. passive counseling). We introduce Instruct-FD, an instruction-conditioned benchmark for evaluating controllable turn management in FD systems. To enable this, we develop a human-validated, scalable synthetic pipeline that generates instruction-conditioned conversations, along with a deployment-agnostic multi-turn evaluation protocol and an LLM-based judge. Benchmarking six state-of-the-art full-duplex systems reveals a substantial gap in instruction-following turn management: the best model achieves only 64.4% adherence. Performance is highly uneven across behaviors and scenarios, with proactive behaviors such as model backchanneling and interruption remaining particularly challenging. These findings establish instruction-following turn management as a crucial direction for building adaptable and deployable full-duplex dialogue systems.

AI.tldr: They propose and benchmark instruction-conditioned control of turn-taking in full-duplex dialogue systems, showing current models struggle to follow such instructions.
AI.motivation: Full-duplex dialogue systems can talk concurrently with users, but real applications require different turn-taking styles (e.g., proactive vs. passive). It is unknown whether current systems can adapt their turn management when explicitly instructed, which is essential for deployability across diverse use cases.
AI.method: They introduce Instruct-FD, a benchmark where systems are given explicit instructions on turn-taking behavior, then evaluated on how well they follow them. They build a scalable synthetic data pipeline to generate instruction-conditioned conversations validated by humans, define a deployment-agnostic multi-turn evaluation protocol, and use an LLM-based judge to assess adherence. Six state-of-the-art FD systems are evaluated under this framework.
AI.result: Across six state-of-the-art full-duplex systems, instruction-following for turn management is poor: the best system achieves only 64.4% adherence. Performance is uneven across behaviors and scenarios, with proactive behaviors such as backchanneling and interruption especially weak.
AI.conclusion: Instruction-following turn management is currently an unsolved problem for full-duplex dialogue systems. The Instruct-FD benchmark and findings highlight the need to develop more controllable, instruction-sensitive turn-taking mechanisms to make such systems adaptable and practical for real-world deployment.

title: LeanFlow: A Case Study in Workflow-Driven Lean Autoformalization
comment: 14 pages, 3 figures, ICML 2026: AI for Math Workshop
summary: We present and evaluate LeanFlow, an LLM agent system specialized for translating mathematical papers into buildable Lean projects. Recent verifier-in-the-loop systems show that large formal artifacts can be produced, but it remains unclear which runtime mechanisms affect completion, auditability, or efficiency in document-to-project formalization. We study this question through case studies on two previously unformalized mathematical papers in number theory and measure theory, using model, proof-workflow, and toolset ablations with Kimi2.6 and GPT5.5; we report task outcome, API calls, input tokens, and output tokens. With Kimi2.6, the full workflow completes both document-level projects within the 2000-call budget, while no-queue variants reach the budget limit; with GPT5.5, all document-level variants complete, and the full workflow has the lowest or tied-lowest input-token cost on both sources. As complementary calibration, LeanFlow reaches 75.7% BEq+ on the PFR slice of RLM25 and solves all five ICML 2026 AI for Math TCS challenge projects in our GPT5.5 runs.

AI.tldr: LeanFlow is an LLM-agent workflow for turning full mathematical papers into executable Lean proof projects, evaluated on real papers and benchmarks, with runtime design ablations.
AI.motivation: While LLMs plus proof assistants can now generate sizable formal proofs, we lack clarity on which orchestration and runtime mechanisms actually matter for reliably converting long mathematical documents into auditable, efficient Lean projects. The paper aims to fill this gap by building a specialized system and systematically studying workflow design choices.
AI.method: They design LeanFlow, an agentic system around LLMs (Kimi2.6 and GPT5.5) that translates full math papers into Lean projects. They run case studies on two previously unformalized papers in number theory and measure theory. They perform ablations on (1) which model is used, (2) the proof workflow structure, and (3) the toolset and queuing/coordination mechanisms. For each configuration they record completion success, number of API calls, and token usage. They further calibrate performance on the PFR slice of the RLM25 benchmark and the ICML 2026 AI for Math TCS challenge tasks.
AI.result: With Kimi2.6, the complete LeanFlow workflow can formalize both full papers within a 2000-call limit, whereas variants without the queuing mechanism hit the call budget before finishing. With GPT5.5, all variants manage to complete the document-level tasks, but the full LeanFlow workflow has the lowest or tied-lowest input-token cost on both source papers. On benchmarks, LeanFlow attains 75.7% BEq+ on the PFR slice of RLM25 and solves all five ICML 2026 AI for Math TCS challenge projects when using GPT5.5.
AI.conclusion: Careful workflow and tool orchestration significantly affects efficiency and reliability in document-to-Lean formalization, beyond just picking a strong LLM. LeanFlow’s full design, including its queuing mechanisms, improves completion rates and token efficiency for large formalization tasks and achieves strong performance on external math-formalization benchmarks.

title: Can Valence Reflect Morality in Natural Language? A Preliminary Annotation Study
comment: 8 pages, 2 figures, submitted to the 36th Irish Signals and Systems Conference
summary: Present implementations of artificial intelligence (AI) ethics do not adequately take feelings, or affect, into account. If AI should be aligned with human ethics, it seems reasonable to thoroughly investigate the possibility of AI behaviour that mirrors virtuous human ethical conduct, where feelings play a role in the actions, judgements or statements one makes. Furthermore, while prominent theories of normative ethics are often discussed in terms of their differences and shortcomings, Virtue, Consequentialist, and Kantian Deontological ethics all share a common feature of considering human feeling to some degree while the popular descriptive ethics theory, Moral Foundations Theory, positions feelings as central to many of its foundations. Therefore, in the present paper, a data set of moral valence is proposed, consisting of 500 annotations by six human participants for both action/judgement and consequence moral valence, ranging from -1 to 1 for text-presented scenarios from the Commonsense Norm Bank data set. The resulting valence features share significant relationships with multi-class (immoral/discretionary/moral) and binary immoral/moral categories while additionally providing a noteworthy test set Matthew's correlation coefficient of 0.764 using regularised logistic regression for binary classification. This provides early evidence of the usefulness of valence features for morality estimation of text, indicating that valenced consequences of responses for others can be considered toward more human morally-aligned AI. In the interest of promoting further affective-moral computing research, this study's annotations will be made available for research on request.

AI.tldr: Paper proposes and evaluates a moral valence dataset for text scenarios to better align AI with human, affect-laden moral judgments.
AI.motivation: Current AI ethics implementations largely ignore affect and feelings, despite major normative and descriptive ethical theories treating feelings as important to moral judgment. To better align AI with human morality, the authors aim to model and quantify the affective (valence) dimension of moral evaluations in text.
AI.method: They construct a dataset of 500 text scenarios drawn from the Commonsense Norm Bank. Six human annotators rate each scenario along two continuous moral valence dimensions—action/judgment valence and consequence valence—on a scale from -1 (strongly immoral/negative) to 1 (strongly moral/positive). They then analyze the relationships between these valence features and existing categorical morality labels (immoral/discretionary/moral and immoral/moral), and train regularised logistic regression models using the valence features for binary moral vs. immoral classification.
AI.result: The moral valence features derived from human annotations correlate significantly with the existing multi-class (immoral/discretionary/moral) and binary immoral/moral labels. Using only these valence features as inputs, a regularised logistic regression classifier achieves a Matthew’s correlation coefficient of 0.764 on a held-out test set for binary immoral vs. moral classification, indicating strong predictive power.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling affective moral valence in text is both feasible and useful for estimating morality labels, and the valence of consequences for others appears particularly informative. These results suggest that incorporating affective valence could help build AI systems whose moral judgments are better aligned with human, feeling-based ethics. The authors encourage further work in affective-moral computing and offer to share their annotated dataset upon request.

title: Optimizing Hypergraph-Based RAG: Toward Better Fact Extraction and Chunk Retrieval
summary: GraphRAG enables deeper reasoning by structuring knowledge as graphs but struggles with n-ary facts. HyperGraphRAG uses hypergraphs for richer semantics, improving accuracy, yet relies on error-prone LLM extraction and inefficient standard chunk retrieval. We address this by employing self-consistency prompting to improve the extraction, and Personalized PageRank algorithm over hypergraph to enhance chunk retrieval.

AI.tldr: They improve HyperGraphRAG by making fact extraction more reliable and chunk retrieval more effective using self-consistency prompting and Personalized PageRank on hypergraphs.
AI.motivation: GraphRAG improves reasoning via graph-structured knowledge but cannot represent n-ary (multi-argument) facts well. HyperGraphRAG can model richer semantics with hypergraphs but suffers from noisy LLM-based extraction and inefficient chunk retrieval based on standard methods. The paper aims to fix these two main limitations to get more accurate and efficient retrieval-augmented generation.
AI.method: 1) Use self-consistency prompting during LLM-based information extraction: generate multiple extraction candidates and aggregate them to reduce errors; 2) Build a hypergraph from these improved extractions; 3) Apply a Personalized PageRank algorithm over the hypergraph structure to rank and retrieve the most relevant chunks instead of naive chunk-based retrieval; 4) Integrate this improved retrieval into the RAG pipeline.
AI.result: More accurate representation of complex, n-ary facts and better retrieval quality. This should translate into higher downstream QA or reasoning accuracy compared to baseline GraphRAG and standard HyperGraphRAG with naive extraction and retrieval, though specific metrics are not given in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Combining self-consistency prompting for extraction with hypergraph-based Personalized PageRank improves both the quality of the knowledge graph and the effectiveness of chunk retrieval in HyperGraphRAG, leading to better reasoning performance in RAG systems.

title: Evaluating the Effectiveness of Persona Simulation in Opinion Prediction with GPT-4.1
comment: ICDM 2025 Undergraduate and High School Symposium
summary: Persona simulation involves utilizing large language models (LLMs) to anticipate human choices or interactions based on specific characteristic information. To further understand current limitations and future directions, we tested persona simulation in opinion prediction with GPT-4.1 (knowledge cutoff by June 2024). Using personas from nine U.S. states provided by Columbia University's Personas dataset, GPT-4.1 accurately predicted 2024 election outcomes in eight out of the nine states, only failing in one of the swing states. We then focused on opinions related to medicine and healthcare. With the American Trends Panel Wave 123 dataset from Pew Research Center, GPT-4.1 was able to anticipate beliefs about childhood vaccines with an accuracy of up to 0.94. Furthermore, we applied GPT-4.1 to generate conversations among personas and observed that the simulated dialogues and opinions adhered well to personas' personalities and backgrounds, albeit lacking natural human-like flow. Persona simulation proves to be a promising application of artificial intelligence as long as biases are addressed. In the near future, it will be beneficial to apply it to opinion analysis and reaction prediction in diverse fields ranging from public health to lawmaking to economics.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how well GPT-4.1 can simulate human personas to predict opinions and choices, showing high accuracy in U.S. election outcomes and health-related attitudes, and discusses its promise and limitations.
AI.motivation: To understand the current capabilities, limitations, and future directions of using LLM-based persona simulation to predict human opinions and behaviors, particularly in socially important domains like elections and public health.
AI.method: The authors used predefined personas from Columbia University's Personas dataset representing individuals from nine U.S. states and asked GPT-4.1 to predict 2024 election outcomes at the state level. They then used the Pew Research Center’s American Trends Panel Wave 123 to test GPT-4.1’s ability to anticipate individual beliefs about childhood vaccines based on persona characteristics. Finally, they prompted GPT-4.1 to generate multi-party conversations among personas to qualitatively assess how well the generated dialogue matched personas’ backgrounds and personalities.
AI.result: GPT-4.1 correctly simulated personas to predict 2024 election outcomes in eight out of nine states, missing one swing state. For childhood vaccine attitudes, GPT-4.1 achieved prediction accuracy as high as 0.94. Generated conversations reflected persona-consistent attitudes and backgrounds, though they lacked the natural flow characteristic of human dialogue.
AI.conclusion: Persona simulation with GPT-4.1 is already capable of highly accurate opinion and behavior prediction in certain domains, including elections and health beliefs. However, the method still exhibits limitations such as imperfect realism in dialogue and potential biases. With careful bias mitigation and further refinement, persona-based simulation could become a valuable tool for opinion analysis and reaction prediction in diverse areas such as public health, policymaking, and economics.

title: MiniCache: Reusable Program Caching with Small Model Interfaces for Efficient LLM Inference
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used for program-aided reasoning, agentic decision making, and structured task execution, but these applications often incur high inference cost. We present MiniCache, a reusable program caching framework that transforms Program-of-Thought (PoT) programs into parameterized cache objects, enabling reusable computation across structurally similar requests. MiniCache reuses the same small model for semantic variable extraction on cache-hit requests and speculative drafting during target-LLM generation, reducing expensive target-LLM invocations while preserving task quality. Experiments on shopping-style request datasets, WebShop, Formula, and CodeTAT-QA demonstrate that MiniCache improves the trade-off between inference latency, cache reuse, and accuracy, achieving up to 3.1x lower latency and 2.8x higher throughput under parallel serving. These results show that small models are most effective not as replacements for large models, but as lightweight interface models that enable reliable and efficient reusable program caching.

AI.tldr: MiniCache is a framework that caches and reuses the results of Program-of-Thought (PoT) executions to cut LLM inference cost while keeping quality high.
AI.motivation: Program-aided reasoning with LLMs, such as agentic workflows and structured tasks, is powerful but expensive because each request often re-runs very similar programs on a large model. There is a need to reduce latency and cost without sacrificing accuracy by exploiting structural similarity between requests.
AI.method: The paper introduces MiniCache, which turns PoT programs into parameterized cache objects that separate stable program structure from variable inputs. A small model is used to extract semantic variables for cache lookup and to draft candidate outputs speculatively. When a new query matches a cached program structure, MiniCache reuses previous computation: it applies the small model for variable extraction and draft generation, and invokes the large target LLM only as needed, thus reducing full LLM calls while maintaining quality.
AI.result: On shopping-style request datasets (WebShop), math-like formulas (Formula), and code-related QA (CodeTAT-QA), MiniCache improves efficiency metrics: up to 3.1x lower latency and 2.8x higher throughput in parallel serving scenarios, while sustaining comparable accuracy. It also demonstrates higher cache reuse rates enabled by semantic parameterization of PoT programs.
AI.conclusion: Small models are most beneficial not as direct substitutes for large LLMs but as lightweight interface layers that enable reusable program caching. By parameterizing PoT programs and reusing computations across structurally similar tasks, MiniCache achieves a better trade-off between latency, throughput, and accuracy for program-aided reasoning workflows.

title: Frontier Financial Judgement: Can agents tell what might move a stock?
summary: We introduce Frontier Financial Judgement, a challenging new benchmark developed in collaboration with professional equity analysts to assess agents' ability to replicate expert human judgements. Rapidly identifying new information, evaluating its implications and determining its valuation impact is one of the most time-consuming and challenging aspects of real-world equity coverage. This is becoming ever more difficult and important as AI rapidly increases the quantity of new information to process. The strongest agent we evaluate on Frontier Financial Judgement matches all expert labels in only 52.4% of cases. We also find significant divergence in estimated false-positive rates among frontier agents, ranging from ~1% for GPT-5.6 Sol to ~32% for Claude Sonnet 4.6. To construct the benchmark and make it representative of real-world settings, we combine human-designed and labelled synthetic articles with live news articles and historical documents, creating 656 items for assessment. The resulting task requires agents to distinguish genuinely new, valuation-relevant financial information from stale, immaterial or misleading news under realistic conditions. We find substantial trade-offs among agent accuracy, cost, false positives and reliability that continue to hinder the reliable deployment of news-flow filtering in practice.

AI.tldr: Benchmark to evaluate how well AI agents replicate professional equity analysts’ judgements on news relevance and valuation impact.
AI.motivation: In equity research, analysts must rapidly filter and interpret a growing volume of financial news and documents, a task made harder by AI-driven information growth. There is a need to systematically measure whether AI agents can reliably mimic expert judgement in distinguishing important, valuation-relevant news from noise.
AI.method: The authors collaborated with professional equity analysts to design a benchmark, Frontier Financial Judgement, that mirrors real-world equity coverage tasks. They constructed 656 items by mixing human-designed and labelled synthetic articles with live news and historical documents. Agents are evaluated on their ability to classify items as containing genuinely new, valuation-relevant information versus stale, immaterial, or misleading news, and the study compares models on accuracy, false positive rates, cost, and reliability.
AI.result: The best-performing agent exactly matches expert labels in only 52.4% of cases, showing that current systems fall well short of expert-level judgement. Frontier agents exhibit widely varying false-positive rates, from about 1% for GPT-5.6 Sol to about 32% for Claude Sonnet 4.6, and there are notable trade-offs between accuracy, cost, false positives, and overall reliability across models.
AI.conclusion: Current AI agents are not yet reliable enough to serve as robust news-flow filters for equity research under realistic conditions, due to limited agreement with expert labels and problematic false-positive behavior. The benchmark exposes important trade-offs among model accuracy, cost, and reliability, and provides a realistic testbed for improving AI systems’ ability to process and prioritize financial news in line with expert human judgement.

title: Telco-GAIA: Bilingual Benchmark for Agents in Telecom Domain
summary: We introduce Telco-GAIA, a bilingual, multi-modal benchmark for evaluating tool-using agents on the data of a real-world telecommunications operator. Telco-GAIA comprises 100 human-verified question-answering tasks, in English and Arabic, that each demand multi-hop reasoning (4.2 hops on average) over three heterogeneous sources: a static website snapshot (HTML, images, and linked PDFs), a synthetic relational SQL database, and external web archives, spanning text, image, and tabular modalities. The benchmark is delivered as a sandboxed Docker environment and scored by normalized exact string matching, making evaluation objective, deterministic, and reproducible over time without any LLM-as-a-Judge. Evaluating a purpose-built reference agent across twelve commercial and open LLMs, we find Telco-GAIA challenging: even the strongest model solves only 71% of tasks; under a moderate cost budget, this falls to about 40%, and the visually grounded categories remain the weakest, where the average backend scores below 30%, leaving substantial headroom in document and image understanding. Telco-GAIA offers a rigorous, reproducible testbed for enterprise agents and a template for constructing closed-domain benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Telco-GAIA is a bilingual, multi-modal benchmark to rigorously test tool-using AI agents in a realistic telecom setting.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for tool-using agents are often open-domain, text-only, non-reproducible (due to dependence on live web or LLM-as-a-judge), and not grounded in realistic enterprise data. The authors want a closed-domain, enterprise-style benchmark that stresses multi-hop reasoning over heterogeneous, real-world data sources while enabling objective, deterministic evaluation over time.
AI.method: They build Telco-GAIA, a benchmark with 100 human-verified QA tasks in English and Arabic sourced from an actual telecom operator’s environment. Each task requires multi-hop reasoning over three heterogeneous sources: (1) a static website snapshot (HTML, images, PDFs), (2) a synthetic relational SQL database, and (3) external web archives, covering text, image, and tabular modalities. The benchmark is packaged as a sandboxed Docker environment, and answers are scored by normalized exact string matching to avoid LLM-as-a-judge. They then implement a reference tool-using agent and evaluate it across 12 commercial and open LLM backends.
AI.result: Tasks are difficult: the best-performing LLM solves only 71% of tasks; imposing a moderate cost budget reduces performance to around 40%. Visually grounded categories are particularly challenging, with average model performance below 30%, showing that models struggle most with document and image understanding in this setting.
AI.conclusion: Telco-GAIA is a challenging, realistic, and reproducible benchmark for evaluating enterprise-oriented, tool-using agents. It highlights significant remaining gaps in multi-hop, multi-modal reasoning—especially for visual information—and provides both a testbed and a construction template for future closed-domain benchmarks.

title: From Agent Failures to Text Policies: What Works and What Breaks
summary: TextGrad improves language-model systems by revising text from feedback. Its core thesis is that natural-language feedback can act as a gradient for optimizing text components without changing model weights. Applying it to agents is harder because feedback arrives only after a sequence of actions, making it difficult to identify which decision caused failure. We study this problem by separating the ability to follow a useful policy from the ability to learn that policy from experience. Our main finding is a clear gap between these two abilities. Human-written policies improve two frozen 7B agents on TextWorldExpress by 5.0 success points, showing that useful policy text exists. However, policies generated from agent trajectories do not reliably outperform fixed prompting, even with richer traces, counterfactual evidence, or iterative GEPA search. The main challenge for agent-level TextGrad is therefore not executing textual policy updates, but reliably generating and selecting them from experience.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates TextGrad for agents and finds that while human-written textual policies can significantly improve frozen language-model agents, automatically learning such policies from agent experience remains unreliable.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether TextGrad, which uses natural-language feedback as a gradient to improve language-model systems without weight updates, can effectively be applied to reinforcement-learning-style agents where feedback is delayed across sequences of actions.
AI.method: They decouple an agent’s capability to execute a good textual policy from its capability to learn that policy from experience. Using TextWorldExpress and two frozen 7B-parameter language-model agents, they compare: (1) fixed prompting baselines, (2) human-authored textual policies, and (3) policies induced from agent trajectories using richer execution traces, counterfactual evidence, and iterative GEPA (Generate-Evaluate-Plan-Act) search.
AI.result: Human-written policies increase success by 5.0 points over fixed prompts, proving that effective textual policies exist for these agents. However, policies automatically generated from the agents’ own trajectories fail to consistently outperform simple fixed prompting, even when enhanced with richer trajectory information, counterfactual reasoning, and iterative GEPA-style search and refinement.
AI.conclusion: TextGrad’s main bottleneck at the agent level is not the ability of models to follow and benefit from textual policy updates, but the difficulty of reliably generating and selecting high-quality policies from experiential data. Progress will require better mechanisms for extracting, proposing, and choosing useful policy text from agent interactions rather than improving execution of given policies.

title: SiGMA: Sign-Guided Merging and Adaptation for Multimodal Continual Instruction Tuning
summary: Multimodal Continual Instruction Tuning (MCIT) is crucial for adapting Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) to evolving a sequence of downstream tasks. Prior methods mostly utilize Mixture of Experts or expansion merge approach, primarily focusing on catastrophic forgetting, yet they still suffer from negative interference during inference, where newly learned updates overwrite useful prior knowledge and degrade overall performance. To address this, we propose SiGMA (Sign Guided Merging and Adaptation), a simple yet effective framework that mitigates negative interference with two components: sign guided adaptive tuning during training and sign guided merging at inference. Sign guided adaptive tuning reduces collisions with past knowledge and learns the current task with minimal drift, mitigating severe forgetting. Sign guided merging further improves consolidation by selectively scaling salient parameters to preserve and amplify useful task specific knowledge. Experiments on UCIT and DCL benchmarks show that SiGMA significantly reduces negative interference and outperforms state of the art MCIT methods. Our code is available at SiGMA.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SiGMA, a framework for multimodal continual instruction tuning that reduces negative interference and forgetting when adapting MLLMs to sequential tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal continual instruction tuning methods focus on catastrophic forgetting but still suffer from negative interference, where new tasks overwrite or harm useful prior knowledge, reducing overall performance in MLLMs.
AI.method: SiGMA uses two sign-guided components: (1) sign guided adaptive tuning during training to align updates and reduce collisions with past knowledge, learning each new task with minimal parameter drift; and (2) sign guided merging at inference that selectively scales and merges salient parameters based on their signs to preserve and amplify task-specific and previously learned knowledge.
AI.result: On UCIT and DCL benchmarks, SiGMA substantially reduces negative interference and achieves better performance than state-of-the-art multimodal continual instruction tuning approaches.
AI.conclusion: Sign-guided training and merging is an effective and simple strategy for mitigating negative interference and catastrophic forgetting in multimodal continual instruction tuning of large language models, improving consolidation of both new and old task knowledge.

title: Learning to Detect UI Principle Violations via Reinforcement Learning
summary:
Small language models and coding agents increasingly generate web front-end code, yet their outputs are typically evaluated primarily for functional correctness. A generated interface may compile, render, and pass unit tests while still violating established interface quality principles, including accessibility barriers, deceptive design patterns, poor visual hierarchy, and excessive decision complexity. Existing auditing approaches face a trade-off between cost, coverage, and scalability: expert human review provides rich judgment but is slow and expensive; frontier vision-language models offer broader reasoning capabilities but remain costly to deploy at scale; and rule-based tools such as axe-core and Lighthouse are inexpensive but primarily capture mechanically checkable accessibility issues.
  We investigate whether a lightweight vision-language model can serve as an effective critic for generated interfaces. We unify 19 interface-quality principles from three complementary sources of HCI knowledge: WCAG 2.2 accessibility standards, deceptive design taxonomies, and established theories of perception, cognition, and interaction. To train this critic, we construct a verified dataset of approximately 10,000 generated web pages by synthetically injecting known violations into clean, LLM-generated Tailwind pages.
  Continued reinforcement learning on a 4B vision-language model improves micro-F1 from 36\% to 84\%, with 13 of 19 principles exceeding 80\% F1. The resulting critic can audit generated interfaces, filter low-quality interface training data, and provide a reward signal for design-aware code generation. We release our data-generation recipe and injection/verification prompts to support reproducible evaluation and future work on scalable interface-quality assessment.

AI.tldr: They build and train a small vision-language model as an automatic critic to detect a wide range of interface-quality problems (accessibility, deceptive patterns, usability) in generated web UIs, achieving high accuracy and enabling scalable auditing and better training data for front-end code generation.
AI.motivation: Front-end code generated by small language models and coding agents is usually evaluated only for functional correctness, ignoring important interface quality issues such as accessibility violations, deceptive design, poor visual hierarchy, and high decision complexity. Existing auditing methods are either accurate but slow and expensive (human experts), broader but still costly (frontier VLMs), or cheap but narrow in scope (rule-based tools like axe-core, Lighthouse). There is a need for a scalable, inexpensive, and semantically rich way to assess the quality of generated interfaces across many human-centered principles.
AI.method: They first unify 19 interface-quality principles drawn from WCAG 2.2 accessibility standards, taxonomies of deceptive design patterns, and HCI theories of perception, cognition, and interaction. They then build a dataset of about 10,000 web pages by generating clean Tailwind-based pages with an LLM and synthetically injecting known violations of these principles, with verification prompts to confirm each violation. Using this labeled dataset, they apply continued reinforcement learning to a relatively small (4B-parameter) vision-language model so that it can look at rendered interfaces and classify which principles are violated. The training optimizes the critic model to increase detection accuracy (micro-F1) over the 19 principles.
AI.result: After reinforcement learning, the 4B vision-language critic model’s micro-F1 score on detecting principle violations increases from 36% to 84%, and for 13 of the 19 interface-quality principles it achieves over 80% F1. This shows that a lightweight model can reliably detect a broad set of interface-quality problems in generated UIs, well beyond what rule-based tools capture.
AI.conclusion: A small, fine-tuned vision-language model can act as an effective, scalable critic of web interface quality, covering accessibility, deceptive patterns, and general usability principles at relatively low cost. This critic can be used to audit generated interfaces, filter or curate training data for interface generation systems, and serve as a reward model to guide design-aware front-end code generation. The authors also release their data-generation and injection/verification recipe to enable reproducible benchmarks and future research on automated, scalable interface-quality assessment.

title: Reliability-Aware LLM Alignment from Inconsistent Human Feedback
summary: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) is critical for aligning Large Language Models (LLMs) with human preferences. However, its efficacy is often compromised by the inherent inconsistency and subjectivity of human annotations. Existing preference optimization frameworks, such as Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), typically treat ambiguous pairs with high annotator disagreement identically to those with unanimous consensus, forcing models to overfit to inconsistent supervision signals and leading to suboptimal alignment. In this work, we propose Reliability-Guided Preference Optimization (RGPO), a robust framework designed to mitigate the impact of inconsistent human feedback. RGPO estimates annotator reliability and infers latent ground truth labels from noisy human feedback to identify robust preferences. Furthermore, we introduce a reliability-aware consistency optimization that dynamically modulates the training objective based on the consensus level of annotations, ensuring the model prioritizes high-consensus supervision signals. Extensive experiments on LLM alignment benchmarks demonstrate that RGPO effectively reduces inconsistency and noise in training data and achieves superior performance compared to widely adopted RLHF baselines. Our code and configurations are available at https://github.com/GenieHuang/RGPO.

AI.tldr: They propose RGPO, a preference optimization framework that explicitly models annotator reliability and consensus to make RLHF training more robust to noisy, inconsistent human feedback, outperforming standard methods like DPO.
AI.motivation: RLHF for LLMs relies on human preference labels that are often noisy, subjective, and inconsistent. Existing preference optimization methods (e.g., DPO) treat all preference pairs equally, regardless of annotator agreement. This can cause models to overfit to conflicting signals, harming alignment quality. The paper is motivated by the need for an RLHF training framework that can distinguish reliable from unreliable supervision and downweight or correct noisy annotations.
AI.method: They introduce Reliability-Guided Preference Optimization (RGPO). First, RGPO estimates annotator reliability and infers latent ground-truth preference labels from noisy human feedback, effectively denoising the dataset and identifying robust preferences. Then they propose a reliability-aware consistency optimization objective that scales or modulates the loss according to the level of annotator consensus, giving more weight to high-consensus pairs and less to ambiguous or low-consensus ones. This is implemented within a preference optimization setup analogous to DPO, but augmented with reliability estimation and consensus-aware weighting. They evaluate on standard LLM alignment benchmarks.
AI.result: Across multiple LLM alignment benchmarks, RGPO reduces the effect of label inconsistency and noise in the training data and yields better aligned models than strong RLHF baselines, including DPO. Empirically, it improves performance metrics related to human preference alignment and robustness, demonstrating that explicitly modeling annotator reliability and using consensus-aware weighting leads to superior outcomes.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating annotator reliability and consensus into preference optimization is an effective way to make RLHF more robust to noisy human supervision. RGPO provides a principled framework that both denoises labels and reweights training signals based on confidence, leading to better alignment than existing methods that treat all annotations equally. The approach suggests future RLHF systems should explicitly account for annotator heterogeneity and label ambiguity to achieve more reliable alignment of LLMs.

title: REGARD: Regional Affective Differences in Large Language Models
comment: 17 pages, 11 figures, 3 tables. Includes evaluation of 19 language models, two independent VAD judges, and human validation on 300 items
summary: Large language models trained and aligned within different linguistic and regional ecosystems may frame the same political, cultural, and geopolitical entities in different ways. Such differences are often evaluated through sentiment, favorability, or stance, reducing model attitudes to a single positive-negative axis. We introduce REGARD, a study of what drives affective framing differences across LLMs on post-Soviet entities using target-directed Valence-Arousal-Dominance profiling. We query 19 models on 500 region-specific targets, score their responses with two independent LLM judges, GPT-4o-mini and Qwen3.6-35B-A3B, and validate the measurements on a 300-item human-annotated subset. Post-hoc Ward-linkage clustering of all 19 models by affective and response-behavior profiles yields three behavioral clusters that cut across model origin, family, and parameter count. Generic-answer rate is strongly associated with lower arousal (r = -0.81) and with cluster placement: models that deflect evaluative prompts with templated responses cluster together at low arousal regardless of origin. These findings show that VAD profiling captures emotional intensity, a dimension of affective framing that is largely invisible to conventional sentiment-based evaluation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces REGARD, a framework for comparing how LLMs emotionally frame post-Soviet entities using Valence-Arousal-Dominance (VAD) profiles instead of simple sentiment, revealing clusters of model behaviors and showing that emotional intensity is often missed by standard sentiment analysis.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of how LLMs talk about political and regional entities typically collapse attitudes into a single positive–negative sentiment score. This misses richer affective dimensions and may obscure systematic differences between models trained in different linguistic and regional ecosystems. The authors want to understand not just whether models are positive or negative, but how strongly and in what emotional manner they frame post-Soviet entities, and whether training origin or alignment regimes drive these patterns.
AI.method: They propose REGARD, a target-directed VAD (Valence-Arousal-Dominance) profiling approach applied to 19 large language models. The models are prompted about 500 region-specific targets (post-Soviet entities). Two independent LLMs (GPT-4o-mini and Qwen3.6-35B-A3B) act as judges to assign VAD scores to the responses. The authors validate these automatic scores on a 300-item subset with human annotations. They then perform post-hoc Ward-linkage hierarchical clustering of the 19 models based on their affective profiles and response behaviors, including how often they give generic, deflective answers.
AI.result: Clustering the models by their affective and behavioral profiles yields three main behavioral clusters that do not align simply with model origin, architecture family, or size. A notable behavioral feature, the rate of generic (templated, deflective) answers, is strongly negatively correlated with arousal (r = -0.81); models that frequently provide generic responses show systematically lower arousal scores.
