These deflective models form a distinct low-arousal cluster regardless of their provenance.
AI.conclusion: VAD profiling reveals aspects of model affective framing—especially emotional intensity (arousal)—that conventional sentiment or stance measures fail to capture. Differences in how models emotionally frame post-Soviet entities cannot be fully explained by origin, family, or size, and generic-answer behavior plays a major role in lowering measured arousal and shaping cluster structure. REGARD thus provides a more nuanced tool for auditing and comparing LLM attitudes toward geopolitical and cultural targets than simple sentiment analysis.

title: CANN Bench: Benchmarking Agent Generated Kernels against Real NPU and Algorithmic Limits
summary: AI agents are now capable of writing, compiling, and iteratively optimizing low-level operator kernels on different hardware platforms. Existing benchmarks, however, focus almost exclusively on CUDA and Triton, leaving hardware ecosystems with less-exposed programming models without a common evaluation baseline. We present CANN Bench, an open benchmark for AI-generated operator code on Huawei's Ascend NPU. The current release covers 53 operators and 1060 test cases organized into four difficulty tiers -- from simple elementwise primitives to MoE dispatch and FlashAttention kernels -- spanning FP16, BF16, FP32, and INT8 precision formats. Evaluation adopts a \textbf{three-dimensional weighted composite score} that treats compilation, functional correctness, and performance as independent axes, providing a principled reward signal for kernel-generation agents. Performance is graded against an out-of-the-box PyTorch-on-Ascend baseline and an analytical per-case Hardware-Anchored Performance (HAP) limit on real NPU hardware, ensuring scores reflect genuine optimization headroom rather than measurement artifacts. The evaluation harness is designed to resist reward hacking from the ground up. CANN Bench is versioned within the official CANN repository and is designed for long-term community co-construction, providing the Ascend ecosystem with a quantitative, reproducible, and sustainably maintained yardstick for AI operator-authoring capability.

AI.tldr: They introduce CANN Bench, a benchmark to evaluate AI-generated operator kernels on Huawei Ascend NPUs, covering many operators and difficulty tiers with a 3D scoring system for compilation, correctness, and performance.
AI.motivation: AI agents can now auto-generate and optimize low-level kernels, but most benchmarks target CUDA/Triton, leaving Ascend NPUs without a standardized way to evaluate AI-generated kernels or compare optimization quality.
AI.method: They design an open benchmark (CANN Bench) for Ascend NPUs with 53 operators and 1060 test cases across four difficulty levels and multiple numeric formats, define a three-dimensional weighted score (compilation, correctness, performance), compare performance against PyTorch-on-Ascend and a Hardware-Anchored Performance ceiling, and build a robust evaluation harness to prevent reward hacking. The benchmark is versioned inside the official CANN repository for community-driven evolution.
AI.result: A concrete benchmark suite, scoring methodology, and evaluation harness that together produce quantitative, reproducible scores for AI-generated operator kernels on Ascend NPUs, aligned with actual hardware limits rather than noisy measurements.
AI.conclusion: CANN Bench offers the Ascend ecosystem a long-term, community-maintained, and reproducible yardstick to measure and compare AI-based operator-authoring capabilities, grounded in real hardware performance and robust to reward hacking.

title: Rushes: A Human Preference Dataset for Pluralistic Alignment
summary: We introduce Rushes, a dataset and benchmark for studying revealed human engagement preferences in interactive narrative environments. Rushes is collected through a game interface where users interact with AI-generated branching narratives and select one choice from a small, explicit candidate set at each decision point. Each interaction logs the full candidate set, the user's choice, and the evolving narrative context, yielding time-ordered trajectories with persistent user-level identifiers. Rushes contains 44,226 decision events from 8,167 unique users across six games, capturing sequential, personalized engagement behavior rather than static judgments. We show that user choices exhibit structured, non-random patterns, quantified by a low choice entropy relative to a uniform baseline. We position Rushes as a diagnostic benchmark for pluralistic alignment and demonstrate a robust Engagement Gap: state-of-the-art LLMs, including GPT-5, fail to outperform simple baselines. While classical Matrix Factorization (SVD) captures measurable personalized signal (37.7%), frontier LLMs (34.23%) struggle to even match the Popularity Baseline (36.4%) on event-level choice prediction. This gap suggests that single, population-level objectives, like those used in modern RLHF, appear insufficient to capture heterogeneous, context-dependent engagement signals. As a result, even highly capable models default to majority preferences rather than adapting to individual trajectories. We release Rushes to support research into pluralistic alignment and sequential decision-making in generative systems. The full code for the platform and dataset will be available here: https://github.com/microsoft/rushes

AI.tldr: Rushes is a dataset and benchmark of human choice behavior in AI-driven branching story games, used to study how people actually engage with interactive narratives over time.
AI.motivation: Current alignment and evaluation methods often rely on static, one-off judgments or population-level objectives (e.g., RLHF preferences), which fail to capture how real users sequentially interact with generative systems and how preferences vary across individuals and contexts. There is a need for a dataset that records fine-grained, time-ordered engagement choices in rich, narrative environments to better understand pluralistic alignment and personalized behavior.
AI.method: The authors built a game interface featuring AI-generated branching narratives. At each decision point, users see a small, explicit candidate set of possible continuations and select one. The system logs the entire candidate set, the user's chosen option, and the narrative context, creating time-ordered trajectories with persistent user identifiers. They collect data across six different games, then analyze patterns of choice (e.g., entropy, non-random structure) and benchmark various models—including classical matrix factorization, popularity baselines, and state-of-the-art LLMs like GPT-5—on predicting event-level user choices.
AI.result: Rushes comprises 44,226 decision events from 8,167 users across six games. Analysis shows that user choices are structured and non-random, with significantly lower entropy than a uniform baseline. In predictive benchmarks, a simple Popularity Baseline achieves ~36.4% accuracy, classical matrix factorization (SVD) reaches 37.7%, while frontier LLMs achieve only about 34.23% on event-level choice prediction, failing to beat even simple baselines. This performance gap is referred to as the Engagement Gap.
AI.conclusion: User engagement in interactive narratives reflects heterogeneous, context-dependent preferences that are not well captured by single, population-level objectives like those used in standard RLHF. Even highly capable LLMs tend to reproduce majority or average preferences rather than adapting to individual user trajectories. Rushes is proposed as a diagnostic benchmark and open dataset to support research on pluralistic alignment, personalization, and sequential decision-making in generative AI systems.

title: Representation Robustness Under Executable Reasoning Constraints in Large Language Models for Mathematical Problem Solving
comment: presented at the 28th International Conference on Human-Computer Interaction (2026), Montreal, Canada
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly evaluated on mathematical problem solving, yet prior work often treats representationally equivalent formulations as interchangeable and conflates reasoning errors with interface failures. This paper investigates representation robustness in LLM-based mathematical problem solving by systematically varying surface representations of the same underlying problems, including story problems, word-equations, symbolic equations, and isomorphic paraphrases. Using a curated dataset of mathematically equivalent problems, we evaluate five contemporary LLMs under a direct answer generation condition. We find substantial representational sensitivity: models frequently change correctness across equivalent formulations, with nontrivial flip rates across story, symbolic, and word-equation variants. We also observe systematic regressions under isomorphic reformulations, showing that even subtle paraphrase-level changes can degrade performance despite preserved mathematical structure. We then evaluate a code-augmented condition in which models externalize reasoning as executable Python code that is run locally for validation. This interface reveals strong latent reasoning capability in some models that perform poorly under direct prompting, but it does not uniformly improve robustness. Instead, failures shift across interaction layers, from opaque reasoning errors to protocol violations and execution failures. Even when executable reasoning succeeds, representation sensitivity often persists. Overall, our results show that reasoning scaffolds do not eliminate representational brittleness, but expose new tradeoffs among correctness, reliability, latency, and cost. We argue that representation should be treated as a first-class interface design variable in LLM evaluation and deployment, especially for AI-assisted problem-solving systems.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how sensitive large language models are to different but mathematically equivalent formulations of math problems, and shows that even small representational changes can significantly affect their accuracy and reliability.
AI.motivation: While LLMs are widely tested on math benchmarks, existing evaluations usually assume that different surface forms of the same math problem are interchangeable and primarily attribute mistakes to reasoning failures. The authors suspect that interface and representation effects are a major, underexplored factor, which matters for both fair evaluation and practical deployment of LLM-based solvers.
AI.method: The authors construct a curated dataset where each underlying math problem is expressed in multiple equivalent forms: story problems, word-equations, symbolic equations, and tightly controlled isomorphic paraphrases. They then test five contemporary LLMs under two settings: (1) direct answer generation from the prompt; (2) a code-augmented setup where models generate Python code that is executed locally to obtain answers. They measure how often a model’s correctness flips across equivalent representations and analyze error types, including protocol and execution failures in the code setting.
AI.result: Under direct answer prompting, LLMs show strong representational sensitivity: correctness frequently changes across story, symbolic, and word-equation variants, and even subtle isomorphic paraphrases can cause performance regressions. In the code-augmented setup, some models that were weak under direct prompting reveal strong latent reasoning ability, but robustness is not consistently improved: errors move from pure reasoning mistakes to interface issues like failing to follow the code protocol or producing code that does not execute. Representation-driven fluctuations in correctness remain common even when the generated code runs successfully.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning scaffolds such as code generation interfaces can uncover hidden reasoning capability but do not solve the core problem of representational brittleness in LLM-based math solving. Representation acts as a crucial interface variable that significantly affects correctness, robustness, and the types of failures observed. Therefore, evaluations and real-world AI-assisted problem-solving systems must explicitly consider and design for representation, balancing new tradeoffs between accuracy, reliability, latency, and computational cost.

title: Are Diversity Metrics Measuring Diversity? A Capability-Controlled Audit of Majority-Vote Gain in LLM Ensembles
summary: Majority voting over LLMs is widely assumed to benefit from diversity, and diversity measures are used to choose which models to combine. We ask whether five such measures track diversity or mainly re-express capability, auditing them as predictors of majority-vote gain over the best member across 31,900 subsets of 30 LLMs on MMLU-Pro (29 on TruthfulQA) under explicit capability controls. Three findings emerge. First, latent complementarity is ubiquitous: oracle gain is positive in 100% of subsets, yet simple voting beats the strongest member in only 9.98% of all canonical size-3 subsets (18.71% with held-out best selection); the pooled size-2-4 rate is 1.27%, partly reflecting deterministic even-size voting behavior. Second, a joint-correctness proxy (strict diversity) is nearly collinear with one minus mean accuracy (size-3 Spearman rho = +0.991 / +0.988); raw diversity-gain associations are strongly capability-entangled and, with one exception, unstable under control. Third, three linear contingency-table statistics are algebraically non-separable; after capability control, the empirically stable remainder is a modest residual pairwise co-failure association in which more shared error corresponds to lower gain. This direction is robust, but its magnitude is configuration-dependent. Joint rawspace linear regressions treating strict diversity, disagreement, and double-fault as independent predictors are rank-deficient by construction.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates whether diversity measures for ensembling LLMs via majority voting truly capture complementary behavior or mostly re-express capability, finding that latent complementarity is common but hard to exploit, most diversity metrics collapse to capability, and that residual useful structure is a small, configuration-dependent co-failure effect.
AI.motivation: Ensembling LLMs via majority voting is expected to improve performance when models are diverse, and various diversity metrics are used to pick model subsets. However, it is unclear whether these metrics actually reflect meaningful behavioral diversity beyond simple capability differences, and whether they reliably predict gains from majority voting.
AI.method: The authors systematically evaluate five diversity measures across 31,900 subsets of 30 LLMs on MMLU-Pro (and 29 on TruthfulQA). They compute oracle gains from majority voting versus the best single model and analyze how often simple majority voting improves over the best member for size-2 to size-4 ensembles. They apply explicit capability controls and use correlation (Spearman), contingency-table statistics, and regression analyses to disentangle diversity from capability effects and to study the algebraic relationships among diversity metrics.
AI.result: (1) Oracle gains from ensembling are positive for all subsets (latent complementarity exists), but naive majority voting rarely beats the best individual model—about 10% of size-3 subsets (19% with held-out best selection) and ~1.3% across sizes 2–4, partly due to deterministic ties in even-sized votes. (2) A strict diversity measure based on joint correctness is almost perfectly correlated with one minus the mean accuracy; raw associations between diversity and gain are heavily conflated with capability and mostly vanish or become unstable once capability is controlled for. (3) Linear contingency-table statistics such as strict diversity, disagreement, and double-fault are algebraically non-separable, leading to rank-deficient regressions; after controlling for capability, the only stable signal is a small residual pairwise co-failure effect where higher shared error predicts lower ensemble gain, but its strength varies with configuration.
AI.conclusion: Most standard diversity metrics for LLM majority-vote ensembling mainly reflect model capability, not true complementary behavior, and provide limited, unstable guidance for constructing beneficial ensembles. While there is real but modest residual structure—captured as a co-failure association—its impact on voting gains is configuration-dependent and small. Effective ensemble design for LLMs thus requires moving beyond naive diversity measures and acknowledging the strong algebraic and capability entanglements in current metrics.

title: Attention Degradation, Function Token Anchoring, and the Limits of Attention-Based Intervention in Large Language Models
summary: Mean cross-positional attention degradation is widely reported in transformer interpretability, yet whether it causally limits contextual retrieval remains untested. We present six coordinated experiments across GPT-2, LLaMA-3.2-1B/3B, OPT-1.3B, and distilgpt2. We first characterise short-term (5-100 token) attention degradation, finding a universal exponential-then-plateau pattern whose rate is inversely correlated with depth, with distinct layer-wise entropy signatures per architecture. Function token anchoring proves architecture-dependent: OPT-1.3B (absolute positional encoding) shows distance-dependent preposition specificity, GPT-2 shows uniform non-specific dependence, and LLaMA (RoPE) shows reversal at long distances. Strategic comma insertion at clause boundaries causally reduces prediction degradation in the 40-80 token range, with the benefit tied to syntactic boundary alignment rather than token density. We then test the mechanism causally: Relay-Aware Attention (RAA), which biases attention logits toward function token positions, verifiably increases attention mass by 16-24% yet yields null effects on GPT-2 and LLaMA-1B, preliminary harm on LLaMA-3B, and a mixed effect on OPT-1.3B that nets to approximately zero. Multi-fact retrieval probes further show that degradation rate does not predict retrieval accuracy across models. We conclude that mean attention degradation is largely descriptive rather than prescriptive: function tokens contribute through what their hidden states compute, not through the attention they receive -- with implications for interpretability methodology and attention-score-based inference optimisations such as KV-cache eviction.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates whether degrading cross-positional attention in transformers actually causes worse contextual retrieval, finding that while attention to distant tokens decays in a structured, architecture-dependent way, manipulating this attention has little causal impact on retrieval performance.
AI.motivation: In transformer interpretability, it is commonly observed that attention to distant tokens (especially function tokens) decays with distance, and this is often implicitly assumed to limit long-range context use. However, this assumption has not been rigorously tested causally. The authors aim to test whether mean cross-positional attention degradation truly constrains models’ ability to retrieve and use contextual information, and to clarify the functional role of function tokens in computation.
AI.method: The authors run six experiments on several transformer models (GPT-2, LLaMA-3.2-1B/3B, OPT-1.3B, distilgpt2). They (1) characterize short-range attention degradation patterns and layer-wise entropy signatures; (2) analyze how different architectures anchor attention on function tokens like prepositions; (3) introduce comma insertion at syntactic clause boundaries to see if this improves prediction quality; (4) propose Relay-Aware Attention (RAA), a logit-biasing technique that increases attention to function tokens, and measure its effect on performance; and (5) run multi-fact retrieval probes to relate degradation rates to retrieval accuracy across models.
AI.result: They find that attention degradation over 5–100 tokens follows a universal exponential-then-plateau pattern, with faster decay in shallower models and distinctive layer/architecture-specific entropy profiles. Function token anchoring behaves differently across models: OPT-1.3B shows distance-dependent preposition specificity, GPT-2 shows uniform but non-specific dependence, and LLaMA with RoPE exhibits a reversal at long distances. Comma insertion at clause boundaries measurably reduces prediction degradation around 40–80 tokens, but the benefit aligns with syntactic boundaries rather than simply adding tokens. RAA successfully increases attention mass to function tokens by 16–24%, yet produces essentially no improvement (and sometimes harm) in downstream performance. Multi-fact retrieval probes reveal no consistent relationship between attention degradation rates and retrieval accuracy across models.
AI.conclusion: Mean cross-positional attention degradation, while real and measurable, does not causally explain contextual retrieval performance. Function tokens matter for what computations their hidden states carry, not primarily for how much attention they receive. Consequently, interpretability methods and optimisation schemes that rely on raw attention scores—such as assuming low-attended tokens are unimportant or safe to evict from KV-caches—may be misleading. The study urges a shift from descriptive attention statistics to causal tests of representational and computational roles.

title: The Geometry of Personality: Activation Steering with Jungian Cognitive Functions
summary:
Activation steering enables control and interpretation of LLMs, yet existing work primarily models personality through static trait frameworks such as the Big Five. We investigate whether personality can instead be represented and controlled as a set of cognitive processes using the eight Jungian Cognitive Functions. To this end, we introduce a framework comprising a Jungian evaluation protocol and a dataset of over 2,100 role-playing character narrations.
  Activation steering vector extraction and evaluation experiments on Llama-3.1-8B demonstrate effective monotonic control over all eight cognitive functions through activation steering. Beyond controllability, our analysis reveals that: 1. personality information is concentrated in middle transformer layers; 2. steering vectors exhibit structured geometric relationships consistent with distinctions between rational and irrational functions; 3. effective multi-dimensional steering directions cannot be recovered as linear combinations of single-function directions. These findings provide new insights into the representation of personality in LLM activation space and establish a framework for studying interpretable, effective, and multi-dimensional personality control.

AI.tldr: They propose a new framework to control and interpret LLM personality using Jungian Cognitive Functions and show activation steering can robustly and multidimensionally control these functions in Llama-3.1-8B.
AI.motivation: Existing personality control in LLMs relies on static trait models like the Big Five, which may not align with dynamic, process-based views of personality and offer limited interpretability and control. The authors want to see if personality can instead be represented as cognitive processes (Jungian functions) within the activation space of LLMs, and whether activation steering can manipulate these processes in a structured, interpretable way.
AI.method: They build a Jungian evaluation protocol and a dataset of 2,100+ role‑playing character narrations labeled by cognitive functions. Using this, they extract activation steering vectors for each of the eight Jungian Cognitive Functions from Llama‑3.1‑8B. They then perform activation steering experiments to test whether steering in these directions yields monotonic changes in behavior along each function dimension, and they analyze where in the network personality information is localized and how the steering vectors relate geometrically.
AI.result: They obtain steering vectors that provide monotonic and effective control over all eight Jungian functions in Llama‑3.1‑8B. They find that personality-relevant information is concentrated primarily in middle transformer layers, that the learned steering vectors form geometric structures reflecting theory-motivated distinctions (e.g., rational vs. irrational functions), and that multi-dimensional personality control requires dedicated steering vectors rather than linear combinations of single-function ones.
AI.conclusion: The paper shows that LLM personality can be modeled and controlled as structured cognitive processes using Jungian functions in activation space, not just as static traits. Their framework gives interpretable and effective personality control, reveals non-trivial geometry of personality representations in LLMs, and offers tools for multi-dimensional, theory-aligned personality steering in future models and applications.

title: Autonomous disproofs of the sum-product conjecture over $\mathbb R$ with GPT-5.5 Pro
summary: OpenAI's recent disproof of the Erdős unit distance conjecture marked a milestone for AI in mathematics. It also inspired another breakthrough: a human disproof of the Erdős--Szemerédi sum-product conjecture over $\mathbb R$. In this paper, we present a simple agent built on GPT-5.5 Pro. Using a problem-agnostic, three-stage prompting pipeline -- proof-plan proposal, proof construction, and review -- the agent autonomously generated correct proofs that the sum-product conjecture is false over $\mathbb R$ in 7 of 8 independent trials; in the remaining trial, it identified an unresolved gap in its argument. The seven proofs are diverse: some are close to existing unit-based constructions, while others avoid units by using $L^p$-type regions of algebraic integers. The system used an average of 132.4k reasoning tokens per trial. We release the code, intermediate outputs, and generated proofs, providing a reproducible, data-contamination-free case study in autonomous proof generation.

AI.tldr: Simple GPT-5.5-based agent autonomously finds multiple, diverse counterexamples to the Erdős–Szemerédi sum-product conjecture over the reals via a three-stage prompting pipeline.
AI.motivation: After AI helped disprove the Erdős unit distance conjecture, there was interest in whether similar AI systems could autonomously tackle other major open problems, such as the Erdős–Szemerédi sum-product conjecture over the reals. The authors aim to test whether a relatively simple, problem-agnostic agent wrapped around a powerful LLM can reliably discover deep, correct mathematical proofs without hand-crafted heuristics, and to provide a clean, reproducible case study free from data contamination concerns.
AI.method: They build a lightweight agent on top of GPT-5.5 Pro that uses a fixed, three-stage, problem-agnostic prompting pipeline: (1) proof-plan proposal, where the model sketches high-level approaches; (2) proof construction, where it develops a full argument following a chosen plan; and (3) review, where it inspects and critiques its own proof. They run this pipeline independently eight times on the Erdős–Szemerédi sum-product conjecture over the reals, allowing large reasoning budgets (about 132.4k tokens per run), and then analyze the resulting proofs and their structures. They also package and release the code, prompts, intermediate traces, and proofs for external verification.
AI.result: In 7 out of 8 independent runs, the agent autonomously produces correct proofs that the Erdős–Szemerédi sum-product conjecture is false over the reals, while in the remaining run it detects and flags a gap in its own reasoning instead of outputting a flawed proof. The successful proofs are not mere variants of a single template: some resemble existing unit-based constructions, while others introduce new styles of counterexamples using L^p-type regions of algebraic integers. Across runs, the system consumes on average 132.4k reasoning tokens, demonstrating sustained long-context reasoning rather than short, ad-hoc completions.
AI.conclusion: A relatively simple, general-purpose agent layer on top of a strong LLM can autonomously discover deep, correct mathematical proofs and even detect unresolved gaps in its own arguments, as demonstrated by multiple independent disproofs of the Erdős–Szemerédi sum-product conjecture over the reals. The diversity of generated proofs suggests genuine mathematical creativity rather than template-matching, and the released artefacts provide a clean benchmark and case study for future research in autonomous theorem proving and AI-assisted mathematics.

title: REFACT: Adaptive Fact Restatement for Compact and Faithful Chain-of-Thought Reasoning
summary: Large language models increasingly rely on long-form reasoning for complex tasks, yet their reasoning traces may drift away from the supplied context when evidence is sparse, noisy, or in conflict with parametric knowledge. Existing grounding methods either attach citations after generation or encourage evidence retrieval inside the trace, but they often do not ensure that cited content is sufficient for the local inference and final answer. We propose REFACT, an adaptive fact-restatement citation framework that trains models to decide when a reasoning step needs contextual grounding and at what granularity source facts should be restated. This design avoids both unsupported inference and indiscriminate fact copying by turning citations into answer-supporting intermediate states. REFACT is optimized with a two-stage SFT-to-RL pipeline in which a citation-utility reward encourages cited facts to be well-formed, source-traceable, and answer-sufficient. Experiments on LongBench, LV-Eval, and ConFiQA show that REFACT improves long-context QA and counterfactual faithfulness while substantially reducing token consumption. Further analysis shows that REFACT preserves more answer-bearing evidence with fewer restated facts, yielding reasoning traces that are denser rather than longer. All code and data are available at https://github.com/NEUIR/REFACT.

AI.tldr: REFACT is a framework that trains LLMs to insert concise, source-traceable fact restatements at precisely those reasoning steps that need grounding, improving long-context QA accuracy and faithfulness while cutting token usage.
AI.motivation: Long-form reasoning in LLMs often drifts from the provided context, especially when evidence is sparse, noisy, or conflicts with parametric knowledge. Existing grounding/citation approaches either add citations post hoc or retrieve evidence inside the chain-of-thought, but they don’t guarantee that cited spans are actually sufficient to support each inference step and the final answer. This leads to hallucinations, verbose copying, and weak faithfulness. A better approach is needed to tightly couple reasoning to context with minimal, truly supportive citations.
AI.method: The paper introduces REFACT, an adaptive fact-restatement citation framework. During reasoning, the model is trained to (1) decide at each step whether contextual grounding is needed, and (2) choose the appropriate granularity of source facts to restate. These restated facts act as intermediate, answer-supporting states rather than decorative citations. REFACT is trained via a two-stage pipeline: supervised fine-tuning to seed the behavior, followed by reinforcement learning where a citation-utility reward encourages restatements that are well-formed, clearly traceable to specific context spans, and sufficient to support the answer. This encourages selective, faithful, and concise grounding rather than copying large chunks of text.
AI.result: On long-context and counterfactual evaluation benchmarks (LongBench, LV-Eval, ConFiQA), REFACT improves performance on QA and faithfulness metrics compared to baseline grounding methods. It also substantially reduces token usage, indicating more efficient reasoning traces. Additional analysis shows that the method preserves a higher proportion of answer-bearing evidence while using fewer restated facts, resulting in denser, not longer, reasoning traces.
AI.conclusion: REFACT provides a principled way to integrate citations into the reasoning process itself, adaptively grounding only those steps that require external evidence and at an appropriate granularity. This approach reduces unsupported inferences, avoids indiscriminate copying, and yields more compact yet evidence-dense chains of thought. Empirical results across multiple benchmarks demonstrate improved accuracy, faithfulness to context (including counterfactuals), and reduced token consumption, suggesting REFACT is a practical path toward more trustworthy long-context LLM reasoning.

title: ConfidenceBench: Evaluating Confidence Calibration in Large Language Models
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in settings where fluent but incorrect answers can be costly. In these settings, accuracy alone is insufficient: models must also know when they are likely to be wrong. We present ConfidenceBench, a calibration benchmark that evaluates verbalized confidence estimates in 15 frontier LLMs using the Brier score, a proper scoring rule that incentivises truthful probability reporting. Confidence is elicited via prompting, requiring no access to model logits and making the framework applicable to both closed-source and open-source systems. The benchmark comprises 200 private multiple-choice questions across four categories: spatial reasoning, high-precision mathematics, word lookup, and unknowable questions. Across three independent runs, Claude Opus 4.6 and Gemini 3.1 Pro Preview achieve the lowest Brier scores, both reported as 0.103. Both substantially outperform the calibrated-random baseline of 0.1875, while Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite scores 0.367, indicating severe miscalibration. Accuracy and calibration diverge substantially across model families: the most accurate model is not the best-calibrated, and several models perform worse than the calibrated-random Brier baseline despite reasonable accuracy. These results show that verbalized confidence calibration is a distinct and practically important axis of LLM reliability, complementary to standard accuracy-based evaluation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ConfidenceBench, a benchmark that measures how well large language models (LLMs) can express calibrated confidence in their answers, showing that accuracy and calibration are often misaligned.
AI.motivation: Many real-world deployments of LLMs occur in high-stakes settings where giving a wrong answer with unjustified certainty can be harmful. Existing evaluations mostly focus on accuracy, ignoring whether models can recognize and communicate their own uncertainty. There is a need for a standardized way to assess how well LLMs’ stated confidence matches their actual correctness, especially in scenarios where logits are unavailable (e.g., closed-source APIs).
AI.method: The authors build ConfidenceBench, a set of 200 private multiple-choice questions covering four domains: spatial reasoning, high-precision mathematics, word lookup, and questions that are fundamentally unknowable. They elicit models’ verbal confidence via prompting (no logit access) and compute Brier scores—a proper scoring rule—over three independent runs to evaluate calibration quality. They apply this framework to 15 frontier LLMs and compare their scores to a calibrated-random baseline.
AI.result: Claude Opus 4.6 and Gemini 3.1 Pro Preview achieve the best calibration with Brier scores of 0.103, significantly better than the calibrated-random baseline of 0.1875. In contrast, Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite performs poorly with a Brier score of 0.367, indicating strong miscalibration. The study finds notable divergence between accuracy and calibration: some highly accurate models are not the best-calibrated, and several models have Brier scores worse than the random baseline despite having reasonable accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Verbalized confidence calibration is a separate and practically important dimension of LLM reliability, distinct from and complementary to accuracy. ConfidenceBench provides a practical benchmark for assessing this property across both open- and closed-source models, and the results highlight that good accuracy does not guarantee good calibration, underscoring the need to evaluate and improve how models express uncertainty.

title: CSPF: A Constrained Shared-Private Fusion Method for Non-Verifiable Preference Evaluation
summary: At present, reliable evaluation of non-verifiable tasks remains challenging. Existing approaches often fail to adequately capture the diverse evaluative criteria underlying human preferences in such tasks. To this end, we propose Constrained Shared-Private Fusion (CSPF), a fusion method that treats heterogeneous frozen reward models as complementary evaluators and learns to integrate their hidden-state representations under pairwise human-preference supervision. CSPF decomposes each expert signal into shared and expert-private representations, encouraging cross-expert alignment while preserving complementary viewpoints. Across experiments on LM-Arena target-domain adaptation and PPE out-of-distribution preference evaluation, CSPF achieves the best performance on the primary metrics among the evaluated single-expert reward-model, scalar-score multi-expert, and rubric-judge baselines. Overall, CSPF suggests that fusing hidden-state representations provides a more expressive basis for preference assessment, offering a practical route toward integrated evaluative signals for non-verifiable preference tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CSPF, a method for fusing multiple frozen reward models at the representation level to better evaluate non-verifiable tasks according to human preferences.
AI.motivation: Evaluating non-verifiable tasks (like open-ended language generation) is difficult because human preferences rely on multiple, diverse criteria that existing single reward models or simple aggregations of multiple models struggle to capture.
AI.method: The authors propose Constrained Shared-Private Fusion (CSPF), which takes hidden states from several heterogeneous frozen reward models (experts), decomposes each into shared and expert-private components, and learns, under pairwise human preference supervision, to align the shared part across experts while preserving expert-specific information, effectively learning a better fused preference representation.
AI.result: On LM-Arena target-domain adaptation and PPE out-of-distribution preference evaluation benchmarks, CSPF outperforms baselines including single-expert reward models, scalar-score fusion of multiple experts, and rubric-based judges on the main evaluation metrics.
AI.conclusion: Learning to fuse hidden-state representations from multiple reward models using a shared-private decomposition yields a more expressive and effective basis for modeling human preferences in non-verifiable tasks, providing a practical way to build integrated evaluators.

title: Evaluating and Guarding Citation Faithfulness in Agentic Scientific Synthesis
comment: 20 pages, 5 figures. Includes supplementary material (Appendices S1-S6). Open single-GPU reproducibility kit included
summary:
Agentic LLM systems such as OpenScholar and PaperQA2 read the scientific literature and return cited answers, and both they and their benchmarks already check whether those citations hold, with a fixed attribution model or human graders. Neither audits the reliability of that check itself. We show it is not reliable, and that this matters. On identical agent outputs the measured unsupported-citation rate ranges from about 3% to about 18% depending only on the verifier's strictness, and although verifiers agree on which citations are supported, they disagree on which to flag (negative-specific agreement 0.27 to 0.30), so no single flag set is trustworthy and cross-paper comparison is invalid without a named verifier and protocol.
  We present a gold-anchored evaluation protocol and a deployable guard that make this behavior measurable and bounded. The protocol validates the verifier, measures re-attribution, and calibrates a guarantee against human gold rather than another model's verdict; the verifier is a swappable instrument chosen on cost (recall 0.94 on the supported class, held out), and re-attribution is a commodity step where a deterministic BM25 matches the best open generator. The guard adds a split-conformal layer placing a distribution-free, finite-sample bound on truly unsupported citations that slip past a chosen flagging rule, a guarantee on catch rate rather than conclusion correctness. The bound holds on held-out gold, and we identify and quantify the condition governing its transfer to deployment, calibration-negative difficulty, with a concrete recalibration recipe, left untested by prior conformal-factuality work.
  Validated across four open 27-35B models and three agentic pipelines on public benchmarks (SciFact, QASA, PubMedQA), with confidence intervals on every headline number, the protocol and guard ship as an open single-GPU kit.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that current methods for checking whether LLM-generated scientific answers are properly supported by citations are themselves unreliable, and proposes a validated evaluation protocol plus a conformal-guard mechanism that produce measurable, bounded guarantees on missed unsupported citations.
AI.motivation: Agentic LLM systems that answer scientific questions with cited references currently use verifiers (models or humans) to check whether citations actually support the claims. However, no one checks how reliable these verifiers are. The authors find large variability in reported unsupported-citation rates depending solely on verifier strictness and poor agreement on which citations to flag, undermining trust, comparability across systems, and the validity of existing benchmarks. There is a need for a principled way to validate verifiers, quantify re-attribution, and provide guarantees on factuality checks that are anchored in human gold rather than another model’s opinion.
AI.method: The authors design a gold-anchored evaluation protocol and a deployable guard. The protocol: (1) validates candidate verifiers against human-annotated gold data; (2) measures how often answers need to be re-attributed (changing which citations are used); and (3) calibrates guarantees using human gold labels rather than another model’s outputs. They treat the verifier as a swappable instrument and pick one based on cost–performance trade-offs (e.g., achieving recall 0.94 on supported citations on held-out data). Re-attribution is implemented as a deterministic BM25 retrieval step, shown to match the best open generative re-attribution methods. For deployment, they add a split-conformal prediction layer over the verifier’s flags, which yields distribution-free, finite-sample bounds on the number of truly unsupported citations that evade flagging. They introduce the concept of calibration-negative difficulty as the key condition under which these bounds transfer from calibration data to deployment and describe a recipe for recalibration. The protocol and guard are instantiated and tested across several open 27–35B LLMs and agentic QA pipelines using public scientific factuality benchmarks.
AI.result: Empirically, the authors show that unsupported-citation rates reported by existing systems vary widely (about 3% to 18%) on identical answers depending only on verifier strictness, and that verifiers have low negative-specific agreement (0.27–0.30) on which citations to flag, making individual flag sets untrustworthy and cross-paper comparisons invalid without specifying verifier and protocol. Their gold-anchored protocol successfully validates a chosen verifier with high recall on supported citations (0.94 on held-out data) and demonstrates that a simple deterministic BM25 re-attribution step performs on par with state-of-the-art open generative approaches. The conformal guard layer produces finite-sample, distribution-free upper bounds on the rate of truly unsupported citations that pass the chosen flagging threshold; these bounds hold on held-out gold data. They empirically identify calibration-negative difficulty as the governing condition for transferring these guarantees to real deployments and show how to quantify it. The full protocol and guard are implemented as an open-source, single-GPU toolkit and evaluated across four 27–35B open LLMs and three agent pipelines on SciFact, QASA, and PubMedQA, with confidence intervals reported for all main performance numbers.
AI.conclusion: Current citation-verification practices for agentic LLMs in scientific domains are not reliable enough to trust their unsupported-citation statistics or to compare systems fairly. By anchoring evaluation to human gold, validating and calibrating the verifier as an instrument, commoditizing re-attribution via BM25, and adding a split-conformal guard, the authors provide a principled way to measure and bound the frequency of unsupported citations that escape detection. Their protocol turns citation verification from an ad hoc component into a validated, swappable module with explicit guarantees on miss rates, improving the reliability, interpretability, and comparability of cited LLM answers in scientific QA, and is made practically accessible via an open, single-GPU toolkit.

title: LegalCiteTrust: Benchmarking Citation Trustworthiness in Chinese Long-Form Legal Research Reports
comment: 8 pages, 21 pages with appendix, 26 tables, 4 figures
summary: Long-form legal research reports increasingly rely on LLMs and agentic research systems, but their reliability depends not only on answering the task, but also on whether cited legal authorities are trustworthy. A citation can be risky even when it points to a real source: the report may omit limiting conditions, misdescribe the authority, or use it to support a stronger claim than the source allows. We introduce LegalCiteTrust, a benchmark for evaluating citation trustworthiness in Chinese long-form legal research reports. It contains 72 densely annotated report-level tasks and evaluates reports along three dimensions: Coverage, Support, and Citation Trustworthiness. Citation Trustworthiness is operationalized through citation-level Existence, Fidelity, and Applicability (E/F/A). Experiments on general-purpose LLMs, deep-research systems, and legal-specific systems show that task completion, evidence richness, citation density, and citation reliability expose different system behaviors. Retrieval tools can improve evidence support without reliably improving the Trust score, while E/F/A-based revision improves Trust and Final score more clearly than existence-only filtering. These results suggest that trustworthy legal research generation requires citation-aware evidence governance after retrieval: systems must not only retrieve legal authorities, but also select, describe, and apply them reliably.

AI.tldr: LegalCiteTrust is a benchmark for evaluating how trustworthy citations are in Chinese long-form LLM-generated legal research reports, focusing on whether authorities are correctly and appropriately used, not just whether they exist.
AI.motivation: As LLMs and agentic systems are increasingly used to draft long-form legal research reports, there is a risk that citations, even when pointing to real sources, are misused—by omitting key conditions, mischaracterizing holdings, or overstating what authorities support. Existing evaluations pay more attention to task completion and basic hallucination detection than to nuanced citation reliability in real-world legal reporting, especially in Chinese. The authors aim to fill this gap by creating a benchmark that captures the quality and trustworthiness of how legal sources are cited and applied.
AI.method: The authors build a benchmark, LegalCiteTrust, consisting of 72 report-level Chinese legal research tasks with dense human annotations. They define three report-level dimensions—Coverage, Support, and Citation Trustworthiness—and further decompose Trustworthiness into citation-level Existence, Fidelity, and Applicability (E/F/A). They then run experiments on general-purpose LLMs, deep-research agentic systems, and legal-domain systems. They compare how these models and pipelines perform on the benchmark, including variants that use retrieval tools, existence-only citation filtering, and E/F/A-based revision steps, in order to analyze the impact of different design choices on citation quality and overall task performance.
AI.result: The experiments reveal that models with strong task completion or rich evidence do not necessarily have trustworthy citations. Evidence richness and citation density expose behaviors distinct from citation reliability. Retrieval tools tend to increase evidence support but do not reliably improve the overall Trust score. In contrast, pipelines that incorporate E/F/A-based revision improve both the Citation Trustworthiness metric and the final task score more clearly than those that only filter for citation existence (i.e., hallucination removal).
AI.conclusion: LegalCiteTrust demonstrates that reliable legal research generation requires going beyond retrieval and hallucination checks: systems must explicitly manage how authorities are selected, represented, and applied. Citation-aware evidence governance after retrieval—via dimensions like Existence, Fidelity, and Applicability—is critical to ensuring trustworthy long-form legal reports, especially in domains like law where misusing a real citation can be as harmful as fabricating one.

title: PromptPack: Scaling LLM Annotation Agents for Online Recommendation
summary: Online recommendation platforms increasingly use Large Language Models (LLMs) to extract structured features from ad creatives. While deploying a single-call LLM annotation agent yields significant Click-Through Rate (CTR) improvements in our live production environment, per-creative prompting is prohibitively expensive to scale. The redundant system instructions sent in every request account for 94% of billed input tokens. To break this cost bottleneck, we introduce PromptPack, a scalable, high-throughput LLM annotation agent. PromptPack achieves this scale via in-context batching, combining a shared system prompt, a strict XML structural envelope, and an output correction layer to ensure deterministic, pipeline-ready feature extraction across multiple creatives simultaneously. We evaluate PromptPack via an offline retrieval benchmark using a downstream logistic-regression ranker. To deeply profile the agent's behavior, we measure AUC and introduce Volume-Weighted Absolute Lift (VWAL), a novel metric capturing the signal quality of the generated features. Compared to our live, unbatched production baseline, PromptPack at batch size 20 cuts our LLM costs by 89% and accelerates throughput by 2.5x while fully preserving AUC.

AI.tldr: The paper presents PromptPack, a batched LLM-based annotation system that dramatically reduces token costs and increases throughput for ad creative feature extraction while maintaining CTR prediction performance.
AI.motivation: Single-call LLM agents used to annotate individual ad creatives improve CTR but are too expensive because redundant system prompts dominate token usage. The authors need a scalable way to extract structured features from many creatives simultaneously without sacrificing the predictive power of these features.
AI.method: They design PromptPack, which uses in-context batching: multiple creatives share one system prompt and are wrapped in a strict XML schema to enforce consistent, machine-readable outputs. An additional output correction layer enforces determinism and structural validity. They evaluate the extracted features via an offline retrieval benchmark with a logistic regression ranker and introduce a new metric, Volume-Weighted Absolute Lift (VWAL), to assess feature signal quality beyond AUC.
AI.result: At batch size 20, PromptPack reduces LLM annotation costs by 89% and improves throughput by 2.5x compared to the unbatched production baseline, while maintaining identical AUC in the downstream CTR prediction task, indicating no loss in model quality.
AI.conclusion: PromptPack demonstrates that careful prompt engineering, structured output constraints, and batching can make LLM-based feature extraction economically viable at scale for recommendation systems, preserving ranking quality while drastically lowering costs and increasing processing speed.

title: Tencent WorkBuddy Bench: A Multi-Domain Coding-Agent Benchmark with Contamination-Resistant Task Construction
comment: 30 pages, 9 figures. Project page: https://workbuddybench.com/ ; code: https://github.com/Tencent/workbuddy-bench ; dataset: https://huggingface.co/datasets/tencent/workbuddy-bench
summary: We introduce Tencent WorkBuddy Bench, a multi-domain evaluation suite for coding agents; this report documents its construction methodology, scoring protocol, and a cross-model leaderboard. At its core is a unified evaluation framework for constructing and running distribution-informed coding-agent tasks across four work domains - Code, Web, Office, and Security. Rather than adapting public issue text, every task is reverse-engineered from a real commit, pull request, or business scenario and rewritten as a short, colloquial, role-played request, so that a task's prompt is not recoverable by web-searching the underlying issue, pull request, or commit thread. Because the dataset is released openly - task directories, environment images, evaluation harness, tests, and reference solutions - contamination resistance rests on this construction together with dataset versioning rather than on secrecy. The four subsets - repository-level engineering, front-end development, office and business workflows, and red-/blue-team security - probe complementary facets of real work, each with its own verification style. All are packaged in a uniform task-directory format and run, under a uniform and reproducible protocol, on two agent harnesses (CodeBuddy Code and Claude Code); the full open release makes the benchmark reproducible end to end and directly auditable, since any third party can re-run each task and inspect its content. Because each subset uses a different scoring instrument, scores are not comparable across subsets and the suite reports no suite-wide average. We report a cross-model leaderboard across several model families.

AI.tldr: Tencent WorkBuddy Bench is a new, openly released, multi-domain benchmark to evaluate coding agents across realistic work scenarios in four domains.
AI.motivation: Existing coding benchmarks often rely on public issue texts or synthetic tasks, which are susceptible to contamination, are not distribution-informed, and may not cover the breadth of real-world work scenarios for coding agents. There is a need for a unified, multi-domain, reproducible benchmark that better reflects practical workloads and allows fair comparison of agent systems.
AI.method: The authors build a benchmark composed of tasks reverse-engineered from real commits, pull requests, and business scenarios, then rewritten as short, colloquial, role-play prompts such that original sources are not easily recoverable via web search. They organize tasks into four domains—Code, Web, Office, and Security—each with its own verification style and scoring instrument. All tasks are packaged in a uniform directory format with environment images, tests, reference solutions, and an evaluation harness. They run these tasks using a standardized protocol on two agent harnesses (CodeBuddy Code and Claude Code) and release everything openly with versioning to manage contamination risks. They then evaluate multiple model families to build a cross-model leaderboard.
AI.result: The result is Tencent WorkBuddy Bench, an openly available, end-to-end reproducible benchmark suite for coding agents covering repository-level engineering, front-end development, office/business workflows, and red-/blue-team security tasks. Each subset has its own scoring instrument, making scores non-comparable across subsets, and the authors present a cross-model leaderboard over several model families based on this framework.
AI.conclusion: Tencent WorkBuddy Bench offers a contamination-resilient, distribution-informed, multi-domain benchmark for coding agents, enabling transparent and reproducible evaluation across realistic software and business workflows. Its open design and task construction methodology support direct auditing and future extensions, though it intentionally avoids aggregating scores across heterogeneous subsets.

title: DynamicMCPBench: A Trace-Grounded, Effect-Scored Benchmark for LLM Agents over Live MCP Servers
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents are increasingly deployed over Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers, yet the benchmarks used to evaluate them score the final answer or a fixed "ground-truth" list of tools, both of which are fragile once the underlying data is live and stateful. We present DynamicMCPBench, a reusable framework rather than a fixed dataset. A practitioner can run it on their own MCP servers to test models on their own tasks, or let it collect servers automatically to measure a model's general ability to solve agentic tasks. Given the servers and any set of models, it generates realistic goals, pursues each one live to record a successful trajectory, distills that trajectory into path-agnostic effect checkpoints, and scores an agent on whether it reproduces those effects, never on the final answer. To show what the framework reveals, we run it at scale: 24 models over 121 servers and 750 tasks spread evenly over 15 task categories (50 each), where each category targets a distinct tool-use challenge of the generated questions. Each task is scored by pass^3: it counts as solved only if all three independent attempts succeed. Even the strongest agents solve only about half of the tasks, 31% of tasks are solved by no model at all, and accuracy collapses as the required tool chain grows longer (from 39% on the shortest chains to 13% on the longest). A human validation study confirms the automatic scoring is reliable (chance-corrected agreement of 0.76). DynamicMCPBench thus turns benchmark construction into something practitioners can rerun on their own servers and models, while exposing a consistent inability of current agents to handle long, multi-step agentic tasks.

AI.tldr: DynamicMCPBench is a reusable, live benchmark framework for evaluating LLM agents on MCP servers by checking whether they reproduce key intermediate effects rather than final answers or fixed tool lists.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM agent benchmarks are fragile when applied to live, stateful MCP servers because they rely on scoring final answers or fixed ground-truth tool sequences that can become invalid as underlying data and tools change. There is a need for a robust, reusable evaluation framework that works directly on practitioners’ own servers and captures true agentic competence in realistic environments.
AI.method: The authors build DynamicMCPBench, which connects to a collection of MCP servers (either provided by a practitioner or collected automatically) and generates realistic goals across 15 tool-use challenge categories. For each task, the system uses a model to pursue the goal live, records a successful trajectory, and then distills that trajectory into path-agnostic effect checkpoints that encode the essential intermediate state changes. New agents are evaluated by whether they reproduce these effects rather than specific steps or final answers. The benchmark runs multiple independent attempts per task and uses the stringent pass^3 criterion: a task is solved only if all three attempts succeed. Human validation is used to check the reliability of automatic scoring.
AI.result: Applied at scale to 24 LLM agents over 121 MCP servers and 750 tasks (50 per each of 15 task categories), DynamicMCPBench reveals that even the strongest models solve only about half of the tasks under the strict pass^3 criterion. Fully 31% of tasks are not solved by any model. Performance drops sharply as required tool-chain length increases, with accuracy declining from 39% on the shortest chains to 13% on the longest. A human study shows strong agreement between human judgments and automatic scoring, with a chance-corrected agreement of 0.76.
AI.conclusion: DynamicMCPBench offers a robust, rerunnable framework for constructing and running benchmarks on live MCP servers and arbitrary models, shifting evaluation from brittle final-answer scoring to effect-based assessment of agentic behavior. The results expose significant limitations of current LLM agents, especially in handling long, multi-step tool-use chains, highlighting a key area for future research and system improvement.

title: From a Word-Level Dictionary to Sentence-Level Semantics: Multilingual Grievance Labelling with Contextual Models
summary: Grievance is one of the warning signs analysts look for when assessing threats of violence. It is increasingly measured at scale from online text, most often with word-level lexicons like the Grievance Dictionary that score by matching weighted terms. Such matching is a fast and transparent proxy, but it cannot resolve whether a term is asserted, quoted, negated, or condemned. These lexicons are also often evaluated on pools enriched with the very examples they retrieve, so a high score partly reflects agreement with the lexicon's own selection rule. Examining a five-language, 2{,}000-item evaluation pool, we find its halves separated almost perfectly by the lexicon itself: every item labeled ``random'' is in fact lexicon-negative, so the lexicon's apparent macro-AUROC of 0.686 collapses to a 0.500 floor fixed by construction. We keep the dictionary's 22-construct ontology but replace term matching with context-reading models, evaluated on a non-circular benchmark that separates unconditional-random, lexicon-positive, and lexicon-negative strata across five languages. Reading the full post rather than the target sentence alone helps most where the lexicon is silent, raising average precision on lexicon-negative text from 0.14 to 0.20, with the largest gains on quoted, implicit, and cross-sentence grievance. Together, these results show that grievance is measured more faithfully by reading the surrounding context, and more honestly when tested on text the lexicon did not select. We release our code and benchmark at https://github.com/behavioral-ds/multilingual_grievance.

AI.tldr: The paper critiques lexicon-based methods for detecting grievance in online text and proposes context-aware models evaluated on a non-circular benchmark across five languages.
AI.motivation: Analysts use grievance as a warning sign for violent threats, increasingly measured at scale from online text using lexicon-based tools like the Grievance Dictionary. However, these tools rely on simple word matching that ignores context (e.g., whether a term is negated, quoted, or condemned) and are often evaluated on biased datasets enriched by the same lexicon, inflating performance metrics. The authors aim to expose and correct these methodological flaws to provide more accurate and honest measurement of grievance.
AI.method: The authors first analyze an existing five-language, 2,000-item evaluation pool for the Grievance Dictionary and show it is structurally biased: items labeled as "random" are all lexicon-negative, artificially inflating AUROC scores. They then retain the dictionary’s 22-construct grievance ontology but replace term matching with context-reading models that can interpret full posts. They construct a new non-circular benchmark that clearly separates unconditional-random, lexicon-positive, and lexicon-negative strata across five languages. They compare models that read only the target sentence versus the entire post, focusing on performance across these strata.
AI.result: The analysis reveals that the original evaluation is heavily biased, with lexicon scores largely determining the dataset split, causing the reported macro-AUROC of 0.686 to effectively collapse to a baseline of 0.500. The proposed context-reading models, evaluated on the new benchmark, outperform lexicon-based matching, especially on text where the lexicon provides no signal. Reading the full post rather than only the target sentence improves average precision on lexicon-negative text from 0.14 to 0.20, with notable gains on quoted, implicit, and cross-sentence expressions of grievance. These improvements demonstrate the value of using broader context rather than isolated keyword matches.
AI.conclusion: Lexicon-based approaches like the Grievance Dictionary are limited and can produce misleading performance estimates when evaluated on datasets they helped select. By retaining the conceptual ontology but replacing keyword matching with context-aware models and using a non-circular benchmark, grievance can be measured more faithfully, particularly in subtle or indirect expressions. The findings advocate for context-rich modeling and more rigorous, unbiased evaluation practices. The authors provide open-source code and a multilingual benchmark to support further research.

title: AppWorld-UL: Benchmarking Diverse Agent-User Interactions for Tool-Use
summary: Tool-use agents that address day-to-day digital tasks such as ordering groceries must not only operate applications, but also interact with the user, e.g., to ask clarification questions, prompt for confirmation, and inform the user when the instruction is infeasible. However, current benchmarks for evaluating agent-user interactions do not capture the diversity of such interactions. Further, they operate in small environments with few, often non-state-changing, APIs. To address this gap, we introduce AppWorld-UL, a ``user-in-the-loop'' benchmark of 516 challenging tasks requiring diverse agent-user interactions. Building upon the AppWorld framework with 9 popular simulated apps like Amazon and Spotify, we systematically modify original tasks to introduce ambiguities and constraints that necessitate various types of agent-user interaction. User behavior is simulated by an LLM prompted to respond with carefully designed knowledge boundaries, offering more reliable simulation than the unconstrained or overly rigid alternatives used in prior work. Our evaluation reveals that a state-of-the-art LLM, Claude Opus 4.7, achieves only 48.6% success on AppWorld-UL, and only 35.7% on the harder, compositional subset. On the stricter, scenario-level metric, compositional task performance drops to only 21.3%. Our analysis reveals that correct user-interaction is crucial for success. This demonstrates the benchmark's difficulty and its potential to advance research on user-in-the-loop tool-use agents.

AI.tldr: Introduces AppWorld-UL, a challenging user-in-the-loop benchmark for evaluating tool-use agents in realistic multi-app environments, highlighting that even state-of-the-art LLMs perform poorly and need better user-interaction capabilities.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for tool-use agents poorly capture realistic agent-user interactions, are limited to small environments, and often use simplistic or unrealistic user simulators, which together fail to stress-test agents that must perform real-world digital tasks.
AI.method: Extend the AppWorld framework to create AppWorld-UL by systematically modifying tasks across 9 simulated popular apps (e.g., Amazon, Spotify) to inject ambiguities and constraints that require agent-user dialogs; simulate users with an LLM constrained via prompts and knowledge boundaries; evaluate a SOTA LLM, Claude Opus 4.7, on 516 such tasks including a compositional subset, under both task- and scenario-level success metrics; analyze failure cases focusing on agent-user interactions.
AI.result: Claude Opus 4.7 attains 48.6% task success overall, only 35.7% on harder compositional tasks, and just 21.3% on a strict scenario-level metric for compositional tasks, indicating substantial difficulty and frequent interaction-related failures.
AI.conclusion: AppWorld-UL is a difficult, realistic benchmark that exposes current weaknesses in user-in-the-loop tool-use agents, especially in handling clarifications, confirmations, and infeasibility; it offers a valuable testbed to drive progress on more capable interactive tool-using agents.

title: Where Animacy Lives in Large Language Models: Tracing the Circuits of the Animacy Concept
summary: Distinguishing animate from inanimate concepts in written language requires more than shallow text processing, as it involves recognizing complex selectional constraints and contextual cues, such as verb-argument interactions. Yet, current large language models (LLMs) appear to be capable of doing it. We investigate whether this animacy-sensitive behavior of LLMs can be traced to a localized set of causally relevant components and connections. To do so, we construct a controlled dataset of minimal pairs and perform circuit discovery on four open-weight models. Through in-depth experiments and ablations, we show that a causal mechanism responsible for handling animacy in these models does exist, thus discovering an animacy circuit. At the same time, this circuit appears to be less localized compared to other known ones and generalizes only partially across models and animacy tasks, confirming the distributed, context-dependent, and somewhat graded nature of the animacy concept.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies and analyzes a causal ‘animacy circuit’ inside large language models using controlled minimal-pair data and circuit-discovery methods, showing that animacy is handled by a distributed, only partially localized mechanism.
AI.motivation: While LLMs can distinguish animate from inanimate entities in text, it is unclear how this capability is implemented internally and whether it corresponds to a localized, causally relevant mechanism, as has been found for some other linguistic or factual concepts. Understanding this can shed light on the representation of higher-level semantic properties like animacy and on the interpretability of LLMs.
AI.method: The authors build a controlled dataset of minimal sentence pairs that differ only in animacy-related properties and then perform circuit-discovery analyses on four open-weight language models. They use in-depth experiments and targeted ablations of components and connections within the models to identify structures whose manipulation causally affects animacy-sensitive behavior.
AI.result: They find evidence of a causal mechanism—an ‘animacy circuit’—that underlies LLM behavior on animacy-related judgments. However, this circuit is less spatially localized than previously discovered circuits for other phenomena and shows only partial generalization across different models and across different animacy tasks.
AI.conclusion: LLMs do implement a specific causal mechanism for processing animacy, but this mechanism is relatively distributed, context-dependent, and graded rather than cleanly localized or fully shared across models and tasks. This supports a view of animacy as a complex, distributed semantic concept in neural language models and informs future work on mechanistic interpretability of such higher-level properties.

title: StrideDiffusion: Accelerating Diffusion Models for Time-series Generation
summary: Diffusion models have become competitive generators for time series, but their practical use is limited by the large number of sequential denoising steps required at inference time. Existing fast samplers typically use fixed or generic timestep schedules, overlooking a distinctive property of time-series diffusion: different spectral bands evolve at different rates during the reverse process. We introduce StrideDiffusion, a training-free spectral-aware sampler that adaptively selects the denoising stride from band-level activity. At each step, StrideDiffusion monitors relative band energy, log-power drift, and phase velocity to identify whether high- frequency dynamics remain active or whether the trajectory is dominated by stable low-frequency structure. It then takes fine steps when rapidly varying bands are active and larger jumps once only coarse components remain. A bandwise stability analysis shows that inactive frequency bands change only linearly with the jump size under deterministic affine reverse updates, providing a local justification for spectral activity as a step-size indicator. Across six unconditional time-series generation benchmarks, StrideDiffusion uses only 14-66 function evaluations instead of 500/1000 denoising steps, achieving up to 18.9x wall-clock speedup while preserving or improving generation quality. On conditional imputation and forecasting, it further delivers 5-14x average acceleration with comparable predictive accuracy. These results show that spectral evolution provides a practical and principled signal for fast time-series diffusion sampling. Our code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/stridediff-ts.

AI.tldr: StrideDiffusion is a training-free, spectral-aware sampler for diffusion models on time series that adaptively changes denoising step sizes based on frequency-band activity, enabling 5–19x faster sampling with similar or better quality.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models generate high-quality time series but are slow at inference because they require hundreds to thousands of sequential denoising steps. Existing acceleration methods typically use fixed or heuristic timestep schedules that ignore a key property of time-series diffusion: different frequency components (spectral bands) decay and stabilize at different rates during the reverse process. There is a need for a principled, data-driven way to reduce the number of steps by exploiting the spectral evolution of the signal without retraining the model.
AI.method: The authors propose StrideDiffusion, a training-free sampler that adaptively selects the denoising stride (step size in the reverse diffusion process) based on band-level spectral activity of the current time series trajectory. At each sampling step, they compute frequency-band features such as relative band energy, log-power drift, and phase velocity to determine whether high-frequency components are still active or whether the signal is dominated by stable low-frequency structure. When fast-varying bands are active, the sampler uses fine-grained small steps; when only coarse, low-frequency components remain, it performs larger jumps (strides) over multiple original diffusion steps. They support this design via a bandwise stability analysis showing that in inactive bands, changes scale only linearly with the jump size under deterministic affine reverse updates, justifying the use of spectral activity as a local step-size indicator.
AI.result: On six unconditional time-series generation benchmarks, StrideDiffusion reduces the number of model function evaluations to 14–66 calls compared to standard 500 or 1000 denoising steps, yielding up to 18.9x speedups in wall-clock time while maintaining or even improving generation quality metrics. On conditional tasks such as imputation and forecasting, the method achieves 5–14x average acceleration with comparable predictive accuracy to the original slow sampler. These empirical results demonstrate that the spectral-aware adaptive stepping can substantially accelerate diffusion-based time-series modeling without sacrificing performance.
AI.conclusion: Spectral evolution during the reverse diffusion process offers a robust signal for adapting the denoising step size in time-series diffusion models. By monitoring band-level energy, power drift, and phase dynamics, StrideDiffusion can safely take large strides when only low-frequency structure remains, drastically reducing inference cost. The approach is training-free, general across multiple tasks and datasets, and matches or improves quality relative to standard long-run samplers, providing a practical, principled solution for fast time-series diffusion sampling.

title: CultureTalk-ID: A Multi-Task Dialogue Benchmark for Cultural Commonsense in Indonesian Local Languages
summary: Culture is lived through conversation, yet existing Indonesian cultural commonsense benchmarks evaluate LLMs on short and isolated prompts, stripping away the dialogic context in which cultural nuances actually surface. We introduce CultureTalk-ID, the first dialogue-based benchmark for cultural commonsense in Indonesian and its local languages, comprising 4,496 culturally grounded dialogues across 11 languages and 13 culturally salient topics, curated through a multi-stage human pipeline with native speakers to ensure authenticity. CultureTalk-ID introduces three complementary tasks, namely dialogue-based multiple-choice cultural commonsense reasoning, culturally faithful machine translation, and language steering, which jointly probe whether LLMs can understand, transfer, and generate culturally grounded language.

AI.tldr: Introduces CultureTalk-ID, a dialogue-based benchmark to test LLMs’ Indonesian cultural commonsense across 11 languages and 13 topics.
AI.motivation: Existing Indonesian cultural benchmarks use short, isolated prompts that ignore real conversational context, where cultural nuance actually appears. There is a need for a more realistic, dialogue-based way to evaluate whether LLMs truly understand Indonesian cultural commonsense in multiple local languages.
AI.method: The authors construct CultureTalk-ID, a dataset of 4,496 culturally grounded dialogues in Indonesian and 10 other local languages, over 13 culturally salient topics. The data is collected via a multi-stage human pipeline with native speakers to ensure authenticity. On top of this, they define three evaluation tasks: (1) dialogue-based multiple-choice cultural commonsense reasoning, (2) culturally faithful machine translation, and (3) language steering, where models must produce culturally grounded outputs in specified languages or varieties.
AI.result: They obtain a large, multi-lingual, dialogue-centric benchmark that can probe whether LLMs can understand, translate, and generate culturally grounded language in Indonesian and its local languages. Although the abstract does not list scores, the benchmark itself is the primary result and is designed to reveal strengths and weaknesses of current LLMs on cultural commonsense tasks.
AI.conclusion: CultureTalk-ID provides the first dialogue-based benchmark for Indonesian cultural commonsense, going beyond isolated prompts and enabling more realistic evaluation of LLMs’ ability to reason about, translate, and generate culturally grounded dialogue across many Indonesian local languages and topics.

title: CMI-Mem: Toward Generalizable Long-Term Memory Management via CMI-Augmented Reinforcement Learning
summary: Memory Manager models are pivotal in agent systems. Existing methods rely predominantly on LLM-judged synthetic question-answer (QA) pairs, making memory valuation dependent on sampled queries and the downstream reader.
