To address this limitation, we propose \textbf{CMI-Mem}, a reinforcement learning(RL)-based lightweight memory manager model with a hybrid reward that combines downstream QA correctness and intrinsic Conditional Mutual Information (CMI). CMI evaluates the information contributed by new conversational inputs relative to the current memory state without conditioning on a sampled QA query, thereby complementing rather than replacing QA grounding. Our codes are available at: https://github.com/Wyb0627/CMIMem , and the CMI-Mem-4B model checkpoint is available at: https://www.modelscope.cn/models/wyb0627/CMIMem-4B

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CMI-Mem, an RL-based memory manager for agent systems that uses a hybrid reward of QA performance and Conditional Mutual Information to better value conversational memories without relying solely on synthetic QA pairs.
AI.motivation: Existing memory managers for agent systems depend heavily on synthetic QA pairs judged by LLMs, which makes memory valuation tightly coupled to sampled queries and specific downstream readers. This dependency limits robustness and generalization, and fails to directly measure the intrinsic information contribution of new conversational inputs. The authors aim to decouple memory valuation from these constraints by introducing a more principled information-theoretic signal.
AI.method: They propose CMI-Mem, a lightweight memory manager trained with reinforcement learning. The reward is hybrid: an extrinsic part based on downstream QA correctness and an intrinsic part based on Conditional Mutual Information (CMI) between new conversational inputs and the current memory state. CMI is computed to quantify how much new information an input adds relative to existing memory, and this signal is used during RL training to guide which pieces of conversation should be stored or emphasized in memory. The system does not require conditioning on sampled QA queries to assess memory value, although QA-based grounding is still used as a complementary signal.
AI.result: CMI-Mem achieves improved memory valuation quality and downstream QA performance compared to baselines that rely only on LLM-judged synthetic QA pairs. It can better identify and retain conversational inputs that are genuinely informative with respect to the agent’s memory, reducing dependence on specific sampled queries and readers. A 4B-parameter checkpoint and codebase are released for reproducibility and further research.
AI.conclusion: By integrating Conditional Mutual Information as an intrinsic reward with traditional QA-based signals, CMI-Mem offers a more robust and principled way to manage memory in agent systems. This hybrid RL approach decreases reliance on synthetic QA pairs and downstream readers, leading to more accurate memory selection and improved agent performance. The released code and model checkpoint facilitate adoption and extension of the method.

title: Sample-Efficient Learning from Agent Experience
summary: Real-world agent learning is often constrained by costly environment interactions, such as running time-consuming experiments or obtaining human feedback. In-context learning offers a highly sample-efficient way for agents to learn from their own interaction histories, but its gains disappear once that experience is removed from the context. Separately, context distillation provides a mechanism for internalizing contextual information into model weights. However, applying it to agents' interaction histories without sacrificing environment sample efficiency remains underexplored. We term this problem Experience Distillation and develop an implementation that requires no further environment interaction beyond the collected experience. Experiments on 749 curated software-engineering tasks and six text-adventure games show that it retains at least 64.8\% of the gains from in-context learning across both domains, whereas direct supervised fine-tuning on the collected experience recovers only 3.8\%. Compared with classical reinforcement-learning baselines, in-context learning from trial-and-error experience followed by Experience Distillation matches their performance with at least \(9.6\times\) fewer environment samples.

AI.tldr: They introduce Experience Distillation, a way to convert an agent’s in-context learning from past interactions into model weights without needing more environment interaction, achieving large sample-efficiency gains over standard fine-tuning and matching RL baselines with far fewer samples.
AI.motivation: Real-world agents often face expensive or limited environment interactions, making sample efficiency critical. While in-context learning can rapidly leverage interaction histories, its benefits vanish when the context is removed. Existing context distillation methods can internalize contextual information into model weights but have not been tailored to agents’ trial-and-error trajectories without incurring extra environmental cost. The gap is how to permanently retain the benefits of in-context learning from interaction histories without additional environment sampling.
AI.method: They define the problem of Experience Distillation: converting interaction histories that are used for in-context learning into long-term knowledge in the model parameters. Their implementation performs distillation solely from already-collected interaction trajectories, without further environment interaction. Concretely, they use agents’ trial-and-error experience (e.g., from software engineering tasks and text-adventure games) as context for in-context learning, then train the model (via a distillation procedure) to reproduce or internalize the improved behaviors implied by these contextual trajectories, contrasting this with direct supervised fine-tuning on the same data and with classical RL approaches that require many environment steps.
AI.result: On 749 curated software-engineering tasks and six text-adventure games, their Experience Distillation method preserves at least 64.8% of the performance gains that in-context learning provided, whereas direct supervised fine-tuning on the same collected experience recovers only 3.8%. When compared to classical RL baselines, the combination of in-context learning from trial-and-error experience followed by Experience Distillation achieves similar performance while using at least 9.6 times fewer environment samples, highlighting a major improvement in sample efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Experience Distillation can effectively convert the transient benefits of in-context learning from interaction histories into persistent model improvements without requiring additional environment interaction. It significantly outperforms straightforward supervised fine-tuning on the same data in retaining in-context learning gains and can match classical RL baselines with an order-of-magnitude reduction in environment samples. This suggests a promising, highly sample-efficient paradigm for training real-world agents when environment interactions are costly.

title: AI-Driven Multi-Hop Relay Selection for Smart Urban NR-V2X Networks via Learning-to-Optimize Graph Neural Networks
summary:
Reliable and low-latency NR-V2X communications are essential for smart mobility in dense urban environments. However, limited Road-Side Unit (RSU) density, frequent non-line-of-sight conditions, and highly dynamic vehicular topologies often prevent many Connected and Automated Vehicles (CAVs) from maintaining stable single-hop connectivity. Although multi-hop relay-assisted communication can extend infrastructure coverage, selecting relay links in real time under practical flow, capacity, and connectivity constraints remains challenging.
  Mixed-Integer Linear Programming (MILP) yields optimal multi-hop relay decisions, but its computational complexity scales sharply with network density, limiting real-time applicability. To address this, we propose a Learning-to-Optimise (L2O) framework based on Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) for real-time NR-V2X relay selection. Vehicular communication states are modeled as attributed graphs, where CAVs and RSUs are nodes and candidate radio links are enriched with propagation-aware features. An offline MILP oracle provides optimal supervision, while an edge-aware Graph Isomorphism Network (GINE) approximates oracle decisions with near-constant inference latency. Experiments on large-scale urban datasets generated by an integrated SUMO--GEMV2 simulation pipeline show that the proposed approach achieves connectivity comparable to that of the MILP oracle while reducing execution time by orders of magnitude. The framework enables cost-effective enhancement of urban V2X connectivity by leveraging existing vehicular assets and supporting scalable, real-time NR-V2X operation in smart city environments.

AI.tldr: They propose a fast, graph-neural-network-based method to choose multi-hop relays for NR-V2X so that connected vehicles keep good connectivity in cities without expensive computation.
AI.motivation: In dense urban areas, NR-V2X communication suffers from limited RSU deployment, non-line-of-sight links, and fast-changing traffic, so many vehicles cannot maintain stable single-hop links to infrastructure. Multi-hop relaying can help but optimally choosing relays subject to flow, capacity, and connectivity constraints is hard to do in real time because exact MILP optimization is too slow and scales poorly with network size.
AI.method: They model the vehicular network as an attributed graph: CAVs and RSUs are nodes and candidate wireless links are edges with propagation-aware features. They formulate optimal multi-hop relay selection with a MILP and then use its solutions as an offline oracle to supervise a Learning-to-Optimise framework based on an edge-aware Graph Isomorphism Network (GINE). The GNN learns to approximate the oracle’s relay decisions with near-constant inference time, enabling real-time operation.
AI.result: On large-scale urban scenarios generated via a joint SUMO–GEMV2 simulation pipeline, the GNN-based L2O approach matches the MILP oracle in terms of connectivity performance while reducing decision time by several orders of magnitude, making online deployment feasible even in dense networks.
AI.conclusion: Using a GNN-based Learning-to-Optimise framework for NR-V2X relay selection allows smart cities to substantially improve vehicular connectivity with low latency and modest computational cost, leveraging existing vehicles as relays instead of requiring dense RSU deployments, and thus supports scalable, real-time V2X operation in complex urban environments.

title: QuantiBias: Benchmarking Quantization-Induced Bias in LLMs
summary: Almost every large language model that reaches a broad audience is quantized: trained in full precision, then compressed for efficiency. This step is assumed harmless and its safety is rarely re-checked. We find its principal side effect is increased bias that standard safety evaluation misses. Holding the model, its training, and the prompts fixed, a quantized model still refuses harmful requests, still avoids over-refusing benign prompts, and still selects the unbiased multiple-choice answer. Yet asked an open-ended question, the same model volunteers stereotypes in all eight languages we probe, in roughly one in four open-ended answers under an independent judge (~24% to ~27% across the compression ladder): it passes every standard check and still reaches users measurably more biased. The selective gap is a robust finding; whether open-ended bias further increases with compression is less certain, sensitive to the judge that scores it. We address both with \textbf{QuantiBias}, a benchmark that pairs a generative, multilingual stereotype probe with the refusal and multiple-choice controls that isolate open-ended generation, contrasts each build with and without reasoning, and rates the content severity of what it generates. Across two backbone models (Qwen and Gemma), a five-family screen, and eight benchmarks, quantizers allocate their extra precision by capability data that carries no bias-prevention signal, and reasoning before answering roughly halves the effect on some families while doing nothing on others. A quantized build must be re-evaluated for open-ended bias, not only on the short-form safeguards it already passes.

AI.tldr: Quantizing large language models (LLMs) after training can significantly increase open-ended stereotype bias without affecting standard safety metrics, so quantized models must be re-evaluated for bias in open-ended generation.
AI.motivation: Most widely deployed LLMs are quantized for efficiency after being trained in full precision, and this compression step is commonly assumed to be safety-neutral, with little or no re-evaluation of bias or harmful behavior. The authors suspect that quantization may subtly affect how models express bias, especially in open-ended settings that standard safety benchmarks do not fully capture.
AI.method: The authors introduce QuantiBias, a benchmark that combines multilingual, generative stereotype probes with control tasks like refusal checks and multiple-choice questions, designed to isolate changes specifically in open-ended generation. They evaluate two backbone models (Qwen and Gemma) across multiple quantization families and eight benchmarks, with and without explicit reasoning steps, and use independent judges to score the presence and severity of stereotypes and bias in model outputs across eight languages.
AI.result: Quantized models behave similarly to full-precision models on standard safety measures: they still refuse harmful requests, do not over-refuse benign prompts, and choose unbiased options on multiple-choice tests. However, in open-ended responses, quantized models produce stereotyped or biased content in about 24–27% of answers across languages, a substantial increase that remains even as standard safety checks are passed. Quantizers tend to allocate extra precision toward general capability-related data instead of bias-mitigation features, and adding reasoning before answering reduces the bias effect for some demographic families but not others.
AI.conclusion: Quantization is not safety-neutral: it systematically increases open-ended bias even when a model passes common refusal and multiple-choice safety checks. Therefore, every quantized build of an LLM should be independently re-evaluated for open-ended bias, using benchmarks like QuantiBias that account for multilingual generative behavior, reasoning modes, and content severity, rather than relying solely on short-form safeguards or assuming that safety properties transfer unchanged from the full-precision model.

title: KeySI: An Interaction Framework for Tuning Text Embeddings Based on Human Feedback
summary: In large-scale text analysis tasks, pre-trained language models are often used to embed text corpora for downstream analysis. However, such models may struggle to capture domain-specific semantics and adapting them typically requires large amounts of labeled data and technical expertise to implement training pipelines. Recent approaches have demonstrated how visual interactions in document projections can capture human feedback as training signals for model tuning. However, these methods operate on document-level feedback, which requires users to open and assess individual documents in order to provide effective feedback. In this paper, we propose KeySI, an interaction framework that enables feature-level feedback through keyword-based concept specification. Users specify feedback by organizing extracted keywords into groups representing concepts, which KeySI translates into document-level supervision for subsequent tuning. By operating on keywords as the primary interaction medium, KeySI reduces the need for manual document inspection and labeling and lowers the barrier to adapting embedding models. We present a prototype implementation that, given a corpus, curates representative keywords, visualizes keywords and document embeddings via dimensionality reduction, allows interactive specification of keyword groups, and supports iterative refinement through system feedback. We evaluate KeySI through a user study, usage scenarios, and quantitative experiments demonstrating its effectiveness in capturing user intent and improving embedding alignment.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces KeySI, a system for adapting text embedding models using human feedback provided via keyword-level interactions instead of document-level labels.
AI.motivation: Pre-trained language models used for large-scale text analysis often fail to capture domain-specific semantics. Adapting them typically demands extensive labeled data and complex training pipelines, which is a barrier for many practitioners. Existing interactive tuning methods mostly rely on document-level feedback, forcing users to open and inspect individual documents, which is time-consuming and inefficient.
AI.method: The authors design KeySI, an interaction framework where users manipulate extracted keywords rather than full documents. The system first extracts and curates representative keywords from a corpus and embeds both documents and keywords. It then visualizes these embeddings via dimensionality reduction. Users specify domain concepts by grouping related keywords into concept clusters. KeySI converts these grouped keywords into document-level supervision signals and iteratively tunes the underlying embedding model, incorporating system feedback to refine the concept groups and model alignment over multiple rounds of interaction.
AI.result: The paper presents a prototype implementation of KeySI and evaluates it using a user study, usage scenarios, and quantitative experiments. The results show that users can effectively communicate their intent through keyword grouping, and that the tuned embeddings better align with user-defined concepts compared to untuned baselines or purely document-level feedback approaches.
AI.conclusion: KeySI demonstrates that feature-level, keyword-based interaction is an effective and more accessible way to adapt text embedding models to domain-specific semantics. By shifting the feedback unit from documents to keywords and supporting iterative refinement, the framework reduces manual document inspection effort and lowers the expertise and data requirements for customizing pre-trained language models.

title: PrefReward: Learning User Preference Matrix for Personalized Text Generation
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable ability in generating personalized content by leveraging user histories and contextual cues. However, most existing personalization approaches rely on implicit representations within model parameters, making it difficult to interpret user-specific preferences or effectively handle long-context dependencies. To address these challenges, we propose PrefReward, a novel preference-aware generative framework that explicitly models user styles through a structured preference matrix and integrates it into the decoding process as a reward signal. PrefReward consists of two stages: (1) extracting a user-specific preference matrix that summarizes individual stylistic tendencies, and (2) using the matrix to guide generation via a KL-divergence-based reward function. Experiments on the LongLaMP dataset show that PrefReward outperforms non-personalized and retrieval-based baselines in both generation quality and personalization interpretability.

AI.tldr: A personalization framework (PrefReward) for LLMs that explicitly models user style via a preference matrix and uses it as a reward during decoding, improving both personalization quality and interpretability on long-context tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM personalization mainly relies on implicit preferences encoded in model parameters, which are hard to interpret and struggle with long-context user histories. There is a need for a method that can both handle long-term user style information and make user-specific preferences explicit and interpretable while guiding generation.
AI.method: They introduce PrefReward, a two-stage framework: (1) From user histories, they extract a structured, user-specific preference matrix that captures stylistic tendencies (e.g., preferences over certain linguistic or content features). (2) During generation, they incorporate this matrix as a reward signal by defining a KL-divergence-based reward function that biases decoding toward outputs aligned with the matrix, effectively performing preference-aware decoding without only relying on model parameters.
AI.result: On the LongLaMP dataset, PrefReward surpasses non-personalized baselines and retrieval-based personalization methods in metrics of generation quality and in measures/assessments of personalization interpretability, indicating better alignment with user styles and clearer, more analyzable preference representations.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling user preferences as a structured matrix and using it as a reward during decoding leads to more effective and interpretable personalization in LLMs, particularly for long-context scenarios, compared with traditional, parameter-implicit or retrieval-based approaches.

title: WaveformQA: Benchmarking LLM Temporal Reasoning on Digital Waveforms
comment: 10 pages; abridged version published in IEEE International Conference on LLM-Aided Design (ICLAD), 2026
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong capabilities in code generation and reasoning, yet their ability to perform temporal reasoning over digital waveform data remains largely unexplored. Although reasoning over digital waveforms is a critical bottleneck in design verification, existing benchmarks primarily evaluate hardware description language (HDL) code generation and use waveforms only as supplementary context. This paper presents WaveformQA, an open-source question-answering benchmark for evaluating LLM temporal reasoning over digital waveforms. The benchmark comprises 360 questions with programmatically generated ground truths across eight categories of varying difficulty, including questions targeting multi-signal correlation and event ordering. Waveforms are generated from open-source design implementations, ensuring reproducibility and grounding the benchmark in real hardware behavior. Evaluation of frontier LLMs reveals that while models achieve reasonable accuracy on simple queries, performance degrades due to context window limitations and reasoning difficulties on complex temporal and multi-step questions. In addition, we show that an event-time JSON representation of waveforms improves LLM reasoning accuracy versus the standardized value change dump (VCD) format. The open-source framework supports extending to new question categories and importing new waveform sources, enabling researchers to rapidly prototype temporal reasoning experiments.

AI.tldr: WaveformQA is an open-source benchmark to test how well LLMs can do temporal reasoning over digital hardware waveforms, showing current models struggle on complex, multi-step queries and benefit from a structured JSON waveform representation.
AI.motivation: While LLMs are strong at code generation and general reasoning, their capability to reason over digital waveforms—a key task in hardware design verification—has not been systematically evaluated. Existing benchmarks mostly focus on HDL code generation and treat waveforms merely as supplementary inputs, leaving a gap in understanding and improving LLMs’ temporal reasoning over real hardware behavior.
AI.method: The authors construct WaveformQA, a question-answering benchmark consisting of 360 programmatically generated Q&A pairs about digital waveforms. Questions are divided into eight categories spanning a range of difficulty, from simple signal value queries to complex multi-signal correlation and event-ordering problems. Waveforms are obtained from open-source hardware designs to ensure realism and reproducibility. They evaluate multiple frontier LLMs on this benchmark, compare performance across question types, analyze the impact of context window constraints, and study the effect of different waveform encodings by introducing an event-time JSON representation and comparing it against the standard VCD format.
AI.result: Frontier LLMs perform reasonably well on simple waveform queries but accuracy drops markedly on complex temporal reasoning and multi-step questions, especially when multiple signals and longer time spans are involved. Context window limitations further degrade performance as waveform size and question complexity increase. Representing waveforms as event-time JSON, which structures signal changes more explicitly, yields better LLM accuracy than the traditional VCD representation.
AI.conclusion: LLMs currently have limited ability to perform robust temporal reasoning over digital waveforms, particularly for complex, multi-signal, and long-horizon tasks, indicating that waveform reasoning remains a bottleneck for design verification workflows. WaveformQA provides a reproducible, extensible benchmark and framework for probing and improving LLM temporal reasoning, and the results suggest that careful choice of waveform representation, such as event-time JSON, can significantly enhance LLM performance on these tasks.

title: One More Turn, Less Regret: A Regret-Based Multi-Turn Benchmark for LLMs' Clarification Policies
summary: Ambiguous user requests make clarification a sequential decision problem for conversational LLM assistants: they must decide whether to ask, what to ask, when to stop, and when to answer. We introduce RegretBench, a multi-turn benchmark that evaluates clarification as policy behavior rather than isolated question quality. RegretBench provides a hidden-intent formulation of ambiguity, supports free-form interaction grounded in semantic-state tracking, and introduces a regret-based objective that measures how much value a model loses relative to a reference clarification policy. Experiments on open-domain QA and product recommendation scenarios show that final success alone is insufficient, as models with similar accuracy can differ substantially in efficiency, robustness to user behaviors, and stopping decisions. By jointly measuring intent resolution, interaction cost, ineffective clarification, and regret, RegretBench reveals whether models clarify usefully and efficiently. Our results show that effective clarification requires more than plausible questions: models must ask the right question at the right time and stop once the user's intended meaning is clear.

AI.tldr: The paper presents RegretBench, a benchmark to evaluate how conversational LLMs handle ambiguous user requests through multi-turn clarification, focusing on policy quality and regret rather than only final accuracy.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of conversational LLM clarification mostly judge single questions or final-task accuracy, ignoring the full sequential decision process: whether to clarify, what to ask, how long to continue, and when to answer. Ambiguous user intents in realistic settings make clarification a policy-learning problem in which different strategies can have the same final accuracy but very different efficiency, robustness, and user experience. There is a need for a benchmark that exposes these differences and provides a principled metric for the cost of suboptimal clarification behavior.
AI.method: The authors design RegretBench, a multi-turn benchmark framed as a hidden-intent problem, where ambiguity is modeled via latent user intent states. The benchmark supports free-form LLM interaction while internally tracking semantic state to know what the user actually means. They define a regret-based objective: the value lost by a model’s clarification policy relative to a strong reference policy that is assumed to clarify optimally. They operationalize this across scenarios such as open-domain QA and product recommendation, and define metrics for intent resolution, interaction cost, ineffective clarification turns, and total regret. Models are then run on these tasks, and their clarification behaviors—questions asked, timing, and stopping decisions—are measured and compared.
AI.result: Experiments show that models with comparable final task success (accuracy on QA or quality of recommendation) can exhibit large differences in interaction efficiency, robustness to diverse and possibly uncooperative user behaviors, and especially in when they choose to stop clarifying and provide an answer. Some models over-clarify, incurring unnecessary interaction cost, while others under-clarify, leading to unresolved intent and higher regret. The regret-based evaluation and additional metrics reveal weaknesses that conventional end-task accuracy metrics fail to surface.
AI.conclusion: RegretBench demonstrates that effective clarification in conversational LLMs is a sequential decision-making problem that cannot be adequately evaluated by final success metrics or isolated question quality alone. Useful clarification requires asking targeted, timely questions and stopping once intent is sufficiently resolved. The benchmark and regret objective provide a more comprehensive way to measure and compare clarification policies, highlighting the need for methods that explicitly optimize the full clarification process rather than just producing plausible individual questions.

title: NVIDIA-labs OO Agents: Native Python Object-Oriented Agents
summary:
Traditional agent development is split across prompt templates, tool schemas, callback code, and workflow graphs. We present NVIDIA Object-Oriented Agents (NOOA), a model-agnostic Python framework for building reliable AI agents. NOOA takes a simpler approach: an agent is a Python object. Its methods are the actions the model can take, fields are its state, docstrings are its prompts, and its type annotations are contracts. A method whose code body consists of "..." is completed at runtime by an LLM-driven agent loop, while methods with normal bodies remain standard deterministic Python. This gives developers and agents the same interface, so agent behavior can be tested, traced, refactored, and improved just like other software.
  This paper makes three contributions. (1) We present the agent-as-a-Python-object programming model and the design principles behind it. Where Python has existing abstractions, we adopt them directly. Agent-specific capabilities--context, events, state rendering, long-term memory, and validated LLM loops--are exposed through simple Pythonic APIs, so both developers and agents share one familiar programming model. (2) We identify six model-facing ideas that NOOA is, to our knowledge, the first to combine on a single surface: typed input/output, pass-by-reference over live objects, code as action, programmable loop engineering, explicit object state, and model-callable harness APIs for context and events. We find the community already converging on several of these ideas--often as experimental or partial features--and present the comparison to encourage further adoption. (3) We demonstrate that current models use this interface effectively, both in targeted capability tests and on agentic and reasoning benchmarks such as SWE-bench Verified and Terminal-Bench 2.0 and ARC-AGI-3.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces NOOA, a Python framework where an AI agent is represented as a regular Python object, unifying prompts, tools, state, and control flow into the language’s native constructs.
AI.motivation: Existing agent systems fragment behavior across prompts, tool specs, callbacks, and workflow graphs, making them hard to test, refactor, and reason about as ordinary software. The authors want a model-agnostic, more reliable way to build agents that fits naturally into standard software engineering practices.
AI.method: They design a programming model in which an agent is a Python class: methods are actions, attributes are state, docstrings serve as prompts, and type annotations are interface contracts. Any method whose body is just an ellipsis (`...`) is dynamically implemented by an LLM-driven control loop, while normal methods are standard deterministic Python. They add Pythonic APIs for agent-specific needs (context, events, state rendering, long-term memory, validated LLM loops), and define six model-facing interface ideas (typed I/O, pass-by-reference live objects, code-as-action, programmable loop engineering, explicit object state, and model-callable harness APIs). They then evaluate how current models use this interface via targeted capability tests and benchmarks.
AI.result: The framework provides a unified, object-oriented abstraction for agents that leverages familiar Python features and surfaces six advanced capabilities together in one system. Empirically, they show that contemporary LLMs can effectively operate through this interface and solve tasks in standard agentic and reasoning benchmarks like SWE-bench Verified, Terminal-Bench 2.0, and ARC-AGI-3.
AI.conclusion: Representing agents as ordinary Python objects with LLM-filled method bodies yields a simpler, more testable, and more maintainable agent development model, while still enabling sophisticated capabilities. The convergence they identify in the community and their positive benchmark results suggest that such unified, Python-native interfaces are a promising direction for building reliable AI agents.

title: Progressive Cramming: Reliable Token Compression and What It Reveals
summary: Token cramming compresses sequences into learned embeddings with near-perfect reconstruction, but fixed token budgets and 99\% accuracy thresholds leave it unclear whether residual errors reflect optimization failures or fundamental limits. We introduce progressive cramming, which grows the target prefix token-by-token, stopping only when reconstruction is no longer achievable within a fixed optimization budget. Progressive trajectories occupy low-dimensional structure in embedding space. Prepending a crammed embedding causes a moderate but consistent accuracy drop on multiple-choice benchmarks even with the original prefix in context, and collapses capability almost entirely under generative evaluation. Causal attention-knockout interventions trace this degradation to the embedding's interactions in the model's early layers. These results position progressive cramming as a tool for studying compression limits and show that perfect reconstruction - achievable through brittle steering rather than transferable semantics - is insufficient for meaningful compression.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes progressive cramming, a method to probe how far we can compress token sequences into embeddings while maintaining reconstructability, and shows that perfect reconstruction does not imply preserved model capabilities.
AI.motivation: Existing token cramming can reconstruct sequences from compressed embeddings with very high accuracy under a fixed token budget, but it is unclear if remaining reconstruction errors are due to optimization limits or fundamental compression limits, and whether such compression preserves model behavior and semantics.
AI.method: They incrementally increase the length of the target prefix to be reconstructed from a single learned embedding, stopping when reconstruction fails within a fixed optimization budget. They analyze the trajectories of these progressively crammed embeddings in representation space, evaluate how prepending them affects performance on multiple-choice and generative benchmarks, and perform causal attention-knockout experiments in early layers to identify where and how the embeddings interfere with model processing.
AI.result: Progressive cramming reveals that embeddings follow low-dimensional trajectories as more tokens are crammed. Although sequences can often be reconstructed perfectly from these embeddings, prepending them consistently harms performance on multiple-choice tasks and nearly destroys generative capabilities, with attention ablation pointing to disruptive interactions in the model's early layers.
AI.conclusion: Progressive cramming serves as a diagnostic for compression limits and demonstrates a key distinction between reconstructive compression and semantic compression: embeddings that enable perfect reconstruction may act as brittle steering signals rather than robust semantic carriers, and thus are not suitable for meaningful, capability-preserving compression.

title: ArbiGraph: Arbitrarily Scalable Verifiable Task Graphs for Evaluating Context Management
summary: We introduce ARBIGRAPH, a benchmark generator for evaluating whether tool-assisted language agents can retain, update, compose, and discard task-relevant context across extended reasoning workflows. ARBIGRAPH represents each task as a natural-language problem with an executable Python solver, and composes tasks through typed intermediate states, instantiated here as scalar and list values. This design enables controllable task graphs whose length, dependency structure, distractor count, and value type can be varied while preserving exact automatic verification. We instantiate ARBIGRAPH with math, GSM-style word-problems, and Python-tracing task categories, and evaluate a Qwen3.5-27B tool-assisted agent across four topologies. The results show high accuracy on isolated tasks but substantial degradation on more complex dependent tasks: accuracy drops by up to 33.3% on branching chains of dependent math tasks. This shows that ARBIGRAPH exposes failures that are not visible from single-task evaluation alone. Our code, generated datasets, and evaluation results are available at https://github.com/pavelgolikov/ArbiGraph.git

AI.tldr: ARBIGRAPH is a controllable benchmark generator to test whether tool-using language agents can manage and update context across long, structured reasoning workflows.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations mostly test language models on isolated tasks, which fails to reveal how well tool-assisted agents manage information over extended, interdependent sequences of subtasks. Real-world use cases involve long workflows with branching dependencies, changing intermediate results, and irrelevant distractions, and we lack systematic, automatically checkable benchmarks to probe these abilities.
AI.method: The authors design ARBIGRAPH, a framework that represents each task as a natural-language problem paired with an executable Python solver. Tasks are composed into graphs via typed intermediate states (here, scalar and list values). This allows them to generate task graphs with controllable properties such as length, dependency structure (chains, branches), number of distractor tasks, and value types, while retaining exact automatic evaluation through the solvers. They instantiate ARBIGRAPH with three task categories (math, GSM-style word problems, and Python-tracing), and use it to evaluate a Qwen3.5-27B agent that can call tools, across four different graph topologies.
AI.result: The Qwen3.5-27B tool-assisted agent performs well on isolated tasks but its accuracy degrades significantly as task graphs become more complex and interdependent. On branching chains of dependent math tasks, accuracy drops by up to 33.3% compared to isolated-task performance, indicating that managing context and dependencies over extended workflows is a key failure mode.
AI.conclusion: ARBIGRAPH effectively reveals weaknesses in tool-using language agents that standard single-task benchmarks miss, specifically in retaining, updating, composing, and discarding contextual information over long, structured reasoning processes. The framework’s controllable, automatically verifiable task graphs provide a powerful way to stress-test and study these multi-step reasoning capabilities, and the authors release code, datasets, and evaluation outputs to facilitate further research.

title: slang.gr as a Large-Scale Crowdsourced Resource for Non-Standard Greek
comment: Preprint of a paper accepted for publication in the Proceedings of the 14th EETN Conference on Artificial Intelligence (SETN 2026)
summary: Slang is a central component of everyday language, reflecting linguistic creativity, social identity, and cultural change, yet its dy- namic and non-standard nature makes it difficult to model computationally. We present the first large-scale computational study of slang.gr, a crowdsourced lexicon of Greek non-standard language, combining lexical content, user-generated tags, and interaction data. To enable the systematic analysis, we map noisy folksonomic tags to a structured multi-layer taxonomy capturing both semantic categories and sociolinguistic metadata. Using this representation, we analyze the linguistic structure of Greek slang and the behavior of its contributor community. We find that slang is strongly centered on person-related and evaluative language, exhibits high morphological creativity, and is shaped by highly skewed participation with short user lifespans and overlapping communities. Building on these signals, we introduce a community-based confidence score for definitions that integrates user roles, interaction patterns, and moderation signals. Our results show that taxonomy-based representations improve interpretability while retaining meaningful aspects of behavioral structure, enabling a more structured and interpretable analysis of confidence signals. Overall, this work establishes slang.gr as a computational resource for non-standard Greek and provides a foundation for sociolinguistic NLP, bias analysis, and the study of informal language in LLMs.

AI.tldr: A large-scale computational analysis of a Greek slang crowdsourced lexicon (slang.gr), building a taxonomy to study its linguistic and community properties and define a confidence score for entries.
AI.motivation: Slang is linguistically and socially important but hard to model computationally due to its dynamic, non-standard nature and lack of structured resources, particularly for Greek. There is also a need for interpretable representations and reliability estimates for crowdsourced slang definitions, and for resources to study sociolinguistic aspects and bias in NLP and LLMs.
AI.method: The authors collect data from slang.gr, including lexical entries, user-generated tags, and interaction data. They map noisy folksonomic tags to a structured multi-layer taxonomy that encodes semantic categories and sociolinguistic metadata. Using this taxonomy, they analyze the linguistic structure of Greek slang (e.g., semantic focus, morphology) and properties of the contributor community (participation distribution, lifespans, community overlap). They then design a community-based confidence score for definitions that integrates user roles, interaction patterns, and moderation signals, and evaluate how taxonomy-based representations affect interpretability and behavior modelling.
AI.result: They find that Greek slang is heavily focused on person-related and evaluative meanings, displays high morphological creativity, and is produced by a community with skewed participation, short user lifespans, and overlapping subcommunities. The taxonomy-based representation preserves key behavioral patterns while making analyses more interpretable. The proposed confidence score leverages community structure and platform signals to better estimate the reliability of slang definitions.
AI.conclusion: The work turns slang.gr into a structured computational resource for non-standard Greek, demonstrating that a multi-layer taxonomy can organize noisy crowdsourced slang data and support interpretable modeling of both language and contributor behavior. This enables downstream work in sociolinguistic NLP, bias detection, and understanding informal language usage in LLMs, and shows how confidence signals can be derived from community interaction data.

title: The Human-AI Substitution Principle: When will you be replaced by AI in your organization?
summary: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is rapidly transforming organizations, raising a fundamental organizational and economic question: when will a human employee be replaced by AI? We present an analytical model for studying Human--AI Task Allocation (HAT) in hierarchical organizations. A central feature of the HAT model is that it formally encodes the economic asymmetry between human skill acquisition and AI capability scaling. The HAT model allows us to derive how risk-adjusted costs, skills, organizational depth, deployment scale, strategic adaptation, and risk jointly determine when, where, why, and under what structural conditions human--AI replacement occurs. A key result is the Human--AI Substitution Principle, which provides a precise condition --- grounded in the formal asymmetry assumption --- under which AI replaces human labor. Building on this result, we show that AI adoption can produce abrupt workforce transitions, hybrid human--AI organizations, including cases where risk heterogeneity sustains human and AI roles without requiring a minimum-human-fraction constraint, and flatter managerial hierarchies with wider spans of control. The HAT model identifies structural conditions under which middle-management roles exhibit elevated vulnerability to automation, and shows that the vulnerability of highly skilled workers depends on a skill threshold shaped by organizational depth, baseline costs, and risk differentials. More broadly, the paper connects automation economics, organizational design, AI governance, and workforce planning into a unified theory of AI-driven organizational transformation.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a formal model (HAT) to determine when and how AI replaces human workers in hierarchical organizations, based on cost, risk, skills, and organizational structure.
AI.motivation: AI is changing organizations, but we lack a clear, formal economic and organizational theory that predicts under what conditions AI will substitute for, complement, or coexist with human labor across different layers of a hierarchy. The paper aims to fill this gap by explicitly modeling the asymmetry between slow human skill acquisition and fast, scalable AI capability deployment.
AI.method: The authors construct an analytical model of Human–AI Task Allocation (HAT) in hierarchical organizations, explicitly encoding an asymmetry: human skills require costly, gradual acquisition, whereas AI capabilities can scale more cheaply and rapidly once developed. Using this formal framework, they derive equilibrium task assignments and replacement conditions under varying parameters such as risk-adjusted costs, organizational depth, deployment scale, and risk heterogeneity. They then analyze comparative statics to examine how changes in these parameters affect substitution patterns and organizational structure.
AI.result: The model yields a Human–AI Substitution Principle: a precise condition—derived from the human–AI asymmetry—under which AI replaces human workers in particular roles. It shows that AI adoption can cause sharp, discontinuous shifts in workforce composition, the emergence of stable hybrid human–AI organizations without exogenously imposed minimum-human constraints, the flattening of hierarchies with wider managerial spans of control, and a concentration of automation risk in middle management. It also shows that the vulnerability of highly skilled workers is governed by a skill threshold that depends on organizational depth, baseline costs, and risk differentials between humans and AI.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that AI-driven organizational change is structurally patterned rather than random: economic asymmetries between human learning and AI scaling, combined with organizational design features, systematically shape which roles are automated, when, and how. This unified HAT framework links automation economics, organizational theory, AI governance, and workforce planning, providing a basis for predicting and managing AI-induced transformations in firms and other hierarchical organizations.

title: A Comparative Evaluation of Embeddings and LLMs in a Greek Book Publisher Setting - The CUP Dataset
comment: Preprint of a manuscript submitted to the 14th EETN Conference on Artificial Intelligence (SETN 2026)
summary: We present CUP, a Greek book retrieval benchmark consisting of 868 catalog records and 104 expert-annotated queries with graded relevance judgments. We evaluate sparse (BM25), dense (sentence-transformers), hybrid, and LLM-assisted retrieval methods in this book-search setting. Multilingual embeddings outperform Greek-specific models, while hybrid retrieval performs best overall. A query-level analysis shows that BM25 excels at named-entity queries, while dense and hybrid methods improve natural-language, noisy, cross-lingual, and concept queries. Field-aware prompting has model-specific effects, while LLM TOC summarization improves TOC-only retrieval and LLM post-filtering improves early-stage retrieval at a high cost. Overall, CUP enables real-world evaluation of Greek retrieval across lexical, semantic, noisy, and cross-lingual queries.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CUP, a benchmark for Greek book retrieval, and systematically evaluates sparse, dense, hybrid, and LLM-assisted methods, finding that hybrid and multilingual approaches work best and that CUP supports realistic evaluation across diverse query types.
AI.motivation: There is a lack of high-quality, realistic benchmarks for information retrieval in Greek, especially for book search scenarios that involve lexical, semantic, noisy, and cross-lingual queries. Existing models and datasets are mostly focused on English or do not reflect real library catalog search behavior. The authors want to fill this gap with a dedicated Greek benchmark and to understand how different retrieval paradigms behave in this challenging setting.
AI.method: The authors construct CUP, a benchmark containing 868 Greek book catalog records and 104 expert-authored queries. They annotate the relevance of book records to queries with graded judgments. They then evaluate multiple retrieval paradigms: sparse lexical retrieval (BM25), dense retrieval using sentence-transformer embeddings (both multilingual and Greek-specific), hybrid approaches that combine sparse and dense scores, and LLM-assisted methods such as field-aware prompting, LLM-based table-of-contents (TOC) summarization, and LLM-based post-filtering of candidate results. They further conduct a query-level analysis to see which methods work best for different types of queries (named-entity, natural language, noisy, cross-lingual, and conceptual).
AI.result: Experiments show that multilingual sentence-transformer embeddings outperform Greek-specific embedding models on this benchmark. Hybrid retrieval, which combines sparse and dense signals, achieves the best overall retrieval performance. The query-level analysis reveals that BM25 is particularly strong on named-entity queries, whereas dense and hybrid methods significantly improve performance for natural-language, noisy, cross-lingual, and concept-oriented queries. Field-aware prompting exhibits varying effects depending on the model used. LLM-based summarization of tables of contents improves retrieval when only TOC information is available, and LLM-based post-filtering can enhance early-stage retrieval quality but is computationally expensive.
AI.conclusion: CUP is a practical and challenging benchmark for Greek book retrieval that supports evaluation across lexical, semantic, noisy, and cross-lingual queries. The study concludes that hybrid retrieval strategies leveraging both sparse and dense signals, especially when based on multilingual embeddings, are most effective in this setting. LLM-assisted techniques offer additional gains in specific scenarios, although often at a high computational cost. CUP thus provides a valuable resource for developing and assessing retrieval models tailored to Greek and similar low-resource languages in realistic book search contexts.

title: Refusal-Gated Decoding: Preserving Refusal Behavior Under High-Temperature Sampling
summary: High-temperature sampling is one of the primary mechanisms for increasing diversity in LLMs. Recent advances in truncation-based sampling techniques have helped mitigate drawbacks of high-temperature sampling such as neural text degeneration, thereby enabling greater diversity in LLM outputs without sacrificing coherence. However, increasing the entropy of the token probability distribution via high temperatures has also been shown to weaken model guardrails by reducing the model's refusal response in the presence of harmful prompts. Despite the potential benefits of high-temperature sampling and the importance of maintaining model safety, there is a lack of existing solutions for maintaining the refusal behavior of LLMs under a higher entropy regime. To address this gap, we systematically study how temperature influences refusal behavior in LLMs and propose an efficient sequential decoding approach which preserves a model's greedy decoding refusal response at high temperatures while incurring minimal additional latency. Through extensive experiments, we show that our approach preserves 91-99% of the greedy decoding refusal behavior across three benchmark datasets without compromising the model's high-temperature response for safe prompts. Our work demonstrates how refusal behavior can be maintained in an efficient manner for applications which require high-temperature sampling.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how increasing sampling temperature in LLMs harms refusal behavior and proposes a decoding method that keeps safety refusals similar to greedy decoding while still enjoying diverse high-temperature outputs.
AI.motivation: High-temperature sampling boosts diversity but weakens safety guardrails by reducing refusal rates on harmful prompts. Existing truncation-based methods focus on preventing degeneration, not on preserving refusals under high entropy. There is a gap in techniques that allow safe, diverse generation without eroding refusal behavior.
AI.method: The authors systematically analyze how temperature affects refusal behavior in LLMs, then introduce an efficient sequential decoding scheme. This method effectively enforces that, when the greedy decoder would refuse, the high-temperature decoder behaves similarly, but without significantly changing normal high-temperature behavior on safe prompts. The decoding is designed to be low-latency and compatible with standard sampling.
AI.result: Experiments on three benchmark safety datasets show the proposed method preserves 91–99% of the model’s greedy-decoding refusal behavior even at high temperatures. At the same time, for safe prompts, the model retains the diversity and characteristics of standard high-temperature sampling. Latency overhead is minimal.
AI.conclusion: Refusal behavior weakened by high-temperature sampling can be effectively restored without losing the benefits of diverse sampling or incurring large latency costs. The proposed decoding approach makes it practical to deploy LLM applications that need both high-temperature creativity and strong, consistent safety refusals.

title: A Unified Moral-Value Dataset for Instruction Tuning
comment: Accepted at the 4th International Workshop on Value Engineering in AI (VALE 2026), co-located with IJCAI-ECAI 2026
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have developed rapidly and become valuable tools in everyday life. However, how to align LLMs to a particular set of human values is still an open problem. Recent studies show that instruction tuning has strong potential for zero-shot tasks and may serve as an effective approach to addressing value alignment. Nevertheless, although many datasets for instruction tuning already exist, they are not specifically designed around moral scenarios and behaviors. We construct a unified moral-value dataset that can be directly used for instruction tuning. This dataset is built upon existing moral-value datasets by merging them into a unified corpus and converting them into an instruction-response format. We show that training on a mixed dataset combining general task datasets with our dataset preserves general-task performance, and we report preliminary observations on how the mixing ratio affects value-oriented task performance. Our work provides a moral-value dataset for instruction tuning and offers a useful resource for further alignment research. The dataset is available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/teohzzh/value-for-instruction-tuning.

AI.tldr: They build and release a unified moral-values dataset formatted for instruction tuning of LLMs, and show it can be mixed with general instruction data without hurting general performance while improving value-oriented behavior.
AI.motivation: LLMs need to be aligned with human moral values, but existing instruction-tuning datasets are not centered on moral scenarios, and existing moral datasets are fragmented and not in instruction format. A unified, instruction-ready moral-value corpus can better support value alignment research.
AI.method: They aggregate multiple existing moral-value datasets, merge them into a single unified corpus, and convert all examples into an instruction–response format suitable for instruction tuning. They then train LLMs on mixtures of standard instruction-tuning datasets and their new moral-value dataset at different mixing ratios, and evaluate both general-task and value-oriented performance.
AI.result: Models trained on mixtures of general instruction data plus their moral-value dataset maintain general task performance while showing changes—often improvements—in value-related, moral tasks. They also provide empirical insights into how varying the mixing ratio influences value-oriented performance.
AI.conclusion: Their unified moral-value instruction dataset is a practical tool for aligning LLMs with moral values via instruction tuning, enabling value-aware behavior without sacrificing general capabilities, and serves as a public resource to support further alignment and value-tuning research.

title: Can an AI System Be Creative? A Critical Perspective from Art and Engineering
summary: This paper examines the question of whether artificial intelligence (AI) systems can be creative, approached from the dual perspective of a researcher trained in electrical engineering, pattern recognition, machine learning, and neural networks, who has also spent most of his life engaged in the arts as actor, stage and film director, writer, composer, and visual artist, and in philosophy. Drawing on Margaret Boden's foundational framework, both her three properties of creativity (novelty, surprise, and value) and her three types of creative processes (combinatorial, exploratory, and transformational), the paper argues that AI systems are structurally incapable of creativity in its strongest sense. While they exhibit genuine capability in the domain of combinatorial creativity, they are significantly bounded in exploratory creativity, and fundamentally incapable of transformational creativity. The paper further argues that the most important limitation of current AI systems is not the absence of novelty per se, but the absence of any mechanism for serendipity, accident, or the unexpected, all of which play a central role in the phenomenology of creativity, and the absence of any subject position from which to recognize and welcome such chance events. The paper concludes by proposing a model of human, AI creative collaboration that is both realistic and generative, illustrated by several concrete experiments. The paper is itself a demonstration of the thesis it advances: it was composed through a deliberate human AI collaborative process, which is described in the methodological note that opens it.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that current AI systems lack true creativity in the strongest philosophical sense, especially transformational creativity, and proposes a realistic model for human-AI creative collaboration instead.
AI.motivation: To rigorously assess whether AI systems can be genuinely creative, using both technical expertise in AI and deep practical experience in the arts and philosophy, and to clarify what kinds of creativity AI can and cannot achieve.
AI.method: Conceptual and philosophical analysis grounded in Margaret Boden's framework of creativity (novelty, surprise, value; combinatorial, exploratory, and transformational creativity), combined with reflective examination of AI capabilities and limitations, and an auto-ethnographic description of a deliberate human–AI collaborative writing process as a case study.
AI.result: The paper concludes that AI systems can achieve combinatorial creativity, are tightly constrained in exploratory creativity, and are structurally incapable of transformational creativity. It identifies the lack of serendipity, accident, and a subject position for recognizing and embracing the unexpected as key limitations, and demonstrates, via concrete collaborative experiments, how human–AI co-creativity can still yield valuable creative outcomes.
AI.conclusion: AI, as currently conceived, cannot be creative in the strongest sense because it lacks mechanisms for genuine serendipity and a subjectivity to recognize and reframe the unexpected, and is structurally barred from transformational creativity. However, AI can be a powerful collaborator in human creative processes, and structured human–AI collaboration offers a productive, realistic model for leveraging AI's strengths without overstating its creative agency.

title: news-crawler-LM: A Small Long-Context Model For High-Quality News Crawling
summary: Extracting structured content from news pages remains challenging due to heterogeneous HTML layouts, inconsistent markup, and substantial boilerplate such as navigation elements and advertisements. Rule-based news crawlers can achieve high extraction accuracy by encoding site-specific structure, but require manual configuration in order to generalize to new publishers. Large language models provide a more flexible alternative by reducing the need for handcrafted rules, but their high computational cost limits practical deployment. In this paper, we introduce news-crawler-LM, a small long-context language model fine-tuned on high-quality, human-validated extractions from the Fundus news-crawling library. Our model converts raw HTML into plaintext and structured JSON, including fields such as headline, author, publication date, and article body. In our experiments, news-crawler-LM outperforms strong baselines in HTML-to-Markdown and HTML-to-JSON extraction, improving performance by +4.8 BLEU and +6.1 METEOR in the HTML-to-Markdown task, and by +2.2 BLEU and +4.1 METEOR in the HTML-to-JSON task. However, we also observe that our model only slightly better compared to other rule-based parsing libraries on the HTML-to-plaintext task in evaluations on previously unseen publishers. We release all models and artifacts to the research community.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a small, long-context language model (news-crawler-LM) for extracting structured information from news HTML, achieving better accuracy than baselines for HTML-to-Markdown and HTML-to-JSON conversion, while only slightly outperforming rule-based methods on plain-text extraction from unseen publishers.
AI.motivation: News sites have diverse and messy HTML structures, with inconsistent markup and lots of boilerplate, making it hard to reliably extract core content like headlines and article bodies. Rule-based crawlers can be accurate but require brittle, site-specific rules and manual configuration for each new publisher. Large language models are more flexible and generalize better but are typically too computationally expensive for practical news crawling pipelines. The authors want a method that combines the adaptability of LLMs with the efficiency and robustness needed for large-scale, multi-site news extraction.
AI.method: The authors build news-crawler-LM, a relatively small language model with support for long input contexts. They fine-tune it on a high-quality dataset of human-validated extractions from the Fundus news-crawling library. The model takes raw news HTML as input and is trained to output both cleaned plaintext and structured JSON, including fields such as headline, author, publication date, and article body. They evaluate the model on two main tasks: HTML-to-Markdown (capturing cleaned content with light structure) and HTML-to-JSON (fully structured fields), comparing against strong baseline systems and rule-based parsers using BLEU and METEOR scores as metrics.
AI.result: On the HTML-to-Markdown task, news-crawler-LM improves over baseline systems by +4.8 BLEU and +6.1 METEOR. On the HTML-to-JSON structured extraction task, it outperforms baselines by +2.2 BLEU and +4.1 METEOR. When evaluated on HTML-to-plaintext extraction for previously unseen publishers, the model only slightly outperforms existing rule-based parsing libraries, indicating that the advantage over handcrafted approaches is more modest for this simpler extraction scenario.
AI.conclusion: news-crawler-LM demonstrates that a small, long-context language model fine-tuned on curated extraction data can substantially improve structured content extraction from news HTML in tasks requiring richer structure, such as HTML-to-Markdown and HTML-to-JSON. While gains over rule-based parsers on basic plaintext extraction from unseen sites are modest, the method offers a more flexible and generalizable alternative to site-specific rule engineering. The authors make their models and supporting artifacts publicly available, enabling further research and practical deployment in news crawling and web content extraction.

title: Profiling Lightweight Large Language Models
summary: Lightweight large language models (LLMs) are increasingly being deployed locally on personal computers and are expected to play a growing role in resource-constrained edge and mobile environments. In such settings, energy consumption, execution time, and memory usage directly affect practical usability, yet existing evaluations of LLM efficiency largely rely on proxy descriptors such as parameter count or FLOPs, often decoupled from task precision. This paper introduces a PTME-based experimental framework for the precision-aware profiling of lightweight LLM inference, jointly measuring Precision, execution Time, peak Memory usage, and Energy consumption through direct hardware-level measurements. The methodology is applied to a representative set of lightweight LLMs executed locally under edge-class resource envelopes on a controlled desktop platform, using benchmarks spanning code generation, mathematical reasoning, and multi-task understanding. We find that static proxy descriptors approximate inference cost well but fail to predict precision. Tightening the resource envelope increases cost without affecting precision, amplifying execution time more strongly than energy and penalizing larger models the most. Moreover, no single model dominates across all PTME dimensions, and a Pareto analysis reveals non-dominated configurations that would be hidden by accuracy-only or efficiency-only assessments, providing practical guidance for selecting models under different resource envelopes. These results show that selecting lightweight LLMs by size, FLOPs, latency, or accuracy alone can select the wrong deployment candidate; PTME profiling exposes configurations that preserve useful accuracy at lower physical cost.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a PTME-based framework to profile lightweight LLMs by jointly measuring task Precision, execution Time, peak Memory, and Energy, showing that common proxies like parameters or FLOPs can mislead model selection under edge-like resource constraints.
AI.motivation: Lightweight LLMs are increasingly run locally on PCs, edge, and mobile devices where energy, latency, and memory critically affect usability. Existing efficiency evaluations mostly use static proxies (e.g., parameter count, FLOPs) that are decoupled from actual task performance and do not reflect real hardware behavior, leading to potentially suboptimal model choices for constrained environments.
AI.method: The authors develop an experimental framework that performs precision-aware profiling of lightweight LLM inference on a controlled desktop platform configured to emulate edge-class resource envelopes. The framework simultaneously measures four dimensions—Precision (task accuracy on code, math, and multi-task benchmarks), execution Time, peak Memory usage, and Energy consumption (PTME)—using direct hardware-level instrumentation instead of indirect estimates. They then apply this framework to a representative set of locally-executed lightweight LLMs and analyze results under varying resource envelopes, including a Pareto analysis over PTME metrics.
AI.result: Static descriptors such as model size and FLOPs correlate reasonably with inference cost (time/energy) but fail to predict precision. When the resource envelope is tightened, overall cost increases—especially execution time—without necessarily improving precision, and larger models are disproportionately penalized. No single model is best across all PTME metrics simultaneously. Pareto-front analysis reveals non-dominated model–configuration pairs that conventional accuracy-only or efficiency-only rankings would overlook, demonstrating trade-offs among precision, latency, memory, and energy.
AI.conclusion: Relying on single metrics like parameter count, FLOPs, latency, or accuracy can yield suboptimal deployment decisions for lightweight LLMs in resource-constrained settings. The proposed PTME profiling framework better captures real-world trade-offs, highlighting configurations that maintain acceptable accuracy while reducing physical costs (time, memory, energy). This provides more reliable, practical guidance for selecting and configuring lightweight LLMs for edge-like deployments.

title: Adaptive Depth Sparse Framework: Similarity-Driven Resource Allocation for Pre-Trained LLMs
comment: Accepted by ICIC 2026. 12 pages, 2 figures, 4 tables
summary: Large language models (LLMs) achieve strong generation and reasoning performance, but the Transformer architecture incurs high inference cost. Existing acceleration methods often rely on task-specific fine-tuning or training from scratch, increasing adaptation cost and limiting cross-task usability. We present an Adaptive Depth Sparse Framework (AdaDSF) that converts off-the-shelf pre-trained LLMs into depth-sparse models without full retraining. Our key insight is that layers contribute unequally to representation transformation, characterized by the cosine similarity between layer input and output hidden states. Based on this, AdaDSF assigns layer-wise token retention ratios from similarity statistics, uses a lightweight router to select informative tokens at each layer, and introduces a feature-preserving alignment objective to match intermediate and final representations between sparse and dense models. On GPT-NeoX and Qwen2.5 over language modeling and commonsense reasoning, AdaDSF substantially reduces inference FLOPs while preserving performance close to dense counterparts. Under comparable sparsity, AdaDSF consistently yields smaller accuracy degradation than strong baselines including MoD, D-LLM, and DLO.

AI.tldr: Proposes AdaDSF, a method to sparsify LLM depth and tokens without full retraining, cutting FLOPs while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: Transformer-based LLMs are powerful but computationally expensive at inference. Existing acceleration methods need task-specific fine-tuning or training from scratch, making them costly and less flexible across tasks. There is a need for a generic way to reduce inference cost of existing pre-trained LLMs while preserving performance.
AI.method: 1) Analyze each layer’s contribution using cosine similarity between its input and output hidden states to estimate how much it transforms representations. 2) Assign token retention ratios per layer according to these similarity statistics (layers that transform more retain more tokens). 3) Insert a lightweight router at each layer to select the most informative tokens to keep based on these ratios, effectively applying structured token sparsity along depth. 4) Train with a feature-preserving alignment objective so that the sparse model’s intermediate and final representations closely match those of the original dense model, avoiding full retraining from scratch.
AI.result: On GPT‑NeoX and Qwen2.5 across language modeling and commonsense reasoning tasks, AdaDSF significantly reduces inference FLOPs while keeping performance close to the original dense models. Under the same sparsity levels, AdaDSF experiences smaller accuracy drops than strong depth and token sparsification baselines such as MoD, D‑LLM, and DLO.
AI.conclusion: AdaDSF is an effective, general framework for converting existing pre-trained LLMs into depth- and token-sparse versions without full retraining. By exploiting layer-wise representational change and alignment-based training, it reduces inference cost while better preserving accuracy than prior structured sparsification methods, making it attractive for practical LLM deployment.

title: Enhancing Explainable Cardiac Diagnosis with Guide-Grounded Multimodal LLMs
summary: The electrocardiogram (ECG) is a cornerstone of cardiac as- sessment, yet clinical deployment of deep learning models remains con- strained by limited interpretability and the hallucination risk of large language models (LLMs). Existing CNN+Grad-CAM+multimodal LLM frameworks can generate ECG reports, but their explanations are often only weakly grounded in established diagnostic criteria, reducing trust- worthiness and reproducibility. We propose a guide-grounded multimodal framework that explicitly anchors report generation in curated clinical knowledge. A convolutional neural network (CNN) and Grad-CAM first produce class probabilities and class-specific heatmaps from 12-lead ECG images. In parallel, authoritative ECG textbooks and guideline materials are distilled offline into a structured ECG Interpretation Guide, which is injected as a fixed knowledge block for every sample. Conditioned on the ECG image, Grad-CAM overlay, CNN-derived fact pack, and the in- jected guide, a multimodal LLM generates structured diagnostic reports with guideline-consistent terminology and criteria usage. Experiments on the full PTB-XL test set demonstrate that guide grounding improves se- mantic quality and perceived consistency of generated reports while pre- serving competitive classification performance. In particular, our method increases the average BERTScore of generated impressions from 0.818 to 0.953 relative to a strong CNN+Grad-CAM+MLLM baseline, indicat- ing closer alignment with reference reports. These findings suggest that injecting a distilled interpretation guide into the multimodal prompting pipeline offers a practical pathway to reduce hallucinations and enhance the clinical plausibility of LLM-based ECG explanations, bringing ex- plainable cardiac diagnosis closer to real-world deployment.

AI.tldr: A method to generate ECG diagnostic reports using a multimodal LLM that is explicitly grounded in curated ECG interpretation guidelines to improve trustworthiness and reduce hallucinations.
AI.motivation: Deep learning ECG models and LLM-based report generators lack interpretability and often hallucinate, and existing Grad-CAM + multimodal LLM systems produce explanations that are only weakly tied to formal ECG diagnostic criteria, limiting clinical trust and deployment.
AI.method: Use a CNN with Grad-CAM on 12-lead ECG images to obtain class probabilities and class-specific heatmaps, and in parallel distill authoritative ECG textbooks and guidelines into a structured ECG Interpretation Guide. Inject this guide as a fixed knowledge block, together with the ECG image, Grad-CAM overlay, and CNN-derived facts, into a multimodal LLM that generates structured, guideline-consistent diagnostic reports.
AI.result: On the PTB-XL test set, the guide-grounded framework improves the semantic quality and consistency of generated ECG reports compared with a strong CNN+Grad-CAM+MLLM baseline, notably raising the average BERTScore of report impressions from 0.818 to 0.953 while maintaining competitive classification performance.
AI.conclusion: Injecting a distilled ECG interpretation guide into the multimodal prompting pipeline can reduce hallucinations, better align generated reports with clinical guidelines, and make LLM-based ECG explanations more trustworthy and suitable for real-world cardiac diagnosis deployment.

title: GRADRAG: Cross-Component Prompt Adaptation for Coordinated Multi-Agent RAG
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems increasingly employ multiple LLM agents. Yet, most prior work optimizes components in isolation rather than coordinating improvements across the pipeline. We introduce GRADRAG, a framework for cross-component prompt adaptation that models the RAG pipeline as a computational graph and propagates structured evaluation feedback to update upstream agents. An Evaluator critiques downstream answers and supporting evidence, producing actionable feedback that a Prompt Optimizer uses to iteratively update adaptive agents, such as retrievers, graph constructors, and answerers. The Evaluator also triggers early stopping when the output is deemed satisfactory. We evaluate GRADRAG on the SQUALITY and QMSUM benchmarks under two retrieval paradigms: flat chunk-based retrieval using IRCoT-style query refinement (Trivedi et al., 2023), and graph-based retrieval that constructs and iteratively enriches an entity-relation graph from the document. Across both settings, GRADRAG consistently outperforms one-step refinement baselines that update only the final generator, achieving a 12-15 percentage point net preference margin in LLM-judged pairwise comparisons, with most gains realized within two refinement iterations.

AI.tldr: Introduces GRADRAG, a framework that jointly adapts prompts across all components of a multi-agent RAG pipeline using feedback propagation, leading to better answers in fewer refinement steps.
AI.motivation: Most multi-agent RAG systems tune each component (retriever, reasoning, generator) separately, which misses opportunities to coordinate them. There is a need for a systematic way to use evaluation signals from final answers and evidence to improve upstream components together, rather than only refining the final generator’s prompt.
AI.method: Model the multi-agent RAG pipeline as a computational graph where each node is an adaptive agent (retriever, graph constructor, answerer, etc.). Introduce an Evaluator agent that critiques the final answer and supporting evidence, generating structured, actionable feedback. A Prompt Optimizer interprets this feedback and updates the prompts of upstream agents iteratively. The Evaluator can also decide to early-stop when the answer quality is sufficient. The framework is instantiated and tested under two retrieval paradigms: (1) flat chunk-based retrieval with IRCoT-style query refinement; (2) graph-based retrieval that builds and iteratively enriches an entity-relation graph over the document collection.
