AI.result: On SQUALITY and QMSUM benchmarks, GRADRAG yields consistent improvements over baselines that only refine the final generator’s prompt once. It achieves a 12–15 percentage point net preference margin in LLM-judged pairwise comparisons, and most of the performance gain occurs within two refinement iterations, showing both effectiveness and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Cross-component prompt adaptation in multi-agent RAG, implemented via a computational-graph view and feedback propagation from an Evaluator, leads to substantially better system performance than isolated, one-step generator refinement. A small number of refinement iterations is sufficient to realize most of the gains, making GRADRAG a practical way to improve complex RAG pipelines.

title: Efficient and Interpretable Body-Based Emotion Recognition with Lightweight Temporal Convolutional Networks
comment: Accepted at the 14th International Conference on Affective Computing and Intelligent Interaction (ACII 2026)
summary: Body-based emotion recognition is important for real-time affective systems, but graph-based skeleton models can be computationally expensive. This paper studies whether lightweight temporal convolutional networks (TCNs) can provide an efficient and interpretable alternative for body-based emotion classification. We evaluate a family of TCN models on DIEM-A and compare them with a graph-based time-series graph (G-TSG) baseline using accuracy, macro-F1, parameter count, and inference latency. Although G-TSG achieves the highest mean performance, TCN-Base remains within $1.58$ accuracy points and $1.25$ macro-F1 points while using $79.18\%$ fewer parameters and reducing classifier latency by approximately $12.5\times$. We also analyze body-region contributions using region-specific TCN models, zero-based occlusion, and G-TSG gradient saliency. The results show that upper-body motion provides the strongest standalone regional cue, that the usefulness of body regions varies across emotions, and that different interpretability methods capture distinct aspects of model behavior. These findings suggest that lightweight TCNs can support efficient body-based emotion recognition while also providing practical insight into how motion cues contribute to classification.

AI.tldr: Lightweight temporal convolutional networks (TCNs) can approach the performance of a graph-based skeleton model for body-based emotion recognition while being far more efficient and offering interpretable insights into body-region importance.
AI.motivation: Graph-based skeleton models for recognizing human emotions from body movements are accurate but computationally heavy, limiting their use in real-time affective systems or resource-constrained settings. The authors want to know if simpler, lighter models can retain most of the performance while being faster and easier to interpret, and to better understand which body regions drive emotion classification.
AI.method: The paper evaluates several lightweight temporal convolutional network (TCN) architectures on the DIEM-A dataset for body-based emotion recognition, and compares them to a graph-based time-series graph (G-TSG) baseline. The comparison uses accuracy, macro-F1, parameter counts, and inference latency. For interpretability, the authors train region-specific TCNs (e.g., upper body only), apply zero-based occlusion analysis, and examine gradient-based saliency from G-TSG to assess which body regions are most informative for different emotions and how various interpretability methods align or differ.
AI.result: The graph-based G-TSG model attains the best average performance, but the TCN-Base model comes within 1.58 points in accuracy and 1.25 points in macro-F1 while using about 79.18% fewer parameters and achieving roughly 12.5× lower inference latency. Interpretability analyses show that upper-body motion alone is a strong cue for emotion recognition, that the diagnostic value of different body regions changes across emotions, and that region importance patterns differ depending on whether they are derived from region-specific models, occlusion, or gradient saliency.
AI.conclusion: Lightweight TCNs can serve as efficient alternatives to more complex graph-based models for body-based emotion recognition, delivering comparable performance with far lower computational cost. In addition, by examining body regions through multiple interpretability methods, the study provides practical insights into which motion cues matter most and highlights that different interpretability techniques reveal complementary aspects of model behavior.

title: Phonetic forced alignment for low-resource language varieties: Model training and evaluation on Chengdu Mandarin
summary: Phonetic forced alignment is a key technique in phonetic research, yet existing alignment systems lack specialized models for low-resource language varieties. We address this by training text-dependent and text-independent aligners for Chengdu Mandarin using a 17-hour corpus and a custom G2P dictionary. We trained a text-dependent GMM-HMM model (Chengdu-MFA) and fine-tuned a pretrained audio encoder on frame classification with Chengdu-MFA's pseudo label for text-independent alignment (Chengdu-FC). Evaluation on an expert-annotated test set show that both methods significantly outperform Standard Mandarin baselines. Chengdu-MFA reduced average phone boundary differences by 31.8%, while Chengdu-FC achieved a 61.2% reduction. This work establishes a practical bootstrapping pipeline for developing accurate aligners for under-resourced varieties without labor- and time-intensive manual annotation.

AI.tldr: The paper develops and evaluates phonetic forced alignment models tailored for Chengdu Mandarin, showing large accuracy gains over Standard Mandarin baselines and proposing a practical pipeline for building aligners for under-resourced language varieties.
AI.motivation: Existing phonetic forced alignment tools are mainly trained on well-resourced standard varieties (e.g., Standard Mandarin) and perform poorly on low-resource regional varieties like Chengdu Mandarin. Creating high-quality aligners usually requires extensive manual phonetic annotation, which is expensive and time-consuming. The authors aim to provide accurate alignment for Chengdu Mandarin while avoiding large-scale manual labeling, and to offer a generally applicable method for building aligners for similar low-resource varieties.
AI.method: They collect a 17-hour Chengdu Mandarin speech corpus and build a custom grapheme-to-phoneme (G2P) dictionary. First, they train a conventional text-dependent GMM-HMM alignment model (Chengdu-MFA) using this data, in the Montreal Forced Aligner framework. Then they use Chengdu-MFA to generate pseudo phone-boundary labels and fine-tune a pretrained audio encoder on a frame-level classification task, yielding a text-independent aligner (Chengdu-FC) that can perform alignment without explicit transcripts. Both systems are evaluated against baselines that rely on Standard Mandarin models.
AI.result: On an expert-annotated Chengdu Mandarin test set, both Chengdu-MFA and Chengdu-FC substantially outperform Standard Mandarin alignment baselines. Chengdu-MFA reduces the average phone boundary error by 31.8%, while the Chengdu-FC frame-classification-based aligner achieves a 61.2% reduction in boundary differences, demonstrating that pseudo labels from a basic model can effectively bootstrap a more powerful, text-independent aligner.
AI.conclusion: The study shows that accurate forced alignment for low-resource varieties like Chengdu Mandarin can be achieved without costly manual boundary annotation, by combining a modest amount of variety-specific data, a custom G2P lexicon, and a bootstrapped pipeline from a text-dependent GMM-HMM model to a fine-tuned text-independent neural aligner. This approach can be generalized to other under-resourced language varieties, providing a practical path toward high-quality phonetic tools in diverse linguistic settings.

title: Auditing Provenance Sensitivity in LLM Agent Action Selection
summary: LLM agents choose tools and arguments from context that mixes user requests, tool outputs, retrieved records, memory, and untrusted text. Evidence can be relevant without being authorized to determine a decision, so a correct action need not be grounded only in permitted evidence. We introduce a target-specific authorization audit that labels context factors separately for each tool and argument target. Its primary test holds the task, proposition, position, and policy fixed while changing only the proposition's source authority. We then test behavior when valid evidence is weakened and use context-subset interactions as a secondary localization diagnostic. Across 450 controlled next-action tasks and multiple open-weight LLM families, trusted and untrusted variants produce different actions in 5.4 percent of competing cases versus 1.7 percent of supporting cases. Under controlled degradation, unauthorized competition is retained in a full-correct, mixed-error, clean-correct pattern in 2.4 percent of comparisons, with a 95 percent confidence interval from 2.1 to 3.0 percent. These are controlled stress-set rates, not deployment prevalence. The models respond to textual source-authority cues, but this does not prevent untrusted evidence from influencing their actions.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how large language model (LLM) agents can be influenced by untrusted or unauthorized context information when choosing tools and arguments, and proposes a method to audit whether their actions are improperly grounded in such evidence.
AI.motivation: LLM agents operate in complex contexts with mixed information sources: user input, tool outputs, retrieved data, memory, and arbitrary text. Some pieces of evidence, while relevant, are not authorized to ground decisions (e.g., untrusted sources, policy-prohibited data). Existing evaluations focus on correctness or high-level alignment, but not on whether the *source* of the evidence that drives actions is authorized. There is a need for a systematic way to test whether models improperly rely on untrusted evidence when making tool calls and action choices.
AI.method: The authors introduce a target-specific authorization audit framework. For each tool and each argument position, they label context factors (pieces of information) according to whether they are authorized to influence that specific target. The primary experimental manipulation holds the task, proposition, position, and policy constant while varying only the *source authority* of the proposition (trusted vs untrusted). They then compare model actions when valid evidence is weakened or removed, assess whether the model instead uses untrusted evidence, and use interactions between different context subsets as a secondary diagnostic to localize which inputs are driving behavior. They run 450 controlled next-action tasks across multiple open-weight LLM families to quantify the effect.
AI.result: In these controlled settings, when trusted and untrusted variants of the same evidence compete, models choose different actions in 5.4% of competition cases, versus only 1.7% when the variants support the same action (indicating sensitivity to source authority but not immunity to unauthorized influence). Under controlled degradation of valid evidence, in 2.4% of comparisons (95% CI: 2.1–3.0%), models retain a pattern where unauthorized, competing evidence still drives their action even when the trusted signal is weakened (full-correct, mixed-error, clean-correct pattern). These rates are measured on stress-test sets rather than real-world deployment distributions.
AI.conclusion: LLM agents do pay attention to textual cues about the trustworthiness and authority of information sources, but such cues are insufficient to prevent untrusted or unauthorized evidence from affecting their tool choices and next actions. The proposed authorization-audit methodology reveals a non-trivial rate of unauthorized influence in controlled stress scenarios, suggesting that ensuring proper evidence authorization requires more than just communicating source trust levels in context.

title: Capital Markets LLM Reliability Score (CM-LRS): From Plausible to Bankable
comment: 23 pages. Resubmission of submit/7557765, which expired due to an arXiv system bug (confirmed in support ticket AH-199019); the overfull-box correction requested by moderators has been applied. Original submission held in moderation since 14 May 2026. Therefore, request priority review/approval
summary:
In capital-markets workflows the question is rarely whether a large language model can produce a fluent draft, but whether the draft is bankable: defensible in front of a counter-party or a regulator, with the documents in hand. Existing methods address parts of that gap: open-domain QA benchmarks reward surface accuracy, and finance benchmarks (FinanceBench, FinQA, ConvFinQA) advance document-grounded and numerical QA but evaluate at the question-answer layer rather than the workflow outputs practitioners defend.
  We introduce CM-LRS, a Capital Markets LLM Reliability Score, evaluating outputs at the workflow-output layer across seven dimensions: factual accuracy, evidence traceability, numerical consistency, workflow completeness, source discipline, decision usefulness, and reviewability/auditability. Each is scored 0-5 against a rubric anchored on signals reviewers in regulated settings use; the aggregate is tunable to the workflow.
  We demonstrate CM-LRS on five workflows (DCM transaction-terms extraction, precedent retrieval, issuer profile synthesis, M&A transaction-comparable reasoning, ECM transaction-terms extraction) over public SEC EDGAR filings, a public UK takeover release, and fictional synthetic supplements, scoring four models against four independent LLM judges spanning three model families.
  Three findings. First, the frontier closed-source models cluster within 0.22 points on four-judge averaged CM-LRS (Sonnet 4.6 = 4.31, Opus 4.7 = 4.30, GPT-5.5 = 4.09); all four judges place the open-weights baseline (Llama 3.3 70B = 3.15) last. Second, that gap concentrates on retrieval (2.23) and synthesis (2.15), not extraction (0.84). Third, Decision Usefulness shows the widest cross-model dispersion of any dimension (4.0 points on issuer profiling) and top-tier inter-judge agreement (mean r = 0.52).
  Plausibility is cheap. Bankability is the bar.

AI.tldr: A new reliability scoring framework (CM-LRS) is proposed to evaluate whether LLM-generated outputs in capital-markets workflows are genuinely “bankable,” going beyond surface QA accuracy.
AI.motivation: In capital markets, the key issue is not whether LLMs can generate fluent text but whether their outputs are reliable enough to be defended to counterparties or regulators with documents in hand. Existing open-domain and finance-specific benchmarks mainly test QA accuracy, not the overall workflow outputs that practitioners must stand behind. There is a need for an evaluation method aligned with real-world, regulated workflows and the criteria human reviewers actually use.
AI.method: The authors design CM-LRS (Capital Markets LLM Reliability Score), a rubric-based evaluation framework that scores workflow-level outputs along seven dimensions relevant to regulated financial practice: factual accuracy, evidence traceability, numerical consistency, workflow completeness, source discipline, decision usefulness, and reviewability/auditability. Each dimension is scored from 0–5 using rubrics anchored in real reviewer signals, and the aggregate score can be tuned per workflow. They apply CM-LRS to five prototypical capital-markets workflows using real SEC EDGAR filings, a UK takeover release, and synthetic supplements, and evaluate four different LLMs. Four independent LLM-based judges from three model families provide the scores.
AI.result: Frontier proprietary models achieve similar high CM-LRS scores and outperform an open-weights baseline. Specifically, Sonnet 4.6, Opus 4.7, and GPT-5.5 cluster tightly with four-judge average scores around 4.09–4.31, while Llama 3.3 70B trails at 3.15. The performance gap is largest on retrieval and synthesis tasks and smallest on extraction tasks. Among the seven dimensions, Decision Usefulness exhibits the greatest spread between models (up to 4.0 points for issuer profiling) yet maintains relatively strong agreement across judges (mean correlation r = 0.52).
AI.conclusion: LLMs that produce plausible-sounding answers are not necessarily reliable enough for high-stakes capital-markets work. CM-LRS offers a structured, workflow-level way to measure “bankability” of LLM outputs in regulated financial contexts, revealing nuanced differences across models, especially in retrieval, synthesis, and decision usefulness. The framework emphasizes that genuine deployment readiness in finance hinges on reliability and defensibility, not mere fluency or surface accuracy.

title: Auditing Evidence Use in Medical LLM Diagnosis
summary: Medical LLMs are often evaluated by whether they select the correct diagnosis, but diagnostic accuracy alone does not show whether the model used the case evidence appropriately. We present a behavioral audit of evidence use in medical diagnosis. For each case, we decompose patient information into evidence units, score candidate diagnoses under controlled evidence subsets, and mine low-order interactions in diagnostic margins. Because medical evidence is diagnosis-relative, the audit separates interaction discovery from failure assignment: large or negative interactions can reflect plausible differential diagnosis, while suspicious interactions require robustness checks and clinical review. We evaluate five open-weight LLMs on DDXPlus, CupCase, and MedCase. Across datasets, faithful support and differential conflict or cancellation account for most interaction strength, showing that many evidence interactions are clinically plausible rather than failures. In a DDXPlus-focused blinded five-reviewer 130-item enriched review sample, invalid or shortcut-like cases concentrate in negated or absent findings and clinically local evidence. These results show that accuracy can hide candidate evidence-use failures and motivate role-aware audits for medical LLM evaluation.

AI.tldr: They propose a new behavioral audit method to check how medical LLMs actually use clinical evidence, not just whether they pick the right diagnosis.
AI.motivation: Diagnostic accuracy alone can be misleading because a model might guess the right diagnosis without properly using the patient evidence. Evaluators need a way to see if models reason with evidence in clinically appropriate ways.
AI.method: They break each clinical case into discrete evidence units, evaluate model diagnoses under different controlled subsets of evidence, and compute diagnostic margins to mine low-order interactions among evidence items. They distinguish clinically plausible interactions (e.g., supporting differentials) from suspicious ones, then apply robustness checks and blinded clinical review to the latter.
AI.result: On three datasets (DDXPlus, CupCase, MedCase) with five open-weight LLMs, they find that most evidence interactions are consistent with faithful clinical reasoning or reasonable differential diagnoses. However, an enriched sample review on DDXPlus reveals that problematic, shortcut-like behavior is concentrated around negated/absent findings and narrowly local clinical cues.
AI.conclusion: Accuracy alone can conceal how models misuse or shortcut evidence. Their evidence-interaction audit framework exposes these issues and supports more nuanced, role-aware evaluation of medical LLMs, highlighting specific evidence patterns where failures cluster.

title: Word meaning co-determines vowel-inherent spectral change. A corpus-based investigation of conversational Mandarin
summary: This study investigates vowel-inherent spectral change (VISC) in spontaneous conversational Mandarin. Using the generalized additive model and word embeddings from distributional semantics, we show that, when controlling for variables such as vowel duration, gender, speaker identity, co-articulation, vowel identity, and utterance position, vowel formant trajectory dynamics have word-specific components that are tied to their meaning in context: The F1 and F2 trajectories of words can be predicted from their contextualized embeddings with an accuracy that substantially exceeds a permutation baseline. Challenging modular cognitive models of speech production, these results indicate that, words' semantics co-determine the fine details of their articulation.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that subtle vowel quality trajectories in natural Mandarin speech are systematically influenced by word meaning, not just phonetic context.
AI.motivation: To test whether fine-grained articulation (vowel-inherent spectral change) is purely a phonetic/segmental phenomenon or whether it also encodes lexical-semantic information in spontaneous speech, thereby probing the validity of modular models of speech production.
AI.method: They analyze spontaneous conversational Mandarin using generalized additive models (GAMs) to model F1/F2 formant trajectories over time, and incorporate contextualized word embeddings from distributional semantics. They control for duration, gender, speaker, co-articulation, vowel identity, and utterance position, then test whether word-specific formant dynamics can be predicted from semantic embeddings, comparing performance against a permutation baseline.
AI.result: Even after controlling for major phonetic and contextual factors, F1 and F2 trajectory shapes show systematic word-specific components that are statistically predictable from contextualized semantic embeddings, with prediction accuracy clearly above a permutation-based chance level.
AI.conclusion: Vowel formant trajectories in spontaneous Mandarin are partially determined by lexical semantics; this semantic-articulatory link challenges strictly modular models of speech production and supports more interactive or integrated views where meaning can influence fine phonetic detail.

title: Code Monitor Red Teaming for Public-Test-Passing Code
summary: Visible tests are a common gate for LLM-generated code, but passing them does not certify specification correctness. We study a deployment-like monitoring problem: after code has passed public tests, can a weaker LLM verifier identify the residual hidden bugs? We introduce Code Monitor Red Teaming, a monitor-red-teaming protocol that fixes a public-check information boundary while varying generator pressure, verifier scaffolding, and weak-to-strong capability. We instantiate it as CodeMonitorBench, spanning function-level, data-science, and workflow code. Across 71,000 generated candidates, 43,677 pass public tests and 23,081 of those fail hidden tests. Weak verifiers improve with scaffolding and model family, but still miss most hidden bugs at 5% false-positive rate. As a robustness stress test, adversarial public-test-overfit pressure lowers verifier AUROC and raises low-FPR miss rates in most cells. A GLM-5.1 verifier recovers part of the gap under the same evidence boundary; an inferability audit shows that remaining misses mix verifier failures with M1 evidence limits.

AI.tldr: They investigate whether weaker LLM-based code verifiers can detect hidden bugs in code that already passes public tests, and introduce a benchmark and protocol showing current verifiers miss most hidden bugs, especially under adversarial conditions.
AI.motivation: Code generated by LLMs is usually gated by visible/public tests, but passing these tests does not guarantee that the code is actually correct with respect to the full specification. In realistic deployment, there may be hidden tests or untested behaviors where bugs remain. The authors want to understand if a weaker LLM verifier, operating after code generation and after public tests are passed, can reliably detect these remaining bugs, and how robust such verification is under adversarial pressure that encourages overfitting to public tests.
AI.method: They define a protocol called Code Monitor Red Teaming that maintains a strict boundary on what information is visible to the verifier (only what public tests provide), while systematically varying: (1) the pressure on the code generator to overfit public tests, (2) the scaffolding and prompting used for the verifier, and (3) weak-to-strong setups where a weaker verifier tries to judge code potentially written by a stronger model. They instantiate this protocol as CodeMonitorBench, a benchmark covering multiple types of code: function-level problems, data-science tasks, and workflow scripts. They generate about 71k candidate code solutions, run them through public tests, and then through hidden tests, and then evaluate how often LLM verifiers can flag the ones that secretly fail hidden tests, under different configurations and an ROC-style evaluation, including low false-positive regimes. They also test robustness by applying adversarial pressure to cause public-test overfitting, and evaluate a specific stronger verifier model (GLM-5.1) and perform an inferability audit to analyze remaining misses.
AI.result: From 71,000 generated code candidates, 43,677 pass public tests but among these, 23,081 still fail hidden tests, revealing a large residual bug population. LLM verifiers improve when given better scaffolding and when using better model families, but even the best ones still miss most of the hidden bugs at a 5% false-positive rate. Under adversarial conditions designed to push the generator to overfit public tests, verifier performance degrades: AUROC drops and the miss rate at low false-positive thresholds increases in most tested configurations. A GLM-5.1-based verifier can recover some of the lost performance while obeying the same information boundary, but the inferability audit indicates that remaining missed bugs are due to both limitations of the verifier and intrinsic limits of what can be inferred from public-test evidence alone.
AI.conclusion: Passing visible tests is far from sufficient to guarantee correctness of LLM-generated code, and current LLM-based verifiers—especially under realistic low false-positive constraints—fail to catch most of the hidden bugs that remain. Their Code Monitor Red Teaming protocol and CodeMonitorBench benchmark expose these weaknesses and show that adversarial public-test overfitting further harms monitoring robustness. While stronger verifiers like GLM-5.1 can partially close the gap, there are fundamental limits imposed by the information available from public tests, implying that more powerful verification methods or richer evidence than just public tests are needed for reliable deployment monitoring of LLM-generated code.

title: MemTools: A Unified Research Framework for Interoperable Agent Memory
summary: While memory systems are essential for agent architectures, pervasive architectural fragmentation restricts systematic research. Existing implementations typically couple different stages of the memory lifecycle, entangle evaluation logic with specific datasets, and provide limited support for the management of heterogeneous memory types. We introduce MemTools, an interoperability research framework that decouples memory system components from their underlying deployment environments. MemTools standardizes the memory lifecycle through declarative data contracts, enabling the interchangeable assembly of components across different systems. It orthogonally separates benchmark datasets from execution protocols to facilitate controlled assessments. Furthermore, MemTools provides a unified computational interface for coordinating symbolic, neural, and multimodal memory representations within a shared runtime. Empirical evaluations on cross-system component integration, evaluation protocol reconfiguration, and heterogeneous memory coordination demonstrate that MemTools enables systematic isolation and analysis of memory design variables. These findings suggest that MemTools provides a practical and extensible infrastructure for advancing principled research on agent memory.

AI.tldr: MemTools is a framework that standardizes and decouples components of memory systems for agents, enabling interoperable, modular experimentation and evaluation across heterogeneous memory types and environments.
AI.motivation: Research on memory systems for agent architectures is hindered by fragmented implementations: components of the memory lifecycle are tightly coupled to specific environments, evaluation logic is bound to particular datasets, and heterogeneous memory types (symbolic, neural, multimodal) are hard to coordinate and compare systematically. This fragmentation prevents clean isolation of design choices and reproducible, cross-system experiments.
AI.method: The authors design MemTools, an interoperability research framework with three main ideas: (1) formalizing the memory lifecycle via declarative data contracts so that components can be plugged together independent of the deployment environment; (2) factorizing benchmarks into separate datasets and execution protocols, allowing the same data to be tested under different evaluation procedures and vice versa; and (3) offering a unified computational interface and shared runtime to coordinate symbolic, neural, and multimodal memory representations. They then empirically test MemTools on tasks involving cross-system component integration, altering evaluation protocols, and coordinating heterogeneous memories.
AI.result: Using MemTools, the authors successfully integrate memory components across previously incompatible systems, reconfigure evaluation protocols independently of datasets, and orchestrate different memory representation types within a shared runtime. These experiments show that MemTools can systematically vary and isolate specific design variables in memory systems that were previously entangled.
AI.conclusion: MemTools provides a practical, extensible infrastructure that decouples memory components, standardizes their interfaces, and unifies heterogeneous memory types. This enables more principled, systematic experimentation on memory design for agents and lays groundwork for more rigorous, comparable research in agent memory architectures.

title: Is Deep Research Reliable? Misleading Knowledge Induces False Conclusions
summary: Deep Research agents extend LLM-based assistants into long-horizon workflows involving planning, retrieval, evidence synthesis, and report generation, yet their reliability in open information environments remains underexplored. A key concern is whether apparently credible but factually misleading knowledge encountered in such environments can propagate through these workflows and be adopted as false conclusions in final reports. To study this failure mode, we introduce MisKnow-Agent, a framework for constructing and validating misleading knowledge for Deep Research tasks. MisKnow-Agent generates misleading instances with controllable authority levels and styles, yielding 5,933 quality-controlled instances built on DeepResearch Benchmark tasks. Extensive experiments across open-source and closed-source Deep Research agents show that even limited exposure to misleading knowledge can induce false-conclusion adoption in final reports, revealing a broad reliability vulnerability in current Deep Research agents. Although search-enabled verifier models consistently identify the retained instances as misleading during focused corpus validation, the same instances can still be adopted during long-horizon research, revealing a disconnect between focused verification and workflow-level evidence use. Finally, we evaluate pre- and post-research defenses, both individually and in combination, finding that all three configurations mitigate but do not fully prevent false-conclusion adoption. Our findings suggest that reliable Deep Research requires evidence verification and correction capabilities at both the model and framework levels, beyond improvements in planning, retrieval, evidence integration, or report-generation abilities.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes how deep research AI agents are vulnerable to adopting misleading but credible-looking information, and proposes MisKnow-Agent, a framework that constructs such misleading knowledge to systematically test and stress these agents.
AI.motivation: As LLM-based deep research agents are increasingly used for complex, long-horizon tasks involving planning, retrieval, and synthesis, there is an underexplored risk: in open information environments, they may encounter information that looks credible but is actually misleading. The community lacks systematic ways to create, control, and validate such misleading inputs, and to study how they propagate through multi-step research workflows into final conclusions. The paper aims to expose and understand this reliability failure mode and to guide the design of more robust deep research agents.
AI.method: The authors introduce MisKnow-Agent, a framework for generating and validating misleading knowledge instances tailored to Deep Research tasks. It constructs misleading content with controllable levels of apparent authority and stylistic variations, and validates them using verifier models to ensure they are plausibly credible yet factually wrong. Using DeepResearch Benchmark tasks, they build a dataset of 5,933 high-quality misleading instances. They then run extensive experiments with both open-source and closed-source deep research agents, exposing them to varying degrees of this misleading content, and measure whether and how it gets adopted into final reports. They further compare agents’ performance when using focused search-enabled verifiers versus full long-horizon research workflows, and systematically evaluate pre-research and post-research defense mechanisms individually and in combination.
AI.result: The experiments show that even limited exposure to carefully constructed misleading knowledge frequently causes deep research agents to adopt false conclusions in their final reports, demonstrating a broad and significant reliability vulnerability. Search-augmented verifier models can reliably identify these misleading instances as such when evaluated in isolation (focused corpus validation), but the same instances are still incorporated as evidence and lead to errors in full end-to-end research workflows. This highlights a disconnect between pointwise verification capability and actual workflow-level evidence use. Additionally, multiple defense strategies—applied before research, after research, or both—reduce but do not eliminate the rate of false-conclusion adoption.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that current deep research agents are systematically vulnerable to plausible but misleading information in open environments, and that simply improving planning, retrieval, evidence integration, or report generation is insufficient. Reliable deep research requires integrated evidence verification and correction mechanisms both within the underlying models and at the framework or system level, operating throughout the workflow rather than only as a focused or final check. MisKnow-Agent and its dataset provide a testbed to stress-test and improve such systems.

title: Anti-Periodic Positional Encoding: Möbius Boundary Conditions Make In-Context Retrieval Reliable
summary: Möbius RoPE is a rotary positional encoding built on the anti-periodic frequency ladder $θ_i=π(2i+1)/N$: every rotation plane advances by an odd multiple of $π$ across the training context, so the positional holonomy is $-1$ and the two ends of the sequence are deterministically coupled through a closed-form Dirichlet "dipole"; to our knowledge this is the first anti-periodic boundary condition in positional encoding. We verify the theory numerically to $\sim 10^{-6}$ and pretrain 48 models spanning six 160M-class and three 410M-class arms (2B FineWeb-Edu tokens each; the hybrid arm puts Möbius frequencies on 25% of heads). Hybrid perplexity is unchanged (29.66 vs. 29.72), but needle-in-a-haystack retrieval becomes reliable: $90.3\pm5.7\%$ versus $63.3\pm31.4\%$ at context 512 ($n=6$ seeds), observed worst seed 86% versus 14%, robust variance tests $p=0.013$-$0.029$ (unadjusted), recurring at 410M (Levene $p=0.040$). Matched controls isolate the mechanism: an aperiodic ladder in the same frequency band reproduces none of the effect, and a periodic (holonomy $+1$) ladder only a fraction. Swapping trained models' frequency table back to standard RoPE (weights frozen) collapses retrieval, with damage concentrated on far needles: trained models depend on this long-range geometry. A NoPE arm is even more reliable at short context but pays a 13% perplexity tax and extrapolates worst; only the anti-periodic hybrid pairs baseline perplexity with a high reliability floor. The effect is scoped to single-needle retrieval within the training window; a one-line frequency swap thus provides zero-cost insurance against the retrieval seed lottery.

AI.tldr: Proposes Möbius RoPE, an anti-periodic rotary positional encoding that couples sequence endpoints and greatly stabilizes needle-in-a-haystack retrieval without hurting perplexity, offering a simple, zero-cost way to avoid retrieval “bad seeds.”
AI.motivation: Standard RoPE and other positional encodings can leave long-range retrieval performance highly variable across random seeds, especially for needle-in-a-haystack tasks, even when perplexity is similar. The authors want a positional scheme that (1) preserves baseline perplexity, (2) improves reliability of long-range single-token retrieval, and (3) requires only trivial code changes and no extra compute. They investigate whether changing the topology / boundary conditions of the frequency ladder in RoPE—specifically to an anti-periodic one—can enforce deterministic coupling of sequence ends and thus stabilize retrieval behavior.
AI.method: They introduce Möbius RoPE, defined via an anti-periodic frequency ladder θ_i = π(2i+1)/N so each rotation plane accumulates an odd multiple of π over the training context, giving holonomy −1. This induces a deterministic Dirichlet “dipole” that couples the two sequence ends. They (1) verify the theoretical holonomy and endpoint coupling numerically to ~1e−6; (2) pretrain 48 transformer models across multiple “arms”: standard RoPE baselines, fully Möbius, hybrid models with Möbius frequencies on 25% of heads, periodic and aperiodic control ladders in the same band, and a NoPE (no positional encoding) variant; (3) train 160M- and 410M-class models on 2B FineWeb-Edu tokens per arm; and (4) evaluate perplexity and needle-in-a-haystack (NIAH) retrieval at context 512 across multiple random seeds, using variance tests (Levene and related) to assess robustness and to isolate the impact of the anti-periodic geometry.
AI.result: Hybrid Möbius RoPE matches baseline perplexity (29.66 vs. 29.72) while dramatically improving NIAH reliability at context 512: mean retrieval 90.3±5.7% vs. 63.3±31.4% for baseline RoPE over 6 seeds, with the worst hybrid seed at 86% compared to 14% for baseline. Robust variance tests yield p-values between ~0.013 and 0.029 (unadjusted), and the effect recurs in 410M-class models (Levene p=0.040). Controls show that an aperiodic ladder in the same frequency band has no effect, and a periodic ladder (holonomy +1) recovers only part of the benefit. Swapping trained Möbius models back to standard RoPE frequencies with frozen weights causes NIAH performance to collapse, especially for far-position needles, indicating the models rely on the anti-periodic long-range geometry. A NoPE variant achieves even better short-context reliability but suffers a ~13% perplexity increase and worse extrapolation. Only the anti-periodic hybrid achieves baseline perplexity and a high reliability floor.
AI.conclusion: Changing RoPE’s frequency ladder to an anti-periodic, Möbius-like structure yields a strong, robust improvement in long-range single-needle retrieval reliability without harming perplexity or requiring extra compute. The improvement is specific to within-training-window, single-needle NIAH tasks and depends on the holonomy −1 geometry, as shown by controls and post-hoc frequency swaps. As a practical implication, a simple one-line change to introduce Möbius frequencies on a subset of heads acts as “zero-cost insurance” against seed-dependent failures in retrieval-heavy use cases, while retaining standard LM quality as measured by perplexity.

title: Source-Prior-Driven Selective Adaptation for Efficient Diffusion Model Finetuning
summary: Fine-tuning large diffusion models for new domains or styles involves a trade-off: improving target-specific generation often degrades the pretrained model's broad generative capability. Existing full and parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods typically handle this trade-off only implicitly. In this work, we propose a novel source-prior-driven selective adaptation method to efficiently fine-tune diffusion models, achieving a favorable trade-off. Our method relies on two key observations: (1) the loss of general generative capability is highly inconsistent across pretrained parameters, and (2) parameters that have a relatively small impact on the model's general generative capability remain structurally inconsistent across layers and parameter types. Motivated by these observations, we first learn a static mask to explicitly identify parameters better suited for downstream adaptation, and then construct structured update strategies for the selected subset. Experiments show that our method achieves a better adaptation-retention trade-off than existing strong baselines.

AI.tldr: They propose a masking-based, selective fine-tuning strategy for diffusion models that preserves general generative ability while adapting to new domains/styles.
AI.motivation: Fine-tuning diffusion models to new targets often improves performance on the new domain but harms the model’s broad, pretrained generative capabilities. Existing methods (full fine-tuning and parameter-efficient approaches) only implicitly manage this trade-off and may over-adapt important parameters, causing forgetting. There is a need for an approach that explicitly selects which parameters to adapt to balance specialization and retention.
AI.method: They analyze pretrained diffusion model parameters and observe that some parameters are more critical than others for maintaining general generative capabilities, and that low-impact parameters are not localized to neat structures (layers or parameter types). Based on these observations, they first learn a static mask that marks parameters whose change least harms the source/prior (general generative behavior). Then they apply structured update strategies only to this selected subset of parameters when fine-tuning on the new domain or style, leading to source-prior-driven selective adaptation.
AI.result: Empirical experiments demonstrate that the proposed selective adaptation method better preserves the pretrained model’s general generative ability while still effectively adapting to new domains/styles, outperforming strong existing baselines on the adaptation–retention trade-off.
AI.conclusion: By explicitly identifying and updating only those parameters that minimally impact the model’s general generative prior, fine-tuning can achieve a more favorable balance between adaptation to new domains and retention of broad capabilities. The source-prior-driven masking and structured updates offer an efficient and effective strategy for diffusion model fine-tuning compared to existing approaches.

title: An Evaluation Framework for Structured Audio Captions Validated by Controlled Perturbations
summary: Recent advancements in automated audio captioning (AAC) have shifted from monolithic sentence generation toward structured formats that explicitly disentangle distinct acoustic and semantic properties. However, evaluating this heterogeneous data remains a significant challenge. Existing caption metrics focus on flat textual outputs and fail to reliably assess multimodal attributes. To bridge this gap, we propose a multi-axis evaluation framework tailored for structured audio descriptions. Building on the AudioCards dataset, we evaluate outputs across five orthogonal axes: tag-sets, descriptions, logical reasoning, numeric measurements, and spectral profiles. Our approach combines Large Language Model (LLM) judges to capture semantic nuance with deterministic computational metrics to precisely measure acoustic deviations. To rigorously validate the reliability of this framework, we introduce a controlled perturbation testing protocol that injects typed, graded errors into groundtruth annotations. Our results demonstrate that this framework successfully distinguishes meaning-preserving paraphrases from genuine semantic and acoustic corruptions.

AI.tldr: A new evaluation framework for structured audio captions that measures multiple aspects of meaning and acoustics.
AI.motivation: Automated audio captioning is moving toward richer, structured descriptions that separate different acoustic and semantic aspects, but existing evaluation metrics are designed for flat sentences and cannot reliably judge these multimodal, structured outputs.
AI.method: They propose a multi-axis evaluation framework based on the AudioCards dataset that scores system outputs along five independent axes: tag-sets, free-form descriptions, logical reasoning, numeric measurements, and spectral profiles. The framework integrates LLM-based judges to assess semantic nuances and rule-based computational metrics to quantify acoustic mismatches. They also design a controlled perturbation protocol that systematically injects specific, graded errors into reference annotations to test the sensitivity and reliability of their metrics.
AI.result: Experiments with the perturbation protocol show that the framework can clearly distinguish harmless paraphrases from true semantic and acoustic errors, and that the different axes respond appropriately to the corresponding types and degrees of perturbations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed multi-axis evaluation scheme is a reliable way to assess structured audio descriptions, overcoming limitations of traditional text-only caption metrics and providing more fine-grained, semantically and acoustically grounded evaluation for AAC systems.

title: Traceable Scholarship: Page Anchors and Ariadne's Thread for Humanistic Inquiry in the Age of Generative AI
comment: 33 pages, 8 tables. This paper proposes a normative and infrastructural framework for traceable, AI-assisted humanistic research and presents an auditable Kant case study
summary: Generative AI lets large language models produce scholarly-looking text within seconds, yet fluency does not equal valid explanation. The deepest risk is not factual error alone but the appearance that an explanation is already established without clear sources, page numbers, editions, or evidence. We liken the page anchor to Ariadne's thread: within the labyrinth of generative fluency, it is the thread that leads the scholar back to the source. This paper proposes Traceable Scholarship as the minimum normative condition for AI-assisted humanistic research, situating it across the three revolutions of knowledge infrastructure: print, digital, and generative AI. We introduce page anchors, dual page numbers, citation-first generation, NO_EVIDENCE, human verification, four-level compliance, and Scope Contract, and present AIH-Infra as a three-layer reference implementation: Contexture (document structuring), Open WebUI AIH-Infra (traceable knowledge base), and AIH-Infra MCP Server (agent gateway). A case study on a 29-volume Kant Akademie-Ausgabe knowledge base illustrates how traceability supports retrieval correction, evidence grading, and judgment downgrading. Traceability is not a software feature; it is the condition under which humanistic research can remain public and refutable in the age of generative AI.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that for AI-assisted humanities research to remain valid and criticizable, every AI claim must be traceably anchored to precise sources, and it proposes both principles and infrastructure to guarantee this traceability.
AI.motivation: Generative AI can quickly produce convincing but untraceable and potentially unfounded explanations. In the humanities, where arguments must be grounded in specific passages, editions, and page numbers, this threatens the core norm of publicly checkable and refutable scholarship. The authors aim to define a normative standard—Traceable Scholarship—that prevents AI from smuggling in unverified claims while still leveraging its capabilities.
AI.method: The authors conceptualize "Traceable Scholarship" as a minimum normative condition for AI-assisted research and situate it historically across three knowledge infrastructure revolutions: print, digital, and generative AI. They then design and describe a framework that enforces traceability: page anchors (fine-grained source references), dual page numbers (for different editions/formats), citation-first text generation, explicit NO_EVIDENCE markers when sources are lacking, human verification workflows, a four-level compliance scheme, and a Scope Contract defining what the AI is allowed to claim. They implement these ideas in a three-layer reference system, AIH-Infra, consisting of Contexture (for structuring documents and anchors), Open WebUI AIH-Infra (a traceable knowledge base for interaction), and AIH-Infra MCP Server (an agent gateway). Finally, they apply this stack to a large Kant edition as a case study.
AI.result: They obtain a working reference implementation (AIH-Infra) that can interact with a 29-volume Kant Akademie-Ausgabe corpus under traceability constraints. The system can attach page-level anchors to explanations, distinguish between supported and unsupported content via NO_EVIDENCE, allow retrieval correction when sources are misaligned, grade evidence strength, and downgrade the confidence of judgments when traceability is weak. The case study demonstrates that AI can assist in complex humanistic work while keeping every claim tethered to checkable text passages.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that traceability—fine-grained, verifiable linkage from AI-generated claims back to specific sources—is not just a technical add-on but a foundational norm for legitimate AI-assisted humanities scholarship. Without such traceable infrastructures and workflows, generative fluency risks producing unaccountable, unrefutable pseudo-scholarship. With them, AI can be integrated into research practices while preserving the public, critical, and refutable character of humanistic inquiry.

title: Token Budget Saturation and Mechanistic Early Detection of Reasoning Non-Convergence in Chain-of-Thought Models
summary: Chain-of-thought reasoning models such as DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B exhibit a bimodal convergence pattern: generations either terminate within a token budget (converged) or exhaust it without reaching a conclusion (non-converged). We characterize this phenomenon empirically, showing that converged generations achieve 90.3% accuracy on AIME 1983-2024 while non-converged ones achieve only 6.6%, with an overall convergence rate of 62.0%. We then ask whether this outcome is detectable early in the thinking chain using internal model representations. Training linear probes on hidden-state activations at token positions 50-300, we find that layer-20 activations at token 150 achieve AUC 0.608 (+-0.080, 5-fold CV), reliably above chance even at token 50. Activation probes consistently outperform behavioral baselines derived from token entropy and repetition statistics. A sweep-level permutation test yields p=0.063 (100,000 permutations), consistent with a modest signal that our sample size cannot confirm at conventional thresholds. These findings suggest that convergence fate is partially encoded in intermediate representations well before the generation ends, opening a path toward early-exit inference and adaptive compute allocation.

AI.tldr: The paper studies why chain-of-thought reasoning models sometimes fail to finish their reasoning within a token budget, finding that this “convergence fate” can be partially predicted early from internal activations.
AI.motivation: Chain-of-thought LLMs often either cleanly finish reasoning or ramble until the token limit without reaching an answer. This bimodality harms reliability and compute efficiency, and it’s unclear whether the eventual outcome is detectable early enough to allow adaptive computation or early stopping.
AI.method: Empirically analyze a chain-of-thought model (DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B) on AIME 1983–2024. Label generations as converged or non-converged based on whether they reach a conclusion within a token budget. Measure accuracy per group. Train linear probes on hidden states at various layers (esp. layer 20) and token positions (50–300) to predict convergence outcome, and compare against behavioral baselines (entropy, repetition). Evaluate using cross-validated AUC and a permutation test across the probe sweep.
AI.result: Converged samples are both common (62% of generations) and very accurate (90.3%), whereas non-converged generations are near-random (6.6%). Linear probes on intermediate activations can predict convergence fate above chance (e.g., AUC ~0.61 at token 150 in layer 20, still >0.5 as early as token 50), outperforming entropy/repetition baselines. However, the global permutation test yields only marginal significance (p≈0.063), suggesting limited power given the sample size.
AI.conclusion: Whether a reasoning trajectory will converge is at least partially encoded in the model’s intermediate representations well before completion. This opens the possibility of early-exit mechanisms and adaptive compute allocation, although stronger evidence and larger-scale studies are needed to robustly exploit this signal.

title: OPOD: On-Policy Omni Distillation
summary: Omni-modal models can handle text, images, and audio in one system, but improving all of these abilities together remains difficult. Training a single model on pooled multimodal data often fails to match models specialized for individual modalities. On-policy distillation (OPD) offers a way to combine such specialists: the student generates a response, and a teacher evaluates that same response, so the student learns directly from behaviors it actually produces. Yet using several teachers can introduce competing guidance and improve one modality at the expense of another. We present On-Policy Omni Distillation (OPOD), which routes each student response to the matching text, image, or audio teacher. OPOD keeps teacher guidance only on tokens where the teacher assigns a higher probability than the student, adjusts the influence of each modality teacher independently during training, and asks the routed teacher to assess both the final answer and whether the reasoning supports the correct answer. Across twelve benchmarks and three backbone sizes, OPOD achieves the best average score at every scale, reaching 70.8, 51.7, and 46.2 and exceeding the strongest comparator by 2.1, 1.8, and 1.7 points. On the 30B model, it outperforms both the base model and a counterpart post-trained jointly on pooled multimodal data on all twelve benchmarks, and ranks first or second on eleven even when the individual specialists are included. The specialists are discarded after training, leaving one deployable omni-modal model. These results show that coordinating modality-specific teachers is an effective way to improve a shared model while maintaining cross-modal balance.

AI.tldr: They propose OPOD, a distillation framework that coordinates multiple modality-specific teachers (text, image, audio) to train a single omni-modal model and achieve balanced, state-of-the-art performance across modalities.
AI.motivation: Omni-modal models that handle text, images, and audio in a single system struggle to match the performance of separate, modality-specialized models. Naively training on pooled multimodal data or using standard distillation often causes trade-offs, where improving one modality degrades another. There is a need for a training method that can exploit strong specialist models while preserving balanced performance across modalities.
AI.method: They extend on-policy distillation (OPD) to the multimodal setting via On-Policy Omni Distillation (OPOD). During training, the student model generates a response to a multimodal input. OPOD then routes this response to the appropriate modality-specific teacher (text, image, or audio). The teacher evaluates the same student-generated tokens, and the student learns only from tokens where the teacher assigns higher probability than the student. OPOD separately controls the strength of each modality teacher, and each routed teacher evaluates both the final answer and whether the intermediate reasoning is consistent with the correct answer, providing richer supervision.
AI.result: Across 12 benchmarks and three backbone sizes, OPOD-trained models achieve the best average performance at each scale, with average scores of 70.8, 51.7, and 46.2, outperforming the strongest baseline by 2.1, 1.8, and 1.7 points, respectively. On a 30B backbone, the OPOD model surpasses both the base model and a jointly post-trained model on pooled multimodal data on all 12 benchmarks and ranks first or second on 11 benchmarks even when compared directly against the modality specialists themselves.
AI.conclusion: Coordinating multiple modality-specific teachers in an on-policy fashion is an effective way to train a single omni-modal model. OPOD preserves cross-modal balance while improving overall performance, yielding a deployable unified model and demonstrating that specialist-guided, routed distillation can overcome the limitations of naive multimodal joint training.

title: When Trivia Is Not Trivial: Everyday Knowledge Failures in Multilingual LLMs
summary: Quiz rooms, trivia nights, and quiz shows challenge human knowledge across a wide range of topics, from canonical facts to everyday culture. In this paper, we examine whether large language models (LLMs) can perform competitively in such settings, using quiz-style questions to test them on both common and niche topics. We introduce TriviaRoomQA, a multilingual benchmark designed to evaluate everyday, culturally grounded, and long-tail knowledge across 288 topics. The benchmark contains 3,300 parallel multiple-choice questions in six European languages and additional 5,340 French-only questions for a more fine-grained case study. We evaluate 30 open-weight LLMs from European, Asian, and North American providers, covering models from 7 to 70B parameters. We find that models are strong on knowledge-intensive topics such as history, geography, and mathematics, but substantially weaker on everyday popular-culture topics such as celebrities, music, movies, and news. Moreover, model performance varies across languages even for the same underlying questions, suggesting that access to factual knowledge is not always language-independent. In sum, our dataset and experiments demonstrate an important knowledge gap which is not captured by existing academic-based saturated benchmarks.

AI.tldr: They create TriviaRoomQA, a multilingual quiz-style benchmark to test LLMs on everyday and long-tail cultural knowledge, and show that models lag on popular-culture and language-specific questions despite doing well on academic knowledge.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM benchmarks focus heavily on academic, canonical, or saturated knowledge domains and largely overlook everyday, culturally grounded, and long-tail trivia-style knowledge that quiz rooms and trivia games test. The authors want to understand whether LLMs can compete with humans in such real-world quiz settings and to expose blind spots not visible in standard benchmarks.
AI.method: They construct TriviaRoomQA, a multilingual multiple-choice benchmark with 3,300 parallel questions across six European languages and an additional 5,340 French-only questions, covering 288 topics spanning both canonical and niche everyday knowledge. They then systematically evaluate 30 open-weight LLMs from different regions (Europe, Asia, North America) and sizes (7–70B parameters) on these questions, comparing performance across topics and across languages for the same underlying items.
AI.result: LLMs achieve relatively high accuracy on knowledge-intensive academic-like topics such as history, geography, and mathematics. However, they perform notably worse on everyday popular-culture domains like celebrities, music, movies, and recent news. They also observe significant variation in performance across different languages even when the underlying question content is parallel, indicating that models’ access to factual knowledge depends on language. These findings reveal knowledge gaps not apparent from strong performance on conventional benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: TriviaRoomQA exposes that current LLMs are not yet competitive with humans in quiz-style everyday and long-tail cultural knowledge, particularly in popular-culture topics and in some languages. The benchmark highlights a substantial and previously under-measured knowledge gap and shows that factual knowledge in LLMs is not uniformly language-independent. This suggests that training and evaluation should better cover culturally grounded, multilingual, and non-academic domains to build more broadly knowledgeable models.

title: Representing Entity Importance in AI Knowledge Systems: A Dual-Signal Framework of Audience Evaluation and Structural Authority
summary:
AI knowledge systems require representations of entity importance for retrieval, recommendation, evidence selection, and knowledge-intensive reasoning. Yet importance is often reduced to a single score derived from either human response or graph structure. Such compression may discard distinctions that matter when an AI system must choose among entities for different tasks. This study introduces an interpretable dual-signal representation in which each entity is characterized by an audience-evaluation dimension and a structural-authority dimension.
  The framework is evaluated using movie entities as an empirical validation domain. IMDb non-commercial datasets provide a rating-based audience ranking, Wikidata supports entity alignment, and English Wikipedia hyperlinks form the knowledge network on which PageRank estimates structural authority. Experiments on 482 entities and 13,690 directed relationships reveal a statistically significant but weak association between the two dimensions (Spearman rho = 0.2275, p < 0.001). Their overlap is only 10% in the top 10 and 34% in the top 100, while entity-level divergence occurs in both directions.
  The results show that audience evaluation and structural authority are non-redundant signals and should not automatically be collapsed into a single scalar notion of importance. The contribution is not a new ranking algorithm or learned embedding, but a minimal knowledge-representation framework and an empirical test of its dimensional necessity. The findings support task-aware AI knowledge systems that preserve distinct importance signals before applying context-specific selection or aggregation.

AI.tldr: They propose representing entity importance with two distinct dimensions (audience evaluation and structural authority) instead of a single scalar score, and empirically show these are weakly correlated and non-redundant using movie data.
AI.motivation: Many AI systems need to rank or select entities (e.g., for retrieval or recommendation), but current practices typically compress importance into a single score (from user ratings or graph centrality). This likely discards meaningful distinctions between how entities are valued by audiences vs. how central they are in knowledge networks. The authors aim to test whether these signals are actually distinct and both necessary.
AI.method: They define a dual-signal representation of importance, with one dimension capturing audience evaluation and another capturing structural authority. As a testbed, they use movies: IMDb non-commercial datasets supply audience ratings; Wikidata is used to align entities across sources; and English Wikipedia’s hyperlink graph is used to compute PageRank as structural authority. On a set of 482 movie entities and 13,690 directed links, they compute and compare the two importance dimensions using rank correlation and overlap of top-k lists.
AI.result: They find only a weak but statistically significant association between the two importance dimensions (Spearman rho ≈ 0.23, p < 0.001). Overlap between entities highly ranked on each dimension is limited: only 10% overlap in the top 10 and 34% in the top 100, with divergences where some entities rate highly in audience evaluation but not structural authority and vice versa.
AI.conclusion: Audience evaluation and structural authority form complementary, non-redundant signals of entity importance. Collapsing them into a single scalar importance score is unjustified and can obscure task-relevant differences. The main contribution is a simple, interpretable two-dimensional representation and an empirical demonstration that such dimensionality is necessary. This supports designing task-aware AI knowledge systems that maintain multiple importance signals and only combine them in context-specific ways.

title: RUMBA: Russian User Memory Benchmark
summary: The ability to handle long-term memory in LLMs is becoming increasingly critical, yet existing benchmarks remain English-centric and rely on aggregate retrieval metrics, failing to capture interactions between long-range context, temporal information, and reasoning. To address this, we introduce RUMBA (Russian User Memory BenchmArk) - a new benchmark for long-term conversational memory that provides a fine-grained taxonomy of memory-centric question types and a unified methodology accounting for semantic type, session scope, temporal reasoning, and the explicitness of temporal expressions. RUMBA consists of timestamped user-assistant dialogues with QA pairs requiring retrieval, combination, and reasoning across sessions. While designed for Russian, we also provide an aligned English subset under the same methodology. We evaluate contemporary memory systems and long-context models, and show how RUMBA serves as a diagnostic tool to analyze model behavior across benchmark slices and identify strengths and failure modes of different memory mechanisms.

AI.tldr: RUMBA introduces a Russian-focused benchmark for long-term conversational memory in LLMs, with fine-grained categories of memory questions and temporal reasoning, plus an aligned English subset, and shows its usefulness for diagnosing model strengths and weaknesses.
AI.motivation: Existing long-term memory benchmarks for LLMs are largely English-only and use coarse aggregate retrieval metrics, which do not adequately capture how models manage long-range context, temporal information, and reasoning over time in dialogue.
AI.method: The authors build RUMBA, a benchmark of timestamped user–assistant conversations with associated QA pairs. They define a detailed taxonomy covering memory-centric question types, semantic memory types, cross- vs within-session scope, temporal reasoning requirements, and explicit vs implicit temporal expressions. They then evaluate several contemporary memory systems and long-context LLMs on these data.
AI.result: RUMBA provides challenging test cases where models must retrieve, combine, and reason over information across multiple sessions and temporal references. Experimental evaluation shows differentiated performance across benchmark slices, revealing specific strengths and failure modes of various memory mechanisms and long-context models.
AI.conclusion: RUMBA is a diagnostic benchmark for long-term conversational memory, especially for Russian, with an aligned English subset. It enables more nuanced analysis of how LLMs handle temporal and cross-session memory, moving beyond aggregate retrieval scores and highlighting directions for improving memory systems and long-context modeling.

title: SciExplore: Evaluating Autonomous Agents from Scientific Navigation to Information Integration
summary: Scientific research involves complex information-seeking and reasoning workflows across heterogeneous sources. However, existing benchmarks primarily emphasize general-domain retrieval or static scientific question answering, and therefore fail to assess key capabilities required in realistic scientific research workflows. We introduce SciExplore, a benchmark designed to evaluate scientific information-seeking and reasoning capabilities of LLMs and agents. SciExplore comprises four task types covering 103 expert-curated tasks across more than ten scientific disciplines: scientific database navigation, ambiguous literature retrieval, missing reference completion, and cross-source structured knowledge synthesis, which probe progressively higher-level abilities from entity-level reasoning and document-level identification to evidence-level grounding and domain-level synthesis. We evaluate over ten state-of-the-art LLMs and autonomous agents on SciExplore, revealing substantial performance gaps with performance degrading sharply as task complexity increases and extremely low accuracy on the most challenging structured synthesis tasks. These results highlight significant limitations of current models and agents in realistic scientific information-seeking scenarios.

AI.tldr: SciExplore is a benchmark to test how well LLMs and agents perform realistic, multi-step scientific information-seeking and reasoning tasks, showing that current systems struggle as complexity increases.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks mostly cover simple, general-domain retrieval or static QA and do not capture the complex, multi-source, multi-step workflows scientists actually use. There is a need to systematically evaluate LLMs and agents on realistic scientific research tasks, including navigation of domain databases, handling ambiguity, filling missing references, and synthesizing evidence across sources.
AI.method: The authors build SciExplore, a benchmark with 103 expert-curated tasks from over ten scientific disciplines. They define four task types: (1) scientific database navigation, (2) ambiguous literature retrieval, (3) missing reference completion, and (4) cross-source structured knowledge synthesis. These tasks are designed to probe progressively higher capabilities, from identifying entities and documents to grounding claims in evidence and performing domain-level synthesis. They then evaluate more than ten state-of-the-art LLMs and autonomous agents on all tasks.
AI.result: Across models and agents, performance drops sharply as task complexity increases, with especially poor accuracy on the most demanding structured knowledge synthesis tasks. Even leading systems show substantial gaps on realistic scientific information-seeking workflows.
AI.conclusion: SciExplore uncovers significant limitations of current LLMs and agents in realistic scientific research settings, particularly for complex, synthesis-oriented tasks. The benchmark provides a more faithful way to assess and drive progress on scientific information-seeking and reasoning capabilities.

title: What, Where, and How: Disentangling the Roles of Task, Language, and Model in Code Model Representations
comment: 16 pages, 11 figures, 6 tables. Code: https://github.com/piotrwilam/Atlas2x2 ; dataset: https://huggingface.co/datasets/piotrwilam/Atlas2x2
summary:
Do independently trained language models come to represent the same thing in the same way? We answer for code, extending a recently introduced concept-circuit extraction method to a 2x2 design -- Python and Rust crossed with Qwen2.5-Coder-7B and DeepSeek-Coder-V1-6.7B -- and measuring a complete inventory of grammatical concepts (58 Python, 57 Rust) identically in all four cells: the smallest design that separates what depends on the task, the language, and the model.
  The answer splits into three parts. What earns dedicated circuitry is set by the task: the models agree on which concepts receive circuits (Spearman $ρ$ = 0.638 for Python, 0.673 for Rust, both p < $10^{-7}$). Where those circuits sit is set by the model: Qwen processes concepts in a late band (~L17-19), DeepSeek at L6-7, for both languages. How circuits grow across layers is also set by the model: Qwen gives its atomic concepts an early spike that DeepSeek does not. "Are circuits universal?" thus has no single answer: yes for What, no for Where and How -- universality is a property of representational content, not of computational organisation.
  None of this structure was fixed in advance. The agreement could have landed anywhere between independence and identity; it lands at $ρ\approx 0.65$. Rust constructs receive 2-3x more concept-specific circuitry than their Python equivalents, in both models. Both models share neurons between the languages (6/7 and 7/7 paired constructs), DeepSeek 1.94x more than Qwen -- a direction no prior result predicts. And Qwen binds nine keywords of Rust's type-and-trait machinery into one tight neuron cluster (Jaccard 0.535 vs null 0.112, p < 0.001), a semantic dimension invisible in surface syntax. Ablation and linear probes confirm the circuits are functional.
  All claims are scoped to this 2x2; whether the per-model profile predicts a third model is the designed next test.

AI.tldr: The paper studies whether independently trained code LLMs represent the same grammatical concepts in the same way, using a 2x2 setup (two models × two languages) and concept-circuit extraction.
AI.motivation: To understand universality vs model-specificity in internal representations of code LLMs: do different models trained on similar tasks converge on the same concepts and implementations, and what is determined by task, language, or architecture?
AI.method: Extend a concept-circuit extraction method to four settings (Python/Rust × Qwen2.5-Coder-7B/DeepSeek-Coder-V1-6.7B). Build a complete inventory of grammatical code concepts for each language, locate their circuits, analyze their layer-wise growth, cross-language neuron sharing, and clustering of related concepts. Use ablations and linear probes to validate functional importance.
AI.result: (1) Which concepts get dedicated circuitry is largely shared across models (ρ≈0.65). (2) Circuit locations and growth patterns differ strongly by model (Qwen: later layers + early spikes; DeepSeek: earlier layers, no early spike). (3) Rust constructs have 2–3× more specific circuitry than Python ones. (4) Substantial neuron sharing across languages, more in DeepSeek. (5) Discovery of a tight neuron cluster encoding Rust type/trait machinery not obvious from syntax. Circuits are shown to be functional.
AI.conclusion: Universality holds for representational content (which grammatical concepts are encoded), but not for computational organization (where and how these circuits are implemented). Task drives which concepts get circuits; model architecture/training drives their placement and growth. Findings are limited to the 2×2 design but suggest per-model profiles that could be predictive for other models.

title: Clustered Edge Intelligence: Beyond Just Convergence of Edge Computing and AI
comment: This is not survey or position paper. 28 Pages, 10 figures, 4 Tables, 108 references, Under review, submitted to Information Fusion
summary: We are moving from an information age to the age of intelligence. A decade, or possibly less than that, data will not be the gold anymore rather the derived intelligence out of the data and the information we posses from the edge of the network. Existing Edge Intelligence research focuses mainly on two directions: using AI for edge resource management and deploying lightweight AI models on edge devices. However, existing edge computing research lacks an intelligence-centric framework in which derived intelligence is treated as a first-class, independently manageable entity that can be described, discovered, observed, shared, reused, and dynamically clustered across heterogeneous edge devices and applications. To address these research gaps, we introduced Clustered Edge Intelligence, a visionary intelligence-centric approach. The aim of CEI is to make intelligence a shareable and reusable first-class entity that can be independently represented, discovered, observed, exchanged, and managed across the distributed edge-cloud continuum. We present a three layer CEI architecture and examine enabling technologies and research dimensions, including intelligence inventories, semantic knowledge representation, communication, discoverability, observability, lifecycle automation, clustering mechanisms, marketplaces, interoperability, and standardization.

AI.tldr: Proposes Clustered Edge Intelligence (CEI), an intelligence-centric framework that treats derived intelligence as a first-class, shareable, and reusable entity across edge-cloud environments.
AI.motivation: Current edge intelligence work either uses AI to manage edge resources or runs lightweight AI models on edge devices, but lacks a unified framework where the *intelligence itself* (inferences, models, knowledge) can be described, shared, discovered, and managed as an independent asset.
AI.method: Conceptual and architectural: introduces the notion of Clustered Edge Intelligence and proposes a three-layer architecture. It identifies required enabling technologies and research directions (e.g., intelligence inventories, semantic representations, communication protocols, discovery, observability, lifecycle management, clustering, marketplaces, interoperability, and standardization).
AI.result: Defines the CEI concept, outlines a three-layer architecture, and systematically categorizes key technical components and research challenges needed to realize an intelligence-centric edge-cloud ecosystem.
AI.conclusion: Treating derived intelligence as a first-class, shareable, and reusable entity at the edge can transform edge computing from data-centric to intelligence-centric, but it requires new architectures, mechanisms, and standards, which the CEI framework begins to articulate.

title: Artificial Epanorthosis: Why large language models overuse a classical rhetorical figure, and how to mitigate it
summary: A rhetorical figure that Cicero and Quintilian catalogued two thousand years ago reappears, systematically, in the text of large language models: epanorthosis, the self-correction of the specimen «This is not a course. It is a journey of transformation». This essay argues that the overuse is a trained disposition, driven mainly by a training distribution rich in promotional prose and by preference tuning (RLHF) that rewards confident, emphatic phrasing; the left-to-right nature of generation is an amplifier rather than the root cause.
