AI.method: The authors build on recent matrix-based logic programming semantics to create a differentiable, matrix-encoded representation of logical rules and constraints. They propose a unified encoding where both rules and constraints are captured in a single matrix, and explore different fuzzy logic t-norms for differentiable inference, analyzing their gradient properties. They design neurosymbolic architectures where neural network outputs are grounded one-to-one to logical atoms rather than treated as soft probability distributions over atoms. They then run controlled experiments on variants of MNIST to measure the impact of these architectural and semantic choices on shortcut behavior.
AI.result: Experiments on MNIST variants show that enforcing a one-to-one grounding between neural outputs and logical atoms significantly reduces both constraint satisfaction shortcuts and cognition shortcuts compared with prior approaches that rely on soft probabilistic distributions. The empirical comparison of different t-norms reveals differences in gradient flow that affect training behavior, and the unified matrix encoding proves effective for jointly handling rules and constraints. Overall, the results demonstrate that specific architectural and semantic design choices can substantially mitigate shortcut reasoning in neurosymbolic systems.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that shortcut reasoning in neurosymbolic systems is not an unavoidable artifact of combining neural and symbolic components, but is heavily influenced by how logical semantics and neural outputs are coupled. A matrix-based differentiable logic programming framework with unified rule/constraint encoding and one-to-one grounding of neural predictions to logical atoms can significantly reduce both constraint satisfaction and cognition shortcuts. The work highlights that careful architectural design and choice of differentiable logical semantics are crucial for building more reliable and semantically faithful neurosymbolic models.

title: Identifying Good Rules for Efficient SAT Encodings of Single-Constant Multiplication Using Machine Learning
summary: The Single Constant Multiplication problem is a fundamental NP-hard optimization task in hardware design, which seeks to decompose a fixed constant using only additions, subtractions, and bit-shifts. Although dynamic programming methods can produce near-optimal SAT encodings for SCM, their encoding cost remains high for large constants. We propose a neuro-symbolic framework that accelerates SCM SAT encoding by identifying good rules for guiding operator selection during decomposition. Our approach employs a graph neural network model to predict promising operator types from constant decompositions, and exploits the resulting confidence scores to prune no-good choices in the symbolic search. Experimental results on unseen 17-32 bit constants demonstrate one to two orders of magnitude reductions in encoding time, over 97% reduction in memory usage, and an order-of-magnitude decrease in branching, while preserving near-optimal encoding quality in terms of additions. These results show that learning-guided symbolic strategies can significantly improve the scalability and efficiency of SCM encoding. Our code and data are publicly available at: https://github.com/Chufeng-Jiang/SCM_MLDP

AI.tldr: They speed up solving the Single Constant Multiplication (SCM) problem by using a graph neural network to guide a SAT-based encoder, achieving much faster runtimes and lower memory while keeping near-optimal solutions.
AI.motivation: SCM is important in hardware design for implementing constant multipliers with minimal resources, but finding optimal decompositions is NP-hard. Dynamic-programming-based SAT encodings give good, near-optimal solutions but become very slow and memory-hungry for large constants (17–32 bits and beyond). There is a need for more scalable methods that retain high solution quality while reducing encoding cost.
AI.method: They build a neuro-symbolic system where: (1) SCM decompositions are represented as graphs; (2) a graph neural network is trained to predict which operator types (add, subtract, shift variants) are promising during the decomposition process; (3) the GNN outputs confidence scores over operator choices; and (4) a symbolic search / SAT encoder uses these scores to prune low-confidence (likely bad) branches, thereby reducing the search and encoding space while still aiming for near-optimal decompositions.
AI.result: On unseen 17–32 bit constants, their method reduces SAT encoding time by 1–2 orders of magnitude, cuts memory usage by more than 97%, and reduces search branching by about an order of magnitude, all while keeping the number of additions (a key quality metric) near the optimum obtained by DP-based encoders.
AI.conclusion: Learning-guided (GNN-based) heuristics can effectively steer symbolic SCM encoders, dramatically improving scalability and efficiency without sacrificing decomposition quality. This neuro-symbolic approach is promising for large SCM instances and potentially for other combinatorial optimization and hardware synthesis tasks that rely on SAT encodings.

title: Euclid-MCP: A Model Context Protocol Server for Deterministic Logical Reasoning via Prolog
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) excel at natural language understanding and generation but remain unreliable for multi-step logical reasoning, especially in safety-critical or compliance-sensitive domains. Recent neuro-symbolic approaches address this gap by coupling neural models with external symbolic engines, yet most integrations are bespoke and lack a standardized interface for tool-augmented agents. This paper presents Euclid-MCP, an open-source MCP server that provides deterministic logical reasoning via SWI-Prolog. Euclid-MCP introduces Euclid-IR, an engine-agnostic intermediate representation for Horn-clause logic that is human-readable, easy for LLMs to generate, and straightforward to compile into Prolog or alternative backends. The server exposes a compact tool interface that supports a translate-run-inspect-repair loop, enabling LLM clients to delegate inference while retaining full access to proof traces and derivation logs. We evaluate Euclid-MCP on a realistic IT security and compliance use case. Results show that while LLMs alone are sufficient on small knowledge bases, they hallucinate systematically on larger problems, whereas Euclid-MCP delivers exact answers with lower latency and more compact outputs. We argue that semantic RAG is fundamentally unsuited for rule enforcement, and that Euclid-MCP can serve as a stable, shared reasoning substrate for both RAG-based assistants and agentic systems.

AI.tldr: Introduces Euclid-MCP, an MCP server that gives LLMs access to deterministic logical reasoning via Prolog using a new intermediate representation, Euclid-IR.
AI.motivation: LLMs are strong at language tasks but unreliable at multi-step logical reasoning, which is problematic in safety-critical and compliance-sensitive domains. Existing neuro-symbolic integrations with symbolic engines are ad hoc and lack a standard, reusable interface, making it hard to systematically augment LLMs with trustworthy reasoning.
AI.method: Design and implementation of Euclid-MCP, an open-source MCP server wrapping SWI-Prolog. The system defines Euclid-IR, an engine-agnostic Horn-clause logic intermediate representation that LLMs can easily generate. Euclid-IR is compiled into Prolog or other backends. The server exposes a minimal tool interface that supports a translate-run-inspect-repair loop, letting LLMs submit logic, run proofs, inspect traces, and refine rules. The authors evaluate the system on an IT security and compliance reasoning task, comparing pure LLM reasoning to LLM+Euclid-MCP across knowledge base sizes.
AI.result: On small knowledge bases, LLMs alone solve the tasks adequately. As the knowledge base grows, pure LLM reasoning begins to hallucinate and produce incorrect answers. Euclid-MCP, in contrast, gives exact logical answers, with lower latency and more compact responses. The system also exposes proof traces and derivation logs that are not easily obtained from raw LLM reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Euclid-MCP provides a standardized, deterministic logical reasoning substrate for LLM-based systems. The results suggest that semantic RAG is ill-suited for strict rule enforcement, whereas delegating logic to Euclid-MCP yields more reliable and efficient reasoning. The authors propose Euclid-MCP as a shared reasoning layer for both RAG-based assistants and more complex agentic systems, improving trustworthiness in compliance and safety-critical applications.

title: Bound-Founded Semantics for Answer Set Programming with Difference Constraints: Preliminary Report
summary: While the integration of linear constraints has significantly expanded the reach of Answer Set Programming (ASP), existing hybrid solvers often rely on disparate semantic underpinnings that lack a unified logical foundation. We address this gap by introducing a many-sorted variant of the Bound-founded Logic of Here-and-There (HTb), providing a versatile framework capable of characterizing equilibrium models across a wide spectrum of alternative semantics for extensions of ASP with linear constraints. We apply this framework to the setting of difference constraints, focusing on the semantic characterization of clingo[DL]. Central to our approach is the formalization of foundedness for numeric variables. By investigating how different hybrid systems - such as clingo[DL], clingcon, and flingo - justify constraint atoms, we uncover the semantic roots of their varying behaviors. This investigation results in a single, consistent framework that not only formalizes the foundations of current systems like clingo[DL] but also facilitates the rigorous study of program simplifications and the future integration of diverse semantic principles.

AI.tldr: They propose a unified logical framework (a many-sorted version of Bound-founded Logic of Here-and-There) that captures and explains the semantics of ASP systems with linear/difference constraints such as clingo[DL], clingcon, and flingo.
AI.motivation: Hybrid ASP solvers that combine logic programming with linear constraints are widely used, but their semantics are currently given in heterogeneous, tool-specific ways without a single unifying logical foundation. This makes it hard to compare systems, reason about their behavior, justify design choices, and study transformations or optimizations systematically.
AI.method: They extend the Bound-founded Logic of Here-and-There (HTb) to a many-sorted setting, so that different domains (e.g., numeric variables) are handled cleanly. Within this framework they define equilibrium models for programs with linear constraints and, in particular, formalize the notion of foundedness for numeric variables. They then analyze how different existing hybrid ASP systems (clingo[DL], clingcon, flingo) justify constraint atoms and map those behaviors into their unified logical framework, especially focusing on programs with difference constraints and the semantics of clingo[DL].
AI.result: They obtain a single, general semantic framework that can characterize the equilibrium models of several alternative semantics for ASP with linear constraints. They show how clingo[DL] and related systems can be captured within this framework and explain, at a semantic level, why these systems behave differently in the treatment and justification of constraint atoms. The framework also supports reasoning about program simplifications.
AI.conclusion: The many-sorted HTb framework provides a common, rigorous logical foundation for ASP with linear/difference constraints, subsuming and explaining the semantics of current hybrid solvers such as clingo[DL], clingcon, and flingo. This unification clarifies the role of foundedness for numeric variables, illuminates semantic differences among systems, and offers a principled basis for future extensions, optimizations, and the integration of new semantic principles in ASP with constraints.

title: Agentic coding without the cloud: evaluating open-weight large language models on longitudinal data preparation tasks
summary: Large language models (LLMs) and agents are now widely used tools in code development, with data typically sent to third-party cloud-based models. Their adoption in research using personal data is constrained by governance requirements that typically prohibit data transmission to external services. Locally deployable open-weight models offer an alternative since sensitive data never leave the local environment. We introduce an open-source framework for evaluating the efficacy of AI agents powered by open-weight LLMs on one of the most persistent bottlenecks in research on longitudinal population studies: data preparation. The framework comprises: a curated ground-truth dataset (cleaning scripts preparing six sweeps of data from a British cohort study), task definitions encompassing tasks such as category harmonization and multi-wave merging, and automated routines for evaluating the LLM-produced R code and outputted data. We benchmark LLMs across the (consumer grade) deployment spectrum to assess their efficacy in 20 data preparation tasks (creation of 102 variables). Current state-of-the-art, 31-35B parameter models almost saturated our benchmark ("average task completion" up to 87.9%). The performance of open-weight LLMs running on consumer-grade hardware shows promise of a viable path toward AI-assisted data preparation in governance-restricted research settings. Our framework is publicly available at: https://github.com/UCL-ARC/RRBench.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an open-source benchmark and evaluation framework to test how well locally run, open-weight LLM-based agents can handle data preparation for longitudinal population studies, showing that current 31–35B models achieve high task completion and are practical under strict data governance constraints.
AI.motivation: Researchers working with longitudinal population data face strict governance rules that often forbid sending sensitive personal data to external cloud LLMs, while data preparation remains a major bottleneck. There is a need to know whether locally deployable, open-weight LLMs running on consumer hardware can effectively assist in such data preparation tasks without violating governance constraints.
AI.method: The authors build an open-source framework, RRBench, using a British longitudinal cohort study. They curate a ground-truth dataset consisting of cleaning scripts for six data sweeps, define 20 realistic data preparation tasks (e.g., category harmonization, multi-wave merging) involving creation of 102 variables, and implement automated procedures to run AI agents that generate R code, execute it, and evaluate both code and resulting data against the ground truth. They then benchmark a range of open-weight LLMs of different parameter sizes that can be deployed on consumer-grade hardware.
AI.result: State-of-the-art open-weight LLMs in the 31–35B parameter range achieved high performance, with average task completion up to 87.9% across the 20 data preparation tasks, nearly saturating the benchmark. Smaller or less capable models performed worse, but overall the results indicate strong potential for open-weight LLMs in this setting.
AI.conclusion: Open-weight LLMs, especially larger models that still run on consumer-grade hardware, can effectively automate a substantial portion of data preparation work in longitudinal population studies while complying with governance that prohibits sending data to external cloud services. The RRBench framework and benchmark provide a reproducible, publicly available way to evaluate and compare such models for AI-assisted data preparation in restricted research environments.

title: A New Well-Supported Semantics for Description Logic Programs
summary: Description logic programs are a powerful formalism for combining rules with ontologies. The well-supported semantics for description logic programs ensures that no answer sets rely on cyclic dependencies. Most popular semantics for logic programming have this property of well-supportedness. We recognize two limitations of the current well-supported semantics for DL programs: its increased computational complexity for the consistency problem and its lack of a reduct transformation characterization.  In this work, we present a new semantics which evaluates ontological atoms more strictly than the current semantics. This keeps the complexity of its consistency problem NP-complete, rather than increasing it to the second level of the polynomial hierarchy. Additionally, we identify a syntactic class of description logic programs for which our new semantics is equivalent to the current semantics. We characterize our semantics using a fixpoint operator and a reduct-based transformation. Our new semantics is a strict subset of the current well-supported semantics, so it maintains the prior notion  of well-supportedness while inducing its own stricter notion. We prefer our new notion of well-supportedness due to its similarities with logic programming.

AI.tldr: They propose a stricter well-supported semantics for description logic programs that keeps reasoning complexity at NP-complete and provides a reduct-based characterization, forming a strict subset of the existing well-supported semantics.
AI.motivation: Existing well-supported semantics for description logic (DL) programs avoid cyclic justifications but have two drawbacks: higher computational complexity for checking consistency (up to the second level of the polynomial hierarchy) and the absence of a reduct-based characterization commonly used in logic programming. There is a need for a semantics that preserves well-supportedness while being computationally simpler and closer in style to standard logic programming semantics.
AI.method: The authors define a new semantics for DL programs that evaluates ontological atoms more strictly than the current well-supported semantics. They formally specify this semantics, prove its properties, and show that its consistency problem remains NP-complete. They also identify a syntactic fragment of DL programs for which the new semantics coincides with the existing one. Moreover, they provide two characterizations of the semantics: one via a fixpoint operator and another via a reduct-based transformation, analogous to the Gelfond–Lifschitz reduct in logic programming.
AI.result: The new semantics is shown to be a strict subset of the existing well-supported semantics: every model under the new semantics is a model under the old one, but not vice versa. The consistency checking problem under the new semantics is NP-complete, improving on the higher complexity of the prior semantics. For a particular syntactic class of DL programs, the new and old semantics are equivalent, so the stricter evaluation does not lose solutions there. The authors also establish fixpoint and reduct-style characterizations of their semantics.
AI.conclusion: The proposed semantics refines the notion of well-supportedness for DL programs while restoring NP-complete consistency checking and providing a reduct-based characterization familiar from logic programming. Because it is a strict subset of the original well-supported semantics and better aligned with established logic programming intuitions and tools, the authors argue that their new notion of well-supportedness is preferable in practice, especially for applications where complexity and reduct-style reasoning are important.

title: The Boundaries of Automation: A Theory of Persistent Human Participation
summary: The rapid progress of AI has intensified the long-standing pursuit of automation: replacing human participation with algorithms wherever possible. Implicit in this pursuit is the assumption that humans remain in the loop only because current AI systems are not yet sufficiently capable. This paper challenges that assumption. Rather than asking how far automation can extend, we ask where its conceptual limits lie and argue that human participation may persist even with highly capable AI systems for three distinct reasons. Technical or complementarity grounds arise when humans contribute capabilities or perspectives unavailable to AI. Normative or developmental grounds arise when participation itself is valuable for human agency or learning. Most importantly, emergence grounds arise from target emergence: in some activities, the target is not fully specified in advance but instead emerges through the interaction itself. In these cases, human participation is not merely a means of improving execution but is constitutive of the target being produced. Human--AI co-construction, understood as the joint production of outcomes by humans and AI systems, is therefore not simply a temporary response to imperfect AI, but a persistent feature of activities whose objectives emerge through participation. This perspective has important implications for the limits of automation and for the design, evaluation, and ethics of future AI systems.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that, even with highly capable AI, human participation will remain essential in certain activities because goals and values sometimes emerge through interaction rather than being fully specifiable in advance.
AI.motivation: The authors are motivated by the dominant narrative in AI that sees automation as a trajectory toward fully replacing human involvement wherever possible, assuming humans are only present because AI is not yet capable enough. They aim to question this assumption and clarify where the true conceptual limits of automation lie.
AI.method: The paper uses conceptual and philosophical analysis rather than empirical experiments. It distinguishes three types of grounds for persistent human participation—technical/complementarity, normative/developmental, and emergence grounds—and develops the idea of target emergence to explain when and why human involvement is constitutive of the activity’s goals.
AI.result: The authors identify and articulate three distinct reasons why human participation may persist despite very capable AI: (1) technical or complementarity grounds, where humans provide perspectives or skills AI cannot; (2) normative or developmental grounds, where participation itself is important for human agency and learning; and (3) emergence grounds, where the goals of an activity emerge through participation and thus cannot be pre-specified or fully automated.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that human–AI co-construction should be seen as a stable and central feature of many activities, not a temporary stage before full automation. This has significant implications for how we understand the limits of automation and how we design, evaluate, and regulate future AI systems—emphasizing collaboration, goal emergence, and human agency rather than full replacement.

title: How Rules Represent Causal Knowledge: Causal Modeling with Probabilistic Logic Programming
summary:
Pearl famously argues that causal knowledge enables the prediction of intervention effects. By contrast, purely descriptive knowledge supports only conclusions drawn from observations. His theory of causality, however, is developed exclusively within Bayesian networks and causal models. Consequently, it is largely restricted to acyclic causal relationships, and transferring its ideas to other formalisms risks misinterpretation or inconsistency.
  This paper brings Pearl's approach to causality into probabilistic logic programming (PLP). To this end, such programs are aligned with philosophical foundations established in prior work that do not rely on temporal notions; that is, all relevant events are assumed to occur simultaneously. A formal causal semantics for these programs, together with a notion of intervention and an implementation, is proposed. It is shown that this semantics coincides with the P-log semantics for stratified ProbLog programs, while the two may differ in the non-stratified case and for other PLP formalisms.

AI.tldr: The paper extends Pearl's causal framework to probabilistic logic programming, defining a causal semantics and interventions, and relating it to existing PLP semantics.
AI.motivation: Pearl’s causal theory is powerful for predicting intervention effects but is mainly formulated for acyclic Bayesian networks and structural causal models, which limits its direct applicability to other formalisms like probabilistic logic programming. There is a need to adapt and ground these causal notions within PLP so that causal reasoning and interventions can be studied in that setting without misinterpretation.
AI.method: The authors conceptually align probabilistic logic programs with atemporal philosophical foundations of causation, assuming all relevant events occur simultaneously. They then formally define a causal semantics for PLP, introduce a corresponding notion of interventions, and implement this framework. Finally, they compare the induced semantics with existing semantics such as P-log, especially on stratified ProbLog programs versus non-stratified cases or other PLP formalisms.
AI.result: The proposed causal semantics is shown to coincide with P-log semantics for stratified ProbLog programs. However, differences emerge in the non-stratified case and in other PLP formalisms, indicating that the new semantics both captures and extends existing approaches to causality in PLP.
AI.conclusion: Pearl-style causality can be coherently embedded into probabilistic logic programming via an atemporal causal semantics that supports interventions. This semantics is compatible with P-log for stratified ProbLog but diverges in more complex settings, clarifying and extending the landscape of causal reasoning in PLP.

title: OpenForgeRL: Train Harness-native Agents in Any Environment
summary: Modern AI agents rely on elaborate inference harnesses such as Claude Code, Codex, and OpenClaw to drive multi-turn reasoning, tool use, and access to external systems. While powerful, these complex harnesses also make agents hard to train end-to-end with open infrastructure, whose SFT/RL stacks cannot natively express stateful, multi-process harness inference. To address this, we present OpenForgeRL, an open-source framework for training harness-based agents end-to-end in diverse environments. OpenForgeRL achieves this with a lightweight proxy that serves the harness's model calls while recording them as training data for a standard RL codebase (e.g., veRL), and a Kubernetes orchestrator that runs each rollout in its own remote container, together enabling training on any harness in any environment at scale. By decoupling training and inference, OpenForgeRL allows researchers to easily train, study, and improve agents directly in the real harnesses and environments they are deployed with. We validate our framework across diverse, complex harnesses and environments, spanning tool/claw-based agents and multimodal GUI browser- and computer-use agents. Using only hundreds to a few thousand tasks, OpenForgeClaw reaches 31.7 pass^3 and 55.9 pass@3 on ClawEval and 33.7 on QwenClawBench. OpenForgeGUI reaches 37.7 on OSWorld-Verified, 63.0 on Online-Mind2Web, and 72.3 on WebVoyager. Both outperform open baselines of similar size on nearly all benchmarks, and in the GUI setting match or surpass models several times larger. Beyond benchmarks, we analyze how harness choice (e.g., ZeroClaw, OpenClaw, Codex) and RL shape agent behavior. We find that some harnesses are substantially harder to learn than others, and that RL improves agentic reliability, such as self-verification, tool coverage, and completing multi-step plans, though critical abilities such as error recovery remain weak.

AI.tldr: OpenForgeRL is an open-source framework that enables end-to-end RL training of complex, harness-based AI agents (code, tools, GUI/web agents) using standard RL stacks by proxying harness model calls and orchestrating rollouts in Kubernetes.
AI.motivation: Modern agent systems use sophisticated, stateful inference harnesses (e.g., Claude Code, Codex, OpenClaw) for multi-turn reasoning and tool use, but these harnesses are difficult to train end-to-end with typical SFT/RL infrastructures that assume simple, stateless model calls. This creates a gap between research training setups and real deployed agents. The authors want an open, scalable way to directly train agents inside their real harnesses and environments.
AI.method: Introduce OpenForgeRL, which separates training from inference via: (1) a lightweight proxy that sits in front of the harness to serve all model calls while logging them as trajectories compatible with a standard RL codebase (e.g., veRL), and (2) a Kubernetes-based orchestrator that runs each rollout inside its own isolated remote container. This design allows plug-and-play use with arbitrary harnesses and environments (tool-based, code-based, multimodal GUI/browser agents) while using existing RL algorithms and infrastructure.
AI.result: They train agents across several complex harnesses and environments. For tool/claw-based agents (OpenForgeClaw) they obtain 31.7 pass^3 and 55.9 pass@3 on ClawEval and 33.7 on QwenClawBench. For GUI/browser agents (OpenForgeGUI) they achieve 37.7 on OSWorld-Verified, 63.0 on Online-Mind2Web, and 72.3 on WebVoyager. These models outperform open-source baselines of comparable size on most benchmarks and, in GUI tasks, match or surpass performance of much larger models.
AI.conclusion: OpenForgeRL makes it feasible to train and study harness-based agents end-to-end, in the same complex tool and GUI environments they are deployed in, using standard RL stacks. Experiments show consistent performance gains over similar-sized baselines and competitiveness with larger models. Analysis of different harnesses and the impact of RL shows that some harnesses are considerably easier to learn; RL notably improves reliability traits such as self-verification, tool usage coverage, and multi-step plan completion, but important capabilities like robust error recovery are still lacking and remain open challenges.

title: ICAE-Bench: Evaluating Coding Agents as Interactive Project Builders
summary:
The recent emergence of vibe-coding workflows is changing what coding agents are expected to do. Instead of merely completing code under fully specified instructions, agents are increasingly expected to transform incomplete product intent into working software by combining various abilities including planning, requirement clarification, tool use, debugging, and repository-level construction. Yet existing benchmarks have not fully caught up with this shift, evaluating agents on static, fully specified tasks.
  In this paper, we introduce ICAE-Bench, a benchmark for evaluating coding agents under interactive project-building settings. The basic idea is to start from a fuzzy product requirement, simulating the dynamic paradigm with an automated User Agent. To make this setting both realistic and evaluable, ICAE-Bench introduces three key designs. First, to avoid the ambiguity of unconstrained fuzzy requirements, each task derives ambiguity from a precise real open-source repository with executable behavior. Second, to ensure high-quality and reproducible user simulation, ICAE-Bench grounds interaction through User Agent Data, allowing the User Agent to reveal hidden constraints without inventing new requirements or leaking implementation artifacts. Third, to evaluate open-ended repositories fairly, ICAE-Bench uses standardized black-box tests together with multi-dimensional diagnostics, including functional correctness, semantic and API similarity, structural fidelity, design quality, and interaction quality.

AI.tldr: ICAE-Bench is a new benchmark that evaluates coding agents in realistic, interactive project-building scenarios starting from fuzzy product requirements.
AI.motivation: Existing coding benchmarks assume static, fully specified tasks and thus fail to assess agents’ emerging role in vibe-coding workflows, where they must interpret vague intent, plan, clarify requirements, use tools, debug, and build repository-level software.
AI.method: ICAE-Bench simulates interactive software construction by (1) deriving fuzzy requirements from precise open-source repositories with executable behavior, (2) using a controlled User Agent driven by pre-collected User Agent Data to reveal hidden constraints without adding new requirements or leaking implementations, and (3) evaluating solutions with standardized black-box tests plus multi-dimensional diagnostics (functional correctness, semantic/API similarity, structural fidelity, design quality, interaction quality).
AI.result: The benchmark defines a realistic and reproducible interactive coding environment in which different coding agents can be fairly compared across multiple dimensions of project-building performance, from correctness to design and interaction quality.
AI.conclusion: ICAE-Bench aligns evaluation with modern coding workflows by providing a structured, interactive benchmark that captures how well coding agents translate fuzzy product intent into working software, supporting more meaningful comparison and development of advanced coding agents.

title: Logic Programming Semantics for Causal Processes
summary: Motivated by challenging modelling issues in the life sciences, we investigate the relationship between logic programming semantics and the eventual states of causal processes compatible with those logic programs. More precisely, we show that while stable models of positive logic programs correspond to the eventual states of processes commencing from a neutral state and continuing undisturbed indefinitely, supported models describe the eventual states reachable from arbitrary starting points. This also contributes to the discussion of the appropriate semantics for logic programming as a causal rule language, adding a temporal perspective to recent interpretations of the stable and supported model semantics from an explanatory viewpoint of causality.

AI.tldr: The paper links logic programming semantics (stable and supported models) to the long-term behavior of causal processes they represent.
AI.motivation: To address difficult modeling problems in life sciences that involve causal processes and their eventual states, and to clarify how different logic programming semantics capture these processes.
AI.method: The authors conceptually and formally analyze how stable and supported model semantics of positive logic programs correspond to eventual states of associated causal processes, considering different initial conditions and temporal evolution.
AI.result: They show that stable models correspond to eventual states of processes starting from a neutral initial state and evolving without disturbance, whereas supported models capture eventual states reachable from any arbitrary initial state.
AI.conclusion: The work connects logic programming semantics with causal process behavior and arguments about causality, providing a temporal perspective that helps justify and distinguish stable vs supported models as causal rule semantics.

title: BasketEvent: Understanding Who Did What and When in Basketball Videos
summary: Comprehensive basketball video understanding requires resolving not only what event occurs, but also who is responsible and when the key evidence appears. However, exist- ing methods typically treat spatial perception and semantic recognition as isolated tasks, failing to ground events to individual players or pinpoint their temporal boundaries within complex collective dynamics. To bridge this gap, we introduce BasketEvent, a player- centric basketball event understanding dataset curated from real NBA broadcasts. In BasketEvent, event labels are grounded to the responsible players, and a manually an- notated subset of 1,000 samples with precise event intervals is provided to evaluate tem- poral evidence localization. Based on this data, we propose PlayNet, a player-centric reasoning framework that maps basketball videos to player-level event predictions with temporal evidence. Concretely, PlayNet tracks key entities, associates player identities, and reasons about events by modeling player-player, player-ball, and global court inter- actions, while aggregating sparse temporal evidence via gated pooling. Extensive experi- ments demonstrate that PlayNet significantly outperforms representative video-level and crop-based baselines, proving the superiority of player-centric modeling for fine-grained sports video understanding. Our data, code, and models will be made publicly available.

AI.tldr: They build a new NBA broadcast dataset and a player-centric model (PlayNet) that jointly detect basketball events, identify responsible players, and localize temporal evidence, outperforming prior baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing basketball video understanding methods usually separate spatial perception (who/where) from semantic recognition (what event), so they can’t reliably tell which player caused an event or when the critical evidence occurs within complex team interactions.
AI.method: They curate BasketEvent, a dataset from real NBA broadcasts where events are labeled at player level and 1,000 clips have manual temporal event intervals. They then propose PlayNet, which tracks key entities and player identities, models interactions among players, the ball, and global court context, and aggregates sparse temporal cues with gated pooling to output player-level event predictions plus temporal evidence.
AI.result: On the BasketEvent benchmarks, PlayNet clearly outperforms video-level and crop-based baselines across event detection, player attribution, and temporal localization tasks, showing that explicitly modeling players and their interactions helps fine-grained understanding.
AI.conclusion: Player-centric modeling with interaction reasoning and temporal evidence aggregation is more effective for fine-grained basketball video understanding than conventional video-level or region-based approaches, and the released BasketEvent dataset plus PlayNet framework provide a strong foundation for future research.

title: An LLM-Driven Workflow for Automated Process Control Strategy Generation and Tuning from Dynamic Process Models
summary: We present a structured large-language-model-driven workflow for automated multi-variable control design from dynamic process models. The workflow decomposes the design task into constrained code-generation steps: plant-interface construction, normalization, manipulated-variable controlled-variable (MV-CV) pairing, controller specification, closed loop simulation, scenario generation, performance evaluation and Bayesian-optimization (BO) based tuning. Generated artifacts are executed and validated before downstream tasks proceed, and failed artifacts are repaired using validation feedback. The proposed approach is demonstrated on a nonlinear gas-preheater benchmark with coupled pressure and temperature dynamics. The generated workflow produces a physically consistent decentralized PI (proportional-integral) feedback-feedforward control structure and an executable tuning environment. Bayesian optimization reduces the closed loop performance objective, which aggregates set-point tracking and disturbance-rejection errors for the controlled variables, by approximately 26.5% relative to the initial controller generated by the workflow, mainly through improved pressure-loop transient performance. This figure quantifies the automated tuning stage rather than a comparison against a manually designed controller. The results demonstrate the feasibility of using structured large-language-model-based code generation to construct executable control-design workflows, while also highlighting the need for broader validation on larger plantwide-control benchmarks.

AI.tldr: A structured workflow uses large language models to automatically generate, validate, and tune multi-variable PI control schemes from process models, demonstrated on a nonlinear gas-preheater, with Bayesian optimization improving closed-loop performance by ~26.5%.
AI.motivation: Manual multivariable control design is complex, time-consuming, and requires expert knowledge. With the emergence of LLMs capable of code generation, there is an opportunity to automate the end-to-end control design workflow, from model interfacing to controller tuning, and to study whether such LLM-driven methods can reliably produce physically meaningful controllers.
AI.method: They design a stepwise, constrained code-generation workflow driven by a large language model. The task is decomposed into stages: building the plant interface, normalizing variables, MV-CV pairing, specifying the controller structure, running closed-loop simulations, generating test scenarios, evaluating performance, and using Bayesian optimization to tune controller parameters. At each step, the generated code or artifacts are executed and validated; any failures trigger repair using validation feedback. The approach is then applied to a nonlinear gas-preheater example with coupled pressure and temperature dynamics to automatically synthesize a decentralized PI feedback–feedforward controller and an associated tuning environment.
AI.result: On the gas-preheater benchmark, the workflow automatically produces a physically consistent decentralized PI control structure and executable simulation/tuning setup. Subsequent Bayesian optimization of controller parameters reduces a composite performance metric (combining setpoint tracking and disturbance rejection for the controlled variables) by about 26.5% relative to the initially generated controller, with most gains in pressure-loop transient behavior.
AI.conclusion: The study shows that a structured, LLM-driven, code-generation workflow can automatically construct executable multivariable control design setups and yield meaningful controllers whose performance can be significantly enhanced via automated Bayesian tuning. However, the authors stress that broader validation on larger and more complex plantwide-control benchmarks is needed to fully assess robustness and generality.

title: Expert Behavior Prior Reinforcement Learning
summary: Behavior prior reinforcement learning (BPRL) has emerged as a promising paradigm to improve sample efficiency in online reinforcement learning (RL) by leveraging policy priors derived from offline demonstrations. However, most existing BPRL methods rely on static offline datasets, which often suffer from low data diversity and suboptimal trajectory quality. This reliance restricts the effectiveness of policy priors, hindering both policy exploitation and stability during online training. Consequently, agents are prone to inefficient exploration and unstable learning dynamics. To address these limitations, we deviate from existing offline pre-training methods and propose an Expert Behavior Prior (EBP) algorithm. Specifically, we introduce a Q-guided conditional variational autoencoder (Q-CVAE) that learns to generate expert policy priors directly from the online replay buffer. This enables the generation of high-value actions for guiding policy updates without relying on pre-collected expert trajectories. To further enhance policy exploitation, we propose an expert policy guidance (EPG) mechanism that selects expert actions from a generative support set, and we integrate a policy gradient correction (PGC) module to harmonize Q-guidance with expert supervision, promoting stable and consistent policy improvement. Extensive experiments conducted on robotic control (Gym, PyBullet) and industrial control (DMControl) benchmarks demonstrate that EBP significantly outperforms state-of-the-art online RL algorithms, achieving higher sample efficiency and more stable convergence.

AI.tldr: They propose Expert Behavior Prior (EBP), an online reinforcement learning method that learns expert policy priors directly from the replay buffer using a Q-guided conditional VAE, plus mechanisms for expert action selection and policy gradient correction, leading to better sample efficiency and stability than existing methods.
AI.motivation: Existing behavior prior RL methods use static offline datasets, which suffer from low diversity and suboptimal trajectories, limiting the quality and usefulness of policy priors for guiding online RL. This causes inefficient exploration and unstable training, so a method that can derive strong priors without fixed offline experts is needed.
AI.method: 1) Learn a Q-guided conditional variational autoencoder (Q-CVAE) on data from the online replay buffer to generate expert-like, high-value actions conditioned on states and Q-values. 2) Use an Expert Policy Guidance (EPG) mechanism to select expert actions from a generative support set provided by the Q-CVAE. 3) Add a Policy Gradient Correction (PGC) module that reconciles Q-function guidance with supervision from the generated expert actions, aiming for stable and consistent policy improvement during online training.
AI.result: On robotic control benchmarks (Gym, PyBullet) and industrial control tasks (DMControl), the proposed EBP method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art online RL baselines in terms of sample efficiency and stability of convergence.
AI.conclusion: Generating expert behavior priors online from the replay buffer via Q-guided generative modeling, combined with expert action selection and policy gradient correction, is an effective way to improve sample efficiency and stability in online RL without relying on static offline expert datasets.

title: Regulating autonomous and agentic AI
summary: Regulating activities where regulatees use autonomous and agentic AI is challenging. Regulatory assumptions about regulatee knowledge and control no longer hold true; much of that lies elsewhere in the AI supply chain which thus needs to be brought within the scope of regulation. Governance systems for autonomous AI cannot replicate existing governance models, but need a fresh approach. Retrospective supervisory oversight becomes ineffective as a risk management tool, and AI autonomy generates new systemic risks which require new solutions. This paper investigate four regulatory systems: UK regulation of content platforms, data protection, UK financial services, and the EU AI Act\'92s cross-sectoral regime. It analyses the challenges posed by autonomous and agentic AI and proposes potential solutions which regulators might adopt. These will transform regulation from a reactive process to an active one, and assist it in adapting to the challenges of AI autonomy.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that traditional regulatory models fail when firms use autonomous, agentic AI, and that regulation must shift from reactive oversight to proactive, system-wide governance involving the AI supply chain.
AI.motivation: Autonomous and agentic AI systems disrupt core assumptions of regulation—namely that the regulated entity understands and controls the risky activity. Because crucial knowledge, design choices, and control over AI behaviour often sit with upstream AI developers and providers, traditional sectoral regulators struggle to manage risks effectively. There is a gap between existing governance tools and the emerging systemic risks created by autonomous AI, prompting the need to rethink regulatory architectures.
AI.method: The paper conducts a comparative analysis of four regulatory systems: (1) UK regulation of content platforms, (2) data protection law, (3) UK financial services regulation, and (4) the EU AI Act’s cross-sectoral framework. It examines how each system conceptualises responsibility, knowledge, and control; identifies where assumptions break down with autonomous AI; and draws cross-cutting lessons to derive design principles for future regulatory models.
AI.result: The analysis shows that, in all four regimes, key regulatory tools—including firm-level accountability, retrospective supervisory review, and ex post enforcement—lose effectiveness when AI systems have autonomy and agency and when critical capabilities are located in other parts of the AI supply chain. The study surfaces common gaps: insufficient leverage over foundational model providers, weak mechanisms for ongoing monitoring and adaptation, and limited treatment of systemic and cross-sectoral risks. It also identifies emerging practices, such as risk-based obligations, duties of ongoing monitoring, and shared responsibility along the supply chain, that can be built upon.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that governance for autonomous and agentic AI must become more anticipatory, continuous, and systemic. Regulators should expand the perimeter of regulation along the AI supply chain, impose proactive duties (e.g., pre-deployment testing, continuous monitoring, incident reporting), and develop tools for managing systemic AI risks rather than just individual firm misconduct. Adopting these solutions would transform regulation from a primarily reactive model into an active, forward-looking regime better suited to AI autonomy.

title: SPORD: A Simulation-Propose-then-OR-Dispose Approach for Supply Chain Planning
summary: For years, supply chain planning at e-commerce firms has operated as a collection of isolated projects. Each planning task from static network planning to dynamic warehouse assortment planning requires analysts to spend weeks building models from scratch, calibrating and persuading executives to act on outputs they cannot verify. Three barriers drive this: bespoke models proliferate because standardization is difficult (operational fragmentation); once unified, the combinatorial scale of millions of SKUs, thousands of nodes, and intricate routing logic exceeds what solvers can handle within a tight window (computational intractability); and a mathematically optimal solution still fails to be implemented if the executives do not trust it (implementation hurdle). To bridge this gap, we propose and implement the Simulation-Propose-then-OR-Dispose method, deployed as JD.com's NetSim platform. The central insight is decoupling: simulation proposes by generating and evaluating the full set of operationally valid candidate paths-absorbing all idiosyncratic business logic, while an integer program disposes by selecting the globally optimal subset. Computationally, matrix-vectorized CPU/GPU accelerated simulation achieves a 10-100 times speedup over serial methods, and a list scheduling algorithm reduces coupled-order processing from hours to minutes. Operationally, we establish a closed loop via an intelligent diagnosis engine. Since 2025, NetSim has optimized end to-end services for over 20,000 suppliers, the cross-regional fulfillment rate dropped from 6.1% to 4.9%, and the average monthly carbon reduction is approximately 5,745 tCO2e. SPORD moves simulation from monitoring to active planning. The transparent outputs turn skeptical executives into engaged collaborators, and the modular architecture ensures that the next planning requires just configuration, not reconstruction.

AI.tldr: They introduce SPORD/NetSim, a simulation-plus-optimization platform for large-scale e-commerce supply chain planning that standardizes planning, makes computation tractable, and increases executive trust, delivering measurable cost and carbon benefits.
AI.motivation: E-commerce supply chain planning tasks are currently tackled as isolated, bespoke projects, leading to duplicated modeling effort, difficulty standardizing across fragmented operations, computational intractability at scale, and a lack of executive trust in black-box optimization outputs. The authors want a unified, scalable, and trusted planning system.
AI.method: They propose the Simulation-Propose-then-OR-Dispose (SPORD) method, implemented as JD.com's NetSim platform. A high-speed, matrix-vectorized CPU/GPU-accelerated simulator first generates and evaluates all operationally valid shipping paths while embedding complex business logic, then an integer programming model selects the globally optimal subset of these candidate paths. They also introduce a list scheduling algorithm to drastically reduce coupled-order processing time and an intelligent diagnosis engine to close the operational feedback loop.
AI.result: The platform achieves 10–100x simulation speedups over serial approaches and cuts coupled-order processing time from hours to minutes. Deployed at JD.com since 2025, it has optimized end-to-end services for over 20,000 suppliers, reduced cross-regional fulfillment rate from 6.1% to 4.9%, and achieved average monthly carbon reductions of around 5,745 tCO2e.
AI.conclusion: SPORD repositions simulation from a passive monitoring tool to an active, scalable planning engine. By separating business-logic-heavy simulation from optimization, and providing transparent, modular outputs, the approach overcomes standardization, computational, and organizational adoption hurdles, enabling repeated planning via configuration instead of rebuilding models from scratch.

title: Towards Faithful Graph Explanations with Synergistic Edge Effects via Granular Balls
summary: Instance-level explanations aim to reveal the rationale behind a model's decisions for a specific graph. Previous methods explain graph neural networks (GNNs) by selecting important edges to induce subgraphs, where edge importance is assessed by perturbing each edge and observing changes in the model predictions. However, they often neglect the synergistic effects among edges, which are crucial for accurately characterizing edge importance. To address this issue, we propose SeeExplainer, a parameter-free explainer to interpret GNNs. Specifically, we first introduce a granular-ball graph refinement mechanism that decomposes a graph into several disjoint granular-balls with no fixed size, and utilize them as nodes to construct a structural graph. This process can better capture the synergistic effects among edges. Then, we perturb nodes and edges in the structural graph to generate explanatory subgraphs based on their respective contributions. Experiments on several graph classification datasets of different networks show that SeeExplainer outperforms state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: A new parameter-free method, SeeExplainer, explains GNN decisions on individual graphs by better capturing synergistic effects among edges using a granular-ball based structural graph, leading to better subgraph explanations and improved performance over prior methods.
AI.motivation: Existing instance-level explanation methods for GNNs typically score edges by perturbing them independently, then selecting important edges to form explanatory subgraphs. This ignores synergistic or collective effects among edges, so edge importance can be misestimated when groups of edges jointly influence predictions. The authors aim to design an explainer that captures these interactions while remaining efficient and model-agnostic.
AI.method: They introduce SeeExplainer, a parameter-free explainer. First, they refine the original graph via a granular-ball mechanism: the graph is decomposed into multiple disjoint granular-balls (flexible-sized local regions), which are then treated as nodes of a new structural graph. This structural graph is intended to encode synergistic edge effects within and between granular-balls. Next, instead of perturbing single original edges, they perturb nodes and edges in this structural graph, assess their contributions to model predictions, and then map the important elements back to the original graph to form explanatory subgraphs.
AI.result: On multiple graph classification datasets from various domains, SeeExplainer yields better explanation quality than state-of-the-art GNN explanation baselines, as measured by standard metrics for instance-level graph explanations.
AI.conclusion: Model-agnostic, parameter-free explanations for GNNs can be improved by first restructuring the graph into granular-balls that capture local edge synergies, then performing contribution-based perturbations at this higher level. This approach produces more faithful and effective explanatory subgraphs and outperforms existing instance-level GNN explainers on benchmark datasets.

title: Multimodal Pretraining for Generalizable EEG Representation Learning
summary: Electroencephalography (EEG) models used for epilepsy are often limited to specific datasets and tasks. This limited approach can make it challenging to apply these models across different datasets or in various situations. However, recent studies in foundation models and self-supervised learning suggest that an adaptable EEG backbone could support a range of EEG related tasks. In this study, we have developed a multimodal EEG foundation model that combines a raw signal encoder based on the Mamba architecture, a Vision Transformer (ViT)-style encoder for time-frequency data, and a lightweight encoder for text, all within a shared embedding space. The pretraining process relies on several innovative techniques, such as masked modeling, cross-view contrastive alignment, and temporal consistency losses. These methods are designed to create rich, seizure-relevant representations without requiring labeled data. To assess the efficacy and generalization of our pretrained model, we fine-tuned it on the canonical CHB-MIT seizure detection benchmark and additional seizure detection datasets, and conducted extensive experiments comparing different model variants. On the standard CHB-MIT split, our best single model achieved an AUROC of 0.874, and an ensemble variant reached 0.878 AUROC, representing state-of-the-art performance on this benchmark. In addition to standard train-test splits, we evaluated performance under a leave-one-subject-out (LOSO) protocol, which is rarely reported in prior EEG seizure modeling work and highlights the difficulty of patient-independent seizure detection, with a mean LOSO balanced accuracy of 0.558 across 19 subjects. Across datasets and evaluation settings, our multimodal foundation model enabled robust seizure detection and straightforward adaptation to new seizure detection scenarios, while also supporting interpretable seizure localization.

AI.tldr: A multimodal self-supervised EEG foundation model for epilepsy achieves state-of-the-art seizure detection and generalizes across patients and datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing EEG models for epilepsy are narrow, dataset-specific, and task-specific, which limits their real-world applicability and generalization, especially for patient-independent seizure detection and adaptation to new datasets or clinical scenarios. Recent progress in foundation models and self-supervised learning suggests that a single, adaptable EEG backbone could serve many EEG-related epilepsy tasks, but such a broadly applicable, multimodal seizure-focused EEG model is lacking.
AI.method: The authors design a multimodal EEG foundation model with three encoders that map into a shared embedding space: (1) a raw EEG signal encoder built on the Mamba architecture, (2) a Vision Transformer-style encoder for time–frequency representations (e.g., spectrograms), and (3) a lightweight text encoder. They pretrain this model in a self-supervised fashion using masked modeling (predicting masked parts of inputs), cross-view contrastive alignment between raw and time–frequency views, and temporal consistency losses to enforce stable representations over time. No labels are used during pretraining. After pretraining, they fine-tune the model on seizure detection tasks using the CHB-MIT benchmark and additional datasets, and they systematically compare different model variants. They evaluate both standard train–test splits and a challenging leave-one-subject-out (LOSO) protocol. They also analyze interpretability for seizure localization.
AI.result: On the canonical CHB-MIT seizure detection benchmark with the standard split, the best single pretrained model achieves an AUROC of 0.874, and an ensemble of models reaches 0.878 AUROC, which surpasses prior state-of-the-art results. Under the more challenging LOSO evaluation, they obtain a mean balanced accuracy of 0.558 across 19 subjects, highlighting both the difficulty of patient-independent seizure detection and the model’s relative robustness. Across multiple datasets and evaluation settings, the model can be fine-tuned effectively for seizure detection and adapted to new seizure detection scenarios. It also enables interpretable seizure localization, indicating where in time or channels seizures are detected.
AI.conclusion: A multimodal, self-supervised EEG foundation model that integrates raw signal, time–frequency, and text modalities within a shared embedding space can learn rich, seizure-relevant representations without labels, achieve state-of-the-art performance on standard seizure detection benchmarks, generalize better to new patients and datasets than traditional models, and offer interpretable seizure localization. This supports the feasibility and value of EEG foundation models for broad, flexible application across epilepsy-related tasks.

title: MSBraM: A Multi-scale Self-supervised Brain Foundation Model for Hierarchical EEG Dynamics Learning
summary: Self-supervised foundation models have recently shown strong potential for electroencephalogram (EEG)-based analysis. However, existing approaches struggle to capture the inherently multi-scale temporal structure of EEG signals, where local neural patterns and long-range dependencies jointly encode task-relevant information. This limitation hampers cross-scale representation learning and generalization across diverse downstream tasks. To address this challenge, we propose MSBraM, a Multi-Scale self-supervised Brain foundation Model designed to learn hierarchical EEG representations. MSBraM follows a two-stage pretraining framework. First, a multi-scale neural tokenizer discretizes raw EEG signals into semantic codes at different temporal resolutions via vector-quantized reconstruction. Second, the model is pretrained to predict masked codes using a curriculum multi-scale masking strategy, progressively integrating fine-grained local patterns with global temporal context. We pretrain MSBraM on over 2,400 hours of EEG data and evaluate it across 10 downstream tasks on 12 public datasets. Extensive experiments show that MSBraM achieves superior performance on other state-of-the-art pretrained models, demonstrating strong generalization and transferability. These results indicate that explicitly modeling multi-scale temporal dynamics is critical for effective EEG foundation models.

AI.tldr: MSBraM is a multi-scale self-supervised EEG foundation model that tokenizes EEG at multiple temporal resolutions and learns to predict masked codes, yielding superior transfer to many downstream tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing self-supervised EEG models fail to fully capture EEG’s inherently multi-scale temporal structure, limiting cross-scale representation learning and generalization across diverse tasks. There is a need for a foundation model that explicitly models both local neural patterns and long-range dependencies.
AI.method: The authors introduce MSBraM, a two-stage pretraining framework. (1) A multi-scale neural tokenizer discretizes raw EEG into semantic codes at various temporal resolutions via vector-quantized reconstruction. (2) The model is then pretrained to predict masked codes using a curriculum multi-scale masking strategy, which gradually fuses fine-grained local information with global temporal context. Pretraining is performed on 2,400+ hours of EEG data.
AI.result: When evaluated on 10 downstream tasks across 12 public EEG datasets, MSBraM outperforms other state-of-the-art pretrained EEG models, indicating better generalization and transferability of its learned representations.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling multi-scale temporal dynamics via a neural tokenizer and curriculum masking leads to more powerful EEG foundation models, as demonstrated by MSBraM’s superior performance across many downstream tasks.

title: Logical Regression for Planning with Axioms
summary: In automated planning, logical regression is an operation that returns the most general condition necessary for an action to achieve a particular formula. It has many applications, such as allowing for more robust plan execution and providing compact policies for non-deterministic planning. Although relatively simple to calculate in basic planning settings, logical regression becomes significantly more complex when additional factors, such as axioms, are present. We introduce a methodology for approximating the logical regression of an action in a domain that includes axioms; an approximation that limits conditions to partial states. Our method produces minimal partial states while avoiding the recalculation of axioms. To demonstrate the impact of our methods, we embed our form of regression in an execution monitoring context, a well-established setting that can benefit greatly from logical regression. Our results show that this form of regression can dramatically generalize partial states across multiple domains, reducing the number of variables considered for execution monitoring by up to 70%, and demonstrate that the resulting execution monitor is robust enough to recover frequently in an environment with unexpected changes: several domains recover over 50% of the time in our tests.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an approximate logical regression method for planning domains with axioms, producing minimal partial states without repeatedly re-evaluating axioms, and shows it greatly simplifies and strengthens execution monitoring.
AI.motivation: Logical regression is powerful in automated planning, enabling robust plan execution and compact policies, but becomes complex in domains that include axioms. There is a need for a practical, computationally manageable regression technique that works in such richer domains, especially to support execution monitoring and policy generalization.
AI.method: The authors define an approximate logical regression operator tailored for domains with axioms, restricting regressed conditions to partial states. They design the algorithm to generate minimal partial states and to reuse axiom computations instead of recalculating them. They then integrate this operator into an execution monitoring framework and evaluate it across multiple planning domains.
AI.result: Empirical results across several domains show that the proposed regression method can generalize partial states substantially, cutting the number of variables that execution monitoring must track by up to 70%. In dynamic environments with unexpected changes, the resulting execution monitor using this regression approach is able to recover successfully in more than 50% of runs in several domains.
AI.conclusion: Approximate logical regression over partial states in axiom-rich domains is both feasible and beneficial. It yields compact, generalized conditions that significantly reduce monitoring complexity while maintaining or improving robustness, enabling execution monitors that frequently recover from unexpected changes in a variety of planning domains.

title: PATS: Policy-Aware Training Scaffolding for Agentic Reinforcement Learning
summary: In long-horizon LLM agent reinforcement learning, weak policies often repeat similar failures, producing uninformative rollout trajectories and limiting effective policy optimization. Existing skill-centric methods improve exploration by optimizing, filtering, or internalizing reusable skills. However, they remain centered on the skills themselves rather than being designed as adaptive training-time support for the evolving policy. To address this, we propose a policy-centric training paradigm that reframes skills as a dynamic training scaffold. Our framework, Pats, converts rollout groups from the latest policy into evidence cards and uses task-specific evaluation to adjust the context used in subsequent rollouts. Concrete guidance helps weak policies to complete challenging tasks. As policy improves, redundant context is revised or removed to reduce reliance on explicit guidance while preserving useful rollout variation. The policy is optimized with environmental rewards using standard RLVR, and the training scaffold is discarded at deployment. On ALFWorld and WebShop, Pats improves over strong baselines by up to 18.6%. Across seven search-augmented QA benchmarks, it remains competitive while using 32.1% fewer prompt tokens than the baseline.

AI.tldr: They introduce Pats, a policy-centric training scaffold that dynamically converts recent rollouts into guidance cards to support weak LLM agents, then gradually removes this support as the policy improves, yielding better long-horizon RL performance with fewer prompt tokens.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon LLM agents trained with reinforcement learning struggle because weak policies repeatedly fail in similar ways, generating low-value trajectories that hinder optimization. Existing methods focus on pre-defined or learned skills, improving exploration but not explicitly supporting the current policy’s training dynamics. There is a need for a mechanism that adaptively helps the evolving policy succeed on challenging tasks while still enabling efficient RL optimization and preserving generalization at deployment.
AI.method: They propose Pats, a policy-centric framework that treats skills as a dynamic training scaffold rather than fixed modules. For each training iteration, Pats groups rollouts from the current policy and converts them into "evidence cards" that encode relevant experience. A task-specific evaluation mechanism selects and adjusts which evidence cards (context) to include in the next round of rollouts, providing concrete guidance to help a weak policy complete difficult tasks. As the policy improves, Pats revises or prunes redundant context, encouraging reduced reliance on explicit guidance while keeping beneficial rollout diversity. The underlying policy is trained only via standard RLVR (reinforcement learning from environmental rewards plus value regularization), and the scaffold is not used at deployment.
AI.result: On two long-horizon interactive benchmarks, ALFWorld and WebShop, Pats outperforms strong baselines by up to 18.6% in task performance. On seven search-augmented QA benchmarks, it achieves competitive results while using 32.1% fewer prompt tokens than a baseline method, indicating improved efficiency without sacrificing quality.
AI.conclusion: Reconceptualizing skills as a dynamic, policy-centric training scaffold can substantially improve the training of long-horizon LLM agents. Pats increases task success, improves sample and prompt efficiency, and allows the final deployed policy to operate without extra training-time scaffolding, demonstrating that adaptive guidance around the evolving policy is more effective than purely skill-centric approaches.

title: Bridging the Gap Between Plausibility and Admissibility: Constraint-Aware Flow Maps for Dynamic Graph Systems
summary: Generative models can support decision-making under uncertainty by producing ensembles of plausible future system trajectories, but statistical plausibility does not ensure structural feasibility. This study investigates whether post-sampling symbolic constraints can improve the reliability of generative trajectory modeling in dynamic graph-structured systems. A conditional diffusion model generates future graph-state trajectories from partial observations, while an external symbolic layer applies hard filtering, soft weighting, or projection-based repair. The framework is evaluated on two controlled synthetic regimes: a compact graph and a medium-complexity dependency graph, using metrics for structural validity, sample efficiency, diversity, robustness, and calibration. In the compact regime, the model produces an invalid probability mass of 0.002996, indicating an almost entirely admissible trajectory manifold. Under the same architecture and training protocol, invalid mass increases to 0.155929 in the medium-complexity regime. Hard filtering removes all invalid retained trajectories while preserving 84.4% of generated samples, whereas soft weighting preserves effective sample size but yields only limited validity gains. Family-level analysis shows that dependency constraints account for nearly all observed inadmissibility. These results indicate that statistical plausibility and structural admissibility are distinct reliability properties and that symbolic constraint handling becomes more valuable as graph-structural complexity increases.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how adding symbolic constraints after sampling improves the reliability of generative models for future trajectories on graphs, showing that structural feasibility is different from statistical plausibility and becomes more critical as graph complexity grows.
AI.motivation: Generative models can simulate possible future trajectories of complex systems but may output trajectories that are statistically likely according to data yet structurally impossible given system rules. There is a need to better understand and improve structural feasibility in such models, especially in graph-structured, dynamic systems used for decision-making under uncertainty.
AI.method: The authors use a conditional diffusion model to generate future graph-state trajectories from partial observations. On top of this, they add an external symbolic constraint-handling layer that can operate in three modes: (1) hard filtering of invalid samples, (2) soft weighting that downweights constraint-violating trajectories, and (3) projection-based repair that attempts to fix invalid trajectories. They evaluate this framework on two synthetic regimes—a compact graph and a medium-complexity dependency graph—using metrics such as structural validity, sample efficiency, diversity, robustness, and calibration.
AI.result: In the simple compact-graph regime, the generative model itself already produces trajectories with very low invalid probability mass (0.002996), meaning nearly all generated samples are structurally admissible. With the same model setup applied to a more complex dependency graph, the fraction of invalid trajectories rises substantially to 0.155929. Hard filtering then eliminates all invalid retained trajectories while keeping 84.4% of the original samples; soft weighting maintains effective sample size but only modestly improves validity. Analysis across families of constraints shows that dependency-related constraints are responsible for almost all structural violations.
AI.conclusion: Structural admissibility and statistical plausibility are distinct dimensions of reliability in generative trajectory modeling. As the underlying graph structure becomes more complex, symbolic constraint-handling mechanisms add substantial value. Hard symbolic filtering is particularly effective at removing invalid trajectories with limited cost in sample efficiency, while soft weighting offers smaller gains. This suggests that future work on generative models for dynamic graph-structured systems should explicitly incorporate symbolic constraint handling, especially in high-complexity settings.

title: Agent-Guided Relational Concept Discovery: Toward Interpretable Surgical Margin Assessment
comment: This paper is accepted to MICCAI 2026, and this is the submission version, not the camera-ready version
summary: Deep learning models can effectively use Rapid Evaporative Ionization Mass Spectrometry (REIMS) data for surgical margin assessment. However, their clinical adoption remains challenging due to limited generalization to operating room conditions. This difficulty arises because models are typically trained on labeled spectra collected from resected tissue samples, while they must operate on noisy, unlabeled data acquired directly during surgery. In addition, the black-box nature of deep learning models makes it difficult to understand and systematically improve their behavior. Concept-based learning offers a promising way to address these challenges by mapping raw measurements to human-understandable concepts. However, supervised concept-based approaches rely on concept annotations, which are difficult to obtain in complex mass spectrometry workflows. We propose Agent-Guided Concept Discovery, a framework that learns meaningful concepts directly from data without requiring predefined concept labels. During training, a reasoning agent refines semantic descriptions of the learned concepts and adaptively adjusts their weight based on diagnostic relevance. These concepts are further grounded using a biochemical knowledge graph to ensure consistency with known metabolic relationships. Across Skin and Breast Cancer datasets, our model improves balanced accuracy and sensitivity over the baseline. In a representative intraoperative case, it shows fewer false positives, indicating better generalization to surgical conditions.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an agent-guided, concept-based deep learning framework that discovers and refines human-interpretable concepts from REIMS data to improve robustness and interpretability of surgical margin assessment, without needing manual concept labels.
AI.motivation: Deep learning models show promise for using REIMS data to assess surgical margins, but they generalize poorly from clean, labeled ex vivo spectra to noisy, unlabeled intraoperative data, and their black-box nature prevents clinicians from understanding or reliably improving them. Existing concept-based learning can add interpretability, but it typically requires manually annotated concepts, which are impractical to obtain in complex mass spectrometry workflows. There is a need for an interpretable, concept-based method that can learn robust, clinically relevant representations directly from data and generalize to real operating room conditions.
AI.method: The authors propose Agent-Guided Concept Discovery, a framework that jointly learns a predictive model and a set of latent concepts from REIMS data. A reasoning agent operates during training to iteratively refine semantic descriptions of these learned concepts and to adapt their importance weights according to their diagnostic relevance. The discovered concepts are then grounded and constrained using a biochemical knowledge graph, enforcing consistency with known metabolic relationships and improving interpretability. The approach is evaluated on skin and breast cancer REIMS datasets and on an intraoperative scenario, comparing performance against a baseline deep learning model.
AI.result: On Skin and Breast Cancer datasets, the proposed framework yields higher balanced accuracy and sensitivity than a baseline model trained directly on spectra. In a representative intraoperative case study, the method produces fewer false-positive detections of cancerous margins, suggesting improved robustness and generalization to real surgical conditions when compared with the baseline.
AI.conclusion: Agent-Guided Concept Discovery enables unsupervised learning of human-meaningful, biochemically grounded concepts from REIMS data, removing the need for manual concept labels while improving both classification performance and generalization to intraoperative conditions.
