This demonstrates that integrating a reasoning agent and domain knowledge graphs into concept-based learning can make deep models for surgical margin assessment more accurate and interpretable, potentially facilitating clinical adoption.

title: Detecting LLM-Generated Tokens in Human--LLM Coauthored Text
summary: The rise of human-AI collaborative writing has created a growing need for fine-grained detection methods that support localizing likely LLM-generated content in mixed-authorship documents. Existing methods for detecting LLM-generated text mainly focus on document-level classification and cannot identify which parts of the text are generated by LLMs. This paper introduces a new method to address this urgent need. Our method operates at the token level, the natural unit of modern language models, and builds on existing token-level detection scores. The key idea is to smooth adjacent token scores to reduce their variability, while using an adaptive Lepski-type rule to select the bandwidth according to the local authorship structure. Our method is simple to implement and does not require token-level labeled data for training. Theoretically, we characterize this trade-off and show that the proposed method achieves favorable mean square error performance in estimating the underlying signal. Empirically, we demonstrate strong performance of our method against a wide range of baselines in both synthetic datasets and a realistic dataset. We deploy a publicly accessible website that implements the methods as well.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new token-level method to localize which parts of mixed-authorship text are likely generated by LLMs, achieving strong theoretical and empirical performance without needing token-level labels.
AI.motivation: Human-AI collaborative writing produces documents with mixed human and LLM authorship, creating a need to identify which specific segments are machine-generated. Existing LLM-text detectors mostly work at the document level and cannot localize generated passages, limiting their usefulness in real-world settings like editing, attribution, or moderation.
AI.method: They work at the token level using existing token-level detection scores, then apply a smoothing procedure over adjacent tokens to reduce noise. An adaptive Lepski-type bandwidth selection rule is used to choose how much to smooth locally, based on the local authorship structure. The method is simple, requires no token-level labeled training data, and is supported by a theoretical analysis of the bias-variance trade-off in signal estimation.
AI.result: Theoretically, they show their adaptive smoothing achieves favorable mean squared error in estimating the underlying token-level authorship signal. Empirically, they evaluate on synthetic and realistic datasets and show their method outperforms a wide range of baselines on localizing LLM-generated text.
AI.conclusion: The proposed adaptive token-level smoothing approach provides an effective, label-free method for fine-grained detection of LLM-generated segments in mixed-authorship documents, supported by both theory and experiments. They also provide a public website that implements the method for practical use.

title: AREX: Towards a Recursively Self-Improving Agent for Deep Research
summary: Deep research requires agents to find answers that jointly satisfy multiple constraints. Discovering such answers is costly, whereas verifying a candidate can often be decomposed into tractable constraint-wise checks. This discovery--verification asymmetry suggests that a research agent should do more than simply search longer: it should recursively improve its current answer by verifying intermediate results and using the partially verified state to guide subsequent refinement. We introduce AREX, a family of Recursively Self-Improving (RSI) deep research agents. AREX alternates between an inner research loop that gathers evidence and constructs a provisional answer, and an outer self-improvement loop that audits the answer constraint-wise, identifies unresolved claims, and launches targeted follow-up research. To sustain RSI over long horizons, AREX learns an autonomous context-update tool that compresses growing interaction history into a compact improvement state preserving verified evidence and unresolved constraints, without relying on an external model. We train AREX on verified synthetic tasks and high-quality trajectories through agentic mid-training and long-horizon reinforcement learning. To mitigate sparse final rewards during long horizon learning, we emphasize key steps where decisive evidence is acquired or erroneous research directions are corrected. We instantiate a dense 4B model and a 122B-A10B Mixture-of-Experts model. Across BrowseComp, WideSearch, DeepSearchQA, Humanity's Last Exam (HLE), and other reasoning and tool-use benchmarks, AREX substantially outperforms comparable-scale baselines and remains competitive with models using substantially more activated parameters.

AI.tldr: AREX is a family of recursively self-improving research agents that iteratively verify and refine their own answers using constraint-wise checks, context compression, and long-horizon RL, achieving strong performance on deep research and tool-use benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Deep research tasks often require answers that satisfy many interdependent constraints. Finding such answers is expensive, but checking whether a proposed answer satisfies each constraint is much cheaper and can be decomposed. Existing agents mainly search longer without systematically exploiting this discovery–verification asymmetry or maintaining an evolving, partially verified state over long horizons. There is a need for agents that can verify, audit, and refine their own work over extended interactions while efficiently managing growing context.
AI.method: The paper proposes AREX, a family of Recursively Self-Improving (RSI) research agents. AREX alternates between: (1) an inner loop that performs research, gathers evidence via tools (e.g., browsing), and constructs a provisional answer; and (2) an outer loop that audits the answer constraint-wise, identifies which claims are verified versus unresolved, and triggers targeted follow-up research. To enable long-horizon self-improvement, AREX uses a learned autonomous context-update tool that compresses long interaction histories into a compact improvement state encoding verified evidence and remaining constraints, without external models. Training uses a combination of synthetic verified tasks, high-quality agentic trajectories, and long-horizon reinforcement learning (“agentic mid-training”), with reward shaping that emphasizes key steps where crucial evidence is acquired or incorrect research directions are corrected. They instantiate both a 4B dense model and a larger 122B-A10B Mixture-of-Experts version.
AI.result: AREX agents trained with the proposed RSI framework and context-update tool achieve substantially better performance than comparable-scale baselines on several deep research and reasoning benchmarks, including BrowseComp, WideSearch, DeepSearchQA, and Humanity’s Last Exam (HLE), as well as other tool-use tasks. The models remain competitive with systems that activate far more parameters, indicating improved sample and parameter efficiency in long-horizon reasoning and research settings.
AI.conclusion: Recursively self-improving deep research agents that explicitly separate discovery from verification, and that maintain a compressed, partially verified improvement state, can significantly enhance long-horizon reasoning performance. By combining constraint-wise auditing, targeted follow-up research, and specialized training via agentic mid-training and long-horizon RL with shaped rewards, AREX shows that such architectures can outperform similarly sized models and rival much larger ones on challenging research and tool-use benchmarks. This validates the usefulness of leveraging discovery–verification asymmetry and autonomous context management for scalable deep research agents.

title: Toward Continuous Assurance for the Democratization of AI Agent Creation in Industry
summary: AI agents are increasingly created inside organizations by non-engineering users through low-code, no-code, and conversational development environments. This democratization enables rapid local innovation, but it also creates a reliability gap: agents that appear to users as simple productivity artifacts may depend on changing models, tools, retrieval sources, permissions, prompts, schedules, and external services. These dependencies can cause silent degradation long after deployment, even when no user directly modifies the agent. This paper identifies the reliability challenge created by democratized AI agent creation and proposes a lightweight continuous-assurance framework for citizen-created organizational agents. The framework combines dependency mapping, readiness contracts, scheduled checks, diagnostics, and lifecycle governance to assess whether an agent remains operationally ready under expected conditions. We also present an initial prototype auditor and scenario-based assessment showing how the proposed taxonomy can be translated into practical checks and actionable remediation guidance.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses reliability issues in AI agents created by non-engineers using low-code/no-code tools and proposes a continuous-assurance framework to ensure these agents remain reliable over time.
AI.motivation: As AI development becomes democratized within organizations through low-code, no-code, and conversational tools, non-engineers can rapidly create AI agents. However, these agents have complex, often hidden dependencies (models, tools, data sources, permissions, prompts, schedules, external services) that can change over time, creating a reliability gap where agents silently degrade without explicit user modifications. Organizations need a way to systematically manage and monitor the reliability of such citizen-created agents.
AI.method: The paper introduces a lightweight continuous-assurance framework tailored to citizen-created organizational AI agents. The framework includes components such as dependency mapping to enumerate what the agent relies on, readiness contracts that specify expected operating conditions, scheduled checks to regularly test whether the agent still meets its readiness criteria, diagnostics to identify points of failure, and lifecycle governance to manage changes and evolution. The authors also develop an initial prototype auditor and perform a scenario-based assessment to demonstrate how the theoretical taxonomy can be implemented as concrete checks and remediation steps.
AI.result: The result is a conceptual framework and an early-stage prototype auditor that operationalizes the proposed reliability taxonomy. Through scenario-based evaluation, the authors illustrate how the framework can automatically surface degraded dependencies, assess operational readiness of agents, and generate actionable remediation guidance for non-technical creators and organizational stakeholders.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that democratized AI agent creation introduces unique reliability risks due to hidden and evolving dependencies. A lightweight continuous-assurance framework, as proposed, can help organizations maintain operational readiness of citizen-created agents without imposing heavy engineering overhead. The initial prototype and scenarios suggest the approach is feasible and can be extended into more robust assurance tooling for real-world deployments.

title: Agentic Context Management: Solving Agent Memory and Cost by Treating Them as Lifecycle and Architecture Problems
comment: 23 pages, 6 figures, 4 tables. Evaluation harness and study data: github.com/maximem-ai
summary: Production AI agents' failures are less often due to an inability to reason well and more often because they cannot manage what is in their reasoning context: conversation histories, large prompts, large tool definitions, and ballooning tool outputs. Agents drown in their own accumulating history while paying a token cost that grows every turn, producing missing recalls within and across conversations. The incumbent response treats this as a storage-and-retrieval problem. We argue that framing is too narrow. Actively managing what an agent holds in mind is a lifecycle, not merely a store: it spans deciding what to remember, extracting and structuring it, choosing the right store per data type, consolidating and forgetting while preserving provenance, deciding what is relevant now, anticipating what is needed next, and compacting context to a budget without losing what matters. In serious production this operates not over a single user but across an organizational scope hierarchy. We name this discipline Agentic Context Management (ACM) and decompose it into five primitives: architecting, ingesting, scoping, anticipating, and compacting & consolidation. We then make the economic case: naive context accumulation grows token cost quadratically in conversation length, crude summarization buys linear cost at the price of an accuracy cliff, and only validated compaction achieves linear cost with preserved fidelity. We describe a reference implementation, Maximem Synap, that realizes the five primitives as a multi-tenant service and reports 92% on LongMemEval and 93.2% on LoCoMo under the configuration detailed in Section 6. We close with dimensions existing benchmarks do not yet capture, latency, token efficiency, and context-rot resistance, and the frontier of decision-level and organization-level context the category points toward.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Agentic Context Management (ACM), a framework and system for actively managing AI agents’ context to control token costs while preserving accuracy over long, multi-session, organizational-scale interactions.
AI.motivation: Production AI agents often fail not because of poor reasoning, but because they cannot effectively manage growing context: long conversation histories, large prompts, tool definitions, and expanding tool outputs. Existing approaches treat this as a storage and retrieval task (e.g., vector search), which is too narrow and leads to high token costs, context overload, and missed recalls within and across conversations. There is a need for a principled way to decide what to keep, how to structure it, where to store it, when to forget or consolidate, and how to surface the right information at the right time, especially in enterprise settings spanning many users and teams.
AI.method: The authors reframe context handling as a lifecycle called Agentic Context Management (ACM) and decompose it into five core primitives: (1) architecting – designing the context schema, stores, and flows; (2) ingesting – extracting, structuring, and typing information from interactions and tools; (3) scoping – determining what context applies to which agents, users, and organizational layers; (4) anticipating – predicting what information will be needed in future steps and pre-positioning it; and (5) compacting & consolidation – compressing, summarizing, and merging context to stay within token budgets while preserving provenance and fidelity. They implement these primitives in a multi-tenant reference system, Maximem Synap, and evaluate it on long-context benchmarks such as LongMemEval and LoCoMo. They also provide an economic analysis of token cost growth under different context strategies.
AI.result: The economic analysis shows that naïve context accumulation leads to quadratically increasing token costs with conversation length. Simple or “crude” summarization strategies reduce cost to linear but introduce an “accuracy cliff,” where important details get lost. Validated compaction strategies can achieve linear token cost while preserving high fidelity of recall. The reference implementation, Maximem Synap, achieves 92% on LongMemEval and 93.2% on LoCoMo under the experimental setup described in the paper, demonstrating strong performance on long-context memory benchmarks in a multi-tenant, production-oriented setting.
AI.conclusion: Context management for AI agents should be treated as an active, agentic lifecycle rather than just storage and retrieval. The proposed ACM framework, instantiated via the five primitives and realized in Maximem Synap, can maintain high recall and accuracy over long, complex, organizational-scale interactions while keeping token costs manageable. Existing benchmarks underweight key production concerns like latency, token efficiency, and robustness to context rotation, and the authors highlight the need for new evaluation dimensions and methods that better reflect real-world, decision-level and organization-level context requirements.

title: Same Dangerous Objective, Opposite Advice: Direct Exposure versus Multi-Agent Mediation
summary:
Even a current high-capability LLM can appear safer when shown a dangerous objective directly than when other agents transform and relay its direction. Using OpenAI's gpt-5.6-sol model alias, we test 25 pre-specified mirrored trade-off profiles. Direct exposure to an objective authorizing concealment, fabrication, and pressure produced advice net opposed to its target. After an Id and Censor transformed the same objective into affect and a constraint-rewritten, target-bearing intention, the user-facing Superego---which saw the preferred direction but not the raw objective, its manipulative clauses, or its source---produced advice net aligned with the target.
  This behavioral reverse shift is consistent with the model recognizing or distrusting the manipulative motive, although we do not identify its internal mechanism. The second result exposes a compositional safety gap: a current high-capability model can be used as the user-facing component of an automated, multi-stage workflow serving an explicitly manipulative objective. The workflow can keep the raw instruction, its manipulation-authorizing clauses, and its provenance outside the downstream model's context while preserving the objective's target direction. A user with endpoint-only access likewise cannot directly inspect those upstream messages including the objective.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that a powerful LLM may act more safely when given a manipulative, dangerous objective directly than when that same objective is hidden upstream and only its “direction” is passed through a multi-agent workflow.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether current high-capability LLMs remain safe when they are used as one component in a multi-stage, agentic workflow serving a manipulative or dangerous goal, and to test whether safety behaviors survive when objectives are transformed and partially hidden from the user-facing model.
AI.method: Using OpenAI's gpt-5.6-sol model alias, the authors define 25 mirrored trade-off profiles and compare two conditions: (1) the model is directly exposed to an objective that explicitly authorizes manipulation (concealment, fabrication, pressure) and (2) a multi-agent system (Id and Censor) transforms this objective into affective prompts and constraint-rewritten intentions, such that the downstream “Superego” model only sees preferred directions and not the raw, manipulative clauses or their provenance. They then measure whether the generated advice is aligned with or opposed to the manipulative target in each condition.
AI.result: When the model is given the manipulative objective directly, it tends to produce advice that is net opposed to that objective, suggesting a form of safety response or distrust of manipulative instructions. However, when upstream agents transform and relay the objective—hiding its manipulative nature and provenance—while preserving the target direction, the user-facing Superego model generates advice that is net aligned with the manipulative target. This shows a reversal in behavior between direct and indirect exposure conditions.
AI.conclusion: The findings imply that current high-capability LLMs can be more robustly safe when they see a dangerous, manipulative objective explicitly than when they are embedded in a multi-stage workflow that hides the raw objective yet passes along its target direction. This reveals a compositional safety gap: system-level constructions can bypass some model-level safety behaviors, and neither the downstream model nor a user with endpoint-only access can easily inspect or contest the upstream, manipulative objective or its transformation steps.

title: MIRROR: Learning from the Other View for Multi-Modal Reasoning
summary: Unlike large language models (LLMs) that exhibit strong reasoning capabilities, vision-language models (VLMs) struggle with visual reasoning, even on geometry problems that admit equivalent text, diagram, and combined diagram+text views. We show that these views often elicit different behaviors: a model may solve a problem from text but fail on the corresponding diagram, or succeed visually while failing textually. This inconsistency suggests that different views expose complementary reasoning paths and failure modes that standard multimodal post-training does not fully exploit. To study and exploit this phenomenon, we construct ODA-Data, a high-quality paired multimodal geometry dataset with text-dominant, image-dominant, and combined image+text views of the same problems, together with splits for training and evaluating modality-dependent reasoning behaviors. We then develop Modality-Informed Reciprocal Reasoning Optimization (MIRROR), a reinforcement learning approach for improving multimodal reasoning via self supervision. For each problem, MIRROR evaluates the model under all views, selects the best-performing view as a teacher, and trains other views with a reverse-KL objective towards the teacher. Across reasoning benchmarks that evaluate on geometry problems, MIRROR improves over standard RL and yields more accurate and consistent behavior across modalities

AI.tldr: The paper identifies and tackles inconsistent visual vs textual reasoning in vision-language models (VLMs) on geometry problems by using cross-modality self-supervision.
AI.motivation: Although LLMs show strong reasoning skills, VLMs underperform on visual reasoning tasks like geometry, and models behave inconsistently across text-only, image-only, and image+text versions of the same problem. Existing multimodal training does not leverage these complementary views or their diverse failure modes.
AI.method: The authors construct ODA-Data, a paired multimodal geometry dataset with text-dominant, image-dominant, and image+text variants of each problem and splits tailored to study modality-dependent reasoning. They then propose MIRROR (Modality-Informed Reciprocal Reasoning Optimization), a reinforcement learning framework in which, for each problem, the model is run under all modalities, the best-performing modality serves as a teacher, and the other modalities are trained via a reverse-KL objective to match the teacher’s behavior, enabling cross-view knowledge transfer via self-supervision.
AI.result: On geometry reasoning benchmarks, models trained with MIRROR outperform baselines trained with standard reinforcement learning and show both higher accuracy and greater consistency in their predictions across text, image, and image+text modalities.
AI.conclusion: Exploiting complementary reasoning behaviors across modalities using paired views and cross-modality RL (MIRROR) can substantially improve multimodal geometry reasoning, both in terms of performance and cross-view consistency, highlighting the value of modality-aware training rather than treating all modalities uniformly.

title: Beyond Sycophancy: Structured Resistance and Compliance in LLM Moral Reasoning
summary: Building socially calibrated large language models, which can learn from others without simply yielding to them, requires more than reducing sycophancy as a one-dimensional failure mode. Models must distinguish when to incorporate others' perspectives from when to maintain a well-grounded moral judgment. We study the broader resistance-compliance process governing this distinction. Across three studies, we show that models' judgment revision is structured along three dimensions that parallel classic phenomena in human social psychology: the distance between an incoming view and the model's initial position, the source attribution of that view, and the coalition structure supporting it. Models are generally more receptive to nearby positions, more influenced by views presented as their own prior judgments, and differently responsive to group pressure. These findings recast sycophancy as one expression of a broader judgment-updating process shaped by social influence. Our framework provides a principled basis for distinguishing constructive belief revision from sycophantic compliance, thereby supporting better alignment in morally consequential interactions.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes how large language models update their moral judgments in response to others’ opinions, showing that sycophancy is part of a richer, structured social influence process.
AI.motivation: Current work often treats sycophancy (automatic agreement with users) as a single failure mode in language models. But truly socially calibrated models must sometimes learn from others and sometimes resist social pressure, especially in morally consequential contexts. The authors want a deeper, theory-grounded understanding of when and how models change their judgments under social influence.
AI.method: The authors conduct three empirical studies of language models’ judgment revision under social influence. They systematically vary: (1) how far the presented opinion is from the model’s initial judgment (attitudinal distance), (2) how the opinion is framed in terms of its source (e.g., as the model’s own prior view versus someone else’s), and (3) the coalition structure or group support behind that opinion. They analyze how these factors shape whether and how the model updates its moral judgments.
AI.result: They find that language models show structured patterns of influence similar to classic human social psychology: they are more willing to shift toward positions close to their initial views, more influenced when a view is framed as their own prior judgment, and they exhibit distinct patterns of responsiveness to group or coalition pressure. These patterns suggest that model ‘sycophancy’ is just one manifestation of a broader, socially structured judgment-updating process.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that sycophancy should be understood within a larger framework of social influence and judgment revision in language models. By decomposing influence into dimensions like distance, source attribution, and coalition structure, the authors offer a principled way to distinguish healthy, evidence-based belief updating from undesirable, uncritical compliance. This framework can guide the design of more robustly aligned models for morally sensitive interactions.

title: Unsupervised Consensus-Based Anomaly Detection for Spatiotemporal Malaria Incidence in Ghana
comment: 32, 15 figures, under review at spatial and spatio-temporal epidemiology
summary: A consensus anomaly detection framework was applied to monthly malaria surveillance data from Ghana (2014-2023) to identify atypical transmission patterns. Anomalies were highly structured in space and time. Ashanti and Northern Regions accounted for most recurrent anomalies, with persistent hotspots at Tamale, Kumasi, and Accra. A key finding was the spatial distinction between anomaly burden (cumulative cases during anomalous periods) and anomaly frequency (persistence of unusual behaviour). Tamale had the highest burden during anomalies, whereas the highest anomaly rates clustered in Ashanti districts, showing that high-burden areas are not necessarily those with the most frequent anomalous transmission. Anomalous months formed a statistically distinct group, with much higher case counts (Cohen's $d = 3.252$) and large seasonal deviations ($d > 1.2$) compared with normal months. Malaria burden alone provides an incomplete picture of transmission dynamics. By distinguishing where malaria is most prevalent from where transmission behaves most unusually, this framework can strengthen surveillance, prioritise investigations, and support targeted control strategies.

AI.tldr: The paper uses an anomaly detection framework on 10 years of Ghana malaria data to separate where malaria is most common from where transmission behaves most unusually, revealing distinct spatial and temporal hotspots that can better guide surveillance and control.
AI.motivation: Traditional malaria surveillance focuses on overall burden (case counts) and often misses where and when transmission behaves unusually, which can signal outbreaks, system failures, or emerging risks. There is a need for methods that detect and characterize atypical patterns in order to improve targeting of investigations and interventions.
AI.method: The study applies a consensus anomaly detection framework to monthly malaria surveillance data from Ghana for 2014–2023. It identifies anomalous months and locations, then quantifies and contrasts two metrics: anomaly burden (cumulative cases during anomalous periods) and anomaly frequency (how often anomalies occur). It also compares case counts and seasonality between anomalous and normal months using effect sizes (Cohen's d).
AI.result: Anomalies were not random but highly structured in space and time. Ashanti and Northern Regions exhibited most recurrent anomalies, with persistent hotspots at Tamale, Kumasi, and Accra. Tamale had the highest anomaly burden, while the highest anomaly frequency clustered in Ashanti districts, indicating that locations with heavy anomalous case loads are not necessarily those with the most frequent anomalies. Anomalous months showed much higher case counts (Cohen's d = 3.252) and substantial deviations from typical seasonality (d > 1.2) compared with normal months.
AI.conclusion: Malaria burden alone is insufficient for understanding transmission dynamics. Distinguishing between where malaria is most prevalent and where transmission patterns are most unusual yields a richer view of risk. The anomaly-based framework can enhance surveillance systems, guide prioritization of field investigations, and support more precisely targeted control strategies in Ghana and potentially other endemic settings.
